title: David Lieb
body:
# David Lieb

David Lieb is a YC Group Partner. He went through YC in Summer 2009 with his startup Bump, which let users share contact information and photos by physically bumping their phones together. Bump was one of the first mobile apps to reach more than 100 million users, but it did not work as a business [1]. After pivots into photo sharing and photo management, Bump was acquired by Google. Lieb's last app formed the basis for Google Photos, which he worked on for nearly a decade. Google Photos serves well over a billion users [1].

## Worldview

Lieb's advice is grounded in the painful experience of building a product with impressive surface metrics (100 million users) that masked fundamental retention problems. His central framework -- [[Cohort Retention]] -- reflects a hard-won conviction that the only thing that matters is whether users keep using the product over time. He describes Googling "cohort retention" after a VC meeting where he gave "a very hand-wavy answer" to the question -- a mistake he aims to save other founders from [1].

His perspective is quantitative but practical. He warns against the most common self-deception patterns in retention analysis because he committed every one of them at Bump: widening time periods to make curves look better, picking too-easy actions, and trusting analytics dashboards without understanding what they actually measure [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **Cohort Retention Curves**: The shape matters more than the absolute number. Curves that get flat signal PMF; curves heading to zero do not. See [[Cohort Retention]] for the complete framework [1].
- **The Triangle Chart and Layer Cake**: Visual tools for tracking retention by cohort over time [1].
- **The Sitting-Next-To-Your-Customer Rubric**: For choosing what action to track, imagine sitting next to a customer and watching them use the product. Whatever signals real value to you in that moment is the right metric [1].

## Key Example: Google Photos

Google Photos had weekly cohort retention curves that dropped off quickly but flattened between 20-40%. "80% of my users leave pretty immediately" sounds bad, but the 20% who stayed demonstrated they would use the product every week "for basically forever." Six weeks after launch, Lieb was confident Google Photos would eventually reach 20% of all humans on the planet. Four years later, it had exceeded a billion users [1].

## References

1. [How to Improve Cohort Retention](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LV-how-to-improve-cohort-retention) -- David Lieb (2024)
2. [How David Lieb Turned a Failing Startup Into Google Photos](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ly-how-david-lieb-turned-a-failing-startup-into-google-photos) -- David Lieb (2024)

title: Kevin Hale
body:
# Kevin Hale

Kevin Hale is a YC Group Partner and co-founder of Wufoo, the form-builder acquired by SurveyMonkey. He is known for his talk on pitch deck design, which introduced the [[Legible Simple Obvious]] framework.

## Worldview

Hale approaches startup communication as a design problem. His central argument is that most pitch decks fail not because the underlying business is bad, but because the presentation is poorly designed. The same principles that make products easy to use -- clarity, simplicity, and obviousness -- apply to investor pitches [1].

He draws on his experience at Wufoo, where the company became known for obsessive attention to user experience and for practices like sending hand-written thank-you notes to users. Hale carries this same attention to craft into how he teaches founders to communicate.

## Key Frameworks

**[[Legible Simple Obvious]]**: Hale's framework for evaluating any piece of communication, particularly pitch decks. Legible means the audience can physically read and parse the information. Simple means the core message is not buried under complexity. Obvious means the conclusion the presenter wants the audience to reach is unmistakable [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Hale is distinctive for treating the pitch deck as a product design challenge rather than a sales exercise. Where other pitch advisors focus on narrative and persuasion, Hale focuses on information design -- font sizes, data density, visual hierarchy, and cognitive load.

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) -- Kevin Hale (December 2015)

title: Tom Blomfield
body:
# Tom Blomfield

Tom Blomfield is a YC Group Partner who founded two billion-dollar companies: GoCardless (a recurring payments company, similar to Stripe) and Monzo (an online bank with 8 million customers in the UK). He also worked at Grouper, a YC-backed dating/social startup that ultimately failed.

## Worldview

Blomfield approaches startups through the lens of metrics, unit economics, and retention. His lectures are among the most data-rich in the YC canon, providing specific benchmarks rather than general principles. He draws heavily from his experience at Monzo, where he saw firsthand how negative unit economics can threaten a scaling consumer company and how they can be fixed.

His central belief: metrics are instruments in a cockpit. Without them you fly blind. But too many metrics, or the wrong ones, are equally dangerous. The ideal is a blend of four to five key metrics, direct customer conversations, and product intuition [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Layer Cake vs. Leaky Bucket**: Blomfield's most distinctive visualization. High-retention businesses stack monthly cohorts into a "layer cake" of growing revenue; low-retention businesses are "pouring water into a leaky bucket" [1]. He argues that 20% retention that flattens is better than higher initial retention that decays to zero [1].

**Value-Based Pricing**: Price at 25-50% of the value delivered to the customer. Never start with cost (that leads to underpricing). Cost is only a floor. The value equation also defines success metrics for pilots [3].

**Organic vs. Paid Growth**: For consumer companies, at least 50% organic growth is necessary. The best consumer companies achieve 80-100% organic growth. Relying on paid acquisition enriches Google and Meta while driving your margins to zero [2].

**The Magic Moment**: A specific user behavior that predicts long-term retention. Facebook: adding 7 friends in 10 days. Monzo: adding 3 friends to send/receive money. Once identified, re-engineer onboarding to push users toward it [2].

## Distinct Perspective

Blomfield is unusually candid about failures. He discusses Monzo's negative unit economics (losing 30-40 pounds per customer), his dating startup Grouper's failure, and specific measurement mistakes. He provides concrete numbers where most speakers offer generalities: retention benchmarks by vertical, growth rate thresholds, NPS targets, and net dollar retention ranges.

He is also the most explicit YC voice on the danger of AI costs for startups: foundation model credits from OpenAI/Anthropic are real COGS, not free money, and will eventually be billed at full price [1].

## References

1. [Key Startup Metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) -- Tom Blomfield (2023)
2. [Consumer Startup Metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KT-consumer-startup-metrics) -- Tom Blomfield (2023)
3. [How To Price For B2B](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LI-how-to-price-for-b2b) -- Tom Blomfield (2024)
4. [Backstory: Tom Blomfield](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KY-backstory-tom-blomfield) -- Y Combinator (2023)

title: Michael Truell
body:
# Michael Truell

Michael Truell is co-founder and CEO of Anysphere, the company behind Cursor, one of the fastest-growing AI coding tools. He co-founded the company at age 23.

## Worldview

**Taste is the enduring human skill.** In a world where code generation is cheap, the differentiator is knowing what good software feels like. Truell hired Cursor's first 10 employees for their aesthetic judgment about user experience as much as their technical ability [1].

**The goal is beyond code.** Cursor's long-term vision is a world where programming languages are an intermediate representation, not the primary interface. The human specifies intent; the machine handles implementation. Truell sees programming as a transitional technology -- powerful but destined to be abstracted away [1].

**Years of false starts are normal.** Cursor's success followed years of wrong directions: the team initially worked on AI-powered CAD tools before pivoting to code. Multiple rewrites and product directions preceded the breakthrough. Truell does not frame this as wasted time but as essential learning about what AI tools needed to be [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **Taste-first hiring**: Select for aesthetic judgment and product intuition, not just coding ability. The first 10 hires set the culture [1].
- **Pivoting toward leverage**: When choosing between multiple promising directions, choose the one where AI capability creates the most leverage over the status quo [1].

## Advice to Engineering Students

In a separate interview, Truell advises engineering students to focus on building things that people actually use, rather than optimizing for academic metrics. The experience of shipping a product to real users and watching how they interact with it teaches more about product development than any course [2].

## References

1. [Cursor CEO: Going Beyond Code, Superintelligent AI Agents and Why Taste Still Matters](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oOylEw3tPQ8) -- Michael Truell, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)
2. [Michael Truell: Building Cursor at 23, Taking on GitHub Copilot, and Advice to Engineering Students](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Michael Truell (2025)

title: Emmett Shear
body:
# Emmett Shear

Emmett Shear is co-founder of Justin.tv and Twitch. His contribution to the YC knowledge base centers on product development methodology -- specifically, how to decide what to build next as a company evolves through different stages.

## Worldview

Shear views product prioritization as a progression through distinct frameworks, each suited to a different stage of company maturity. He rejects one-size-fits-all approaches and instead maps frameworks to conditions. A startup's job is not to pick the "best" prioritization method but to recognize which one fits its current circumstances [1].

He is notably skeptical of idea validation: "Validating an idea you already have is an anti-goal. You can't improve an idea through any amount of validation" [1]. The goal of customer research is to generate better ideas, not to confirm existing ones.

## Key Frameworks

**Three Prioritization Frameworks**: Built For Me (founder as exemplar user), Switch To Us (interview-driven competitive displacement), and Three Numbers Matter (metric-driven focus areas). Startups progress through them in order [1].

**The Three-Number Rule**: Three is the maximum number of focus areas people can keep in mind consistently. "X, Y, and Z" feels natural; "W, X, Y, and Z" does not [1].

**Operational Excellence as Hygiene**: For startups, winning through pure operational excellence is rare. Do just enough that it is acceptable to customers, then focus on what they really care about [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Shear is one of the few YC voices to provide a complete product prioritization system. Most YC advice focuses on what to build (something people want) rather than how to decide what to build next. Shear's framework fills this gap with concrete methods for each stage.

His candor about the limitations of each framework is distinctive. He openly acknowledges that certain product types (new categories where users cannot articulate preferences and the founder is not the user) do not fit any framework well. "It's really really hard!" [1].

## References

1. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- Emmett Shear (n.d.)

title: Aaron King
body:
# Aaron King

Aaron King is the founder and CEO of [[Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles|Snapdocs]] (YC W14), the leading digital closing platform for the mortgage industry, valued at $1.5B. He built the company with minimal outside funding through multiple market cycles.

## Worldview

King's philosophy centers on discipline forged by constraints. He argues that capital efficiency is not just a necessity for bootstrapped companies but a virtue that should persist even after raising large rounds. Capital constraints forced good behaviors — ruthless prioritization, deep customer involvement, short feedback loops — that served Snapdocs well even after raising more funding [1].

He views market cycles as natural and unavoidable rather than threatening. His approach is to selectively ignore some market changes while reacting quickly to others, filtered through alignment with resources, vision, and a north star metric (market share growth) [1].

King is candid about the cost of his approach: the company built many things not engineered for scale or flexibility. But he frames this as a worthwhile trade because "we can afford to reengineer those unscalable solutions because we built something people want" [1].

## Key Frameworks

### Selective Ignoring
Not every market change requires a response. When well-resourced and growing, ignore downturns and stay focused on market share. When the market demands trendy technology that underlying factors will prevent from scaling, ignore it or invest minimally — even over the protests of the sales team [1].

### Concentric Circles for Decision Communication
When rapid directional changes are needed, work outward: start with 3-4 people for the hardest conversations, expand to senior leadership to arm them for communication, then hold a company-wide meeting. This process took about two weeks during Snapdocs' COVID pivot [1].

### Effectiveness vs. Efficiency Toggle
Companies should ask: "Is what I did yesterday the thing that's going to get me to where I need to be tomorrow?" At times, prioritize effectiveness (expand aggressively). At others, return to efficiency (ruthless prioritization). The toggle is driven by market conditions [1].

### Intellectual Honesty About Growth
Resist taking credit during up cycles. Message to the team: the industry works in cycles, do not overly congratulate yourselves now, and do not be too hard on yourselves during inevitable downturns [1].

## Distinct Perspective

King's perspective is shaped by building a company in the mortgage industry — a sector defined by dramatic cyclicality driven by interest rates and economic conditions. Most startup advice assumes continuous growth; King's experience provides a rare lens on how to build through contraction and expansion. His advice on hiring (be fearless about recruiting talented people into risky startups) and on transparency (share cash balance, growth rate, burn, and biggest challenges with the entire team) reflect someone who learned by doing rather than theorizing.

## References

1. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — Aaron King, Lindsay Amos (2022)

title: Jessica Livingston
body:
# Jessica Livingston

Jessica Livingston is a co-founder of Y Combinator (with [[Paul Graham]], Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell). She is the author of "Founders at Work" (2007), a book of interviews with early-stage startup founders. Graham gave her the nickname "the social radar" for her focus on the personal and personality dimensions of founders, complementing his more technical evaluation [2]. After 11 years of working on YC and seeing over 1,000 companies go through the program, she delivered a synthesis of her observations in a 2017 talk called "How Not to Fail" [1].

## Worldview

Livingston's advice is structured as seven things to remember. Her recurring themes:

**Make something people want, above all else.** "Nothing else you do will matter if you are not making something people want. You can be the best spokesperson, the best fundraiser, the best programmer, but if you aren't building a product that satisfies a real need, you'll never succeed" [1].

**Focus is what separates winners.** "One of the most conspicuous patterns we've seen among the thousand startups we've funded is that the most successful founders are always totally focused on their product and their users. To the point of being fanatical" [1].

**Denial is the silent killer.** Growth targets force honesty: "The numbers, good or bad, are staring you in the face" [1]. Many founders say "we're not focusing on growth right now" -- and the outcome is usually predictable [1].

**Overhiring kills.** "Overhiring is the big mistake in the second phase of a startup, as making something no one wants is in the first phase" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Livingston provides a list of distractions she has seen derail founders, which she calls "the startup equivalent of wolves in sheep's clothing": grabbing coffee with investors, talking with acquirers, networking, recruiting boards of advisors, doing partnerships, spending time on PR before PMF, arguing on social media, going to conferences, and worrying about being a woman in tech [1].

On the last point, she is direct: "While I'll tell you that it is going to be harder for you as a woman, it's not going to be so much harder that it will make the difference between success and failure. If you want to start a startup, just go ahead and do it" [1].

## Role in YC's Founding

YC originated from a failed attempt to join a Boston VC firm. The firm took so long in their process that during the waiting period, Livingston and Graham spent nightly dinners brainstorming what a better early-stage funder would look like [2]. Graham would suggest ideas and eventually said, "Let's just start our own thing" [2].

The original plan was to fund companies asynchronously under the name "Cambridge Seed." The batch model came later. Key decisions that shaped YC's DNA [2]:
- **Standardized legal paperwork** -- unprecedented at the time, eliminating the need for expensive legal representation.
- **Standard deal terms** -- a single, non-negotiable deal structure.
- **Weekly dinners as events** -- VCs did one or two events per year; YC did them weekly.
- **Believing in founders before success** -- Livingston describes this as the core of YC's DNA: "We love founders. It's not about the returns. It's about the founders themselves" [2].

## The First Batch (Summer 2005)

The first batch included eight companies. Among them: [[Sam Altman]], Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian (Reddit), Justin Kan and Emmett Shear (Twitch), and Aaron Swartz [2]. Paul cooked chili in crock pots for the weekly dinners [2].

Livingston recalls: "It was like one of the most fun times in my life because everyone really wanted to be there and really wanted to start a startup" [2]. Steve Huffman's mother was convinced YC was a scam [2].

## The DNA of YC

Livingston identifies elements that have remained constant for nearly 20 years [2]:

**Community.** Alumni returned to help new batches, creating a flywheel of knowledge and support.

**Events as core infrastructure.** "Any time you can bring people together with an event, good things always happen" [2].

**Believing in people pre-success.** Most investors prefer to socialize with famous founders. YC partners engage most deeply with founders before they have achieved anything. "Confidence is a huge thing that YC brings to the table" [2].

**Resisting bureaucracy.** Graham resisted: "I don't do meetings." Livingston describes 99% of incoming requests as "useless time-wasting" [2].

## References

1. [How Not to Fail](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5l-how-not-to-fail) -- Jessica Livingston (2017)
2. [How YC Was Created with Jessica Livingston](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LX-how-yc-was-created-with-jessica-livingston) -- Jessica Livingston, [[Garry Tan]], [[Jared Friedman]], [[Harj Taggar]], Diana Hu (2024)

title: Pete Koomen
body:
# Pete Koomen

Pete Koomen is a YC Group Partner and co-founder of Optimizely (YC W10), which he scaled to $100M ARR. His background is technical (CTO), making his perspective on sales particularly valuable: he learned to sell as an engineer and argues that technical founders are often better at sales than they expect.

## Worldview

Koomen believes sales is a learnable skill, not a dark art. "Sales isn't about tricking people. It's fundamentally about helping people solve their problems, and engineers are great at doing that" [1]. His central argument: founders must sell before hiring salespeople, because pre-PMF sales requires vision, credibility, and tight product feedback loops that only founders can provide [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Founder-Led Sales Funnel**: Prospect, outreach, qualify, demo, price, close, implement -- each step has specific founder actions. The most common mistake at every stage is not asking enough questions [1].

**Demo as Story**: Great demos are not feature tours. They are structured like movies: introduce the main character (the user), establish the problem, then show the resolution. Personalize with the customer's logo, website, and team names [1].

**Implementation as Sales**: "The single biggest mistake founders make is thinking implementation is the customer's job." Optimizely lost six-figure customers who never ran a single A/B test because marketing could not convince engineering to install the code [1].

**Conversion Design**: From his design review work, Koomen emphasizes reducing friction to the "aha moment." Every step between landing on a website and experiencing value should be scrutinized for removal [2].

## Distinct Perspective

As a technical co-founder who became a skilled seller, Koomen directly contradicts the assumption that engineers cannot sell. He provides the most detailed step-by-step sales playbook in the YC canon, with specific email templates, question frameworks, and pricing tactics drawn from Optimizely's growth.

## References

1. [Enterprise Sales for Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LF-enterprise-sales-for-founders) -- Pete Koomen (2024)
2. [How to Convert More Visitors into Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KD-how-to-convert-more-visitors-into-customers) -- Aaron Epstein, Pete Koomen (2023)

title: Harj Taggar
body:
# Harj Taggar

Harj Taggar is a partner at Y Combinator and co-founder of Triplebyte (YC S15), which he ran for five years. His experience building and ultimately winding down a recruiting marketplace gives him a distinctive perspective on both the hiring process and the category of startup ideas that failed pre-LLM but work now.

## Worldview

**Hiring is a founder's most underrated skill.** Taggar's body of work at YC centers on making the hiring process more systematic and less painful. He treats hiring as analogous to sales: it requires a refined pitch, a structured funnel, and relentless follow-through [1] [2].

**AI resurrects failed startup categories.** Having personally experienced the limitations of pre-LLM technology at Triplebyte -- spending years building labeled datasets and managing a three-sided marketplace to do what AI can now do on day one -- Taggar is a leading voice on which old ideas deserve a second look. His recruiting marketplace experience led directly to his enthusiasm for companies like Mercor and Appora [3].

**LLMs have distinct personalities.** Through internal work at YC building scoring rubrics for investor evaluation, Taggar discovered that O3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro handle the same rubric very differently: O3 is rigid and penalizes exceptions, while Gemini reasons through edge cases like "a high-agency employee" [4].

## Key Frameworks

- **The personal network exhaustion method**: For first hires, systematically work through every person you know, ask for intros, and repeat [1].
- **Speed as competitive advantage**: Every candidate who drops out before receiving a reply is a failure, not evidence they "weren't interested" [2].
- **The collapsed marketplace test**: What multi-sided marketplaces become simpler when AI replaces the human intermediary? [3]

## References

1. [How to Hire Your First Engineer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer) -- Harj Taggar (July 2018)
2. [Convincing Engineers to Join Your Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team) -- Harj Taggar (July 2018)
3. [Startup Ideas You Can Now Build With AI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K4s6Cgicw_A) -- Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Jared Friedman, Diana Hu (2025)
4. [State-Of-The-Art Prompting For AI Agents](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DL82mGde6wo) -- Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Diana Hu, Jared Friedman (2025)
5. [How to Spend Your 20s in the AI Era](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ShYKkPPhOoc) -- Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Diana Hu, Jared Friedman (June 2025)

title: Paul Graham
body:
# Paul Graham

Paul Graham is a programmer, essayist, and co-founder of Y Combinator (with Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell). Before YC, he co-founded Viaweb in 1995 with Robert Morris, which was acquired by Yahoo in 1998 and became Yahoo Store. His essays on startups, published primarily at [paulgraham.com](http://www.paulgraham.com), constitute one of the most influential bodies of startup advice in existence. He is the single most prolific source in this wiki, with 14 essays spanning 2005 to 2020.

## Worldview

Graham's worldview on startups rests on a small number of principles he returns to across all his essays:

**Make something people want.** This is the Y Combinator motto, originally chosen as the text for the Summer Founders Program T-shirt [7]. Graham treats it as the root cause of nearly all startup success and failure: "In a sense there's just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want. If you make something users want, you'll probably be fine, whatever else you do or don't do" [2]. Every other piece of advice flows from this. "The most reliable way to become a billionaire is to start a company that grows fast, and the way to grow fast is to make what users want" [14].

**Determination matters more than intelligence.** "The most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination" [3]. Graham found this somewhat depressing but arrived at it empirically. VCs invest in professors; they should invest in students. Microsoft, Yahoo, and Google were all founded by dropouts [3].

**Startups are counterintuitive.** Nearly every natural instinct about startups is wrong [5]. The correct approach often feels wrong: release before you are ready, do things that don't scale, don't worry about the business model, ignore competitors. "Starting a startup is a task where you can't always trust your instincts" [5].

**The organic method for ideas.** The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of them but to notice problems -- "Live in the future, then build what's missing" [1]. Deliberately generated ideas are dangerous because they sound plausible but lack real demand [1].

**Users over everything else.** Users are the most powerful force acting on a startup. "If they take you up, no competitor can keep you down" [3]. The three internal threats that kill startups -- disputes, inertia, and ignoring users -- are all more dangerous than any external competitor [3].

**Truth over performance.** Graham consistently argues that the way to persuade is not through salesmanship but through genuine understanding. In fundraising, the way to seem formidable is to stick to the truth [13]. In YC interviews, the best preparation is to deeply understand your users rather than rehearsing a pitch [14]. The throughline: founders who truly understand something will communicate it convincingly without tricks.

**Power law thinking.** Startup outcomes follow an extremely steep power law. Roughly 15 startups per year become the big successes, and investors treat this as binary [13]. This shapes everything about how fundraising works: investors are looking for any chance, however small, of being one of those 15.

**Startups are designed to grow fast.** A startup is not merely a newly founded company or one that works on technology -- it is a company designed to grow fast. Everything else associated with startups -- venture funding, exits, technology focus -- follows from this single property [12].

**Authenticity of motivation.** Graham distinguishes between founders driven by genuine interest in the problem and those driven by money or status. The former build billion-dollar companies; the latter take the first acquisition offer or find easier ways to seem cool [14]. When YC detects exploitative founders, it rejects them -- not on moral grounds alone, but because bad people make bad founders [14].

## Distinct Perspective

What makes Graham's lens distinctive:

**Anti-credentialism.** Graham consistently argues that formal business knowledge, MBA degrees, and "work experience" are unnecessary and potentially harmful. "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup" [4]. Getting a job may actually make you less able to start a startup by "turning you into a tame animal" [4]. He openly mocks the concept of enterprise content management solutions and the vocabulary of business school [3].

**The negative approach.** Rather than prescribing what to do, Graham often works by elimination -- listing reasons not to do something and showing they are bogus [4], or listing 18 specific mistakes to avoid [2]. His essay "Why to Not Not Start a Startup" systematically dismantles 16 common excuses.

**Concrete over abstract.** Graham's essays are full of specific companies, dollar figures, and named examples. Artix and Viaweb. Stripe's Collison installation. PayPal nearly switching to Windows. Wufoo sending hand-written thank-you notes. The Apple II being cheap because Woz was poor. These specifics carry his arguments more than any general principle.

**Historical perspective.** Graham frequently places startups in deep historical context. Getting a job is only a hundred years old as the default way to make a living [4]. The shift to startups may be comparable to the shift from farming to manufacturing. Medieval serfs would have found it insanely risky to move to a city of three thousand strangers [4].

**The theoretical voice.** Graham is the most theoretical voice in the YC canon. Where others offer tactical advice, Graham provides the conceptual architecture. His "Startup = Growth" essay is one of the most referenced pieces in the YC library because it provides the definition that everything else builds on. His writing style is distinctive for its use of mathematical reasoning and analogies to other domains (scientific research, evolution, optimization) [12].

## Key Frameworks

Graham is responsible for several frameworks that have become standard vocabulary in the startup world:

- **[[Schlep Filter]]** / Schlep Blindness: The unconscious mental filter that prevents you from seeing good ideas because they involve tedious work. Stripe is the canonical example [1].

- **[[Default Alive or Default Dead]]**: The binary diagnostic for startups past their first 8-9 months. Will you reach profitability on the money you have? Half the founders Graham asks do not know [8].

- **[[Equity Equation]]**: The 1/(1-n) formula for whether any trade of company equity is worth it [9].

- **The Well Model**: Good startup ideas look like wells (narrow but deep), not broad shallow holes. Facebook and Microsoft both started as wells [1].

- **Sitcom Startup Ideas**: Ideas generated by trying to think of startup ideas, which sound plausible but nobody actually needs. "A social network for pet owners" [1].

- **The Still Life Effect**: Smart people's tendency to commit to the first idea they have, then rationalize the time invested [7].

- **Relentlessly Resourceful**: The two-word description of the ideal startup founder -- not merely determined, but adaptive. "'Make something people want' is the destination, but 'Be relentlessly resourceful' is how you get there" [6].

- **The Contained Fire**: Starting with a deliberately narrow market to get it really hot before expanding [10].

- **Collison Installation**: The technique of immediately setting users up rather than sending them a link. Named after the Stripe founders [10].

- **Pulling a Meraki**: For hardware startups, assembling the product yourself initially instead of waiting for factory production [10].

- **The Fatal Pinch**: Default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [8].

- **Playing House**: Young founders who go through the outward motions of a startup (raise money, rent office, hire people) while neglecting the one essential thing: making something people want [5].

- **Growth as Compass**: Use a target weekly growth rate to make every decision. "Anything that gets you the growth you need is ipso facto right" [12]. This converts the bewildering problem of starting a startup into an optimization problem.

- **The S-Curve**: Successful startups follow three phases -- slow/no growth while figuring things out, rapid growth as product-market fit is found, and eventual slowdown from internal limits and market saturation [12].

- **YC Growth Benchmarks**: 5-7% per week is good during YC; 10% is exceptional; 1% signals the startup has not figured out what it is doing [12].

- **The Formidability Test**: Formidable means "justifiably confident" -- someone who seems like they will get what they want regardless of obstacles. Most investors decide within minutes whether a founder is a winner or a loser. The way to pass this test without faking it is to genuinely understand why your startup is worth investing in [13].

- **The Larval Market**: A small but growable market. Apple's market in 1976 is the perfect example -- not many people wanted a computer, but the market was growing. The ideal is founders who are "living in the future" at the leading edge of change [14].

- **The "Why Now?" Question**: Every company that gets really big is riding an external wave. The critical question is why this idea only recently became possible [13].

- **Expected Value of Startups**: The high failure rate is not a bug. If a successful startup can make a founder $100 million, even at 1% probability the expected value is $1 million. For the right people, the probability may be 20-50% [12].

## Evolution of Thinking

Graham's position on certain topics shifted over the decade and a half covered by these essays:

**On when to start.** In 2005-2007, Graham was actively pushing the age of founders lower, trying to find the lower bound. YC began as a summer program partly to let college students participate [7]. By 2014, he explicitly told Stanford students "Do not start a startup in college" and argued that most 20-year-olds should be searching breadth-first, not depth-first [5]. The shift reflects experience: too many young founders were "playing house" rather than solving real problems.

**On cofounders from college.** Remained consistent: school is the best place to find cofounders, and the window closes quickly after graduation [11] [4].

**On the importance of ideas.** Shifted from "we fund people, not ideas" (going so far as accepting applications with no idea in 2007 [4]) to a richer view that the organic method of idea generation -- living in the future and building what is missing -- is actively the best strategy, not just a fallback [1] [5].

**On fundraising.** His 2013 essay on convincing investors emphasizes truth and domain expertise over salesmanship [13]. By 2020, he reiterates this even more strongly: the best preparation for a YC interview is talking to users, not rehearsing slides [14].

## Personal Experience

Graham draws heavily on his own experiences as a founder. His startup Viaweb (originally called Webgen, then renamed after losing the name) made software for building online stores. Before Viaweb, he and Robert Morris started a company called Artix to put art galleries on the Web, which failed because galleries did not want to be online [7]. The Artix experience taught him the lesson that would become YC's motto: make something people want [7].

He references his own poverty as a Viaweb founder: he and Morris were 29 and 30 but lived like 23-year-olds with roughly zero assets [11]. Viaweb's top service tier was $300/month, an order of magnitude below the norm, because that seemed like a lot of money to them [11]. At Viaweb, when merchants refused to use their software, Graham and Morris built stores for them -- selling luggage, pens, and men's shirts. He describes feeling "pretty lame" but recognizes in retrospect it was exactly the right thing [10].

He was also, before Viaweb, "trying to be a starving artist" in New York, which is how he came up with the doomed gallery idea [7].

## Recurring Examples

Certain companies and people appear across many of Graham's essays as touchstones:

- **Stripe** -- The Collison installation, the schlep filter, manual merchant accounts behind the scenes [1] [10]
- **Airbnb** -- Going door to door in New York, waiting 4 months to hire, the rolly bags at YC dinners [8] [10]
- **Facebook** -- The contained fire (Harvard only), Mark Zuckerberg as someone who succeeded despite being a "complete noob at startups" [1] [5] [10]
- **Microsoft** -- The well model (Altair Basic), Traf-o-data as the Artix-equivalent failure [1] [7]
- **Apple / Wozniak** -- The TV monitor, cheap because Woz was poor, "all the best things I did came from not having done it before" [1] [11]
- **Google** -- Larry and Sergey initially tried to sell the algorithm, made search work then monetized, investors created grief [2] [3]
- **Viaweb** -- Graham's own startup, used as a recurring case study throughout [3] [7] [10] [11]
- **Sam Altman** -- Cited as the most successful young founder YC had funded (at 19), seeming "like he had a 40-year-old inside him" [4] [11]
- **Wufoo** -- Released the form-builder before the database, hand-written thank-you notes [3] [10]
- **PayPal** -- Nearly switched to Windows; Max Levchin showed it scaled only 1% as well [2]
- **Pebble** -- Assembled first several hundred watches themselves, learned the value of good screws [10]
- **Dropbox** -- Rejected by Boston VCs, illustration of how investors miscalculate market size [13]

## References

1. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- Paul Graham (November 2012)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- Paul Graham (October 2006)
3. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- Paul Graham (April 2006)
4. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- Paul Graham (March 2007)
5. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- Paul Graham (October 2014)
6. [Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful) -- Paul Graham (March 2009)
7. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- Paul Graham (April 2005)
8. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- Paul Graham (October 2015)
9. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) -- Paul Graham (July 2007)
10. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- Paul Graham (July 2013)
11. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- Paul Graham (October 2006)
12. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- Paul Graham (September 2012)
13. [How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) -- Paul Graham (August 2013)
14. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) -- Paul Graham (December 2020)

title: Brad Flora
body:
# Brad Flora

Brad Flora is a Y Combinator group partner who has seen startup fundraising from every angle: as founder of Perfect Audience (YC S11, acquired 2014), as one of Silicon Valley's most prolific angel investors (150 YC company investments through a fund, including Retool, Razorpay, and OpenSea), and as a YC partner [1]. He is a core participant in the "Group Partner Lounge" video series alongside [[Michael Seibel]] and [[Harj Taggar]].

## Worldview

Flora's advice centers on demystifying processes that founders over-complicate. His recurring themes:

**Fundraising is a grind, not a spectacle.** Actual fundraising looks like a bunch of coffee chats and Zoom calls, not Shark Tank [1]. Flora dispels myths systematically: you do not need money before starting, your startup does not need to be impressive (it needs to convince, not impress), and SAFEs have made early fundraising fast, cheap, and founder-friendly [1].

**Build first, explain simply.** Retool's founder David had no deck -- he just opened his laptop, showed the software, made a crude internal tool in minutes, and talked about why early customers liked it. That was enough to raise at what became a $4B valuation [1].

**Believe the no, not the why.** When investors reject you, accept the rejection but do not internalize their stated reasons. Investors are not experts on your business, and the stated reason is rarely the real one [3]. The way to change an investor's mind is not with words but with actions -- make progress, and they will come back [3].

## Key Frameworks

- **Seven Fundraising Myths**: Fundraising is glamorous (no, it is coffee chats), you need money before starting (no, build first), your startup needs to be impressive (no, convince not impress), fundraising is complicated (no, SAFEs), you'll lose control (no, SAFEs give no board seats), you need a fancy network (no, make something people want), rejection means bad startup (no, even $3.7B WhatNot could barely raise at seed) [1]
- **The KPI Test for Pivoting**: If you do not have a main KPI you are obsessively tracking, you may not realize your business is not working. Flora discovered this when Creative Market's marketplace revenue gave immediate clarity that Color Lovers' community never did [5]
- **The Civilization Fallacy**: Founders who want to treat their startup like a real-time strategy game, clicking on things from a computer and watching numbers go up, rather than doing actual sales [6]

## References

1. [How Startup Fundraising Works](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Il-how-startup-fundraising-works) -- Brad Flora (n.d.)
2. [YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- Multiple speakers (n.d.)
3. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: Investors Said No, Now What?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J1-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-investors-said-no-now-what) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (n.d.)
4. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: Don't Make These Hiring Mistakes](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J2-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-don-t-make-these-hiring-mistakes) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (n.d.)
5. [YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- Multiple speakers (n.d.)
6. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: The Ideal Customer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J5-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-the-ideal-customer) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (n.d.)

title: Garry Tan
body:
# Garry Tan

Garry Tan is President and CEO of Y Combinator. Prior to leading YC, he was a partner at YC (2011-2015), co-founded Initialized Capital (an early-stage venture fund), and co-founded Posterous (YC S08). He was early at Palantir, which directly informs his advocacy for the [[Forward Deployed Engineers]] model. His content bridges the technical founder and investor perspectives.

## Worldview

**The "Why Now" question is paramount.** Tan's central thesis is that the most important question for any startup is "Why now?" [2]. Startup opportunities arise from three forces: new technology (orders-of-magnitude improvements), behavior change (mass adoption of new platforms), and regulation (government mandates creating waves of demand) [2].

**Utility is the only real measure.** Tan urges founders to "think about the area under the curve of utility that you could contribute to society" and warns that status-seeking without substance is the path to disgrace: "When you think about SBF, when you think about Theranos, when you peel back a little bit, you realize there's nothing. It was a lie" [8].

**The forward deployed engineer is the ideal founder.** Drawing on his Palantir experience, Tan argues that the best founders combine deep technical ability with the willingness to sit next to end users and understand their workflows at the most granular level. This is how "a really good engineer with a weak handshake" beats Salesforce, Oracle, or Booz Allen [7].

**First principles over analogy.** The educational and professional systems are designed to keep people "in the box" [1]. The three qualities needed to break out: curiosity (asking why repeatedly), empathy (direct experience with users), and serenity (fighting battles you can win) [1].

**Platform neutrality matters.** Tan draws parallels between net neutrality fights and the current AI platform landscape, arguing that government intervention to prevent self-preferencing is necessary for a healthy AI ecosystem [6].

## Key Frameworks

- **The "Why Now" Test**: Every startup idea should have a clear answer to why this can succeed now when it could not before. Instacart succeeded where Webvan ($800M loss) failed because smartphone penetration enabled an on-demand labor force [2]. General Magic failed at smartphones in 1994 because the technology was 16,000x worse than what the iPhone delivered 13 years later [2].
- **First Principles via Curiosity, Empathy, Serenity**: DoorDash exemplifies serenity -- retreating to suburbs where competitors were not, finding profitability, then using those profits to win cities [1]. Airbnb exemplifies empathy -- the founders' experience of literally hosting guests gave them direct conviction [1].
- **Collapsed marketplaces**: AI turns three-sided or four-sided marketplaces into two-sided ones by replacing the human intermediary [6].
- **The FDE test**: Can you sit next to the end user, understand their workflow, and return with a demo that makes them say "I've never seen anything like that"? [7]
- **Follow curiosity, not customer discovery**: In the AI era, living at the edge of technology and following genuine curiosity yields better ideas than extensive pre-build customer discovery [6].
- **Underserved Audiences in Fintech**: Through Kai Stinchcombe (True Link Financial), choosing a specific underserved customer yields lower CAC, higher LTV, and distribution advantages [3].

## Distinct Perspective

Tan repeatedly argues that the best investments come from understanding technology curves and timing -- a skill he developed by funding Instacart ("I saw the future literally right before my eyes in my iPhone") [2]. He is more explicit than most YC partners about the role of government and regulation in creating startup opportunities [2].

## References

1. [3 Steps for Billion Dollar Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Js-garry-s-channel-3-steps-for-billion-dollar-startups-how-airbnb-doordash-succeeded) -- Garry Tan (2020)
2. [Why Now? The Key to Million Dollar Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jt-garry-s-channel-why-now-the-key-to-million-dollar-startup-ideas) -- Garry Tan (2021)
3. [An Interview with Kai Stinchcombe](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ju-garry-s-channel-an-interview-with-kai-stinchcombe-founder-of-true-link-financial) -- Garry Tan (2021)
4. [YC's Group Partners Share Productivity Tips](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/K5-yc-s-group-partners-share-productivity-tips-and-advice-on-actually-getting-things-done) -- Garry Tan et al. (2023)
5. [How Athelas Is Revolutionizing Healthcare](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/K6-how-athelas-is-revolutionizing-healthcare) -- Garry Tan (2023)
6. [Startup Ideas You Can Now Build With AI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K4s6Cgicw_A) -- Garry Tan, [[Harj Taggar]], [[Jared Friedman]], Diana Hu (2025)
7. [State-Of-The-Art Prompting For AI Agents](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DL82mGde6wo) -- Garry Tan, [[Harj Taggar]], Diana Hu, [[Jared Friedman]] (2025)
8. [How to Spend Your 20s in the AI Era](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ShYKkPPhOoc) -- Garry Tan, [[Harj Taggar]], Diana Hu, [[Jared Friedman]] (June 2025)

title: Anu Hariharan
body:
# Anu Hariharan

Anu Hariharan is a partner at YC Continuity, the growth-stage investment arm of Y Combinator. She is one of the most prolific contributors to the YC Library on international growth strategies, Chinese technology ecosystems, board governance, and growth team building.

## Worldview

Hariharan's work reveals several consistent themes:

**International case studies as strategic blueprints.** She repeatedly analyzes Chinese technology companies not as distant curiosities but as blueprints for what will eventually happen globally. Her analyses of [[WeChat]], [[Pinduoduo]], and [[Toutiao]] are structured as lesson sets for Western founders, extracting principles that transcend the Chinese market.

**Social dynamics drive commerce and engagement.** Across her work on Pinduoduo and WeChat, Hariharan returns to the idea that social interaction is a fundamental and underserved layer of online commerce and content. She argues that "Web 1.0 platforms -- including Amazon -- optimize for efficiency and don't serve trust-based categories very well" [2] and that shopping is inherently social.

**Network effects at the group level.** Hariharan draws heavily on Reed's law (the value of a group-forming network grows exponentially with the ease of group formation) rather than Metcalfe's law (value proportional to users squared). She identifies Pinduoduo as having "one of the most powerful network effects in the world" [2] because it taps into groups of social relationships rather than one-to-one connections.

**Board management as a learnable discipline.** In contrast to the anxiety many founders feel, Hariharan treats board management as a set of concrete practices: meeting cadence, deck structure, preparation timelines, and communication rhythms [4]. Her approach is operational and template-driven, drawing on real examples from companies like Gusto, Brex, GitLab, Faire, and Segment.

**Data network effects compound product quality.** Her Toutiao analysis emphasizes that algorithms without data are useless and data without an addictive product is unavailable. The virtuous cycle of user engagement, data accumulation, and algorithmic improvement is a core pattern she identifies across multiple companies [3].

**Growth as a scientific discipline.** Hariharan treats growth as a scientific discipline, not a collection of hacks. She is explicit that "growth hacking" is not a term growth experts relate to -- "hacking implies a haphazard/gut-driven approach, and the reality is quite the opposite" [5]. Companies that achieve sustained growth have intentional, metrics-driven teams that thrive on experimentation.

## Distinct Perspective

Hariharan is distinctive in the YC ecosystem for her deep focus on Chinese technology companies as sources of strategic insight for Western founders. While most YC content is US-centric, Hariharan provides detailed case studies with product screenshots, user flow diagrams, and growth metrics from platforms that most Western founders have never used. She consistently argues that Chinese tech innovations will be replicated globally.

Her approach to growth is methodical and benchmark-driven. She provides specific retention targets by vertical, team composition formulas, and organizational structures backed by consensus from 25 growth experts at companies including Facebook, Airbnb, Uber, Stitch Fix, Square, Slack, and Instagram [5].

## Key Frameworks

- **Content Lifecycle** (from Toutiao analysis): Creation, Curation, Recommendation, Interaction [3]
- **Team Purchase model** (from Pinduoduo): Group buying as a social commerce mechanic that creates network effects [2]
- **Board meeting preparation timeline**: A 28-day preparation framework used by Brex [4]
- **Group Effect**: Product features designed around group behavior rather than individual behavior (from WeChat/Pinduoduo) [1] [2]
- **Retention Before Growth**: Do not invest in growth until retention is proven. The leaky bucket problem wastes resources and jeopardizes the company's future [5]
- **Retention Benchmarks by Vertical**: Specific long-term retention targets (e.g., 55% median for social networks at month 12, 29% for travel at year 2) [5]
- **Absolute Goals**: Growth goals must be absolute numbers, not percentages. "Achieve 5M first-time room nights" not "improve conversion by 10%" [5]
- **Growth Team Composition**: Year 1 team = 1 Growth PM + 2-3 Growth Engineers + 1-2 Data Scientists. First hire when ~15 engineers on the product team [5]

## References

1. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) -- Anu Hariharan (n.d.)
2. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) -- Anu Hariharan, Nic Dardenne (2020)
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) -- Anu Hariharan (n.d.)
4. [How to Create and Manage a Board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) -- Anu Hariharan (n.d.)
5. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- Anu Hariharan, Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)

title: Dalton Caldwell
body:
# Dalton Caldwell

Dalton Caldwell is Managing Director and Group Partner at Y Combinator, and the most prolific video content creator in YC history. He co-hosts the long-running "Dalton & Michael" series with [[Michael Seibel]], which spans 40+ episodes covering topics from startup ideas to fundraising to founder psychology. Before YC, Caldwell founded imeem (a social media and music platform acquired by Myspace) and App.net (a crowd-funded, decentralized social network). As Head of Admissions, he has read thousands of YC applications and conducted thousands of interviews, giving him a unique vantage point on what distinguishes successful applicants.

## Worldview

Caldwell's advice is grounded in psychological realism and pattern recognition from reading YC applications at massive scale. He focuses on what founders get wrong -- and the wrong things are often not what founders expect.

**Don't lie to yourself.** This is Caldwell's most consistent and repeated principle, appearing in his essay on [[Runway Management]] and running through all his advice [1]. Whether discussing low runway, fake [[Product-Market Fit]], or investor dynamics, he returns to the principle that founders must face reality. He systematically catalogs the rationalizations founders use to avoid confronting bad news -- from "the fatal pinch does not apply to me" to "investors will continue to fund my company" [1]. His two closing principles in the runway essay: "Don't lie to yourself" and "Act quickly and decisively" [1].

**Act quickly and decisively.** The complement to honesty. Once you see the facts clearly, the right move is usually to act immediately rather than wait for conditions to improve. Delaying action when a company is default dead only reduces leverage, because a startup's negotiating power deteriorates monotonically with declining runway [1].

**Conviction is the foundation.** Before convincing anyone else -- co-founders, employees, customers, investors -- you must convince yourself. "If you're not fully committed or if you have grave doubts that your idea is any good, how can you ever convince someone else? People can tell if you don't believe in the thing you're doing" [2]. The test: "Do you ever know that feeling where an idea enters your head and you can't sleep at night and you can't think about anything else? That's a good sign" [2].

**Rejection is a feature, not a bug.** Caldwell argues that successful founders are comfortable with rejection from potential customers. Protecting your ego by not talking to users kills startups. The willingness to hear "no" from customers is what separates founders who find PMF from those who don't [5].

**Investors cannot fix your company.** Caldwell is blunt about the limits of investor advice. Founders often give investors power over their business out of fear and self-doubt. But "all they can do is use words" -- investors are not going to beat you up or kidnap your family [10].

**Don't think like a VC.** Founders who approach idea selection with market-sizing frameworks, competitive analysis, and TAM calculations before talking to a single customer are making a category error. "These frameworks are not useful when applied to a startup with no customers, no product, that's not even sure what they should be working on" [16].

**Cargo culting is pervasive.** Caldwell warns against copying the surface behaviors of successful companies without understanding the underlying dynamics. Having a fancy office because Google has one, or hiring a VP of Sales because Salesforce has one, misunderstands causation [11].

**Information diet matters.** "Your thought process as a founder is heavily influenced by the five most common information sources you get." Caldwell urges founders to be deliberate about consuming media that inspires building, not media focused on fundraising and startup celebrity [3].

**Equally hard, so aim high.** "It would appear that it's equally hard to succeed in a startup that you don't care about and one that you do. And it's almost equally hard to succeed in an audacious idea as something incremental. If those are equally difficult, do you see why you should definitely do the way more audacious idea?" [2].

**Game theory applies to startups.** Caldwell uses game theory to explain why generosity and ethical behavior are rational strategies for founders. Positive-sum thinking -- making deals where both sides win -- builds lasting relationships and reputation. Short-term extraction destroys optionality [13] [14].

## Distinct Perspective

Where [[Paul Graham]] provides conceptual architecture and [[Michael Seibel]] provides operational process, Caldwell diagnoses the mental traps and emotional patterns that cause founders to make irrational decisions. His "Dalton & Michael" videos often function as therapy sessions for founders, normalizing the emotional difficulty of startups while insisting on clear-eyed self-assessment.

Caldwell is the more technically-minded of the pair and tends to anchor arguments in what he has seen in applications rather than in theory. He is blunt about protecting technical founders from exploitation and about the gap between startup mythology and reality.

He is notable for his contrarian takes on Silicon Valley culture: challenging the "move to SF or fail" orthodoxy [8], critiquing conformity in a culture that claims to prize nonconformity [12], and using the "midwit meme" to argue that both overthinking and underthinking can be fatal [15]. He is also deeply skeptical of startup "expertise" from people who have not themselves built companies, and he warns against rage-bait discourse (bootstrap vs. VC, college vs. no college) that generates engagement but distorts reality [17] [20].

## Key Frameworks

- **The Leverage Graph**: For companies with declining runway, leverage deteriorates over time. Investors and acquirers benefit by stalling until the startup has no leverage [1].
- **Seven Counterproductive Ideas**: Specific thought patterns that cause founders to rationalize staying default dead, from "the fatal pinch doesn't apply to me" to "my employee morale will plummet if I cut costs" [1].
- **Idea Quality Score**: Rate ideas 1-10 on four dimensions (market size, founder/market fit, problem certainty, unique insight) and take the average [6].
- **Relationship Debt**: Analogous to technical debt -- unresolved co-founder tensions accumulate until the relationship breaks [7].
- **The Information Diet**: Consuming social media and investor Twitter degrades founder performance the same way junk food degrades physical performance [3] [19].
- **Self-Conviction First**: Convince yourself, then others will follow. If you are not obsessed with the idea, no pitch will save you [2].
- **Equally Hard Principle**: Ambitious ideas and incremental ideas are equally hard to execute, so always choose the ambitious one [2].
- **The Pivot Test**: With Seibel, Caldwell distinguishes between pivoting based on market signal (good) and pivoting because you're bored or discouraged (bad) [4].
- **Stop Innovating on the Wrong Things**: Every startup has a limited supply of "innovation juice." Spend it on making something people want, not on novel corporate structures or unusual tools. "Making a startup work is a miracle. The odds that you're going to perform five miracles at once is much, much lower" [18].
- **The Bootstrap vs. VC Non-Debate**: "The vast majority of businesses should not raise venture capital. And venture capital is not interested in investing in the vast majority of businesses." VC is for businesses that can return 100-1000x [22].
- **Hero Worship Is Dangerous**: Sam Altman's first startup was Loopt (a standard mobile app). Elon Musk started with conventional .com businesses. Peter Thiel went to Stanford and law school. "Most mere mortals need to do step one" [21].
- **Ordinary vs. Extraordinary**: Being average among smart people is not enough. The failure rate is so high that you must be many standard deviations better [9].
- **The Two Mindsets That Kill**: Excessive optimism (refusing to see problems) and excessive pessimism (paralysis and inaction) [23].
- **Setting Smarter Goals**: Goals must be numeric, time-bound, and aggressive enough to force creativity [24].
- **The Irreplaceability Test**: 90-day evaluation of whether losing someone would be devastating -- "If this person disappeared tomorrow, how hosed are you?" [25].
- **ZIRP Recovery Signals**: Executive turnover, founder re-engagement, return to office, elimination of vanity projects as positive signs of recovery from zero-interest-rate-era bloat [26].
- **Luck Creation**: Systematically putting yourself in situations where good things can happen, rather than avoiding risk of rejection [27].

## Personal Experience

Caldwell draws heavily on his experience running imeem. He was a neighbor to Facebook in a Santa Clara data center, where he personally witnessed their early server architecture -- each labeled with a university name, each running a separate PHP and MySQL instance [28]. At imeem, the team converted their music streaming product to run on video infrastructure because the open-source video tools were better than music-specific alternatives -- every music file was technically a video file with a zero-bit video field [28]. At Twitch (where he worked closely with Seibel), the team built a button to make any page static during traffic spikes, used "Find My Friends" on iPhones to track delivery drivers at DoorDash, and blocked video for ISPs that refused free peering relationships [28].

## References

1. [Advice for Companies with Less Than 1 Year of Runway](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway) -- Dalton Caldwell (n.d.)
2. [How Do You Convince Someone to Join Your Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5b-how-do-you-convince-someone-to-join-your-startup) -- Dalton Caldwell (2019)
3. [Consume Information That Encourages You to Do More](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5a-consume-information-that-encourages-you-to-do-more) -- Dalton Caldwell (2019)
4. [All About Pivoting](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6p-all-about-pivoting) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
5. [Successful Founders Are OK with Rejection](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DV-dalton-michael-successful-founders-are-ok-with-rejection) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
6. [Where Do Great Startup Ideas Come From?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DU-dalton-michael-where-do-great-startup-ideas-come-from) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
7. [Co-Founder Mistakes That Kill Companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DZ-dalton-michael-co-founder-mistakes-that-kill-companies-and-how-to-avoid-them) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)

title: Gustaf Alströmer
body:
# Gustaf Alströmer

Gustaf Alströmer is a Y Combinator group partner (since 2017), former Head of Growth at Airbnb, and a YC founder from the 2007 batch. He provides the most systematic treatment of user research and early-stage sales in the YC canon.

## Worldview

Alströmer's advice is grounded in direct user contact as the foundation of everything. His recurring themes:

**Users keep you honest.** Users and customers are the only stakeholders actually paying you anything. If anyone will tell you the truth, it will be them [1]. Great founders talk to future customers before they even have a product, and the very best founders learn directly from users throughout the lifetime of their company [1].

**Sales is a numbers game.** Most founders do not send enough outreach because they do not work backwards from their goals [2]. Sending 100 emails and getting zero customers is not evidence that sales does not work -- it is evidence of insufficient volume. Successful startups internalize this [2].

**Focus on problems, not features.** The biggest danger during user interviews is focusing on features instead of problems. Users generally have good problems but bad solutions [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Alströmer bridges growth strategy at scale (his [[Growth]] team guide synthesizes advice from 25 growth experts [3]) with ground-level tactical advice for pre-revenue startups. Where other partners give philosophical advice, Alströmer provides specific email templates, conversion funnel math, and CRM tool recommendations [2].

His background at Airbnb gives him firsthand knowledge of how doing things that don't scale transitions into scalable growth. He emphasizes that Airbnb's eventual growth channels (word of mouth, Google search, referrals, Facebook advertising) are not how they got their first customers. They did things that did not scale [2].

## Key Frameworks

- **The Six Interview Questions**: A structured framework for user interviews: how do you do X today, what is hardest, why is it hard, how often, why is it important, what do you currently do to solve it [1]
- **The Sales Funnel Math**: Working backwards from goals through conversion rates (outreach -> open -> response -> demo -> close -> onboard) [2]
- **Easiest Customers First**: Sell to people you know, then startups, then larger companies. Most people are not early adopters [2]
- **Retention Benchmarks by Vertical**: Specific long-term retention targets for social networks (45-65%), on-demand (20-30%), travel (20-35%), e-commerce (10-25%), and subscription (25-35%) [3]

## References

1. [How to Talk to Users](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iq-how-to-talk-to-users) -- Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)
2. [How to Get Your First Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ip-how-to-get-your-first-customers) -- Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)
3. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)

title: Ali Rowghani
body:
# Ali Rowghani

Ali Rowghani is a partner at YC Continuity and the former COO of Twitter. Before Twitter, he served as CFO of Pixar, where he worked closely with Ed Catmull, Steve Jobs, John Lasseter, and Bob Iger. His writing for the YC Library draws heavily on these experiences to develop frameworks for leadership measurement and CEO scaling.

## Worldview

Rowghani's thinking is built around several core convictions:

**Leadership is measurable.** In a startup culture "obsessed with management by metrics," Rowghani observed that founders struggle to measure their own leadership [1]. He rejects measuring leadership through business results (because "timing and luck can be confounding factors") and instead proposes a three-trait framework that is both diagnostic and actionable.

**Trust is the foundation of all leadership.** Despite working with four leaders of radically different styles, Rowghani concluded that great leadership reduces to the ability to build trust. Trust has a "science" (accurate predictions, good people decisions) and an "art" (empathy, integrity, self-awareness) [1].

**The CEO's job changes dramatically as the company scales.** Rowghani describes three distinct phases: Doer-in-Chief (building product), Company-Builder-in-Chief (building the organization), and investment in transformative new products [2]. The transition between phases is the critical juncture where many startups fail.

**Delegation is the key to CEO scaling.** The CEO must replace themselves in every Phase 1 function by hiring leaders "better than you" into each role [2]. The measure of success is unscheduled time. Steve Jobs told him: "When I'm at my best, 50% of my time is unscheduled" [2].

**Culture cannot be authored by one person.** Unlike mission and strategy, culture must be co-created by founders and early employees to feel authentic [2]. Values that do not reflect past behavior feel contrived.

## Distinct Perspective

Rowghani is unique in combining deep experience at a pre-IPO creative company (Pixar), a high-growth technology company (Twitter), and an accelerator (YC Continuity). His leadership framework draws on observations of four very different leaders rather than abstract principles, lending it a biographical specificity rare in startup advice. He is also unusual in emphasizing the "art" of trust alongside its "science."

## Key Frameworks

- **[[Three Traits of Great Leadership]]**: Clarity of thought/communication, judgment about people, personal integrity/commitment [1]
- **[[Mission-to-Metrics Framework]]**: Mission (purpose) → Strategy (direction) → Metrics (pace and performance) [2]
- **Three Phases of CEO**: Doer-in-Chief → Company-Builder-in-Chief → Transformation investor [2]
- **Three Non-Delegable CEO Tasks**: Hiring leadership team, creating purpose and alignment, nurturing culture [2]
- **Leadership Survey Framework**: Parallel CEO/employee questions measuring the three traits [1]

## References

1. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — Ali Rowghani (n.d.)
2. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — Ali Rowghani (n.d.)

title: Blake Scholl
body:
# Blake Scholl

Blake Scholl is the founder and CEO of [Boom Technology](http://boomsupersonic.com/) (YC W16), a company building supersonic passenger airplanes. He holds a background in computer science and previously co-founded a startup acquired by Groupon. He has been a pilot since college [1].

## Worldview

Scholl believes the biggest ideas are actually easier, not harder, because they motivate the founder, attract exceptional talent, and create energy that smaller ideas cannot match. He argues against starting a startup just to start one: "Start a startup when you're like, 'I must make this happen'" [1].

He traces aviation's stagnation directly to the disappearance of founders from the industry, making the case that innovation in any industry requires founder-led companies rather than optimization by professional managers [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Descending Awesome List**: Make a list of startup ideas in descending order of how awesome it would be if they worked, ignoring all other considerations. Then work down the list. "I figured, 'I'll cross things off the list and I'll end up working on idea number five.' And, as luck had it, the more I got into supersonics, the more I learned that it's not crazy" [1].

**Persistence as Sales Strategy**: "Be so persistent that you're sure you're being annoying. And you'll feel like you must be annoying, but when you have that feeling that means you're probably doing it right" [1].

**Deadline + Sweetness**: Have a believable deadline and make the deal as attractive as possible for the other side. Demo day served as Boom's forcing function with Virgin [1].

**Progress That Is and Looks Like Progress**: For hardware companies at YC, find something that represents real technical advancement and that audiences can appreciate. Avoid the traps of meaningful-but-unappreciable progress and showy-but-empty demos [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) -- Blake Scholl (n.d.)

title: Sam Altman
body:
# Sam Altman

Sam Altman is CEO of OpenAI and former president of Y Combinator. He ran YC from 2014 to 2019 and taught Stanford's CS183B startup class. At OpenAI, he has led the development of GPT-3, GPT-4, ChatGPT, and the O-series reasoning models. Within the YC canon, his writing and talks cover the full lifecycle of a startup from pre-growth through later-stage scaling.

## Worldview

Altman's worldview centers on sequencing, discipline, and a conviction that intelligence improvements compound into civilizational-level change.

**Product quality above all else.** His 2018 Startup School talk opens with what he considers the number one lesson: "The degree to which you're successful approximates the degree to which you build a product that is so good people spontaneously tell their friends about it" [4]. He considers this 80% of the work of a successful startup.

**Growth follows product love.** Founders who focus on growth before achieving [[Product-Market Fit]] are building a "leaky bucket" [1]. Facebook had a product people talked about obsessively from the start; Airbnb slogged for 1,000 days before discovering how to make their product loved [1].

**Ride exponential market growth.** Altman urges founders to identify markets about to undergo exponential growth. "11 years ago, the market for iPhone apps was zero. If you only think about the TAM today, you'll make a big mistake" [4]. The key is distinguishing real trends from fake trends: real trends have early adopters who use the product obsessively; fake trends have buyers who do not actually use the product [4].

**Ambitious ideas attract talent.** "It's easier to start a hard startup than an easy startup" because ambitious projects attract better people. "Why is employee 20 gonna join? Why does this matter to the world?" [4].

**Never lose momentum.** "One of the most important jobs you have as a founder is to never lose momentum." Startups survive on cadence -- continuing to win on short, predictable intervals [4].

**Ambition compounds.** At OpenAI, "we said 'Okay, we're going to go for AGI.' 99% of the world thought we were crazy. 1% of the world, they really resonated with" [8]. The 1% who resonated were the people who built ChatGPT and the GPT series.

## Key Frameworks

**The Leaky Bucket**: Growing before the product is loved creates a leaky bucket -- users come in but do not stay [1].

**The Alignment Test**: Ask 10 employees what the company's top three goals are. If they do not agree, the founder is not communicating effectively. Altman reports that 100% of founders predict alignment and 100% of the time, employees disagree [2].

**Airplane vs. Spaceship**: Most successful startups are airplanes (~$100M outcomes), not spaceships (billion-dollar). Founders and investors get misaligned when both pretend the airplane is a spaceship [3].

**Real vs. Fake Trends**: Real trends have obsessive early adopters who tell friends. Fake trends have buyers who rarely use the product [4].

**Startups Beat Big Companies in Three Ways** [4]:
1. **One yes vs. all yeses.** A startup needs one investor to say yes; a product manager at a big company needs everyone to say yes.
2. **Fast-changing markets.** The more decisions required, the more a startup's speed advantage compounds.
3. **Big platform shifts.** Large companies are not good at making big strategic pivots.

**10-Year Commitment**: Having a strategy that assumes 10 years of work is "an extreme advantage that few founders leverage" [2].

## The OpenAI Origin Story

Altman shared the ChatGPT origin at AI Startup School [8]: the team had built GPT models that were powerful but not widely used. The decision to wrap the model in a simple chat interface was not obvious internally. It became the fastest-growing consumer product in history, reaching 100 million users in two months. The product decision (making it conversational and accessible) mattered as much as the underlying technology [8].

## On AI Hardware

Altman revealed that OpenAI is investing in custom AI hardware, viewing compute as the fundamental bottleneck. He described himself as "CEO of compute" and sees controlling the hardware stack as essential to OpenAI's long-term position [8].

## Distinct Perspective

Altman is unusual in the YC canon for explicitly addressing what happens when things go well but not spectacularly. Most YC advice assumes either massive success or failure. Altman fills the gap for the large middle ground: companies that work but are not on a path to massive scale [3]. He is also distinctive for framing startups as opportunity discovery: "the entire startup ecosystem is best set up to support companies that have a low chance of success, but are huge if they work" [4].

He occupies a unique position at the intersection of the startup world (as former YC president) and the AI frontier (as OpenAI CEO). At AI Startup School 2025, he stated: "In 10 or 20 years, unless something goes hugely wrong, we'll have unimaginable superintelligence" and called the current moment "the best time ever in the history of technology, ever, period, to start a company" [8].

## References

1. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) -- Sam Altman (n.d.)
2. [Later Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) -- Sam Altman (n.d.)
3. [Navigating Success for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4s-navigating-success-for-your-startup) -- Sam Altman (n.d.)
4. [How to Succeed with a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (2018)
5. [Live Office Hours with YC's Paul Graham and Sam Altman](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/84-live-office-hours-with-yc-s-paul-graham-and-sam-altman-2013) -- Sam Altman, [[Paul Graham]] (2013)
