6. [Live Office Hours with Sam Altman and Yuri Sagalov](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8D-live-office-hours-with-sam-altman-and-yuri-sagalov) -- Sam Altman (n.d.)
7. [How to Pick Which Startup to Work At](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/AQ-how-to-pick-which-startup-to-work-at) -- Sam Altman (n.d.)
8. [Sam Altman: The Future of OpenAI, ChatGPT's Origins, and Building AI Hardware](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=V979Wd1gmTU) -- Sam Altman (June 2025)

title: Jared Friedman
body:
# Jared Friedman

Jared Friedman is a partner at Y Combinator and co-founder of Scribd. He leads YC's work on hard tech and biotech companies and has built much of YC's internal AI tooling.

## Worldview

**Hard tech and biotech need founder-friendly infrastructure.** Friedman's work on university spinouts and biotech funding reflects a belief that the startup model -- speed, founder control, iterative development -- can work in domains traditionally dominated by slow-moving academic and corporate research [1] [2].

**Prompting is the new coding.** Through building internal AI tools at YC, Friedman has developed practical techniques for production prompt engineering, including the "debug info" pattern where the LLM reports what is confusing about its instructions -- creating a to-do list for the developer [3].

**Being early looks like being wrong.** Friedman's observation about the 2019 MLOps wave -- more YC applications for ML tooling than for companies using ML -- captures a pattern he has seen repeatedly. Companies like Replicate, Ollama, and Deepgram built in obscurity for years before their markets materialized. The lesson: following genuine curiosity about technology is the best predictor of being in the right place when the market arrives [4].

## Key Frameworks

- **The debug info pattern**: Give the LLM an output parameter for complaining about underspecified instructions, then use those complaints as a development to-do list [3].
- **Follow curiosity, not market timing**: The companies that were positioned for the AI wave followed genuine interest in ML, not market analysis [4].
- **Fork and merge prompts**: The emerging pattern of separating system prompts (company-wide) from developer prompts (customer-specific) to scale without becoming a consulting company [3].

## References

1. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- Jared Friedman (n.d.)
2. [How Biotech Startup Funding Will Change in the Next 10 Years](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4L-how-biotech-startup-funding-will-change-in-the-next-10-years) -- Jared Friedman (n.d.)
3. [State-Of-The-Art Prompting For AI Agents](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DL82mGde6wo) -- Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Diana Hu, Jared Friedman (2025)
4. [Startup Ideas You Can Now Build With AI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K4s6Cgicw_A) -- Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Jared Friedman, Diana Hu (2025)
5. [How to Spend Your 20s in the AI Era](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ShYKkPPhOoc) -- Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Diana Hu, Jared Friedman (June 2025)

title: Aaron Harris
body:
# Aaron Harris

Aaron Harris is a partner at Y Combinator. He is a key contributor to YC's fundraising infrastructure, having created the canonical YC seed deck template and co-authored the Series A diligence checklist that emerged from YC's Series A program.

## Worldview

Harris's advice is built around a principle of radical simplicity:

**Clarity and concision above all else in fundraising.** Harris argues that most seed-stage companies do not have meaningful detail to explore, and when founders pretend otherwise, "their stories get muddled, and the investors get lost" [1]. He distills the entire philosophy of seed pitching to: "Focus on narrative. The rest is commentary" [1].

**Operational preparation reduces closing friction.** In his work on Series A diligence, Harris emphasizes that the closing process is where many founders lose time and momentum. Having a complete data room before signing a term sheet can save a week [2]. His approach is procedural and checklist-driven, reflecting a belief that fundraising overhead can be minimized through advance preparation.

**Team trumps advisors.** In discussing the team slide of a pitch deck, Harris is blunt: "This should be about founders. Nobody cares about your advisors" [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **YC Seed Deck Template**: A 10-slide structure covering title, problem, solution, traction, metrics, insights, business model, market, team, and the ask [1]
- **Series A Diligence Checklist**: Comprehensive checklist for data room preparation, covering corporate records, IP, securities, material agreements, litigation, and employee information [2]

## References

1. [How to Build Your Seed Round Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2u-how-to-build-your-seed-round-pitch-deck) — Aaron Harris (March 2018)
2. [Series A Diligence Checklist](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3h-series-a-diligence-checklist) — Aaron Harris, Jason Kwon (n.d.)

title: Michael Seibel
body:
# Michael Seibel

Michael Seibel is CEO of Y Combinator and a former founder. He cofounded Justin.tv (which became Twitch, sold to Amazon for ~$1B) and Socialcam. His writing and talks draw heavily on concrete operational lessons from these experiences. With 20+ entries in the YC library -- both solo talks and the "Dalton & Michael" series with [[Dalton Caldwell]] -- he is one of the most prolific contributors to the YC canon, covering the full founder journey from "should I start a startup?" through PMF and fundraising.

## Worldview

Seibel's advice is grounded in a belief that startups are primarily execution problems, not idea problems:

**Start with problems, not ideas.** "There's a common misconception that your idea has to be great in order to start a company" -- Justin.tv's original concept was an online reality TV show, which eventually became Twitch [7]. He recommends keeping "problem books" rather than idea books [7].

**Process over talent.** His [[Product Development]] guide is built on the premise that a well-structured development cycle with clear goals, inclusive brainstorming, difficulty grading, and consensus-based prioritization can make even an imperfect team productive [1]. The enemy is not lack of talent but lack of structure.

**Market first, solution second.** "Founders often hold too tightly onto solutions and too loosely onto problems. The problem, i.e. the market, is the real opportunity" [8]. The founder's v1 idea is usually wrong; "the market pulls product out of the startup" [8].

**Products are not art.** "If users don't find products useful, then the products are by definition not useful, and they're a waste of your time to build" [9]. The startup world is unforgiving to artists who refuse to listen to users [9].

**Find desperate customers.** "How do you find people who are willing to use a bad product? They have to be the most desperate" [9].

**Equal equity as motivation.** Seibel advocates equal or near-equal co-founder equity splits as "what we almost always recommend at YC." His reasoning: "Your first thought has to be, 'How do I come up with an equity split that's going to maximize the motivation of my teammates?'" [6].

**Urgency about time.** Seibel places high value on the 20s as the prime working decade. "Every year you are not doing a startup you are losing 10% of this prime time" [2].

**Users you don't want.** Seibel introduces the concept of "users you don't want" -- people who try to hijack your product to solve a problem you did not intend to solve [10].

**Investor as boss is fatal.** One of the five things that kill post-seed startups. "All they can do is use words. If you do not want your investors to have power over you, don't give it to them" [11].

**Three things saved Justin.tv/Twitch** [9]: (1) an extremely technical founding team, (2) spending almost nothing ($500/month per person walking-around money), and (3) ego fully tied to the startup's success. Remove any one and the company would have died [9].

## Key Frameworks

- **Product Development Cycle**: Two-week cycles, one product meeting per cycle, three possible goals, inclusive brainstorm with difficulty grading, consensus-based selection, mandatory analytics [1].
- **Five Things That Kill Post-Seed Startups**: Fake PMF, investor as boss, co-founder conflict, reaching for ordinary, slow product development [11].
- **The Real PMF Test**: Not headcount or fundraising, but "a frantic founding team trying to deal with ever-growing numbers of happy, loyal, and ideally paying customers" [8].
- **Hair on Fire**: If someone's hair is on fire, they will use even a brick to put it out. Find problems that dire [8].
- **Customer Pitch vs. Investor Pitch**: Two fundamentally different pitches for two fundamentally different audiences [12].
- **Problem Quality**: Evaluate problems on frequency, intensity, willingness to pay, and reachability of customers [9].
- **Cold Email Formula**: 60 seconds or less. Problem, solution, traction, market, team, controversial insight [13].
- **Equal Equity Split**: Vesting with a one-year cliff is the safety net; equity inequality kills motivation [6].
- **Order of Operations**: Problem, unique qualification, MVP, hand-picked users, iterate [7].
- **Three Paths in Tech**: Founder, executive, employee -- each with distinct strategies and trade-offs [4].
- **Users You Don't Want**: Not every user who shows up is your user [10].
- **Silicon Valley Fundraising Advantage**: Investors who have missed big deals are psychologically different from investors who have not [14].

## Distinct Perspective

Compared to [[Paul Graham]]'s conceptual architecture and [[Dalton Caldwell]]'s psychological focus, Seibel is the most operational voice in the YC canon. His posts follow a pattern of "here is the broken version, here is what we learned, here is the process that fixed it." He provides specific playbooks -- how to run a product meeting, how to structure a press pitch, how to evaluate whether an early hire is essential.

Seibel is unusually direct about the costs of being a founder: "It probably won't maximize your personal earnings" and the commitment level "can significantly hurt personal relationships" [4].

## References

1. [Guide to Product Development](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4e-guide-to-product-development) -- Michael Seibel (August 2016)
2. [A Letter to College Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students) -- Michael Seibel (March 2017)
3. [Getting Press for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup) -- Michael Seibel (February 2017)
4. [Three Paths in the Tech Industry](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee) -- Michael Seibel (September 2017)
5. [YC's Essential Startup Advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) -- Y Combinator (September 2018)
6. [How to Split Equity Among Co-Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5x-how-to-split-equity-among-co-founders) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
7. [How to Get and Test Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7x-how-to-get-and-test-ideas) -- Michael Seibel (2018)
8. [The Real Product-Market Fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5z-the-real-product-market-fit) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
9. [Building Product](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7s-building-product) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
10. [User You Don't Want](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/67-user-you-don-t-want) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
11. [The 5 Things That Kill Startups Post Seed Rounds](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8a-the-5-things-that-kill-startups-post-seed-rounds) -- Michael Seibel (2020)
12. [How Pitching Investors Is Different Than Pitching Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7u-how-pitching-investors-is-different-than-pitching-customers) -- Michael Seibel (2018)
13. [How to Cold Email Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/65-how-to-cold-email-investors) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
14. [Why Fundraising Is Different in Silicon Valley](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/63-why-fundraising-is-different-in-silicon-valley) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
15. [Biggest Mistakes First-Time Founders Make](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/66-biggest-mistakes-first-time-founders-make) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
16. [Order of Operations for Starting a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/61-order-of-operations-for-starting-a-startup) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
17. [How to Plan an MVP](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6f-how-to-plan-an-mvp) -- Michael Seibel (2019)
18. [Should I Start a Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/60-should-i-start-a-startup) -- Michael Seibel (2019)

title: Andrej Karpathy
body:
# Andrej Karpathy

Andrej Karpathy is a computer scientist who has worked at Stanford, OpenAI, and Tesla (where he led the Autopilot vision team). He is known for making deep learning concepts accessible through teaching and writing.

## Worldview

**Software is entering its third era.** Karpathy frames the history of software as three paradigms: Software 1.0 (human-written code), Software 2.0 (neural network weights learned from data, which he [described in 2017](https://karpathy.medium.com/software-2-0-a64152b37c35)), and Software 3.0 (LLMs programmed via natural language prompts) [1]. Each transition changes not just the tools but the nature of who can build software and how.

**Technology diffusion has reversed.** LLMs are the first technology that reaches everyday users before it reaches expert developers. Non-technical people can get useful output from natural language immediately, while developers are still building the tooling and paradigms. Karpathy describes this as [power going to the people](https://karpathy.bearblog.dev/power-to-the-people/) [1].

**We are building a new kind of computer.** The LLM is the CPU, English is the programming language, prompts plus tools are the applications. This reframes the entire software stack: instead of rigid UIs with predetermined flows, developers build tool ecosystems that agents navigate via natural language [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **Software 1.0 / 2.0 / 3.0**: A taxonomy for understanding how the programming interface has evolved from machine code to learned weights to natural language [1].
- **Vibe coding**: Karpathy coined or popularized this term for the practice of building software by describing what you want in natural language and letting AI generate the code. He [reflected on the practice](https://karpathy.bearblog.dev/vibe-coding-menugen) after building MenuGen this way [1].

## References

1. [Andrej Karpathy: Software Is Changing (Again)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LCEmiRjPEtQ) -- Andrej Karpathy (June 2025)

title: AI in Logistics
body:
# AI in Logistics

Logistics is emerging as one of the largest vertical markets for AI agents. The industry is characterized by high transaction volumes, manual processes, phone-heavy workflows, and thin margins -- making it ripe for AI automation [1].

## Why Logistics

The logistics industry runs on phone calls, emails, and spreadsheets. Freight brokers -- intermediaries who match shippers with carriers -- spend most of their day on calls negotiating rates, tracking shipments, and handling exceptions. Each interaction follows predictable patterns but requires enough judgment that simple automation has historically failed [1].

AI voice agents are particularly well-suited to this domain because:
- **High call volume**: A single broker may make 50-100 calls per day
- **Predictable conversation patterns**: Rate negotiations, shipment status updates, and exception handling follow templates
- **Clear success metrics**: Did the load get booked? At what rate? On time?
- **Willingness to pay**: Even small efficiency gains at scale justify significant software spend [1]

## Case Study: Happy Robot

Happy Robot, a YC company, has sold seven-figure contracts to the top three largest logistics brokers in the world for AI voice agents [1]. The company uses the [[Forward Deployed Engineers]] model: founders talk directly to CIOs of major logistics companies, rapidly customize the product, and close deals through impressive demos that competitors cannot match.

The progression from six-figure to seven-figure deals happened within months, demonstrating how quickly AI companies can move upmarket when the product genuinely solves the problem [1].

## The Broader Pattern

Logistics represents a template for how AI will penetrate traditional industries: start with the most repetitive, phone-heavy workflows; demonstrate 10x improvement in speed or cost; use the FDE model to close initial enterprise deals; then expand into adjacent workflows within the same customer [1].

## References

1. [AI Is Eating Logistics](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: Toutiao
body:
# Toutiao

Toutiao (meaning "today's headlines" in Chinese), a flagship product of ByteDance, is an AI-powered content discovery platform that grew to over 120 million daily active users in China [1]. The average user spends more than 74 minutes per day on the app, more than the average Facebook user and more than twice Snapchat's engagement [1]. More than half that time is spent watching short-form videos, with the platform exceeding 10 billion video views per day [1].

What makes Toutiao remarkable is that it achieved social-network-level engagement without a social graph, without explicit user inputs, and without product purchase history [1]. [[Anu Hariharan]] (who disclosed being a personal investor in Toutiao) analyzed the five hidden forces that drove its growth.

![Toutiao vs. Competitors Daily Time Spent](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/3ce55ba68c9f.png)

## Background

Toutiao launched in 2012 by ByteDance, founded in Beijing [1]. The app uses machine and deep learning algorithms to source and surface personalized content. Each user is measured across millions of dimensions based on taps, swipes, time spent on each article, time of day, pauses, comments, interactions, and location [1]. The result is a personalized content feed that requires no explicit user input.

Revenue growth was extraordinary: on target to hit more than 15 billion RMB (>$2.2 billion USD) by 2017, making it "one of the fastest growing apps in terms of revenue in the history of the internet" [1].

![Toutiao Revenue Growth](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/c9b5f444afbe.png)

## Force 1: Seize the Timing Gap

Toutiao launched as smartphone penetration in China surged from near zero in 2010 to 65% by 2014 [1]. Many large content providers had not yet developed mobile apps or mobile-friendly sites, so "true mobile-optimized information and entertainment was rare" [1].

By mid-2012, there were only six significant news apps on the Chinese Android platform: four were direct extensions of existing news portals with limited mobile optimization, and two were aggregators relying on slow, impersonal editorial curation [1].

Toutiao stepped into this gap with several deliberate design decisions [1]:
- **Zero onboarding friction**: No account creation, no password, no social media linking required
- **Intuitive design**: No tutorials needed
- **Immediate personalization**: From day one, the app tracked user behavior to power recommendations

The catchy name ("today's headlines") and icon drove excellent user growth. Toutiao hit 1 million DAUs only four months after launch [1]. The app was updated almost weekly throughout its first year, and by the time competitors arrived, "it already had an important and valuable foothold" [1].

[![Toutiao Personalized Feeds for Two Different Users](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/90e7a053ef4e.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Toutiao-2-1.png)

## Force 2: Data Network Effect Across the Entire System

Toutiao exhibits a classic data network effect: more users produce more data, more data makes the algorithms smarter, smarter algorithms produce better content, and better content attracts more users [1].

Toutiao applies this virtuous cycle to optimize every stage of what they call the "content lifecycle": Creation, Curation, Recommendation, and Interaction [1].

[![Toutiao Content Lifecycle](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/b040e075c650.jpg)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Toutiao-3-1.jpg)

### Creation: AI-Written Content

During the 2016 Olympics, Toutiao's bot Xiaomingbot published original news stories approximately 2 seconds after events ended, faster than any traditional outlet [1]. The bot-authored articles achieved read rates comparable to human-written articles [1]. Xiaomingbot published more than 8,000 stories total.

The technical approach combined [1]:
- Real-time score data from the Olympics organization
- Images from a recently acquired image-gathering company
- Live text commentary monitoring
- Grammar-based story template generation
- A ranking algorithm for selecting relevant commentary sentences
- Convolutional neural networks for contextual image recognition
- Sequence-to-sequence deep learning for article summarization

Toutiao started with four sports (Table Tennis, Tennis, Badminton, and Women's Soccer) chosen for technical tractability: the first three are turn-based with simpler rules, and Women's Soccer had a high-quality data source [1].

[![Xiaomingbot article example](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/5678238ea111.jpeg)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Toutiao-4.jpeg)

### Curation: Algorithmic Content Discovery

A major early engagement driver was "soft news" (celebrity gossip, pop culture, lifestyle) [1]. This content was distributed across a plethora of individual sites with no central access point. Toutiao centralized and optimized distribution, "reducing the time a user needed to find content to nearly zero" [1].

Algorithms provided three advantages over human editors: speed, scale, and per-user personalization [1]. Toutiao also uses text classification algorithms to identify fake news, clickbait, and content that does not meet quality standards, supplemented by user moderators and human arbitrators [1].

### Recommendation: The Core Engine

Content recommendation is what Toutiao is best known for. The engine solves a specific question: "what are the one hundred articles the platform can recommend to each user that are most likely to result in continued engagement?" [1]

The AI team discovered that 100 headlines is a retention threshold: "users that do not retain long-term tend to drop off dramatically after seeing ~100 headlines, similar to Facebook's '10 friends' rule" [1].

For each new user, the recommendation engine blends three signal types [1]:

1. **User profiles**: Built from demographics (age, location, gender, socioeconomic status)
2. **Content analysis**: Natural language processing determines trending status, article length, and timeliness (evergreen vs. short half-life)
3. **Context**: Location-related data (geography, weather, local news)

The system optimizes for two metrics: the percentage of articles a user clicks on and the percentage of articles a user finishes reading (measured by time on page) [1]. The engine learns quickly: "for most users, it takes less than one day to successfully learn their interests (indicated by 80% read rates)" [1]. The result is user retention above 45%, comparable to social networks [1].

### Interaction: Algorithmic Matchmaking

Toutiao developed a question-and-answer feature where AI matches question-askers with people who can answer them [1]. Their "Conditional Focused Neural Question Answering with Large-Scale Knowledge Bases approach" achieved 75.7% accuracy on a dataset of 108,000 questions, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by an 11.8% margin [1].

## Force 3: From Aggregation to Destination

Toutiao transitioned from content aggregation to content destination through two mechanisms [1]:

### Revenue Sharing for Creators

Starting in 2014, Toutiao rolled out incentive programs for content creators: office space, tools, minimum monthly guarantees tied to milestones, and revenue sharing from ad monetization [1]. By the time of writing, Toutiao hosted more than 800,000 Toutiaohao accounts (professional media, bloggers, influencers) [1].

### Algorithmic Audience Reach

For many creators, Toutiao's recommendation engine delivers larger and more relevant audiences than other platforms [1]. The example of "Huanzi TV," a creator of short videos about rural Chinese life, illustrates this: each video averaged 700,000 views on Toutiao versus less than 1/40th of that on their WeChat Official Account [1].

Content diversity is broad: top 20 categories account for only 60% of supply, with no single category exceeding 10% [1].

[![Toutiao content channels](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/06de1a2b708a.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Toutiao-5-1.png)

## Force 4: Format Flexibility

Rather than being rigid about core format, Toutiao expanded to new formats when data indicated they should [1]. In 2015, observing increased video content supply as connectivity improved, Toutiao added video capability and supported PGC short video content (1-5 minutes) [1].

In March 2016, Toutiao launched a separate short video app (later renamed Watermelon Video), powered by the same recommendation engine [1].

## Force 5: Early Monetization Aligned with Product

Toutiao's advertising model maps directly to its core strength: identifying what users want to see [1]. Ads are matched to users using the same proprietary recommendation technology. Three benefits [1]:

1. **Reduced friction**: Relevant ads feel less intrusive and can actually function as product discovery
2. **Higher ad rates**: Toutiao's native targeting solves the problem advertisers spend enormous sums trying to address
3. **More inventory**: Users reading and viewing content are more receptive to relevant ads

Third-party survey data estimated Toutiao's click-through rates to be 200% better than its peers [1].

## Broader Implications

Toutiao is "chipping away at their end goal, which is essentially to wipe away the concept of search and just serve up aggregated, hyper-relevant content" [1]. The success suggests that content aggregators that previously failed in the US may have been an "idea whose time is yet to come -- and that better algorithms will be the catalyst for success" [1].

## References

1. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Athelas
body:
# Athelas

Athelas is a healthcare technology company co-founded by Tanay Tandon and Deepa Kulkarni that evolved from a single blood diagnostic device into what [[Garry Tan]] describes as "Rippling for healthcare" -- a compound startup providing the operating system for running a healthcare practice [2]. In 2024, Athelas merged with Commure to form an entity valued at over $6 billion, with Tandon serving as CEO [2].

## Origin: Hackathon to Blood Diagnostics

Tandon and Kulkarni met Garry Tan when they won the YC hackathon as high school students [2]. They were working on blood test diagnostics: a device that analyzes a small volume of blood using computer vision and microfluidics to generate a complete blood count (CBC) [1] [2].

After dropping out of Stanford, they commercialized the device -- called the Athelas One, later the Athelas Home -- at a time when one-drop blood testing was "relatively out of favor" due to the Theranos scandal [2]. Tan notes this as a lesson for founders: "Even though the headlines might be saying one thing, the real builders know they should pursue that which is true and that is in front of them" [2].

The first years focused entirely on manufacturing, clinical trials, FDA clearance, and bringing the device into patients' homes. The device generates revenue primarily through pharmaceutical company partnerships, earning "tens of millions of dollars per year in real gross margin" [2].

## Expansion: The Compound Startup

The diagnostic device served as a Trojan horse into healthcare practices. As engineers spent time in clinics, they discovered the back-office problems providers face: billing, credentialing, eligibility verification, insurance portals -- all powered by decades-old software, much of it acquired by private equity and no longer actively maintained [2].

Tandon describes the landscape: "The future is already here but not evenly distributed. We spend a crazy percentage of our GDP on healthcare and yet we're stuck with some of the worst tools in the world" [2]. Providers must learn to use disparate tools across decades of software -- Availity for portals, Waystar for eligibility, various systems for credentialing -- before they can even bill for a claim [2].

Athelas built an integrated suite of software and hardware tools that became "the operating system for running a healthcare business" [2]. This follows the compound startup model pioneered by Rippling: instead of solving one problem well, solve thousands of specific problems in a single industry.

## Work Tax and AI

Tandon coined the term "work tax" to describe the administrative overhead in healthcare: "We walked into an old health system and they were giving out blood pressure devices. Six people needed to sign off on a clipboard before one blood pressure device got to a patient. That's all work tax" [2].

AI -- particularly large language models -- enables Athelas to compress and automate these workflows [2]. Their product Scribe runs during appointments: a microphone records the conversation between doctor and patient, automatically generates transcription, clinical notes, CPT codes, and the insurance claim. "For the private practices that use Athelas Scribe today, they've eliminated hours of work every day" [2].

Administrative spending accounts for 15-30% of all healthcare spending in the US [2]. Tandon frames the mission: if healthcare spending can be reduced from $4 trillion to $2 trillion over 20 years while improving outcomes, that creates "$2 trillion of non-inflationary stimulus in the American economy every year" [2].

## Financial Position

As of 2024, Athelas is near cash-flow positive with hundreds of millions in revenue, having raised capital but spent relatively little of it. The company describes itself as in "full-on aggressive wartime mode" and is hiring aggressively across engineering, design, and product [2]. Tandon notes that domain expertise is not required: "The best engineers at Athelas are those who are able to take concepts around abstraction, concepts around how to automate workflows, and apply them to healthcare. We'll teach you the healthcare piece" [2].

## References

1. [On Starting a Company from a Hackathon Project](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project) -- Tanay Tandon (n.d.)
2. [How Athelas Is Revolutionizing Healthcare](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/K6-how-athelas-is-revolutionizing-healthcare) -- [[Garry Tan]], Tanay Tandon (2023)

title: WeChat
body:
# WeChat

WeChat is a Chinese super app with 889 million monthly active users (as of 2016) who use the app an average of 50+ minutes and 9-11 separate times per day [1]. This usage matches the combined time users spend across the entire portfolio of Facebook apps (Instagram, Facebook, and Messenger) [1]. [[Anu Hariharan]], partnering with China Tech Insights (a Tencent research group), documented the growth strategy that took WeChat from 0 to 800M+ MAUs in less than six years.

WeChat has inspired a new category of "messaging as a platform" globally. Hints of WeChat's influence appear in Apple's iMessage and Facebook's Messenger platform [1]. As Hariharan puts it: "At this point, WeChat is not only an app -- it is simply the internet" [1].

## Lesson 1: Build Your Own Competition

Tencent was already one of China's top internet companies in 2010, with ~650 million monthly active instant messenger accounts through QQ, its desktop messaging app [1]. The company had created a mobile version of QQ in 2008, but it was "weighted down by desktop-centric features" like multiple user statuses, large file transfers, and embedded services [1].

Tencent made a decisive move: it assembled a small skunkworks team from the QQ Mail team (its core product at the time) to build a new mobile app that could potentially compete with other Tencent products [1]. This team of only seven engineers created the first version of WeChat in three months [1]. WeChat launched in January 2011 as a simple messaging and photo sharing app.

WeChat was not the first mobile messenger. Xiaomi's MiTalk led with 5 million registered users [1]. Initial growth was slow. The breakthrough came in May 2011 (three months after launch) when WeChat added voice messaging, allowing users to send short audio notes [1]. This was inspired by TalkBox and Kakao Talk, and was strategic because "the native mobile keyboard to type Chinese characters at the time was not easy to use and consumers found sending voice notes intuitive, personal and convenient" [1]. As one of the first messengers to offer voice, text, and photos in a combined solution, daily downloads spiked to 50,000-60,000 consistently [1].

Mobile QQ continues to operate with 652 million MAUs (as of December 2016), differentiated by entertainment features (animated video stickers, facial beautifying tools), while WeChat focuses on utility services for daily life [1].

The lesson echoes Amazon's cannibalization of book sales with Kindle and Uber's move from town cars to UberX: "If you don't create a culture of disrupting yourself, then a competitor is likely to disrupt you" [1].

## Lesson 2: Design for Groups ("Group Effect")

Allen Zhang, founder of WeChat, was "adamant about developing the product around 'Group Effect'" -- the observation that individual user behavior differs from behavior in groups [1]. The WeChat team focused on how features would be adopted by groups of users, not just individuals. This accelerated adoption rates through bandwagon effects [1].

### Solving the Cold Start Problem

Like all social networks, WeChat faced the zero-friends problem. While Facebook was famous for getting users to 10 friends within 14 days, WeChat took a different approach [1].

**People Nearby** was a location-enabled feature that showed users others nearby who were also using the feature [1]. It satisfied curiosity about what was happening around them and boosted community feeling. User acquisition jumped to over 100,000 per day [1].

WeChat then enabled account linking between WeChat and QQ, allowing users to import their existing social graph. Importantly, the importing feature was only exposed to users already on both platforms, preserving differentiation [1]. This pushed registered users to 50 million by November 2011, overtaking MiTalk [1].

**Shake** used GPS and accelerometer data to let users shake their phones to find random chat partners [1]. It served dual purposes: giving new users engagement and creating an offline-visible phenomenon (similar to Pokemon Go). Shake was used over 100 million times in its first month (October 2011) [1].

**Message in a Bottle** allowed users to exchange random messages by throwing a virtual bottle into a virtual sea [1].

After launching these group interaction features, new user acquisition hit 200,000 per day in November 2011 [1].

## Lesson 3: Extend Features from Users' Inner Desires

Allen Zhang was "passionate about keying into the inner desires behind an exhibited behavior, which often led to features that fit naturally into users' worlds" [1]. WeChat's research approach was observational rather than traditional: the team closely observed how users behaved among groups of friends and strangers in everyday life, rather than relying on surveys, interviews, or competitive analysis [1].

### Moments

Launched in WeChat's second year, Moments (Chinese translation: "friend circles") was a photo-sharing feature for sharing with a private group of friends [1]. Unlike Facebook's News Feed, comments and likes on a post can only be seen by mutual friends, not friends of friends. This was inspired by Path (a US photo-sharing app limited to close connections) but grounded in an old Chinese philosophy called "circle cultures" -- "smaller circles are much stronger at the core and as the circles get bigger, the ties get weaker" [1].

A team of only 10 people spent 4 months making over 30 versions of Moments before deciding on a version to launch [1]. The core product functions remain largely unchanged from the original version.

### Official Accounts (OA)

Released in 2012, Official Accounts allowed a one-way follow (similar to Twitter, competing with Weibo) where fans could subscribe to celebrity content [1]. The differentiator: celebrities could send text, voice, and video updates that looked like regular WeChat messages, "creating a feeling of a personal and private conversation" [1]. This prompted many celebrities to set up WeChat OAs even with millions of Weibo followers.

OAs expanded to brands and businesses, becoming a channel for direct customer communication (booking, customer care, feedback, Q&A) rather than just broadcasting [1]. "Where Twitter/Weibo were tools for businesses to broadcast and brand, WeChat (via OAs) became a channel for direct communication with customers" [1].

### Sticker Store

WeChat created a marketplace for illustrators to sell stickers, with users able to "reward" any amount up to RMB 200 ($25 USD) to creators they liked [1]. This marketplace created competition among illustrators for the top spot, enriching the user experience.

## Lesson 4: Big Ideas Come from Solving Your Own Problems

**WeChat Red Packets** originated from a Tencent company custom: every manager gives employees a red envelope with cash on the first workday after Chinese New Year [1]. As the company expanded, giving out physical red packets became exhausting, so managers asked for a technical solution. The result became WeChat's most transformative feature.

The first version was developed by a team of 20 in 3 weeks [1]. From New Year's Eve 2014 through 4pm the next day, over 5 million users sent Red Packets [1]. Growth exploded in 2015 with over 1 billion Red Packets sent during Chinese New Year [1].

The random Red Packet variant makes receiving money in a group chat like "a lottery combined with a fortune cookie -- you have no idea how much money you will receive before you open the packet, and getting more than others can be a sign of good luck" [1]. This is another example of the Group Effect: since Red Packets are sent in group chats, adoption increased as more users received them.

Red Packets drove WeChat Pay adoption by building trust through close social circles first (friends and family sending money), then extending to merchants, online and offline stores [1]. A partnership with Didi (China's Uber equivalent) expanded the payment network further [1].

By 2016, Tencent had 600 million mobile payment MAUs and over 600 million average daily payment transactions [1].

[![WeChat Pay Growth](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/5b831210586e.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Pay.png)

## Lesson 5: Monetize Subtly

WeChat demonstrates that monetization and user growth are not mutually exclusive [1].

**Game Center** connected WeChat with Tencent's core gaming expertise. A Tencent game called Rhythm Master had 700,000 DAUs after a year, but exploded to 17 million DAUs (>20x growth) after launching in Game Center [1].

**Moments Native Ads** (launched January 2015) limited native ads to one per day in a user's feed, compared to Facebook's roughly one ad per 10 posts [1]. Users interact with and share ads like friend posts. An Airbnb ad on Moments received more than 1.8 million views after sharing, saw a 5x increase in click-through rate, and drove a 600% increase in new sign-ups [1].

[![WeChat Moments Ad Example](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/683b74fa5f3d.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Moment-Ad.png)

[![WeChat Moments Interface](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/507d68f2e342.jpg)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Moment.jpg)

**Social Coupons** adapted the classic coupon model to friend-based endorsement. When a user redeems a coupon, they can opt to share the offer with friends, extending vendor reach through trusted recommendations [1].

## Lesson 6: Measure What You Value, Not What You're Supposed To

WeChat does not measure growth solely by users or messages. Instead, the team also measures "how deeply is the product engaged in every aspect of daily life (e.g., the number of tasks WeChat can help with in a day)" [1]. The daily engineering message is: "are you creating more value for the user?" [1].

WeChat deliberately limits friend counts, promotional messages, and social circle engagement visibility, viewing excess as "noise that may distract from the utility of WeChat" [1].

**QR Codes** became WeChat's bridge between online and offline worlds. Tencent's Pony Ma described QR codes as "a label of abundant online information attached to the offline world" [1]. QR codes failed in the US for three reasons: the dominant phone and social app did not support scanning, dedicated scanner apps added friction, and early use cases focused on low-value marketing content [1].

**Mini Programs** extended Official Accounts to enable in-app service access without downloading or installing apps [1]. A local restaurant or store could operate within WeChat Mini Programs, discovered via social connections or QR code scanning. WeChat claims this drives higher purchasing conversion rates [1].

## Lesson 7: Don't Play Favorites with Features

Allen Zhang actively worried about users spending too much time in any single feature, as this could prevent them from using other valuable services [1]. WeChat's core product tenet is that it is a "tool" with utility-oriented qualities. Zhang compared this to Google: "once the user finds the right link, Google sends the user directly to the desired site" [1].

The team limits marketing content on Moments and restricts broadcast messages from Subscription Accounts to one per day, without push notifications [1].

Despite the deep feature set, WeChat maintains only 4 tabs: chat, contacts, discover, and me [1]. They have been "very disciplined in keeping the app to only 4 tabs, as whenever they experiment with adding one, the left-most tab sees conversion rates drop by 20-30%" [1].

[![WeChat Engagement Metrics](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/fdd1b0a18af3.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Engagement.png)

## Key Growth Metrics Timeline

| Milestone | Date | Key Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Launch | January 2011 | Messaging + photo sharing |
| 50-60k daily downloads | May 2011 | Voice messaging added |
| 100k+ daily acquisitions | Mid-2011 | People Nearby feature |
| 50M registered users | November 2011 | QQ account linking |
| 200k daily acquisitions | November 2011 | Group interaction features |
| 300M MAUs | ~2013 | Moments, Official Accounts |
| 5M Red Packet users in 2 days | January 2014 | WeChat Red Packets |
| 1B Red Packets sent | Chinese New Year 2015 | Red Packet virality |
| 889M MAUs | 2016 | Full platform maturity |

[![WeChat Growth Chart](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/a260bf04ade6.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Growth.png)

## References

1. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Scale AI
body:
# Scale AI

Scale AI, founded by Alexandr Wang as a YC company, evolved from a simple data labeling API into a multi-hundred-million-dollar infrastructure company powering America's AI ecosystem. In early 2025, Meta invested over $14 billion in Scale AI, valuing the company at $29 billion [1].

## Origin and Evolution

Scale AI started with a narrow premise: machine learning teams needed labeled data faster than they could produce it. Wang built an API for human labor -- a way to send raw data and get back labeled training examples. Behind the scenes, the real challenge was turning "messy, real-world data into something AI could learn from" [1].

The company evolved through several phases:
1. **Data labeling API**: The original product, serving ML teams at individual companies
2. **Enterprise data infrastructure**: Powering training data pipelines for Fortune 500 companies
3. **Government and defense**: Real-time military planning and intelligence analysis
4. **Agent infrastructure**: Building the evaluation and training infrastructure for the next generation of AI systems [1]

## Competing with China

Wang is one of the most vocal voices in Silicon Valley on US-China AI competition. He argues that data quality and infrastructure -- not just model architecture -- are the critical battleground. Chinese labs (DeepSeek, Qwen) have demonstrated that scaling laws work regardless of where you train, making the quality of training data and the speed of iteration the differentiators [1].

## Lessons for Founders

- **Start narrow, expand into the platform**: Scale began with a single API endpoint and expanded into the infrastructure layer that frontier AI depends on
- **Government can be a startup customer**: Scale's defense contracts demonstrate that government agencies can move at startup speed when the technology solves an urgent problem
- **The 19-year-old founder can build a $29B company**: Wang started Scale AI at 19, reinforcing the [[Starting Young]] thesis [1]

## References

1. [Alexandr Wang: Building Scale AI, Transforming Work with Agents & Competing With China](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5noIKN8t69U) -- Alexandr Wang, [[Jared Friedman]] (2025)

title: Boom Technology
body:
# Boom Technology

Boom Technology (YC W16) is building supersonic passenger airplanes. Founded by [[Blake Scholl]], the company aims to make high-speed travel economical for airlines and affordable for passengers -- fifty years after the Concorde's design [1].

## Origin

Scholl had been talking about supersonics for 10 years before starting Boom. After his first startup was acquired by Groupon, he made a list of startup ideas in descending order of how awesome it would be if they worked, ignoring feasibility. "I figured, 'I'll cross things off the list and I'll end up working on idea number five.' And, as luck had it, the more I got into supersonics, the more I learned that it's not crazy, and actually the time is now" [1].

His previous work on internet coupons at Groupon was a catalyst: "There's nothing like working on Internet coupons to make you yearn to work on something you really love" [1].

## The Case for Founder-Led Aviation

Scholl observes that supersonic travel represents a unique case in technology where capability went backward. "Our phones are better, our computers are better, we're about to have cars that drive themselves and genetically-engineered medicine, and yet we're still flying at 1960 speeds" [1].

He traces every major breakthrough in aviation to founder-led companies: the Wright brothers (bicycle entrepreneurs), the DC-3 (first practical airliner), and the De Havilland Comet (first jetliner). The last new commercial aircraft company was founded in 1921. The last founder retired from the industry in 1958. Since then, Boeing and Airbus have optimized existing designs but have not advanced human capability [1].

## YC Experience

Scholl was initially skeptical: "Isn't YC for mobile social apps?" But after connecting with other hard tech founders who had done YC, he concluded it would help with traction and raising capital -- the biggest risk for Boom in the early days [1].

YC gave two pieces of advice: get out of stealth mode, and go sell airplanes. Both proved critical [1].

## The Virgin Deal

For demo day, Scholl assessed his pipeline (United, Delta, Lufthansa, Air China) and concluded none would close in eight weeks. He focused on startup airlines and Virgin [1].

The team pursued Richard Branson through two channels: friends inside Virgin Galactic and astronaut Mark Kelly on Boom's advisory board. They secured a 15-minute breakfast meeting by coordinating requests from both avenues and showing up at a Virgin Galactic spaceship rollout [1].

The ask was crafted to be easy: "We're not asking for your money, we're asking, 'When this flies, do you want a Virgin logo on it?'" Using demo day as a deadline, Boom told Virgin, "We are launching the company on demo day, and either you can be part of it or not" [1].

The result: 24 hours before Boom's demo day slot, Virgin committed to the first 10 airplanes with options, plus Virgin Galactic support for building. Boom presented $5 billion in LOIs -- a demo day record [1].

## Lessons

Three sales principles distilled from the experience [1]:
1. Be persistent beyond the point of discomfort
2. Have a believable external deadline
3. Make the deal maximally attractive to the other side

For hardware companies at YC, find progress that "both is and looks like actual, substantive progress." Boom built a physical model of the airplane, 3D-printed the jet engine design, and brought to demo day one of the actual engines that would power the first aircraft [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) -- [[Blake Scholl]] (n.d.)

title: Pinduoduo
body:
# Pinduoduo

Pinduoduo is a Chinese social e-commerce platform that grew from launch in 2015 to 585 million active buyers and over $144 billion in gross merchandise value in just four years [1]. By 2020, it was China's second-largest e-commerce platform by active users, trailing only Alibaba [1]. The company reached a $57 billion market capitalization despite launching into a market dominated by JD and Taobao/TMall, which combined for $433 billion in GMV the year Pinduoduo launched [1].

[[Anu Hariharan]] and Nic Dardenne analyzed Pinduoduo as a case study in how social commerce can create entirely new market categories rather than competing head-on with established transactional platforms.

## The Core Insight: Shopping Is Social

Pinduoduo found space in e-commerce not as a competitor to search-based platforms like JD, but as a new category: interactive and social shopping [1]. The fundamental observation is that in the physical world, "shopping is meant to be 'interactive and fun' and purchases are regularly informed by friends and family" [1]. Traditional e-commerce platforms like JD, Alibaba, and Amazon optimize for efficiency, funnel conversion, and purchase rates. They do not account for the social dimension of shopping.

The evidence for this gap was visible in category penetration data: in 2020 in the US, only 29% of apparel, 11% of health and personal care, and 3% of food and beverage purchases happened online, compared to 55% of books/music and 43% of computers/electronics [1]. Social shopping is particularly important for categories where consumers seek feedback or recommendations from friends.

## Team Purchase: The Core Mechanic

The core of Pinduoduo's experience is team purchase, where buyers form groups to receive discounts from suppliers [1]. The flow works as follows:

1. Merchants set two prices for each item: individual purchase and team purchase [1]
2. A user opting for team purchase can either initiate a new team or join an existing one [1]
3. The initiator shares via [[WeChat]] to recruit friends, or waits for other Pinduoduo users to join [1]
4. The team must form within 24 hours for the order to be confirmed [1]
5. Once formed, the product ships within 48 hours [1]

![Pinduoduo Team Purchase Flow](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/22cf244a2ed3.jpg)

Nearly all Pinduoduo transactions use team purchase [1]. In early days, groups were large (10+ members), but requirements have declined as the platform scaled [1].

### Why Team Purchase Is Not Groupon

The comparison to Groupon is common but misleading [1]:

| Dimension | Pinduoduo | Groupon |
|---|---|---|
| Deal design | By sellers, initiated by consumers | Designed and managed by sellers |
| Product type | Everyday goods (fruits, vegetables, apparel) | One-off products/experiences |
| Merchant geography | Diverse merchants across China and globally | Mostly local sellers |
| Consumer value | Discounts on goods users need anyway | Aggregating demand on low-value/excess inventory |

### Strategic Launch Category

Pinduoduo launched in fruits and vegetables for two strategic reasons [1]:
1. Incumbents were focused on non-perishable items, limiting competition
2. Fruits and vegetables are low order value, high frequency goods, creating habitual usage

When buyers saw good deals, they forwarded them to neighbors and friends via [[WeChat]] to form teams of 10+ buyers. Group initiators received produce for free for helping Pinduoduo acquire 10 incremental users [1]. This drove organic sharing and rapid user growth: only one month after launching their first-party app in January 2016, Pinduoduo had over 10 million customers [1].

For comparison, Alibaba crossed the 500 million active buyer threshold 14-15 years after launch versus just 4 years for Pinduoduo [1].

### Network Effects

Pinduoduo exhibits what Hariharan calls one of the most powerful network effects in the world, governed by [Reed's law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reed%27s_law): "The value of a group-forming network is proportional to number and the ease with which groups form within it" [1]. The comparison is to Slack and WhatsApp Groups, which grew exponentially by tapping into groups of social relationships.

The key takeaway for startups: "New commerce platforms that can leverage social relationships will accelerate the offline to online transition and if you are able to tap into groups of social relationships vs. one-to-one relationship the faster your platforms will scale" [1].

## WeChat as Growth Platform

A significant enabler of Pinduoduo's virality was the widespread use of [[WeChat]] in China. Tencent (WeChat's owner) is a large investor in Pinduoduo and allowed the platform to grow on top of the WeChat ecosystem [1]. Hariharan notes that "it is highly unlikely that Facebook would allow a social commerce application like Pinduoduo to be built on top of Messenger or Instagram in the US" but adds that messaging fragmentation in the US means this is not necessarily a blocker for US-based social e-commerce [1].

## Gamification and Engagement

Pinduoduo operates on the principle that users visit without specific purchase intent, "much like visiting a real-world shopping mall" [1]. Time spent directly correlates with purchases, so Pinduoduo invested heavily in gamified engagement features.

### Daily Check-Ins

Users receive redeemable points for daily check-ins. Rewards accumulate over many months, and users must reach a minimum value (e.g., 30 RMB) to cash out as a no-minimum-spend voucher [1]. This creates a simple daily habit loop that eventually ties back to commerce.

![Pinduoduo Daily Check-In](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/e621e756cab8.png)

### Price Chop

Users can get products for free by sharing a custom link with friends within a 24-hour window [1]. Each friend who clicks and engages with Pinduoduo (no purchase required) drives an incremental discount for the initiator. The difficulty mechanics mirror MMORPG leveling: "it becomes harder to level up as you graduate to higher levels" [1]. Difficulty is customized based on the user (easier for low-engagement users) and the item (expensive items are harder to chop) [1].

Price Chop simultaneously increases customer lifetime value and acquires new customers through user-to-user sharing [1].

![Pinduoduo Price Chop](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/bf7bb9afff8b.png)

### Card Programs

Three key card types drive specific user behaviors [1]:

1. **Free Pass Card**: Loyalty-style card allowing team purchase discounts without joining a team. Earned after two purchases.

![Pinduoduo Free Pass Card](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/cb1438a37151.png)

2. **Brand Black Card**: Encourages reviews by offering discounts on branded products after 2-4 reviews. Improves platform quality for all users.

![Pinduoduo Brand Black Card](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/6ed39bc1ed0c.png)

3. **Brand Card**: Promotes branded products. Users share brand cards with friends via WeChat, leveraging trusted friend recommendations to drive branded product adoption.

![Pinduoduo Brand Card](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/e18ba1c6e7bf.png)

### Mini Games

Pinduoduo hosts in-app games to drive daily engagement. **Duo Duo Orchard** (described as "Farmville except now the rewards are real physical goods") has users nurture a virtual fruit tree that eventually yields a real box of fruit shipped to their address [1]. The game had more than 11 million DAUs. Progress is tied to behaviors that drive revenue: shopping on Pinduoduo yields water droplets for your tree, and team purchases unlock special tools [1].

![Pinduoduo Duo Duo Orchard](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/b33c6dcf07c3.png)

**DD Bank** is a piggy bank game where users accumulate exchangeable coins over time [1].

The games create "a win-win-win situation for merchants, consumers, and themselves. More specifically, merchants see more volume, consumers have fun and receive unique discounts, and Pinduoduo generates revenue" [1].

## Personalized Recommendations

Unlike search-based platforms, Pinduoduo is recommendation-driven [1]. Based on a user's social graph, category preferences, and trust relationships, the platform personalizes the experience. For new customers, Pinduoduo shows different categories based on what they know about the user and their friends, then uses interactions to build a buying persona [1].

Personalization extends to value perception: based on purchase and browsing history, Pinduoduo determines whether a user values high-cost branded or low-cost non-branded items and adjusts recommendations to match willingness to pay [1].

## Implications for Global E-Commerce

Hariharan argues that while team purchase may be specific to China's commercial landscape, the underlying insight about social integration in commerce is "likely entirely universal" [1]. The core takeaway: "Web 1.0 platforms -- including Amazon -- optimize for efficiency and don't serve trust-based categories very well" [1].

YC portfolio companies already pursuing variations of this model [1]:
- **[Snackpass](https://www.snackpass.co/)**: A food app for college campuses with social features (gift-sending, virtual pet hatching through food orders)
- **[Meesho](https://meesho.com/)**: An Indian reseller marketplace where customers create microbusinesses selling to friends and family via WhatsApp

"Social commerce does not just mean connecting user accounts to Facebook. It means investing in creating physical world experiences online -- specifically bringing the fun of shopping offline to online platforms" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne (2020)

title: Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles
body:
# Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles

[Snapdocs](https://www.snapdocs.com/) is the leading digital closing platform for the mortgage industry. As of the interview, the company touches 25% of all US real estate transactions and is valued at $1.5B. Founder and CEO [[Aaron King]] (YC W14) built the company with minimal outside funding through volatile market conditions [1].

## Capital Efficiency as a Discipline

Snapdocs started as a side project years before [[Aaron King]] applied to YC. By the time he applied, the company had a live product, customers, and revenue. Even after YC, the company did not raise much immediately [1].

Three years after YC, Snapdocs raised its Series A. At that point, the company had spent about $1M of its seed round and was at a $5M revenue run rate. The lead investor later told King how capital efficient Snapdocs had been compared to peers [1].

King frames capital discipline not as a constraint but as a virtue: "I've learned that you should take the same approach even when you do have the capital to be less disciplined" [1]. Capital constraints kept the team focused on going deep with paying customers, avoiding the common trap of building products no one wanted [1].

The approach also forced the team to think about the smallest version of every product in order to ship quickly, maintaining a short feedback loop with users [1]. King acknowledges the cost: "We built many things that weren't engineered for scale or flexibility. However, now we can afford to reengineer those unscalable solutions because we built something people want" [1].

## Product Development

Before raising the Series A, Snapdocs was heavy on customer involvement. The team spent time in customers' offices watching them use the product and understanding their work [1]. They also kept prospects in the loop as they built new features:

> "Some of the best feedback came from people who had chosen to not yet work with us. Responding to that feedback with a killer feature was a great way to ultimately get them on board." [1]

The in-person interactions built trust and rapport. Customers would call the team the moment they spotted a problem or saw a competitor doing something compelling. Customer churn has always been low as a result [1].

Despite small size, the team maintained a disciplined product release process while shipping code daily, sometimes multiple times per day [1].

### The Value of Low Investor Pressure

Not having heavy investor pressure early on allowed Snapdocs to experiment in an underappreciated part of the market. The Serviceable Available Market (SAM) of the initial product was roughly only $20M, but King believed it would allow expansion into more critical parts of the mortgage ecosystem [1]. He notes: "It was the type of opportunity that would be hard to discover through market analysis or spreadsheet exercises. You had to get deep into the problem set to see the opportunity" [1].

## Hiring Under Capital Constraints

Before the company could afford full-time employees, King worked with contractors and leaned on friends. Friends would come over to whiteboard or work through code problems [1].

When King could afford to hire, he treated early employees like founding team members — generous with equity and transparent about the business's potential and challenges. "Getting a few really good people into the company early on was foundational to the company's success" [1].

The first full-time hire was an engineer from a previous role (and one of the friends who helped in the early days). The second and third hires came from Hacker News job postings. All three turned out to be excellent [1].

### The Recruiting Regret

King's biggest early hiring mistake was being too timid to approach people he respected:

> "I should have tried to convince them to quit their successful jobs and join our small (yet risky at the time) startup. I'm fearless on this approach now, but back then I was intimidated to try to convince a friend to join a company that might fail. In hindsight I did them a disservice by not trying to recruit them." [1]

His lesson: smart people can evaluate the opportunity for themselves. Be honest about inherent challenges, but give them the opportunity to take a risk on you [1].

The team only hired when there was a clear and painful need. This made running the company harder but kept everyone working on the most important things [1].

## Navigating Market Cycles

A core lesson from Snapdocs is how to selectively respond to market changes. King describes the approach as "selectively ignoring some market changes, while reacting quickly to others" [1]. The filter is how a change aligns with resources, vision, and the north star metric of market share growth.

### Fluctuations in Mortgage Volume

The biggest dynamic change Snapdocs faces is fluctuations in monthly/annual mortgage volume, driven by economic factors. When well-resourced with momentum, the team ignores market downturns and stays focused on market share growth. Other times they scale up or back based on the fluctuation's size [1].

### Resisting Trendy Technology

Industry appetite for new technologies changes quickly. There were times when the market demanded a technology but King believed underlying industry factors would prevent it from scaling. Building it would pull resources from long-term priorities [1]:

> "Sometimes to the protests of our sales team, we ignored it or invested minimally in these trendy areas. By doing so, we were able to stay focused on the things that were truly going to transform the industry." [1]

### The Cost of Change Scales with Company Size

King notes that navigating change was relatively easy in the first few years when the team could all fit in one room, product cycles were short, and the cost of making a change was low. As the company grew, changes to speed or direction became more expensive: investments take longer to play out, headcount changes take longer to scale, and there are more layers to communicate through [1].

## The COVID Pivot (March 2020)

COVID accelerated demand for Snapdocs' product but shifted what customers wanted. The team had to expand quickly and pivot the roadmap immediately [1].

Decision-making worked in concentric circles [1]:
1. **Inner circle (3-4 people)**: Where the hardest and messiest conversations took place. Quickly defined problems, opportunities, and direction.
2. **Senior leadership team**: Further discussion and arming leaders with everything needed to communicate changes.
3. **Company-wide meeting**: Shared new direction and answered questions.

This entire process took about two weeks. For ongoing rapid changes, the company used temporary pods of 4-5 team members focused on specific challenges that would spin up for a few weeks and dissolve. Company-wide all-hands went from monthly to weekly [1].

### Intellectual Honesty About Growth

The team worked hard to be intellectually honest about COVID-driven growth:

> "This industry works in cycles. We're in an up cycle now and that's great. There's going to be a down cycle. We don't know when or how strong it's going to be. But we should not overly congratulate ourselves for the current situation, just as we shouldn't be too hard on ourselves when we're fighting through an inevitable downturn in the future." [1]

## Shifting Gears: From Efficient to Effective and Back

When Snapdocs raised its Series D in 2021, transactions had grown to millions of closings per year with thousands of lenders and title companies using the platform monthly. The company shifted from frugal to aggressive, raising larger rounds and hiring seasoned executives [1].

King describes the key question: "Is what I did yesterday the thing that's going to get me to where I need to be tomorrow?" The answer required changing parts of the culture and capital investment strategy [1].

But when mortgage demand cooled later that year, the company returned to its roots: ditched the motto of being effective over efficient, returned to ruthless prioritization, and shifted away from investing in future scale that would not be needed for years [1].

King's summary: "Market fluctuations are normal and unavoidable. Startups should scale up at times and scale back at others. It's hard and painful... But these ups and downs are natural and a necessary part of building an enduring company" [1].

## References

1. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — [[Aaron King]], Lindsay Amos (2022)

title: Default Alive or Default Dead
aliases: do you make it to profitability
body:
# Default Alive or Default Dead

"Default Alive or Default Dead" is a diagnostic framework introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in October 2015 [1]. It poses a single question to startups past their first 8-9 months: assuming expenses remain constant and revenue growth continues on its current trend, does the company make it to profitability on the money it has left?

"Or to put it more dramatically, by default do they live or die?" [1]

## The Diagnostic

The question is binary. If default alive, you can discuss ambitious new things. If default dead, you need to talk about how to save the company [1].

Half the founders Graham talks to do not know the answer [1]. Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) to help founders determine their status.

The reason founders don't know: it is not a question that makes sense to ask early on, "any more than it makes sense to ask a 3-year-old how he plans to support himself" [1]. But as the company grows older, the question switches from meaningless to critical, and that switch often takes people by surprise [1].

Graham proposes starting to ask too early rather than too late. "It's probably not that dangerous to start worrying too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to start worrying too late" [1].

## The Fatal Pinch

The most dangerous state is what Graham calls the "fatal pinch": default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [1]. Founders end up there by not realizing that is where they are headed.

A secondary cause: founders assume it will be easy to raise more money. "But that assumption is often false, and worse still, the more you depend on it, the falser it becomes" [1].

Graham suggests making the assumption explicit: "Say 'We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save us.' Maybe as you say that, it will set off the same alarms in your head that it does in mine" [1].

## The Investor Dependency Problem

It would be safe to be default dead if you could count on investors. But you cannot. Investor interest is a function of growth, and sometimes something about your business will spook investors even if growth is great [1].

The rule: fundraising should never be more than a plan A. "You should always have a plan B as well: you should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to survive if you can't raise more money, and precisely when you'll have to switch to plan B if plan A isn't working" [1].

Even with steep revenue growth (over 5x a year), you can only start to count on investor interest, never rely on it [1].

## Growth vs. Spending: The False Dichotomy

"In practice there is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [1]. When growth is fast, it is because the product hits a nerve. When spending is high, it is because the product is expensive to build or sell, or the company is wasteful [1].

These are independent variables, not a tradeoff. The implication is that cutting spending rarely hurts growth and often does not affect it at all.

## Overhiring: The #1 Killer

"Don't hire too fast. Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [1].

Three reasons founders overhire:
1. There is so much work to do that they assume hiring will get it done
2. Successful startups have lots of employees, so it seems necessary
3. They do not want to face the real reason for slow growth: the product is not appealing enough [1]

VCs encourage overhiring because kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for portfolios. "VCs want to blow you up, in one sense of the phrase or the other. But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive" [1].

Sam Altman added: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [1].

## The Common Death Spiral

The typical pattern [1]:

1. Startup makes something moderately appealing
2. Decent initial growth
3. First round raised easily (founders seem smart, idea sounds plausible)
4. Growth is ok but not great
5. Founders convince themselves hiring will boost growth; investors agree
6. Growth still doesn't come (product is only moderately appealing)
7. Running out of runway with high expenses and slow growth
8. Unappealing to investors for next round
9. Company dies

What they should have done: address the fundamental problem with the product. Hire slowly. Seek growth by [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]], or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can [1].

## The Airbnb Counter-Example

Airbnb waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee [1]. The founders were terribly overworked, but they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the organism it became. This is the model Graham holds up: founders doing the work themselves, staying lean, letting the product evolve before adding headcount [1].

## Premature Scaling

Paul Buchheit identifies a related pattern: "founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers. This is similar to over-hiring in that it makes the business much harder to fix once it's big, plus they are bleeding cash really fast" [1].

## The Alternative Path

If you are default dead, instead of hiring to boost growth, Graham recommends [1]:
- Doing things that don't scale (see [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]])
- Redesigning the product -- something only founders can do effectively at the early stage
- Operating cheaply to extend runway
- Having an explicit written plan B for survival without more funding

The alarm bells from asking "am I default alive or dead?" can counteract the forces pushing you to overhire and instead compel you toward the paths to growth that actually work for most startups [1].

## References

1. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)

title: Equity Equation
body:
# Equity Equation

The Equity Equation is a formula introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in July 2007 for deciding whether any trade of company stock -- for investment, an employee, or a deal -- is worth making [1]. The formula is:

**1/(1 - n)**

where n is the fraction of the company you are giving up. You should make the trade if what you receive improves your average outcome enough that the (100 - n)% you have left is worth more than the whole company was before [1].

## How the Formula Works

If an investor wants to buy half your company, the investment must more than double your average outcome for you to break even. You have half as big a share of something that must be worth more than twice as much [1].

In the general case, if n is the fraction you are giving up, the deal is good if it makes the company worth more than 1/(1 - n) [1].

### Example: Y Combinator

At the time of writing, YC took about 7% of a company. n = 0.07, and 1/(1 - 0.07) = 1.075. So the deal is worth taking if YC can improve your average outcome by more than 7.5%. If they improve it by 10%, you are net ahead: the remaining 0.93 you hold is worth 0.93 x 1.1 = 1.023 [1].

Graham comments: "This is why we can't believe anyone would think Y Combinator was a bad deal. Does anyone really think we're so useless that in three months we can't improve a startup's prospects by 7.5%?" [1]

### Example: Sequoia

Greg McAdoo from Sequoia said at a YC dinner that when Sequoia invests alone they like to take about 30% of a company. 1/0.7 = 1.43, meaning the deal is worth taking if Sequoia can improve your outcome by more than 43% [1].

For the average startup, this would be an extraordinary bargain. "It would improve the average startup's prospects by more than 43% just to be able to say they were funded by Sequoia, even if they never actually got the money" [1]. The reason Sequoia is such a good deal is that their percentage is "artificially low" -- they limit holdings to leave founders enough stock to feel the company is still theirs [1].

The catch: Sequoia gets about 6,000 business plans a year and funds about 20. The odds of getting this deal are 1 in 300. The companies that make it through are not average startups [1].

## Applying the Formula to Employee Equity

The formula works in reverse for employees. If i is the average outcome multiplier with the addition of a new person, they are worth n such that i = 1/(1 - n), which means n = (i - 1)/i [1].

### Worked Example

Suppose you are two founders and want to hire a hacker so good you estimate he will increase the average outcome by 20%. n = (1.2 - 1)/1.2 = 0.167. You would break even trading 16.7% of the company for this person [1].

But 16.7% is not the right amount to offer because stock is not the only cost. There is salary and overhead too. Graham suggests multiplying the annual salary-plus-overhead rate by about 1.5 to translate it into stock terms. The reasoning: most startups grow fast or die. If they die, you don't pay. If they grow fast, next year's salary comes out of next year's (presumably 3x higher) valuation. So the total stock-equivalent cost is about 1.5 years at the present valuation [1].

Full example: the employee is worth 16.7% but the company wants 50% profit on the hire, so his "retail" price is about 11.1%. If he costs $60k/year in salary and overhead, times 1.5 = $90k total. At a $2M valuation, $90k is 4.5%. So the stock offer is 11.1% - 4.5% = 6.6% [1].

Graham notes: "notice how important it is for early employees to take little salary. It comes right out of stock that could otherwise be given to them" [1].

## The General Principle

"Whenever you make any decision involving equity, run it through 1/(1 - n) to see if it makes sense. You should always feel richer after trading equity. If the trade didn't increase the value of your remaining shares enough to put you net ahead, you wouldn't have (or shouldn't have) done it" [1].

The formula does not eliminate guesswork -- "ultimately you always have to guess" -- but it ensures you know what you are guessing about. Whether you use gut feel or a VC-supplied table of typical grant sizes, understand that those are estimates of the multiplier in the equity equation [1].

## Limitations

The formula assumes you can estimate the outcome multiplier, which is inherently uncertain. For VC deals, there are always factors beyond a straight trade of money for stock [1].
