- **"I started working six months before."** If 99% of the work remains, a six-month head start on a 10-20 year journey is not significant [4].
- **"My co-founder needs a salary and I don't."** Salary and equity serve different purposes [4].
- **"I'm older and more experienced."** If essential enough to be a co-founder, they need enough equity to stay motivated [4].
- **"I hired my co-founder after raising money."** Fundraising does not massively change success probability [4].

Seibel summarizes: "All of these answers are a flavor of short-term thinking. The best founders are long-term thinkers" [4].

## Vesting as the Safety Net

Seibel emphasizes that vesting and a cliff, not unequal splits, are the correct mechanism for protecting against co-founder departures [1]. Standard terms:

- **Four-year vesting**: The co-founder must work for four years to earn their full equity stake.
- **One-year cliff**: If a co-founder leaves within the first year, they receive nothing.
- After the cliff, 25% vests, then 1/48th per month thereafter [1].

"This is your get-out-of-jail-free card. If you made a decision that was incorrect about choosing your co-founders, as long as you correct it within one year, there's no long-term harm to the company" [1].

Seibel is emphatic that vesting applies to all founders: "Giving away founder equity is not something that you should be innovating on" [4].

## Bad Advice to Avoid

**Performance-based equity.** At the start of a tech startup, goals are unclear. Measuring contributions precisely is a "really big fallacy" [4].

**Part-time founders.** A part-time founder is not a founder [4].

**Dynamic equity agreements.** Complicated arrangements are "too fancy and ill-advised" [4].

## Co-Founder Breakup Equity Guidelines

Seibel provides specific YC guidelines for pre-[[Product-Market Fit]] companies [4]:

- **Before the one-year cliff:** The departing founder receives a token amount -- 2-5% of the company.
- **After the cliff but pre-PMF:** The departing founder should retain no more than 5%, which may mean giving back some vested equity.

Additional guidelines [4]:
- A fired founder may receive 1-3 months severance. A founder who leaves voluntarily typically does not.
- Every departing founder should resign from the board, sign a release, and give proxy voting rights to remaining founders.
- The CEO should have the ability to fire non-performing founders regardless of equity split.

## The Activation Energy Argument

Seibel addresses the objection that long-term CEOs like Bezos or Zuckerberg prove one founder deserves vastly more equity [4]:

"The beginning is extremely important. Those first four to six years is where a lot of value is being created, and those first four to six years is when most companies die." Early co-founders provide the activation energy the company needs. "Without them, maybe you're not in the game at all" [4].

## The Co-Founder Conversation

Equity split should be one of the earliest conversations, addressed before the company is incorporated. Key considerations include:
- Relative contributions (past work vs. future contributions)
- Full-time vs. part-time commitment
- Vesting schedules
- What happens if a co-founder leaves

## University Spinouts: A Special Case

[[Jared Friedman]] notes that equity split is particularly fraught in [[University Spinouts]], where universities typically demand 5-15% or more plus licensing fees, creating conflicts with venture investors who expect clean cap tables [2].

## The Equity Equation for Non-Founders

[[Paul Graham]]'s [[Equity Equation]] provides a mathematical framework: if someone joining makes your stock worth more than 1/(1-n) where n is the fraction you are giving up, the trade is worth it.

## References

1. [How to Split Equity Among Co-Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5x-how-to-split-equity-among-co-founders) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
2. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (n.d.)
3. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) -- Y Combinator (2022)
4. [Co-Founder Equity Mistakes to Avoid](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LP-co-founder-equity-mistakes-to-avoid) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
5. [How to Find a Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ld-how-to-find-a-co-founder) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (2024)

title: Conversion Rates
body:
# Conversion Rates

Conversion rate optimization is the process of increasing the percentage of users who take a desired action -- signing up, purchasing, completing onboarding, or any other key step in the user journey. [[Gustaf Alströmer]], YC partner and former growth lead at Airbnb, provides a systematic framework for identifying and fixing conversion bottlenecks [1].

## The Funnel Mindset

Every user interaction is a funnel with drop-off at each step. The goal is not to optimize every step simultaneously but to identify the step with the largest absolute drop-off and focus there [1]. A 5% improvement at a bottleneck that 100,000 users pass through is worth more than a 50% improvement at a step only 100 users reach.

## Measurement Before Optimization

Alströmer emphasizes that most startups try to optimize conversion before they can measure it accurately. The prerequisites [1]:
- Clear definition of each step in the funnel
- Reliable tracking of users through each step
- Enough volume to detect meaningful changes (statistical significance)

Without these, optimization efforts are guesswork.

## References

1. [How to Improve Conversion Rates](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6l-how-to-improve-conversion-rates) -- Gustaf Alströmer (2019)

title: Pitching Investors vs. Customers
body:
# Pitching Investors vs. Customers

[[Michael Seibel]] argues that the most common pitching mistake is using the same pitch for investors and customers [1]. The two audiences have fundamentally different knowledge, motivations, and needs.

## Why the Same Pitch Fails

Customers typically know a lot about the problem being solved and are personally interested in the solution. Investors may not have the problem at all and are interested only insofar as it can build a big company [1].

## The Customer Pitch

When talking to a customer [1]:
- **Use jargon.** Industry language builds credibility with people who know the space.
- **Let them talk more than you.** Get them to speak about their problem.
- **Focus on how the solution helps them personally, right now.**
- **Get into nitty-gritty details.** Screenshots, live demos, specific functionality, onboarding process, and how pricing applies to them specifically.

Where to use it: sales calls, website front page, help pages, FAQs, user interviews [1].

## The Investor Pitch

When talking to an investor [1]:
- **Eliminate jargon.** It hurts rather than helps. Investors do not share the customer's vocabulary.
- **Avoid marketing language.** "We're building a platform" or "the most unique thing ever" is fluff that investors must filter out.
- **Use concrete, simple descriptions.** Instead of "Google organizes the world's information" (which tells an investor nothing), say: "Google's a new type of search engine. You go to our website, type what you want to know, click enter, and we report an ordered list of websites ranked by how other websites link to them. Those results are way more relevant" [1].

What investors want to know [1]:
1. What do you do? (In the simplest language possible)
2. How far along are you?
3. How big is the total addressable market?
4. How do you plan to charge users?
5. What do you know about this market that others don't?
6. Who's on your team -- specifically, can you build the product?

Seibel's test: "If your front page of your website and your pitch deck are saying the same thing and you don't have a product where you assume the customer is also a user -- a product like Yelp or Uber -- you might be doing it wrong" [1].

## References

1. [How Pitching Investors Is Different Than Pitching Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7u-how-pitching-investors-is-different-than-pitching-customers) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2018)

title: Startup Design
body:
# Startup Design

Design is an underappreciated competitive advantage for startups. Karri Saarinen, founder of Linear, argues that brand and product design should be treated as a core competency from day one, not an afterthought [1]. Dylan Field, CEO of Figma, sees AI as fundamentally transforming the design process itself [2]. Ryo Lu, Cursor's head of design, provides practical critique of how startups present themselves on the web [3].

## Brand Design: Lessons from Linear

Saarinen built Linear into one of the most admired B2B products partly through obsessive attention to design. His key principles [1]:

**Design is decision-making.** Every visual choice -- color, typography, spacing, animation -- communicates values. Linear's dark-mode-first, minimal interface signals "serious tool for serious builders" without saying it explicitly [1].

**Consistency compounds.** Small design inconsistencies erode trust over time. Saarinen advocates for design systems even at very early stages -- not a full component library, but a small set of rules about color, type, and spacing that every screen follows [1].

**Brand is not logo.** Brand is the sum of every interaction someone has with your product and company. The name, the domain, the onboarding flow, the error messages, the changelog -- all of these are brand design [1].

**Speed and quality are not opposed.** Linear ships fast precisely because good design systems reduce decision fatigue. When the rules are clear, execution is faster [1].

## How AI Will Transform Design

Dylan Field describes Figma's approach to AI in design tools [2]. The transformation goes beyond auto-generating layouts: AI enables rapid prototyping of entire flows, automatic translation of wireframes into polished designs, and -- most importantly -- closing the gap between design and code. When a designer can go from concept to working prototype in minutes instead of days, the entire product development cycle accelerates [2].

Field's key insight: AI will not replace designers but will shift their role from pixel-pushing to decision-making. The scarce resource becomes taste -- knowing what to build and whether it is good -- not the ability to execute the pixels [2].

## Startup Website Review

Ryo Lu (Cursor's head of design) reviewed startup websites with specific, actionable feedback [3]. Common problems:

- **Hero sections that say nothing**: The most common mistake is a hero that uses buzzwords without communicating what the product does for whom
- **Too many calls to action**: Multiple competing buttons confuse the user about what to do next
- **Ignoring mobile**: Most first impressions happen on phones
- **Generic stock imagery**: Photos of diverse teams in conference rooms signal "we have nothing real to show"
- **Poor typography hierarchy**: When everything is bold or large, nothing stands out

The best startup websites share traits: they show the product immediately, communicate the value proposition in one sentence, and have exactly one call to action above the fold [3].

## References

1. [Brand Design Tips from Linear Founder Karri Saarinen](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Karri Saarinen (2025)
2. [Figma CEO Dylan Field: How AI Will Transform Design](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Dylan Field (2025)
3. [Cursor Head of Design Reviews Startup Websites](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Ryo Lu (2025)
4. [Why Every Founder Should Care About Design](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: MVP
body:
# MVP

The minimum viable product (MVP) is the simplest version of a product that can be put in front of users to start the learning cycle. [[Michael Seibel]] emphasizes that the MVP should be built in weeks, not months, and that founders consistently overestimate what needs to be included in a first version [1] [2].

## Order of Operations

Seibel describes the order of operations for going from nothing to a launched MVP [2]:

1. **Pick a problem you care about.** The blocker for most talented technical people is that they do not have an idea. Seibel argues this is because they learned that "every great startup started with a great idea" -- a belief he considers wrong. His first startup (Justin.tv) started with the idea of livestreaming someone's life 24/7. Airbnb started as a site for finding an airbed during sold-out conferences [2].
2. **Find co-founders.** Ideally people you already know well and have worked with.
3. **Build the simplest possible version.** Not a complete product -- just enough to start learning from real users.
4. **Launch and talk to users.** The MVP exists to generate feedback, not revenue.

## What an MVP Should Look Like

Seibel's core principle: the MVP should be embarrassingly simple. If you are not embarrassed by your first version, you launched too late [1].

The Poppy example from Avni Patel Thompson illustrates this vividly. Thompson, a non-technical solo founder, built an on-demand childcare service with her tech stack being: Squarespace for the landing page, Typeform for signups and feedback, Stripe for payments, Google Calendar for scheduling, SMS for communication, and Excel for her database. She had $200 in her business bank account [3]. Her first product was literally a day planner where she manually tracked bookings in handwritten circles. She gave parents her personal phone number and manually texted sitters to match availability [3].

With this zero-code MVP, Thompson grew 10-20% per week. "I can't tell you how different that felt versus my first startup where the whole time I was trying to push this boulder up the hill. And this one it was just parents telling other parents" [3]. She eventually outgrew the day planner and moved to Excel, then found a technical co-founder to build real software [3].

## The Hair on Fire Standard

[[Michael Seibel]] connects MVP thinking to [[Product-Market Fit]]: "In a good market, an MVP is all you need to get your initial customers in the door" [4]. If users have a problem so dire they are willing to try half-baked solutions, the MVP does not need to be polished -- it just needs to address the core pain [4].

## Common MVP Mistakes

1. **Building too much.** Founders want to build the complete vision before shipping anything. Seibel argues this wastes months and prevents learning.
2. **Optimizing before validating.** Building analytics, scaling infrastructure, or polishing UI before knowing whether anyone wants the product.
3. **Refusing to launch without a technical co-founder.** Thompson's Poppy example shows that even complex marketplace products can be validated with no code at all [3].

## References

1. [How to Plan an MVP](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6f-how-to-plan-an-mvp) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
2. [Order of Operations for Starting a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/61-order-of-operations-for-starting-a-startup) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
3. [Building a Startup Is About Solving a Problem](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5O-building-a-startup-is-about-solving-a-problem) -- Avni Patel Thompson (2017)
4. [The Real Product-Market Fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5z-the-real-product-market-fit) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)

title: Company Culture
body:
# Company Culture

Company culture is how a startup's team behaves when no one is watching. Multiple YC speakers argue that culture is not a nice-to-have -- it is a structural decision that determines hiring quality, retention, and the company's ability to scale. Kathryn Minshew, co-founder of The Muse (YC W12), and Alfred Lin, former COO of Zappos, provide complementary perspectives: Minshew from the operational trenches of building culture at a growing startup, Lin from the theoretical foundations of what makes culture a competitive advantage.

## Culture Is Behavior, Not Posters

Minshew is blunt: "Culture is how you behave when no one's watching. Culture is how the founders behave, how the executives behave" [1]. She distinguishes between stated values and actual culture. Companies that put values on walls but do not live them create cynicism.

Her example: a friend's company served fancy free lunches every Friday. When the 2008 recession hit, the food degraded to hot dogs. When an employee asked if the company was cutting back, the office head replied, "No, it's Americana themed." "That's bullshit, and everybody knows it. When you treat your employees like kids, they're going to behave as such" [1].

## When to Define Culture

Minshew argues culture matters from the very beginning -- when you are 2 people, 10 people, 25 people [1]. The Muse grew from 12 to over 80 people in two years, and Minshew learned that the investment in culture must scale with headcount.

At about 25 people, The Muse wrote down six core values. Every interview candidate is tested against all six values. Minshew warns against the fake culture screen of "Would I want to get a beer with this person?" -- this is overly simplistic and causes founders to miss candidates who are different from them [1].

## The "No Assholes" Rule

One of The Muse's best-known values is "No assholes." Minshew acknowledges many companies claim this but few enforce it. "Culture is tough choices. Culture is needing that hire, looking for that one person who's going to solve the biggest problem for your company's existence, and finally finding someone who has every skill you need, who has a pedigree and a background that will make your investors swoon, and that kind of might be a jerk. And deciding not to hire them and keep bleeding and keep looking, because you realize that the impact of one really bad person is far more important than their behavior, and that toxic behavior spreads to the rest of your team" [1].

## Transparency as Culture

The Muse practices radical transparency [1]:
- Shares board decks with the entire company after every board meeting
- Ran all employees through the full company budget in exhaustive detail
- After raising $10M Series A, created a 20-slide presentation showing every budget line item, explaining trade-offs

The result: executives voluntarily reduced their budget requests. "People were self-editing their requests, because they bought in to where we were going, and why it was so important for us to stay thrifty, stay scrappy and stay lean" [1].

## Culture and Scrappiness

Minshew pushes back on the common advice that raising more money inevitably leads to wasteful spending: "I think when people say, 'If you raise more, you will spend it,' that takes away the agency from the founders" [1]. The Muse raised $10M when they expected $5-6M and used the budget transparency exercise to maintain their scrappy ethos despite the larger war chest.

Her summary: "Say no to ball pits. If you need something like this to recruit, you probably have other problems" [1].

## It Does Get Easier

Minshew challenges the common startup wisdom that "it doesn't get easier." After 148 VC rejections out of 150 pitches post-YC, she learned that "sometimes, it actually kind of does. It never gets easy, but the risk of death is not quite so imminent" [1]. As a company builds an executive team, individual crises become less existentially threatening.

## References

1. [Creating Good Company Culture (and Sticking to It)](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5p-creating-good-company-culture-and-sticking-to-it) -- Kathryn Minshew (2016)
2. [Building Culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6r-building-culture) -- Alfred Lin (2019)

title: Scaling and Later-Stage Operations
body:
# Scaling and Later-Stage Operations

[[Sam Altman]] outlines common issues founders face when their startups are 12-24 months in and doing well. The central shift: before [[Product-Market Fit]], the number one job is building a great product. As the company grows past approximately 25 employees, the main job shifts from building a great product to building a great company [1].

## Management

### The Transition Point

There is very little management in the beginning, and that works well. When there are fewer than 20 employees, most companies have everyone report to the founder. That is optimal for the early stage. But what works at 20 employees can be disastrous at 40. Structure must be in place before it is painfully necessary [1].

The starting structure is simple: every employee knows who their manager is, everyone has exactly one manager, every manager knows their direct reports, and people are clustered into sensible teams. "Clarity and simplicity are important -- I wouldn't try to innovate on management structure" [1].

A clear mission and clear goals reduce the burden of management. The better the job a founder does with these, the less management feels overbearing [1].

### Common Management Failures

**Avoiding senior hires**: In the early days, hiring senior people is usually a mistake. As the company scales, adding executives who have built companies before becomes valuable [1].

**Not delegating**: Many founders take on too much personally. This works temporarily but creates a breaking point as the company grows. Delegation is essential [1].

**Delegating poorly**: Some founders delegate grunt work but retain all decisions. This fails for scalability and for making employees effective. Instead, empower employees to own decision processes. "Let them know how you think about a problem, but let them own the decision process" [1]. Delegate the things you do not want to do. Retain involvement in the one thing you care most about [1].

**Personal disorganization**: Developing a system to track tasks and follow-ups is not critical when focused purely on product, but becomes crucial as the founder becomes a manager [1].

**Undercommunicating**: After a certain point, one-on-one conversations with everyone are impossible. The solution: learn to communicate in clear, succinct writing and share it with everyone. "It's one of the highest leverage things you can do, and it scales to thousands of people" [1]. All-hands meetings at least monthly remain important, as do one-on-ones with direct reports [1].

### The Alignment Test

Altman describes a classic diagnostic: "If I asked 10 employees what the top three goals are for the company, would they say the same thing?" Founders always say yes. When Altman actually asks the employees, no two say the same thing. Founders consistently overestimate how effectively they communicate their ideas. Reiterating the roadmap and goals must become habitual [1].

## HR

Most founders ignore HR early, but continuing to ignore it as the company scales is a major mistake. Good HR means three things: a clear management structure, a way for people to raise workplace concerns, and pathways for career evolution [1].

### Performance and Compensation

Performance feedback should happen frequently, even if light. People benefit from regularly hearing how they are doing [1].

Compensation must be structured with bands to keep things fair. "People talk, and they will eventually find out co-workers' compensation levels. If it's all over the place, it can be a complete disaster" [1].

### Equity

YC company data suggests that the most successful companies give out significant equity. Altman advises planning to give away 3-5% of the company each year for ten years from founding. This includes [refresher grants](https://blog.wealthfront.com/how-long-should-you-stay-at-your-job/) for employees approaching vesting cliffs. "You never want an employee in a place where they start thinking about leaving because they've vested three out of their four years. Always stay in front of people's vesting schedules" [1].

### Hiring Process and Diversity

At the 12-24 month mark with [[Product-Market Fit]] achieved, hire a full-time recruiter. Before that is premature. Additional practices [1]:
- **Build a diverse team**: Unity of vision, diversity of backgrounds. Myopic culture is less effective.
- **Announce offers internally**: Up to a few hundred employees, share potential job offers on an internal mailing list. Often someone will know something relevant about the candidate.
- **Structure onboarding**: Have a program so every new employee's first week is planned.
- **Build paths for early employees**: The original 10-15 employees may not fit new executive roles but are loved by peers and highly productive. Proactively discuss their career paths.

### Burnout Monitoring

Building a company is a marathon. Working 100-hour weeks is not sustainable. Monitor the team for burnout, ensure people take vacations, have new challenges, and do new things [1].

## Company Productivity

Building a company that can innovate repeatedly is the hardest thing in business. Most companies do one great thing then stop. Alignment is the key lever -- companies become unproductive when people are not on the same page or do not understand priorities [1].

The solution is a clear mission, roadmap, and goals. Everyone should know the roadmap for the next 3-6 months or a year. Transparent communication through weekly management meetings with direct reports and monthly all-hands meetings is critical [1].

### Longer-Term Productivity Practices

**Offsites**: Take the best people outside the normal workspace for a weekend of big-picture thinking [1].

**Legal document organization**: Assign someone to collect every agreement the company has signed. This saves headaches later [1].

**FP&A**: Building a great financial model of the business enables optimization that most founders miss. Most companies do not hire for this until they have a few hundred employees, but doing it sooner is worthwhile [1].

**Dedicated fundraiser**: Hiring a full-time fundraiser after the B round, whose job is to prepare for the C round, yields better results and less dilution than hiring an investment banker later [1].

**Unit economics**: Figure out how to make more money from each user than you spend, sooner rather than later. Most great companies historically had good unit economics soon after monetization, even if the company as a whole lost money. "It can be tempting to paper over a problem with the business by spending more money instead of fixing the product or service, but this is a major trap" [1].

**Runway management**: Even with great unit economics, never get down to just a couple months of cash in the bank [1].

## Founder Psychology

As the company succeeds, the highs get higher but the lows keep getting lower. People start rooting against the founder. Journalists and internet critics find ways to pick apart the work. Learning to ignore critics early is important [1].

Altman recommends making a genuine long-term commitment: "Have a strategy that assumes you will work on your company for the next 10 years -- it can be an extreme advantage that few founders leverage" [1].

Taking vacations is non-negotiable. Founders who go three or four years without a real vacation will burn out [1].

Focus must be maintained. It is what made the company successful in the first place. Common focus-killers include burnout and talking to potential acquirers [1].

## Marketing and PR

Ignore marketing and PR for a long time. "Press will not make your startup." But once the product is working, shift to caring about it a little [1].

Critical rules:
- Never outsource key messaging to a PR firm or head of marketing. The founder must figure out the company's message [1].
- Pick three or four journalists to develop close relationships with. "No journalist wants to talk to a PR firm; they're much happier to hear from the founder" [1].

## Dealmaking

Business development can start to matter at 12-24 months, assuming a great product exists. Key principles [1]:
- Develop personal connections with counterparts. No one wants to feel used transactionally.
- Set up competitive situations. This is basic negotiation -- you get good terms when you have alternatives and stay persistent.
- Ask for what you want. "Most of the time you won't get laughed out of the room, and you might actually get it" [1].

## References

1. [Later Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)

title: Scaling Laws
body:
# Scaling Laws

Scaling laws describe the empirical relationship between compute, data, model size, and AI performance. Jared Kaplan, Anthropic co-founder, helped establish the foundational research showing that language model performance improves predictably as these variables increase, following power law relationships [1]. This research underpins the strategy of every major AI lab and has profound implications for AI startups.

## The Core Finding

Kaplan's scaling laws research at OpenAI (later refined by the Chinchilla paper from DeepMind) showed that loss (a proxy for model capability) decreases as a smooth function of compute, dataset size, and model parameters [1]. The relationship is a power law: each 10x increase in compute yields a predictable improvement in capability.

The practical implication: if you know the scaling laws for your domain, you can predict how much better a model will be with more compute before you spend the money to train it. This turns AI research into something closer to engineering, where results are predictable within bounds [1].

## How to Train an LLM

Anthropic's head of pretraining describes the practical process of training frontier language models [2]. Key insights:

- **Data quality dominates data quantity.** Careful curation and deduplication of training data matters more than sheer volume. The best models are trained on carefully selected subsets of the internet, not everything [2].
- **Curriculum matters.** The order in which training data is presented affects final model quality. Starting with high-quality text and gradually mixing in more diverse data produces better results than random sampling [2].
- **Evaluation is continuous.** Training runs cost millions of dollars and take weeks. Catching problems early requires constant monitoring of intermediate checkpoints against evaluation benchmarks [2].

## Open Source LLM Architectures

The open-source LLM ecosystem has produced competitive models through different architectural choices [3]:

- **DeepSeek** pioneered mixture-of-experts architectures that activate only a fraction of parameters per token, dramatically reducing inference cost [3].
- **Qwen** (Alibaba) and other Chinese labs have demonstrated that scaling laws hold across languages and training regimes [3].
- **GPT-OSS** alternatives are narrowing the gap with frontier models, particularly for specialized tasks [3].

## The Transformer Foundation

The transformer architecture, introduced in the "Attention Is All You Need" paper, is the foundation for all current language models [4]. The self-attention mechanism allows models to weigh the relevance of different parts of the input when generating each output token. This architecture scales better with compute than previous approaches (RNNs, LSTMs), which is why scaling laws work as well as they do [4].

## Implications for Startups

For AI startups, scaling laws create both opportunities and constraints:

- **Frontier models will keep improving.** If scaling continues, the capabilities available to startups via APIs will grow predictably. Building on the assumption that models get better is a safe bet [1].
- **Fine-tuning has its own scaling dynamics.** Smaller models fine-tuned on domain-specific data can match larger general models for specific tasks. This creates opportunities for startups that accumulate high-quality domain data [2].
- **Inference cost matters.** The economics of deploying AI at scale depend on inference efficiency. Startups that can achieve the same quality with fewer parameters or smarter architecture have a structural advantage [3].

## References

1. [Scaling and the Road to Human-Level AI: Anthropic Co-Founder Jared Kaplan](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Jared Kaplan (2025)
2. [How to Train an LLM with Anthropic's Head of Pretraining](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Anthropic (2025)
3. [GPT-OSS vs Qwen vs DeepSeek: Comparing Open Source LLM Architectures](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
4. [Transformers Explained: The Discovery That Changed AI Forever](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: Convincing Investors
body:
# Convincing Investors

Convincing investors is not a performance problem. It is a truth problem. [[Paul Graham]] argues that inexperienced founders make the same mistake as people who hurt themselves lifting heavy things: they try to lift with their back instead of their legs. Founders try to convince with their pitch rather than letting the startup do the work [1]. The correct approach is to first understand why the startup is worth investing in, then explain that clearly.

## What Investors Are Looking For

Investors seek startups that will be very successful. Because startup outcomes follow a power law with a steep curve, most investors treat "big success" as binary. The conventional wisdom is that roughly 15 startups per year become the big successes, and investors are interested in a founder only if there is some chance, however small, of being one of those 15 [1].

Three things make a startup look like a sufficiently good bet [1]:

1. **Formidable founders**
2. **A promising market**
3. **Some evidence of success so far**

## Formidable Founders

Most investors decide within the first few minutes whether a founder seems like a winner or a loser, and once that opinion is set, it is hard to change [1]. If investors think you are a winner, they focus on reasons to invest. If not, they focus on reasons not to. The same startup characteristic — say, a rich market with a slow sales cycle — gets framed positively or negatively depending on this initial impression [1].

Graham defines "formidable" as someone who seems like they will get what they want, regardless of obstacles. It is close to "confident" but with the added dimension of being justifiably confident [1].

The way to seem formidable as an inexperienced founder is to stick to the truth. Most people can seem confident saying "one plus one is two" because they know it is true. The same principle scales to more impressive claims, as long as the founder has first convinced themselves [1]. Graham writes:

> "Convince yourself that your startup is worth investing in, and then when you explain this to investors they'll believe you." [1]

This requires being a domain expert. Investors can tell quickly whether founders truly understand their market by how they answer questions. A non-expert who is merely enthusiastic will register as an instance of the Dunning-Kruger effect [1].

### What YC Looks For

The YC interview is not a pitch — it is a question-and-answer session. Interviews are 10 minutes long (7 minutes would suffice in practice, but 10 is socially convenient) [2]. The partners need random access to the founders' knowledge, not a sequential presentation [2].

Graham states that if he had to decide whether to fund startups based on a single question, it would be: "How do you know people want this?" [2]. The most convincing answer is "because we and our friends want it," especially when followed by evidence of a crude prototype spreading by word of mouth [2].

What YC looks for above all is founders who understand some group of users and can make what they want. The motto is "Make something people want" [2]. The best thing a founder can do in a YC interview is teach the partners about their users [2].

The worst advice Graham has heard about YC interviews is to take control and deliver a prepared message. When founders do this, it wastes the 10 minutes on prefabricated pitch material instead of genuine answers [2].

### Honesty Over Polish

When asked what could go wrong, the worst answer is "nothing." That convinces partners the founder is either a fool or a liar. Far better to go into detail about risks — that is what experts do [2]. The same principle applies to competitors. Competitors rarely kill startups (poor execution does), but founders should know who their competitors are and discuss strengths and weaknesses candidly [2].

The partners do not expect a perfect idea at seed stage. They expect promising hypotheses. But they do expect founders to be thoughtful and honest [2].

## The Market

Founders think of startups as ideas; investors think of them as markets [1]. The total addressable market (TAM) is the number of potential customers multiplied by average annual spend. The market has to be big, but it does not have to be big yet. Graham notes that it is often better to start in a small market that will turn into a big one, or from which the founder can move into a big one [1].

The standard of proof varies by company age. A three-month-old company at Demo Day only needs to be a promising experiment. A two-year-old company raising a Series A needs to show the experiment worked [1]. At three months, a growth graph is evidence the founders are effective. At two years, it must be evidence of a promising market and a company tuned to exploit it [1].

### The "Why Now?" Question

Every company that gets really big is "lucky" in the sense that growth comes from riding an external wave. To make a convincing case, founders must identify a specific trend they will benefit from. The question "why now?" is critical: if it is such a great idea, why hasn't someone else done it? The ideal answer is that it only recently became a good idea because something changed, and no one else has noticed yet [1].

Microsoft illustrates this. Selling Basic interpreters was not going to produce huge growth, but it positioned Microsoft to expand up the stack as microcomputers grew powerful enough to support more software. Microcomputers turned out to be a bigger wave than even the most optimistic observers predicted in 1975 [1].

## Handling Rejection

Getting rejected is common — even for the best companies. Some VCs would have rejected Microsoft. Some rejected Google [1]. The most common question from investors is "who else is investing?" which puts rejected founders in an awkward position [1].

Graham advises against trying to bluff about other investors' interest. It is the most common lie told to investors, and most founders cannot pull it off [1]. The better approach is to tackle the problem head-on: explain why investors rejected you and why they are mistaken. Experienced investors know that the best ideas are also the scariest, and they all know about the VCs who rejected Google [1].

This strategy works best with the best investors, who are both hard to bluff and already believe most other investors are conventional-minded. Raising money is not like college admissions — a startup rejected by mediocre VCs may still be funded by top ones [1]. Graham recounts that Dropbox was part of a YC Boston batch. All the Boston investors passed. A couple weeks later, Dropbox raised a Series A from Sequoia [1].

## The Three-Step Recipe

Graham distills the approach for founders who are not naturally formidable [1]:

1. Make something worth investing in.
2. Understand why it is worth investing in.
3. Explain that clearly to investors.

Use the same matter-of-fact language used to convince yourself. Conciseness is a test many investors explicitly use, reasoning correctly that founders who cannot explain plans concisely do not really understand them. Alfred Lin emphasizes this is doubly important because the explanation must work at one remove — it must convince not just the partner you speak with, but that partner when re-telling it to colleagues [1].

## Authenticity and Motivation

Founders' motives are usually mixed: money, status, genuine interest in the problem, and unwillingness to work for someone else. The last two are more powerful motivators than the first two [2]. Founders doing it just for money will take the first large acquisition offer. Founders doing it just for status will discover less painful ways to seem cool [2].

The defining quality of founders who build billion-dollar companies is that they are genuinely interested in what they are building. The company is their project. Every billionaire founder could have stopped sooner — gotten acquired or hired someone else to run the company. They kept working because there was nothing else they would rather do [2].

Graham writes:

> "The most reliable way to become a billionaire is to start a company that grows fast, and the way to grow fast is to make what users want." [2]

YC rejects founders whose approach is to exploit people. Bad people make bad founders: their exploitation begins with co-founders (destroying the company's foundation), then moves to users (the hardest people to fool), and the best outcome is a small acquisition before the deception collapses [2].

## References

1. [How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) — [[Paul Graham]] (August 2013)
2. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]] (December 2020)

title: Doing Things That Don't Scale
body:
# Doing Things That Don't Scale

One of the most common types of advice given at Y Combinator is to do things that don't scale [1]. [[Paul Graham]] wrote the definitive essay on this in July 2013, arguing that startups take off because the founders make them take off. The belief that startups either take off or don't -- that you build something, make it available, and either people beat a path to your door or the market doesn't exist -- is wrong [1].

"Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off. There may be a handful that just grew by themselves, but usually it takes some sort of push to get them going. A good metaphor would be the cranks that car engines had before they got electric starters. Once the engine was going, it would keep going, but there was a separate and laborious process to get it going." [1]

## Recruit Users Manually

The most common unscalable thing founders must do at the start is recruit users manually. Nearly all startups have to do this. You cannot wait for users to come to you [1].

Stripe is the canonical example. Despite solving an urgent problem, the Collison brothers were famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition [1]. They invented what YC calls the "Collison installation": when anyone agreed to try Stripe, instead of saying "Great, we'll send you a link," the Collisons would say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot [1].

Two reasons founders resist manual recruitment [1]:
1. **Shyness and laziness.** They would rather sit at home writing code than go out and talk to strangers and probably be rejected by most of them.
2. **The numbers seem too small.** If you have 100 users and grow 10% a week, after a year you have 14,000 users; after two years, 2 million [1].

Airbnb is another classic example. In the early days, Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia went door to door in New York, recruiting new users and helping existing ones improve their listings [1].

## How to Get Your First Users

Getting the first users is one of the most asked questions by new founders [4]. The practical advice converges on:

- **Start with people you know.** Friends, family, former colleagues. Not because they are representative users, but because they will give honest feedback and forgive imperfections.
- **Go where your users are.** If building for developers, post on Hacker News. If building for restaurants, walk into restaurants. If building for students, go to campuses.
- **The Collison Installation.** Do not give people a link and hope they sign up. Set them up yourself, right then, on their device.
- **Be the product.** Before the product is good enough, manually do what the product will eventually automate. Stripe delivered "instant" merchant accounts by manually signing users up behind the scenes [1].

## The Forward Deployed Engineer as DTDS

In the AI era, the [[Forward Deployed Engineers]] model is the natural evolution of doing things that don't scale. The founder goes to the customer's office, sits next to the end user, understands their workflow, and customizes the AI agent for them -- often by modifying prompts during or immediately after the meeting. This is the Collison Installation applied to enterprise AI [3].

## Delight Your Users

Founders should take extraordinary measures not just to acquire users but to make them happy [1]. Wufoo sent each new user a hand-written thank you note [1].

Graham states: "I have never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy" [1].

## The Contained Fire Strategy

Focus on a deliberately narrow market first. "It's like keeping a fire contained at first to get it really hot before adding more logs" [1]. Facebook started at Harvard. Microsoft started with Altair owners. The narrowness is not the goal -- depth is [1].

## Big Launches Don't Work

Graham explicitly warns against relying on a "Big Launch" [1]. There may even be an inverse correlation between launch magnitude and success. Partnerships also usually fail as growth strategies: "Six months later they're all saying that was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it" [1].

## Startup Ideas as Vectors

Graham proposes thinking of startup ideas not as scalars but as pairs: what you are going to build, plus the unscalable thing you are going to do initially to get the company going [1]. In most cases the second component is: recruit users manually and give them an overwhelmingly good experience [1].

## References

1. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2013)
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
3. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)
4. [How to Get Your First Users](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: B2B Pricing
body:
# B2B Pricing

Pricing is one of the most common questions founders ask, and one of the most common sources of paralysis. [[Tom Blomfield]] provides a systematic framework for B2B pricing built on three elements: value, cost, and competition [1]. [[Pete Koomen]] adds tactical lessons from scaling Optimizely to $100M ARR [2]. The consensus: founders almost always charge too little, and over-optimizing pricing early is a bigger mistake than picking a slightly wrong number.

## The Value Equation

The most important element of pricing -- accounting for 80-90% of getting it right -- is the value equation [1]. Sit down with the champion (the person at the customer who wants to buy) and write down what the product will do for them: cost savings, time savings, or revenue increase [1].

Blomfield walks through a concrete example: selling a customer service AI tool to a company with 100 support agents, each costing $100K fully loaded ($10M total). If the tool eliminates 20% of queries, that is $2M in savings [1].

Price at roughly 25-50% of the value delivered. The customer keeps two-thirds, you keep one-third. In this example: charge approximately $700K [1]. This gives the champion a clear story to tell their CFO: "We spend $700K, we save $2M" [1].

The value equation also defines success metrics for pilots: if the tool saves 20% of agent time during a one-month trial with 10 agents, the value holds. If it saves only 15% or a surprising 25%, adjust pricing accordingly [1].

**Never start with cost.** Cost-plus-a-margin pricing always leads to underpricing software [1]. Cost should only be a floor. If your value equation yields $700K and your costs (e.g., OpenAI API fees, AWS) are $200K, you have healthy 70%+ margins. If value yields $150K and costs are $200K, you are in a bad business [1].

Aim for 80-90% software margins. Treat free credits (AWS, OpenAI, Microsoft) as a cash cost -- "don't assume you'll have unlimited credits forever" [1].

## Competition

If a direct competitor underprices you by half, the instinct is to engage in a price war. This is a race to the bottom where nobody wins [1]. The airline industry averages 2.7% net profit margin because seats are a commodity [1].

Instead, differentiate on functionality or value so the comparison is not apples-to-apples [1]. "Pick a niche, focus on certain integrations or certain industries, and show how your product is dramatically better and really incomparable to competitors" [1].

## The Most Common Pricing Mistake: Charging Too Little

Both Blomfield and Koomen emphasize this independently. Blomfield notes that founders who have never worked at a big company lack calibration on enterprise software pricing and default to consumer prices like $19/month or $49/month [1].

Koomen shares a story from early Optimizely: his co-founder Dan quoted a prospect $10,000/month. The prospect negotiated down to $2,000/month and bought. The initial quote was 5x what they paid, and they still bought [2]. "When a customer really wants your product, it's hard to scare them away by quoting a price that's too high" [2].

The Collison brothers at Stripe deliberately charged more than competitors in the beginning. The fact that customers still bought was evidence they were onto something, and it helped them focus on the most desperate customers [2].

Blomfield's escalation technique for founders who freeze: start at a number, and increase by 50% for each subsequent customer. "When you start to lose more than 25% of potential deals based solely on price, you're now probably in the right ballpark" [1]. If every deal closes immediately, you are underpricing [1].

## Pricing Structure

Ask the champion what pricing structures they are accustomed to: monthly flat fee, per-seat, usage bands, credits [1]. Mirror what they know. People are wary of uncapped usage-based pricing [1].

**Prefer committed recurring revenue (MRR/ARR) over pure usage-based pricing.** During a downturn, committed revenue is protected until renewal. Usage-based revenue can fall off a cliff in a bad month [1]. One technique: start with usage-based pricing for new customers, observe their usage for a month or two, then offer a discounted flat monthly fee with a 12-month commitment [1].

Ask what amount the champion can personally sign off on without CFO or legal approval. If their signing authority is $15,000, price the pilot at $14,999 to move quickly [1].

## Published Pricing vs. "Contact Sales"

For enterprise contracts, publishing a fixed price almost certainly leaves money on the table because the value equation differs for each customer [1]. Typical approach: offer one or two cheaper self-serve plans (individual, small team) with basic functionality, and gate features enterprises require -- SOC 2 audit reports, SSO, audit logs, data residency -- behind an enterprise plan that requires contacting sales [1]. This enables pricing enterprises up to 10x more for compliance and security features [1].

## Pricing Dictates Sales Channels

A useful rule of thumb: a salesperson should generate 5x their total compensation in new ARR [1]. If a salesperson earns $100K (base + commission), they should close $500K in new ARR per year. This can be structured as:

| Contract Size | Deals Per Year | Sales Motion |
|---|---|---|
| $500K | 1 | Whale hunting |
| $25K | 20 (~2/month) | Account executive |
| $1K | 500 (~42/month) | Inside sales / call center |

At $1K annual contract value, an outbound sales team is not viable -- you need self-serve or inbound [1].

## Free Trials and Pilots

Long free trials are counterproductive because the customer has not committed to using the product [1]. Keep pilots to two to four weeks with clear success criteria derived from the value equation [1].

A better technique: push customers to sign an annual contract from the start with a 30-60 day money-back guarantee. This defaults to recurring revenue and can be counted as such immediately [1].

## Playing to Startup Strengths

When a two-person startup sells to enterprises, do not try to look bigger than you are. Instead, offer what large vendors cannot: "You can have the phone number of the founders, and we're on call 24/7 to come and fix your problems. You're certainly not going to get that from Salesforce or Oracle" [1].

## References

1. [How To Price For B2B](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LI-how-to-price-for-b2b) -- [[Tom Blomfield]] (2024)
2. [Enterprise Sales for Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LF-enterprise-sales-for-founders) -- [[Pete Koomen]] (2024)

title: Product-Market Fit
body:
# Product-Market Fit

Product-market fit describes the state where a startup's product satisfies strong market demand. It is one of the most referenced concepts in the YC canon, cited across 11+ articles in this wiki as the prerequisite for nearly every subsequent startup activity -- from [[Growth]] investment to [[Series A Fundraising]]. The consensus across [[Sam Altman]], [[Paul Graham]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], and multiple other YC partners is that PMF is the single most important milestone for an early-stage company.

## The Core Test: Spontaneous Recommendation

Altman provides the clearest test: "The degree to which you're successful approximates the degree to which you build a product that is so good people spontaneously tell their friends about it" [11]. He considers this the number one lesson YC tries to teach. "Startups always ask us for the secret to success. They always want to believe it's something other than this, because this is really hard to do. But this is it" [11]. Google, Facebook, and most successful companies were discovered through friends recommending them [11].

A product that achieves this must be simple to explain and easy to understand. "If you can't explain in a few words what you do, and if at least some people don't say 'Oh, that's pretty interesting,' that's usually a sign of unclear thinking or a need that is not big enough" [11].

## What PMF Actually Looks Like

[[Michael Seibel]] defines product-market fit by quoting Marc Andreessen: "The customers are buying the product just as fast as you can make it -- or usage is growing just as fast as you can add more servers. Money from customers is piling up in your company checking account" [1]. Seibel restates this: a startup has reached PMF when it is overwhelmed with usage, usually to the point where the team cannot even make major changes because they are swamped keeping the product running [1].

Seibel warns that founders routinely believe they have found PMF when they have not. "This is a huge problem because they start hiring people, increasing burn, and optimizing their product before they've actually discovered what needs to be built" [1]. The signal he looks for is not headcount or fundraising milestones but "a frantic founding team trying to deal with ever-growing numbers of happy, loyal, and ideally paying customers" [1]. Until that point, the startup should stay lean and "resemble a Navy SEAL team instead of an Army battalion" [1].

PMF feels like two things happening simultaneously [10]:

1. **Your product is breaking with usage.** Software components, operational components, or human components built without scale in mind are starting to fail.
2. **The usage is profitable.** The users are the type you want with the economics you want [10].

Both components must be present. Breaking without profitable economics is not PMF. Profitable economics without organic growth pressure is not PMF [10].

## The Hair on Fire Test

Seibel extends Sequoia's "hair on fire" metaphor [1]. If a friend were standing next to you with their hair on fire, that fire would be the only thing they cared about. If you handed them a hose (the perfect product), they would put the fire out immediately. If you handed them a brick, they would still grab it and try to hit themselves on the head to put the fire out [1].

"You need to find problems so dire that users are willing to try half-baked, v1, imperfect solutions" [1]. This is why MVPs work: in a good market, an MVP is all you need to get initial customers in the door [1].

## Market First, Not Solution First

Seibel identifies a core mistake: "Founders often hold too tightly onto solutions and too loosely onto problems. The problem, i.e. the market, is the real opportunity" [1]. The founder's initial v1 idea for how to solve a problem is usually wrong. "Founder genius is most often expressed in choosing the right problem to solve," Seibel writes, quoting Andreessen's observation that "the market pulls product out of the startup" [1].

[[Dalton Caldwell]] reinforces this in his video on finding PMF [2]. The search for PMF is not a marketing problem -- it is a product problem [2].

## The Segment Story: Finding PMF Through Pivots

Peter Reinhardt, co-founder of Segment (YC S11), provides one of the most detailed accounts of the search for PMF [9]. Segment tried and abandoned four different products before finding PMF with their analytics API. The pattern across all failures was the same: they would build something, launch it, get modest initial interest, and then usage would flatline [9].

The breakthrough came when they open-sourced a small internal tool -- analytics.js -- and it spread virally through the developer community. The sign of PMF was unmistakable: developers were adopting the tool faster than Segment could support them. "You'll know when you have it" [9].

## Fake Product-Market Fit

Seibel identifies "fake PMF" as one of the five things that kill startups post-seed [10]. It is "extremely common for companies to say they have product market fit when they don't and they have nothing close to it" [10].

Causes of fake PMF [10]:
- **Raising money from impressive people.** Founders believe that if famous investors chose their company, they must have PMF.
- **Raising a Series A pre-PMF.** A significant number of companies raise $5-10M without a product people love.
- **Magical thinking.** Ignoring obvious facts like churn rates and payback periods.
- **Lack of strong technical talent.** Founders convince themselves the product is good enough because improving it feels too hard.

Signs of fake PMF [10]:
- Lots of hiring (team jumps from 4 to 12)
- More business people than engineers
- No metrics dashboards
- Flat growth graphs
- Changing KPIs -- switching from revenue to "monthly usage" because revenue flatlined

Preventive measures [10]:
- Pick an honest KPI (almost always revenue for SaaS) and stick with it
- Track retention carefully
- Cap burn at a fixed monthly amount until PMF is achieved
- Start with strong technical co-founders
- Apply a three-month essentiality test to every hire

## Signals of Product-Market Fit

Altman's test: "When they actively recommend your product to friends, you're in the right place." Net Promoter Score can be a useful measurement [3].

[[Anu Hariharan]] provides quantitative benchmarks through retention metrics. If the retention curve flattens and runs parallel to the x-axis after an initial dip, and the long-term retention rate is in line with vertical benchmarks, the product has achieved fit [4]. See [[Growth]] for specific retention benchmarks by vertical.

[[Emmett Shear]] frames it through feature prioritization: once PMF is established, the question shifts from "does anyone want this?" to "what should we build next?" -- a qualitatively different problem [5].

[[Jessica Livingston]], after seeing over 1,000 YC companies, states that growth is the test: "If you have a good growth rate, which in a startup means at least 10% per month, you're on the right track" [13].

## Measuring Product Quality

Suhail Doshi, founder of Mixpanel, provides a framework for measuring whether a product is approaching PMF [12]:

- **Retention curves**: Do they flatten or approach zero? A flattening curve indicates PMF.
- **Usage frequency**: DAU/MAU reveals engagement depth.
- **Core action completion**: Identify the one action that defines value delivery and measure what percentage of new users complete it.

Doshi warns against vanity metrics: total signups, page views, or app downloads tell you nothing about whether the product solves a real problem [12].

## PMF and Growth

The relationship between PMF and growth is sequential, not simultaneous. The consensus across multiple speakers is that growth investment before PMF is wasteful and dangerous. Hariharan's growth team guide is explicit: do not build a growth team before you have proven Retention [4]. Altman warns that growing on a mediocre product creates a "leaky bucket" -- users come in the door but do not stay [3].

Once PMF is achieved, users become "a free marketing and sales force" [3]. Altman states that "startups need a market that is either started to undergo or is soon going to undergo exponential growth" [11]. Riding a growing market with a product people love is the combination that produces breakout companies.

## PMF and Fundraising

PMF is increasingly the threshold for [[Series A Fundraising]]. [[Jessica Livingston]] describes the gap bluntly: "Seed investors are looking for promise. Series A investors are looking for performance" [13]. The traction slides in a Series A deck are fundamentally evidence of PMF [6].

## What to Do Before PMF

Seibel's prescription: "At YC, we encourage founders to build MVPs. In a good market, an MVP is all you need to get your initial customers in the door" [1]. The advantage small companies have over big ones is speed, unusually good customer service, and lower customer expectations. "So to find product market fit, choose a market where users have a real, meaningful problem, launch quickly, and listen to your users" [1].

## References

1. [The Real Product-Market Fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5z-the-real-product-market-fit) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
2. [How to Find Product Market Fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7V-how-to-find-product-market-fit) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (2020)
3. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)
4. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)
5. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- [[Emmett Shear]] (n.d.)
6. [How to Build a Great Series A Pitch and Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck) -- Janelle Tam (n.d.)
7. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
8. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
9. [Finding Product-Market Fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/86-how-to-start-a-startup-finding-product-market-fit) -- Peter Reinhardt (2017)
10. [The 5 Things That Kill Startups Post Seed Rounds](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8a-the-5-things-that-kill-startups-post-seed-rounds) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2020)
11. [How to Succeed with a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (2018)
12. [How to Measure Your Product](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8C-how-to-measure-your-product) -- Suhail Doshi (2017)
13. [How Not to Fail](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5l-how-not-to-fail) -- [[Jessica Livingston]] (2017)

title: Starting Young
body:
# Starting Young

The question of when to start a startup -- and particularly whether to start one in or immediately after college -- is one [[Paul Graham]] has revisited multiple times, with his position evolving from encouragement (2005-2007) to more nuanced caution (2014). The tension between the advantages of youth and the dangers of starting too early runs through several of his most important essays.

## The Sweet Spot: Mid-Twenties

Graham's considered view, expressed in his 2006 student guide, is that the sweet spot for startup founders is the mid-twenties [1]. The question is what advantages 25-year-olds have over 21-year-olds, and why the answer is not older still.

**Advantages of young founders [1]:**

1. **Stamina.** Long hours are universal in successful startups. Younger founders need this even more because they are probably less efficient than they will be later.

2. **Poverty.** This sounds counterintuitive, but poverty means you can live cheaply, which is critically important because nearly every startup that fails does so by running out of money. A low burn rate gives more opportunity to recover from mistakes. "The guys with kids and mortgages are at a real disadvantage" [1]. Even more important than living cheaply is thinking cheaply. The Apple II was popular partly because Woz designed it for himself and couldn't afford anything more [1].

3. **Rootlessness.** Young people are more mobile -- no house, less stuff, fewer serious relationships. Many startups involve someone moving, and a startup hub matters enormously. Houston, Chicago, and Miami have microscopically small numbers of successful startups per capita [1].

4. **Colleagues.** The number one question at YC is "Where can I find a cofounder?" At 30, your school friends have either lost touch or are tied down by jobs. At a good college, you are concentrated with ambitious, technical people more than you will ever be again [1]. "Co-founders really should be people you already know. And by far the best place to meet them is school" [1]. Google, Yahoo, and Microsoft were all founded by people who met in school [1].

5. **Ignorance (innocence).** Jessica Livingston's interviews with startup founders revealed a pattern: "One after another said that if they'd known how hard it would be, they would have been too intimidated to start" [1]. Steve Wozniak put it strongly: "All the best things that I did at Apple came from (a) not having money and (b) not having done it before, ever" [1].

**Disadvantages of young founders [1]:**

The core problem is that young founders build things that look like class projects -- lacking (1) an iterative definition of a real problem and (2) market-level intensity [1]. See [[Early-Stage Mistakes]] for the full analysis.

The 24-year-old's real advantage over the 20-year-old: "they know what they're trying to avoid" [1]. To the average undergrad, getting rich means buying Ferraris. To someone who has learned about the relationship between money and work, it means opting out of the brutal equation governing 99.9% of people's lives. "Someone who gets this will work much harder at making a startup succeed -- with the proverbial energy of a drowning man" [1].

## The Excuses for Not Starting

Graham's 2007 essay systematically dismantles 16 common reasons people give for not starting a startup [2]. His goal is explicitly to increase self-confidence, but with the constraint that he is motivated to be honest -- encouraging people who shouldn't start startups makes his own life worse [2].

**Excuses that are mostly bogus:**

- **Too young.** The most successful founder YC had funded at the time, Sam Altman, was 19. But Altman is an outlier: "he seemed like he had a 40-year-old inside him" [2]. The real test: whether you have crossed the adult threshold, not your chronological age.

- **Too inexperienced.** "The best way to get experience if you're 21 is to start a startup" [2]. The Kiko founders started right out of college, made a lot of mistakes, and by the time YC funded their second startup a year later, they had become "extremely formidable" [2]. Getting a normal job may actually make you less able to start a startup by "turning you into a tame animal who thinks he needs an office to work in and a product manager to tell him what software to write" [2].

- **Not smart enough.** "If you're smart enough to worry that you might not be smart enough to start a startup, you probably are" [2]. Starting a startup does not require exceptional intelligence. Enterprise software companies are sales companies, and sales depends mostly on effort [2].

- **Know nothing about business.** The coefficient of this variable should be zero. "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup. The initial focus should be the product" [2]. Pretty much 100% of startups that make something popular manage to make money from it. Acquirers buy strategic value, meaning something people want [2].

- **No idea.** In the average YC startup, 70% of the idea is new at the end of the first three months, sometimes 100%. YC is "so sure the founders are more important than the initial idea" that they accept applications with no idea at all [2].

- **No room for more startups.** There is no economic limit on the number of startups. "Startups make wealth, which means they make things people want, and if there's a limit on the number of things people want, we are nowhere near it. I still don't even have a flying car" [2].

- **Fear of uncertainty.** "If you start a startup, it will probably fail. Seriously, though, this is not a bad way to think about the whole experience." No employer will hold it against you. "In America, companies, like practically everything else, are disposable" [2].

**Excuses that are real:**

- **Not determined enough.** Determination is probably the single best predictor of success. But most hackers underestimate their own determination [2].

- **No cofounder.** "A startup is too much for one person to bear." All investors, without exception, are more likely to fund you with a cofounder. If you don't have one, get one -- "It's more important than anything else" [2].

- **Family to support.** Graham won't advise anyone with a family to start a startup. An alternative: start a consulting business and gradually turn it into a product business, or join an existing startup as an early employee [2].

- **Not ready for commitment.** If a startup succeeds, it will consume at least three or four years. This is a legitimate reason to wait, though Graham notes you will probably spend just as long at a regular job with less spare time than you expect [2].

## The 2014 Recalibration

By October 2014, Graham had shifted to more cautionary advice for students [3]. His Stanford lecture argues explicitly: "Do not start a startup in college" [3].

The reasoning: starting a startup at 20 is "like a brutally fast depth-first search. Most people should still be searching breadth-first at 20" [3]. You can do things in your early 20s that you cannot do before or after -- "plunge deeply into projects on a whim and travel super cheaply with no sense of a deadline" [3].

Mark Zuckerberg will never get to bum around a foreign country. "Success has taken a lot of the serendipity out of his life. Facebook is running him as much as he's running Facebook" [3].

The key insight: there is not even a tradeoff. You are not sacrificing anything by waiting, because you are more likely to succeed if you do [3]. The usual way startups take off is for founders to make them take off (see [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]]), and "it's gratuitously stupid to do that at 20" [3].

What students should do instead: "just learn" [3]. Become good at things that matter. Work on problems that interest you. The optimal preparation is not a vocational version of college focused on entrepreneurship but "the classic version of college as education for its own sake" [3].

Graham flags university entrepreneurship programs as misguided. "They go out of their way to ensure their students are well supplied with contraceptives, and yet they're setting up entrepreneurship programs and startup incubators left and right" [3]. The university cannot teach the essential thing -- understanding user needs -- because that requires actually starting the company, at which point you are no longer really a student [3].

## The Google Trap

Graham observes that while Google seems like the ideal place to work, "zero startups come out of there. In that respect it's a black hole" [1]. People like working there too much to leave. If you hope to start a startup one day, a pleasant employer can lull you into staying indefinitely even if leaving would be a net win. You need "activation energy" to start a startup, and a comfortable job suppresses it [1].

The best place to work if you want to start a startup is probably a startup. It is the right sort of experience, and one way or another it will be over quickly: you will either end up rich, or the startup will get acquired and start to suck, or it will blow up and you will be free [1].

## The Default Shift

Graham sees the shift from employment as default to startups as an option in historical terms. "Getting a job" is only about a hundred years old as the default way to make a living -- before that it was farming [2]. The shift from farming to manufacturing would have seemed insanely risky to medieval serfs [2].

"How grim it would be to commute every day to a cubicle in some soulless office complex, and be told what to do by someone you had to acknowledge as a boss -- someone who could call you into their office and say 'take a seat,' and you'd sit! Imagine having to ask permission to release software to users" [2].

Graham believes startups may represent another such historic shift in how wealth is created [2].

## References

1. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
2. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
3. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
4. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
5. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2005)

title: Competing with Big Companies
body:
# Competing with Big Companies

Fear of large competitors is one of the most common psychological traps YC group partners observe in founders. [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], and [[Brad Flora]] describe the pattern: a company doing great will psych itself out because a competitor raised five million dollars or got press coverage, replacing excitement with fear [1]. The advice they repeat: most likely, everyone is going to die. Competitors are not the threat founders think they are.

## The Lies Founders Tell Themselves

### "My competitor raised money, so they're winning"

Raising money is not the same as building a product people want. Founders read TechCrunch and see announcements, then assume the competitor's interior matches their polished exterior [1]. But years later, people who worked at those competitors describe chaos: "everyone was leaving, the CEO was on a bender, we lost all our big customers" [1]. Every startup is badly broken somehow -- founders only see how screwed their own company is, while seeing everyone else's marketing [1].

### "We're in a land grab situation"

A common claim, but when partners ask the follow-up questions -- "Are your customers talking about them? Are you losing deals? Have you looked at their product?" -- the answers reveal that customers are usually not talking about the competitors [1]. It is a "confusion of interfaces" to think that a competitor in the New York Times means customers are having a worse experience with your product [1].

### "My competitors must know something I don't"

Seibel tells the story of Justin.tv building a feature exclusively for copyright owners -- something visible on the site but with no clear user value. Two weeks later, a competitor released the same feature. "We were like, oh crap, this is clearly the blind leading the blind. They don't even know why we have this" [1]. The company only started working when the founders talked to actual users instead of copying competitors [1].

## When to Worry About Competition

### Structural Advantages

Legitimate competitive concerns exist when a platform owner enters your market. Brad Flora's company Perfect Audience used the Facebook Exchange API. When Facebook launched Custom Audiences and sold it directly, they owned the platform, the code, and pricing. "That's a time when you should worry about competition. And so we sold the company" [1].

Microsoft Teams vs. Slack is another example: Microsoft may not have the better product, but "good enough" combined with massive enterprise distribution can win [1].

### The Pattern: Structural Advantage Alone Is Not Enough

A structural advantage with a bad product loses. Microsoft's music player and mobile operating system both failed despite the company's distribution. A structural advantage with a product that is good enough can win, especially when a startup has already specced the product for the incumbent to copy [1].

But even this pattern is rare. The partners struggle to find examples of FAANG companies launching products that killed fast-growing startups [1]:
- Snapchat survived Facebook's copies
- TikTok survived Facebook's copies
- Instacart survived Amazon's grocery efforts -- "picking the right avocado was too big of a challenge for this trillion-dollar company" [1]
- Cruise survived despite Google having a decade head-start on self-driving cars [1]

### Having No Competitors Is a Red Flag

When a founder says "we don't have any competitors" in a YC interview, the partners "buckle up" [1]. It shows a lack of understanding of the customer and what they are doing now to solve the problem. It is very unlikely that no customer has ever tried another solution [1].

## AI and the OpenAI Question

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] address the specific fear that OpenAI and large AI companies will kill all startups [2]. Their answer: unless you believe AGI will be created in the short term, OpenAI is not going to kill all startups. History suggests the opposite -- major technological changes (farming, electricity, internet, mobile, cloud computing) have consistently created more businesses, and startups are relatively advantaged over incumbents during rapid transitions [2].

The key distinction is between "cargo culting AI" -- superficially adding AI features to raise money -- and genuinely using AI to build better features that dramatically increase retention, product quality, or willingness to pay [2]. The second is real value creation, analogous to building high-quality mobile apps when the App Store launched [2].

Seibel notes that until large AI companies solve AGI, that will be their first, second, and third goal. Using existing AI tools to make people's lives better is simply not on their priority list, creating a massive opportunity for startups [2].

## The Core Advice
