"Build a better product for your users. It's the club you can bludgeon everyone else with" [1]. Instead of worrying about land grabs, structural advantages, or competitor fundraising: know what your competitors are building, but do not let it deflate you. Spend time on things you have control over [1].

## References

1. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: How to Compete with Amazon and Google](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iz-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-how-to-compete-with-amazon-and-google) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]] (n.d.)
2. [Will OpenAI Kill All Startups?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ie-dalton-michael-will-openai-kill-all-startups) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)

title: Startup Management
body:
# Startup Management

Most startup founders begin managing teams with zero management experience. As Aaron Epstein, founder of Creative Market, describes it: "One of the most difficult things about starting a company is that you have to create both a product that people love and a company where people want to work at the same time" [1]. This article compiles practical management advice for founders who are building and leading teams for the first time.

## The Core Tension

The dual mandate of startups creates a fundamental challenge: "Great people build great products, and great people won't tolerate a bad environment for very long" [1]. The commonly cited observation that "people leave managers, not companies" applies with particular force to startups, where many first-time founders "raise a bunch of money and start building a team without any management experience at all" [1].

## Delegation and Scaling

Delegation is the foundational management skill for founders. Epstein frames it as a necessary evolution: "As a founder you're used to doing it all, and it can be scary to shed responsibilities you've always owned, but you can't scale yourself and focus on the highest leverage opportunities until you get things off your plate" [1].

The practical signal for when to delegate: "If you think you're going to have to work especially late or can't get to the things you need to in a given day, take that as a signal to delegate to your team" [1].

The flip side of delegation is growth opportunity creation. Great employees often value personal growth more than compensation: "Don't hoard [new responsibilities] all for yourself" [1]. This connects to the broader principle from [[Startup Leadership]] that a CEO's role transitions from Doer-in-Chief to Company-Builder-in-Chief.

## Investing in Star Employees

Epstein draws an analogy to customer retention: "Similar to how it's much easier to retain an existing customer than to acquire a new one, it's much easier to keep an existing star employee happy than find a new star" [1]. The advice is to go "above and beyond" to make stars feel valued, appreciated, and rewarded.

## Setting Standards and Accountability

### High Bar

"Great people want to do big, meaningful things. Push your team with aggressive goals, and you might be surprised what they will accomplish" [1].

### Deadlines and Follow-Through

When assigning tasks, always ask "by when?" and add a calendar note to follow up [1]. When standards or deadlines are not met, give direct feedback to change future behavior.

### Verbalizing Feedback

Epstein emphasizes the power of simply stating feelings: "'I'm disappointed in this work' is extremely powerful in helping people directly understand where you stand. People aren't mind-readers, so rather than just jumping into solutions when giving feedback, state your feelings out loud" [1].

## Leading by Example

"Teams embody the characteristics of their leaders, and the things you care about will be the things your team cares about" [1]. If a founder pays attention to details, the team will learn to do the same. This extends to credit and blame: "Use 'I' when talking about a screw-up, and use 'we' (or better yet, specific contributors' names) when talking about successes" [1].

## Communication

### Sharing the Big Picture

Employees need to understand how their work fits into the company's larger goals. "It's easy to take it for granted that your team is clear on this, but this is one of those things where you want to constantly make that connection" [1]. This reinforces the [[Mission-to-Metrics Framework]] that [[Ali Rowghani]] describes as one of a CEO's three non-delegable tasks.

### Repetition

"You should feel like you sound like a broken record about the things that are important to your company -- your mission, your vision, your KPIs, etc." [1]. The test for adequate repetition: "every single person on your team will be able to explain them to a complete stranger exactly how you would've said it" [1].

## Hiring

### Hire Slow, Fire Fast

"It's the thing everyone says and few have the discipline to do" [1]. The reasoning: "Great people want to work with other great people, so resist the temptation to fill open positions with mediocre candidates, and don't settle. You should be thrilled about each new hire" [1].

### Hire for Hunger

Skills can be learned; passion cannot be manufactured. "Passion for your mission, business, and team will do more to drive an employee to make a big, long-term impact than any specific skills they may have" [1].

### Onboarding

"It's unfair to expect even the best people to hit the ground running on day one without being properly onboarded" [1]. Managers must invest early in setting expectations, showing the ropes, teaching the business, and providing clear direction.

### Earning Trust

New employees should not be given full autonomy immediately: "Make them prove themselves first by working more closely with them and frequently reviewing their work until they've earned your full trust" [1].

## Adapting Management Style to Individuals

Epstein's favorite management interview question is "How would you describe your management style?" and "the best possible answer is 'it depends on the employee'" [1]. Each person has a unique combination of experience, motivations, and personality. The assumption that everyone is self-motivated like the founder is a common mistake: "Get curious to figure out what drives and motivates each individual on your team, rather than assuming everyone's like you" [1].

## Decision-Making

### Including the Team

"If you've hired great people, you'd be crazy not to include them in important decisions" [1]. The key is defining the process upfront: will the decision be made by committee, delegated to someone, or made by the founder after gathering input? "Even if your team disagrees with the final call, by including them in the process they will better understand the decision, feel like their opinion was valued, and be able to get on board" [1].

### Showing Your Work

"Explain your decision-making process" [1]. When taking controversial actions or making difficult decisions, explaining the rationale earns buy-in and teaches the team how to make similar decisions.

### Admitting Uncertainty

"There's nothing wrong with saying you'll need some time to think or learn more before making an important decision" [1].

## Healthy Tension

"Differing perspectives help make teams and products better, so a little professional tension can be really valuable" [1]. The limit: tension must be net-positive. If it forces decisions to stall or shifts from professional to personal, "you need to take action to eliminate it quickly before it drags down the team or blows up" [1].

## One-on-Ones

"Have consistent 1-on-1s every week" [1]. These meetings are for the employee's benefit, so they should drive the agenda. The manager's role is to ask open-ended questions: "How are things going?", "How are you feeling?", "What's your opinion on X?" Prioritizing these meetings and refusing to cancel them "will send a signal to your team that you care about their happiness" [1].

## The Maker-to-Manager Transition

As organizations grow, the best individual contributors are promoted to managers. Epstein warns that "just because someone is an outstanding individual contributor doesn't mean they'll be an outstanding manager right off the bat, because it requires a completely different set of skills and experience" [1].

The common failure mode: new managers try to do both their old maker work and their new management duties, "spending 75% effort on making and 75% effort on managing -- a 150% workload resulting in less than 100% output in each area" [1]. The solution is to "put structure in place to allow them to focus on being a manager first and foremost, and take maker duties off of their plate" [1].

## Career Development

"Every 6 months, ask your employees where they want to be in their career in the next 2-3 years. Work with them to put together a plan, help them get the skills and experience they need, and guide them on a course to get there" [1].

## References

1. [Advice for New Managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers) — Aaron Epstein (n.d.)

title: Startup Metrics
body:
# Startup Metrics

[[Tom Blomfield]], YC Group Partner and founder of Monzo (8 million customers in the UK) and GoCardless, provides two comprehensive lectures on startup metrics -- one for B2B companies and one for consumer companies [1] [2]. The core message: metrics are like instruments in an airplane cockpit. Without them, you are flying blind. But too many metrics -- or the wrong ones -- are equally dangerous.

## Principles Before Metrics

Before launching, pick four or five key metrics to track accurately -- not 30 or 50 [1]. The number will grow over time. Use the simplest analytics solution you can operate, even just SQL queries against your database [1].

Three warnings Blomfield emphasizes:

1. **Don't launch without metrics.** Blomfield has seen founders have great launches on Hacker News or Product Hunt with hundreds of daily users but no idea whether those users are new or returning, active or churning [1].

2. **Don't drown in metrics.** A 500-metric dashboard before launch signals a founder who has watched too many YouTube videos. "You don't have the volume of users or data to make those kinds of split tests sensibly" [1]. Split-test important decisions (should the price be $80/year or $200/year?) but not button colors.

3. **Don't hide behind metrics.** "Brian from Airbnb still hosts Airbnb users in his home. It's an obsession with staying close to customers" [1]. Metrics supplement talking to users, not replace it.

Agree on metric definitions with your entire team and write them down. Constant arguments about definitions destroy meeting productivity. Keep definitions consistent over time -- even if the numbers are disappointing. "You're only fooling yourself" if you switch from weekly active to monthly active to make the numbers look better [1].

## The Three Numbers Every Investor Update Needs

For every company, B2B or consumer, three numbers should be at the top of every investor update [1]:

1. **Revenue** -- the primary metric for most B2B companies. If revenue is zero, report it as zero. One founder Blomfield admired sent ten successive monthly updates with a big zero as the main metric. "She kept herself honest" [1].

2. **Burn rate** -- net monthly costs minus revenues. The amount by which your bank balance decreases each month [1].

3. **Runway** -- cash in bank divided by burn rate. "In ten months, you're going to run out of money, and the startup will be bankrupt" [1].

If these three numbers are not at the top of your investor updates, "I always assume this founder has something to hide" [1].

## Avoiding Vanity Metrics

Vanity metrics are numbers that look big and may keep increasing but are not tied to company success [1]. Common examples:

- **GMV (Gross Merchandise Value)**: The total value of goods sold on a platform, not the platform's actual revenue. eBay reports GMV, but revenue is a fraction of it [1].
- **Gross Transaction Value**: A neobank Blomfield worked with in the Middle East reported 50% growth in transaction value every two weeks. Revenue was flat for two months -- they were signing bigger customers but giving massive cashback rebates [1].
- In the early internet era: page views, unique visitors [1].

"Almost always, especially for B2B companies, your key metric should be revenue" [1]. Whatever metric you pick, people will optimize for it. Make sure it is the right one.

## Retention: The Make-or-Break Metric

Retention is one of the most important metrics for all startups [1]. If you sign up 100 paying customers in January, how many are still paying in February, March, April?

### The Layer Cake vs. The Leaky Bucket

Blomfield uses a visualization of stacked monthly cohorts to illustrate why retention matters [1]:

**High retention (the layer cake):** Each monthly cohort retains most of its users. After 18 months, you have 18 cohorts all paying you. The team could go on holiday for a month and revenue stays consistent. With expanding revenue -- customers growing their usage over time -- the business grows even without new signups [1]. GoCardless and Stripe exhibit this pattern: customers implement a payment solution once and rarely switch [1].

**Low retention (the leaky bucket):** Customers signed up in January are gone by month three or six. Instead of building stable layers, you scramble to fill a bucket that leaks as fast as you pour. "You'll reach some natural plateau where you're working as hard as you can to fill up the customers who simply churned out last month. It's a futile endeavor" [1].

The key insight: "I take a 20% retention that flattens out over a higher retention initially that goes to zero" [1]. What matters is that the retention curve flattens somewhere -- at any level.

### Net Dollar Retention (B2B)

Net dollar retention (NDR) measures whether revenue from a given cohort grows or shrinks over time [1]. Example: sign up 10 customers in January at $10K/month each ($100K MRR). One year later, two have canceled ($-20K), but three have upsold to $20K/month ($+30K). Net change: +$10K. January cohort now generates $110K/month. That is 110% net dollar retention [1].

NDR above 100% means cohorts grow over time. Below 100% means they shrink -- the leaky bucket [1].

**Benchmarks:**
- Early-stage B2B SaaS: aim for 125% to 150% NDR [1]
- Mature B2B SaaS: 110% to 120% is good [1]
- Below 100%: "Something is wrong. You are churning off customers. They don't love the product" [1]

Three reasons early-stage NDR should be high: you have probably underpriced initially, you are adding features constantly, and you should be getting better at upselling [1].

## Gross Margin

Gross margin is revenue minus cost of goods sold (COGS) -- the variable costs that scale per customer [1].

Pure B2B SaaS historically had 95% margins (just AWS/bandwidth costs). AI changes this: credits paid to OpenAI, Anthropic, or other foundation models are now a significant COGS. "Just because you're getting free credits doesn't mean that cost doesn't exist. It just means you're hiding it for the moment" [1].

Operationally intensive businesses (grocery delivery, house painting, heat pump installation) may have 5-15% gross margins. Blomfield advises exploring whether a software-only version of the business exists: "Instead of running a delivery company, can you take the software that powers all of that and sell it to other delivery companies?" [1]

### Scaling Negative Margins

During the ZIRP era (2010-2021), companies like Uber scaled negative-margin businesses using capital as a weapon, subsidizing both sides of the market to reach network-effect density before competitors [1]. This approach became popular in ride-sharing, ten-minute grocery, and electric scooters. "There's a whole wasteland of startups that tried to do that" [1].

Monzo lost 30-40 pounds per customer on its first half million users, then flipped unit economics by bringing technology in-house, introducing charges, and adding paid products [1]. The lesson: "If you start with negative unit economics, you really have to have a plan to fix them. Don't scale your customer base whilst you have negative unit economics. Fix them first, then scale" [1].

## Consumer-Specific Metrics

### Growth Rate Benchmarks

For consumer companies, Blomfield provides specific growth benchmarks [2]:

| Monthly Growth Rate | Assessment |
|---|---|
| 15%+ | Good -- will 5x user base every year |
| 10% | Okay -- will approximately 3x every year |
| 5% or lower | Unlikely to reach breakout success |

### Organic vs. Paid Growth

The best consumer companies have 80%+ organic growth [2]. Facebook and WhatsApp in their early days were 100% organic. A 50/50 split between paid and organic is acceptable. Below 50% organic "is pretty worrisome" [2].

The reason: as you pay ad platforms more to acquire more users, cost per user rises. "All these competing companies bid and bid and bid. Only Google and Meta really win in that battle. You all take your margins to zero" [2]. Platform changes (e.g., iOS tracking changes) can wipe out half your business overnight [2].

"I've not seen any great consumer company get to true scale where more than 50% of their signups are coming from paid ads" [2].

**Organic growth levers:**

- **Virality**: one user using the product introduces it to others. Facebook's photo-tagging sent emails to unregistered friends. Wordle's score-sharing on social media [2].
- **Network effects**: the product gets better with more users. WhatsApp is useless alone but valuable when everyone uses it [2].

Blomfield argues: "On these network effect and viral loops, you will pay back every day for the rest of the life of the company. Whereas with ad spend, you spend the money one day. Tomorrow it's gone" [2].

### Unit Economics

Revenue per customer minus variable costs per customer. At Monzo, each customer generated about $50 in revenue, but costs included card production, customer service, fraud, and transaction fees -- all tracked per customer [2].

This granularity matters: Monzo discovered that customers from one particular money-saving blog were deeply unprofitable (they loaded cards, went on holiday, and withdrew cash from ATMs). They shut down that advertising channel despite its low acquisition cost because lifetime value was negative [2].

"Unit economics should be tracked to an active, monetized, retaining user, not simply a sign-up" because 80-90% may drop off in the first week [2].

### The Magic Moment

When retention periods are long (Airbnb users may only book every six months), look for a "magic moment" -- a user behavior that predicts long-term retention [2].

Facebook discovered that adding seven friends in the first 10 days predicted long-term usage. Monzo found that adding three friends from one's phone book to send/receive money improved retention by 20 percentage points [2].

Once identified, re-engineer the product onboarding to push as many users as possible toward this magic moment [2]. Do not get overly fixated on the precise definition -- "it probably didn't matter that much whether it was seven friends or eight friends" [2].

### Net Promoter Score (NPS)

NPS measures how likely users are to recommend the product to a friend, on a 0-10 scale. Promoters (9-10) minus detractors (0-6) equals the score [2].

Benchmarks for new consumer companies: +50 is a minimum baseline. Monzo hovered between +75 and +80. Tesla is at +96. Old incumbents (cell phone companies, legacy banks) often score zero or negative -- a signal of disruption opportunity [2].

Crucial: keep the measurement method consistent. Blomfield's dating startup Grouper changed the collection method and NPS dropped 20 points overnight -- an artifact, not a real change [2].

Follow up the NPS question with "Why?" Then fix every issue detractors raise [2].

## References

1. [Key Startup Metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) -- [[Tom Blomfield]] (2023)
2. [Consumer Startup Metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KT-consumer-startup-metrics) -- [[Tom Blomfield]] (2023)

title: Seed Fundraising
body:
# Seed Fundraising

Seed fundraising is the process by which startup founders raise initial outside capital to fund operations, growth, and product development. Most startups require external capital because the cost of reaching profitability exceeds what founders and their personal networks can finance [1]. Geoff Ralston, drawing on his experience at YC, describes fundraising as "brutal" but a path "almost all companies and founders must walk" [1].

## When to Raise

Investors write checks when three conditions align: the idea is compelling, the founding team appears capable of executing, and the opportunity is real and sufficiently large [1]. For most founders, this requires having a product with measurable traction. Ralston specifies that a growth rate of 10% per week for several weeks is "impressive" and sufficient to attract investor interest [1].

## How Much to Raise

The ideal amount is enough to reach profitability, eliminating the need for future rounds entirely [1]. When that is not realistic, the target should be enough to reach the next "fundable" milestone, typically 12 to 18 months away [1].

Ralston offers a concrete formula: an engineer costs approximately $15,000 per month all-in. For 18 months of operations with five engineers, the calculation is 15k x 5 x 18 = $1.35 million [1]. Most seed rounds cluster around $600,000, though rounds have been increasing in size [1].

On dilution, founders should aim to give up no more than 10% in a seed round, though most rounds require up to 20% dilution. Exceeding 25% should be avoided [1].

## Why Silicon Valley Is Different

[[Michael Seibel]] explains why fundraising in Silicon Valley differs from other locations [3]. The key factor: concentration of investors who have each missed at least one big deal. Every Silicon Valley investor has a story of passing on a company that became a unicorn. This creates a fundamentally different psychology -- investors in the Valley are more willing to take risks because the cost of missing out is visceral and personal [3].

Outside Silicon Valley, investors are spread thin and have fewer reference points for massive outcomes. Their feedback tends to be more risk-averse, and founders who overcorrect based on this feedback may damage their prospects [3].

## How to Cold Email Investors

Seibel provides a specific framework for cold investor emails [4]:

**Do's:**
- **Make it short.** "If you can make your email something that I can read in 60 seconds or less, you can pretty much guarantee that I'll read it" [4].
- Include in that short email: the problem you are solving, your solution, whether you have launched and any growth, market size, co-founders, ability to write code, and any controversial insight you have about the opportunity [4].
- Use simple language. "There's a difference between a customer pitch and an investor pitch. When talking to a customer, they know the jargon. When talking to an investor, you have no idea whether they know" [4].

**Don'ts:**
- Don't send a wall of text
- Don't lead with a long narrative about how you came up with the idea
- Don't use industry jargon [4]

## Fundraising as a Woman Founder

Amber Atherton (Zyper, YC W18) and Iba Masood (TARA, YC W15) describe their contrasting fundraising paths [5]. Atherton raised a $1.2M seed in London before YC, then raised a $4M round pre-Demo Day from Forerunner. Her Series A process took about 60 days. Masood's seed process was painful -- the first $100K was very difficult -- but her pivot from a careers platform to an AI-powered product management tool attracted $2.8M from investors who "doubled down" [5].

Key lessons from both founders [5]:
- **Write an investment memo.** Atherton wrote a memo doing "the VCs' jobs for them" -- explaining the point of the company, problem, market size, and why perceived risks were not real risks. This went out with the deck after first meetings.
- **Focus on the partner, not the fund.** Masood emphasizes researching specific partners who have led rounds in companies you admire.
- **End conversations early when fit is missing.** Atherton wishes she had been more assertive ending conversations with investors who did not understand her space, rather than being seduced by brand-name fund names.

Masood's Series A closed in nine days from first coffee to term sheet, while the legal process took 60 additional days [5].

## Building the Seed Pitch Deck

[[Aaron Harris]] provides YC's canonical seed deck template (see [[Seed Fundraising]] existing content for full template). The core insight: "Focus on narrative. The rest is commentary" [2].

## Financing Instruments

### SAFEs (Simple Agreement for Future Equity)
The SAFE has almost completely replaced convertible debt at YC [1]. No interest rate, no maturity date, no repayment requirement. Negotiable terms: investment amount, valuation cap, and discount [1].

### Convertible Notes
A loan that converts to equity at a future priced round. Carries principal, interest rate (usually minimum 2%), and maturity date [1].

### Equity Rounds
More complicated, expensive, and time-consuming than SAFEs or notes, which is why they are the exception for seed rounds [1].

## Meeting and Closing Investors

The goal of an initial meeting is not to close but to get the next meeting [1]. Once one investor commits, each subsequent close gets faster and easier. Ralston advises a "greedy" approach: "breadth-first search weighted by expected value" [1].

## Key Rules

- Get fundraising over as soon as possible and return to building
- Once someone says yes, get documents signed and money in the bank immediately
- "Never screw anyone over. The Valley is a very small place, and a bad reputation is difficult to repair" [1]

## References

1. [A Guide to Seed Fundraising](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising) -- Geoff Ralston (n.d.)
2. [How to Build Your Seed Round Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2u-how-to-build-your-seed-round-pitch-deck) -- [[Aaron Harris]] (March 2018)
3. [Why Fundraising Is Different in Silicon Valley](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/63-why-fundraising-is-different-in-silicon-valley) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
4. [How to Cold Email Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/65-how-to-cold-email-investors) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
5. [On Raising Seed and Series A as a Woman Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5G-on-raising-seed-and-series-a-as-a-woman-founder) -- Amber Atherton, Iba Masood (2019)
6. [Understanding SAFEs and Priced Equity Rounds](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6m-understanding-safes-and-priced-equity-rounds) -- (2019)

title: Enterprise Sales
body:
# Enterprise Sales

Enterprise sales is a learnable skill. Two complementary perspectives in the YC canon address it: Ryan Junee's essay frames enterprise selling as "hacking the enterprise" -- understanding and influencing the people-system that makes up a large organization [1]. [[Pete Koomen]], co-founder of Optimizely (which reached $100M ARR), provides a step-by-step walkthrough of the enterprise sales funnel for technical founders [2]. The consensus across both: founders must sell the product themselves before hiring salespeople, and sales is fundamentally about understanding customer problems, not psychological tricks.

## Founders Must Sell First

Koomen states this directly: "If you're the founder of an early-stage startup and you're building a product that you're hoping other businesses will buy, you are capable of selling it. The bad news is that you're probably the only person capable of selling your product" [2]. The reasoning: sales before [[Product-Market Fit]] is fundamentally entrepreneurial. It requires vision, credibility with customers, experimentation, and a tight feedback loop with the product team. This is a founder's role [2].

Technical founders have two advantages that give them a leg up in selling: they are experts in both the problem and the product, and they have genuine conviction that the product solves the customer's problem. "Expertise and conviction are surprisingly important in sales. This is especially surprising to people who mistakenly think that selling is a dark art full of psychological tricks" [2].

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] reinforce this from the co-founder angle: if no one on the founding team considers it their job to do sales, no sales will happen. A technical person is fully capable of sales -- it requires "general intelligence," not specialized business training [3].

## The Sales Funnel

Koomen walks through six stages: prospecting, outreach, qualification, pricing, closing, and implementation [2].

### Prospecting

Prospecting means finding potential customers. The output is a list of companies likely to need your product and the specific humans who might buy it [2].

Start with a sales hypothesis: "Customer X has problem Y, and our product will help them solve it." At Optimizely, the hypothesis was: "Marketers at small and medium tech, media, and e-commerce companies want to run A/B tests on their websites but can't because off-the-shelf experimentation tools require users to write code" [2].

Use tools to identify target companies. Optimizely used Builtwith to check whether prospects used analytics tools and JavaScript frameworks -- signals of web sophistication [2]. For contact information, Apollo and LinkedIn Sales Navigator are common in recent YC batches [2].

### Outreach

The goal of outreach is to schedule a meeting. The easiest path is to get prospects to reach out to you: launch early and often, create technical content prospects find while searching for solutions, build self-served demos, and establish expertise in online forums where customers congregate [2].

For cold emails, Koomen advises writing each email by hand, keeping them short and specific, and making the ask clear. "Only send emails that you yourself would be excited to read" [2].

A critical anti-pattern Koomen flags: talking to anyone who will take your call. This selects for people who are easiest to talk to, not people who will be great customers [2]. Founders make this mistake in two specific ways:

1. **Selling enterprise software to startups.** Other startups are easy to talk to but often do not have the problem your product solves at their scale [2].
2. **Going bottom-up with a top-down product.** If deploying your product requires coordination across multiple teams (e.g., hospital software), talking to individual contributors is futile. You need to talk to a senior leader with decision-making authority [2].

"Talking to bad customers gives you the illusion that you're making progress when you're not" [2].

### Qualification

On the first call, the goal is not to sell but to qualify the prospect and schedule a demo [2]. The biggest mistake founders make is diving straight into a pitch instead of asking questions [2].

Questions to ask: What made you decide to take this call? How long have you had this problem? How bad is it? How do you quantify the impact? Why haven't you solved it already? What's your budget? How does your organization buy software? Who makes the buying decision? [2]

If the prospect lacks the problem, budget, or authority, that discovery saves time. "You just saved yourself and your prospect a lot of time" [2].

### Demos

Most founders think of a demo as a chance to show off the product. Koomen frames it differently: "Your job in a demo is not to show off your product. It's to convince your audience that you can help them solve their problem" [2].

Structure a demo like a movie script: start by recapping who the main character (user) is and the problem they face. This demonstrates how well you listened. Then tell a story showing exactly how the user solves their problem -- not a feature tour [2].

Great demos have "magic moments" where you surprise the audience with how easy or delightful something is. They are personalized: use the customer's logo, website, customer names, and team member names [2].

At Optimizely, competitors demoed on generic websites. Koomen and his co-founder Dan spent weeks building a feature that let them demo right on the customer's own website. "I knew it was worth it when I saw a marketer's eyes light up when they watched us change things on their landing page that would have taken them months to do on their own" [2].

### Closing

Closing is not a single conversation but a series of steps from decision to signature [2]. Big companies have formal procurement processes: security reviews, legal reviews, compliance sign-off. The biggest mistake is getting surprised that a seemingly done deal requires weeks more work [2].

Ask upfront how the company buys software and who needs to sign off. Execute steps in parallel where possible. Keep legal documents simple -- Koomen recommends open-source templates from YC company Common Paper [2].

The champion is your biggest ally at this stage. "They can't solve their problem until you get through procurement, so they're heavily incentivized to help you make it happen" [2].

### Implementation

"The single biggest mistake that founders make is thinking that implementation is the customer's job" [2]. At Optimizely, they closed six-figure deals where customers hadn't run a single A/B test a year later because marketing couldn't convince engineering to install the code [2].

The lesson: customers buy a solution to a problem, not a product. All work required to get from product to solution is the seller's responsibility. Treat implementation like a high-priority internal project: shared roadmap, task owners, regular check-ins [2].

"Your sales funnel only really ends when your customer is using your product habitually" [2].

## The People in the Enterprise System

Junee maps out the typical roles a founder encounters inside a target enterprise [1]:

### Champion

An ally with deep familiarity with the pain the product solves. "When you give your pitch, you will find the champion nodding along and completing your sentences for you" [1]. Their influence depends on seniority -- director-level and above are necessary. Champions sometimes want to quit and join the startup. "These people are gold. Find them early and build strong relationships quickly" [1].

### Detractor

Opposes deployment because it threatens their job, they are invested in a competing product, or they built the current solution. Strategy: stay off their radar and rally enough support from champions to overpower them [1].

### IT

Two types: risk-avoidant (far more common) and forward-thinking. Avoid IT until required for security reviews. By then, business units should be "pulling" so strongly that IT cannot slow things down [1]. Do not build features IT requests without validating with actual users [1].

### Procurement

After a decision maker agrees to buy, procurement negotiates the deal. "Make sure you are prepared to 'give' them something -- once they extract their pound of flesh they will get out of the way" [1]. This dynamic is why enterprise software has high list prices with 80%+ discounting as standard [1].

### Legal

Legal's weapon is "exhaustion -- sending back endless redlines over clauses that in reality won't have much impact" [1]. Decide upfront what matters and concede the rest. Combined with champion pressure, this closes negotiation [1].

### Finance

Finance thinks about budget cycles. They may prefer upfront payment to use a quarter's budget. Understanding capex vs. opex preferences affects pricing model [1].

## Hiring Salespeople

Junee advises optimizing for skills and raw characteristics over experience in early-stage sales hires. A "coin-operated" salesperson from a large company -- someone accustomed to existing playbooks -- is a "script kiddie" rather than a hacker. You need someone comfortable with uncertainty who can learn quickly [1].

Koomen adds that sales hiring only makes sense once the founder has closed deals and can articulate a repeatable process. A salesperson should generate 5x their total compensation in new ARR [4].

## References

1. [Enterprise Sales for Hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers) -- Ryan Junee (n.d.)
2. [Enterprise Sales for Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LF-enterprise-sales-for-founders) -- [[Pete Koomen]] (2024)
3. [Do Technical Founders Need a Business Co-Founder?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KV-do-technical-founders-need-a-business-co-founder) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
4. [How To Price For B2B](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LI-how-to-price-for-b2b) -- [[Tom Blomfield]] (2024)

title: Becoming a Founder
body:
# Becoming a Founder

The question of whether, when, and how to become a startup founder is addressed by multiple YC partners. [[Michael Seibel]] provides the most comprehensive frameworks, covering career path analysis, college preparation, and the order of operations for actually starting. [[Dalton Caldwell]] adds the psychological dimension -- how to think about information consumption and self-conviction. Justin Kan offers the case for (and against) joining a startup as a stepping stone.

## Should I Start a Startup?

[[Michael Seibel]] frames the decision simply: "there is a certain type of person who only works at their peak capacity when there is no predictable path to follow, the odds of success are low, and they have to take personal responsibility for failure (the opposite of most jobs at a large company)" [3].

He acknowledges the counterarguments honestly [3]:
1. The vast majority of startups are not successful
2. For talented technical people, it is relatively easy to get a high-salary job
3. Large companies offer opportunities to work on difficult problems that only occur at scale

But for people who have the founder personality type, a predictable corporate path will never draw out their best work. The startup path is not for everyone, and Seibel is explicit that it requires self-knowledge [3].

## Decisions That Block Future Founders

Seibel frequently talks to people in their late 20s and 30s who want to start startups but cannot. A decision they made when they were younger is either blocking or creating a serious barrier. The most common blockers [1]:

- They did not learn how to write code
- They never met anyone in college who knew how to code
- They significantly increased their expenses early in life, did not save, and now cannot afford to begin a company
- They created roots post-college in a city that is not startup-friendly

## Advice for College Students

Seibel frames this as what those blocked founders would tell their younger selves [1]:

**Learn to code.** "You don't have to be the best developer, and you don't have to write code your entire life if you don't want to -- but knowing how to code gives you the proverbial golden ticket." The number one barrier to starting a tech startup is not knowing how to code [1].

**Meet people with real skills.** College puts you next to designers, programmers, salespeople, and scientists. These people could become cofounders or early employees [1].

**Be ready to hit the ground running.** Seibel disagrees with using your 20s to find yourself. "Your 20s are your most valuable working years: You often have very few obligations (no family), a very low burn rate (you're used to living in a dorm), and you are still young enough to meet new people and learn new skills" [1]. The prime startup years are ages 22 to 32. "Every year you are not doing a startup you are losing 10% of this prime time" [1].

**Be careful with student debt.** Student debt forces people away from startups and into big companies [1].

## Consuming the Right Information

[[Dalton Caldwell]] addresses a subtle trap for aspiring founders: consuming too much startup media about fundraising creates a distorted view of what matters [4]. "Sometimes I meet founders where they don't even have a startup idea yet -- but because they consume all this information about fundraising 24/7, they're completely focused on fundraising without even knowing what their startup idea is" [4].

Caldwell's framework: "your thought process as a founder is heavily influenced by the five most common information sources you get." He recommends being deliberate about consuming media that inspires building things, not media focused on fundraising, valuations, or startup celebrity. "If it's inspiring you to build more things, if it's inspiring you to work harder, if it's inspiring you to be rowing in the right direction -- keep it. If it's making you kind of depressed and sad, stop consuming it" [4].

He also warns against overvaluing thought leadership: "The vast, vast, vast, vast, vast majority of successful founders that we funded at YC have effectively no presence whatsoever, especially not in the early stages" [4].

## Convincing Others to Join

[[Dalton Caldwell]] reframes the question "How do I convince someone to join my startup?" as a self-conviction problem [5]. "If you're not fully committed or if you have grave doubts that your idea is any good or it's even worth trying, how can you ever convince someone else? And people can tell if you don't believe in the thing you're doing" [5].

The test: "Do you ever know that feeling where an idea enters your head and you can't sleep at night and you can't think about anything else? That's a good sign" [5]. If you are immediately cynical about your idea, you are not convinced.

Caldwell makes a key observation about idea quality: "It would appear that it's equally hard to succeed in a startup that you don't care about and one that you do. And it's almost equally hard to succeed in a startup that's a very ambitious, audacious idea as it is to do something super incremental. If those are equally difficult, do you see why you should definitely do the way more audacious idea?" [5].

## Why You Should (and Should Not) Work at a Startup

Justin Kan, co-founder of Twitch and three-time YC founder, gives a characteristically unfiltered assessment [6]:

**Reasons not to join a startup:**
1. **Management generally sucks.** "There are two kinds of YC companies. There are the rocket ships with bad management, and then there are the other companies with bad management" [6].
2. **You probably will not get rich.** "If you think you're gonna join a startup and then be set for life, that is unlikely to happen" [6].
3. **If you want stability, go to a big company.** "We don't have five years of money, so if you want stability, I think you should go join Facebook" [6].

**Reasons to join a startup:**
1. **Access to jobs you are unqualified for.** Kan's example: Guillaume, a programmer recruited from the first Work at a Startup event, was running Justin.tv's entire Rails backend within a year -- a top 10 Rails site. "That was a job he was completely unqualified for and he would never have gotten the opportunity to do if he didn't join a startup" [6]. Guillaume went on to co-found Socialcam and then Triplebyte.
2. **Gateway to starting your own startup.** "Put yourself in positions where you're around people who want to do the things that you want to do" [6].
3. **Maximize speed of learning.** Kan uses Kyle Vogt (co-founder of Cruise) as example: recruited from MIT to Justin.tv, went from knowing nothing about scaling systems to architecting a live video system that was the fourth-largest bandwidth consumer in North America by the time Twitch sold to Amazon [6].

## The Three Paths in Tech

Seibel outlines three primary career paths in the tech industry: founder, executive, and employee [2].

### Founder
**Pros**: Work on something you are passionate about; high responsibility inspires extreme productivity; choose who you work with; learn at an extremely fast pace [2].
**Cons**: Incredibly stressful; probably will not maximize personal earnings; large-scale success often requires a decade-plus commitment; can significantly hurt personal relationships [2].

### Executive
**Pros**: Stable income; high prestige; higher likelihood of huge impact (given most startups fail) [2].
**Cons**: Internal politics matter as much as results; success can be blocked by others; takes a long time to get significant responsibility [2].

### Employee
**Pros**: More work and fewer meetings; flexibility; more time for friends and family [2].
**Cons**: No control over what you work on; no voice in major decisions; can be boring [2].

**Experience is overvalued.** "In almost all cases, no matter what knowledge you bring to the table, you will learn most of what you need to know about your problem, your customer, and the best solution after you start your company" [2].

## References

1. [A Letter to College Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (March 2017)
2. [Three Paths in the Tech Industry: Founder, Executive, or Employee](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (September 2017)
3. [Should I Start a Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/60-should-i-start-a-startup) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
4. [Consume Information That Encourages You to Do More](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5a-consume-information-that-encourages-you-to-do-more) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (2019)
5. [How Do You Convince Someone to Join Your Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5b-how-do-you-convince-someone-to-join-your-startup) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (2019)
6. [Why You Should or Should Not Work at a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5n-why-you-should-or-should-not-work-at-a-startup) -- Justin Kan (2018)

title: Network Effects
body:
# Network Effects

Network effects occur when a product becomes more valuable as more people use it. The concept is central to understanding why certain startups achieve winner-take-most dynamics. [[Anu Hariharan]]'s case studies of Chinese technology companies provide some of the most detailed analyses of network effects in the YC canon.

## Types of Network Effects

Hariharan draws a distinction between Metcalfe's law and Reed's law in her analysis of [[Pinduoduo]]:

**Metcalfe's law** holds that the value of a network grows proportionally to the square of the number of users. This applies to one-to-one connection networks like telephones or messaging platforms [1].

**Reed's law** holds that the value of a group-forming network grows exponentially with the ease of group formation. Hariharan identifies Pinduoduo as having "one of the most powerful network effects in the world" because it taps into groups of social relationships rather than one-to-one connections [1]. The team purchase mechanic means every user has an incentive to recruit others, and the recruiting happens through existing social groups.

## Network Effects in Practice

### WeChat

[[WeChat]]'s network effects compounded across layers. The messaging platform created a base network effect, then the Moments social feed added a content layer, then WeChat Pay added a financial layer, then Mini Programs added an application layer. Each layer reinforced the others, making switching costs enormous [2]. WeChat grew from 0 to 889 million MAUs by stacking network effects on top of each other.

### Toutiao

[[Toutiao]] demonstrates data network effects: more users generate more engagement data, which improves the recommendation algorithm, which makes the content more addictive, which attracts more users [3]. Algorithms without data are useless, and data without an addictive product is unavailable. The virtuous cycle compounds product quality over time.

### Pinduoduo

Pinduoduo's team purchase model creates network effects at the group level. Users recruit friends and family into purchasing groups to unlock lower prices, which brings new users to the platform, who then form their own groups [1]. The social graph becomes the distribution channel.

## Network Effects and Growth

[[Paul Graham]] notes that raising money lets founders "choose their growth rate," and the most successful startups take VC money even when profitable because it lets them grow faster and fend off competitors, "especially in businesses with network effects" [4]. Network effects create a strategic imperative for fast growth: the first company to reach scale often locks in its advantage.

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne (2020)
2. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) -- [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) -- [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)
4. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- [[Paul Graham]] (September 2012)

title: Startup Outcomes and Optionality
body:
# Startup Outcomes and Optionality

[[Sam Altman]] addresses a common but rarely discussed situation: the startup that is successful but is not on a trajectory to be a billion-dollar company. Using a metaphor attributed to David Weiden, he describes starting a startup as being pushed off a cliff with a bag of aerospace parts [1].

## The Airplane vs. Spaceship Framework

Sometimes the parts make a spaceship -- huge market, great product, natural monopoly, credible path to being the winner. Plenty of advice exists for that case. Sometimes the parts make nothing at all. But "a reasonable amount of the time, it turns out you have the parts for an airplane" [1].

An airplane is a company with profitability within reach, on track to be worth $100 million with several more years of hard work. "This is nothing to be ashamed of -- quite the opposite, obviously. This is a triumph by almost any metric." It is what happens to "most very lucky, very smart, very determined people who decide to start a startup and have everything go their way except happening upon a perfect market" [1].

The founder has two choices: try to get a new bag of parts (new market, new product, very different strategy) and keep trying to build a spaceship, or decide to have a really good airplane [1].

## Founder-Investor Misalignment

The most important thing to understand: this is where founders and investors often get misaligned. Investors are looking for spaceships; airplanes do not matter much to them, though they matter quite a bit to founders [1].

Existing investors will usually be willing to take almost any amount of risk to turn an airplane into a spaceship. The typical advice offered is little more than "point your plane at the moon, burn more fuel, and let's see what happens" [1].

Altman's recommendation: "Be frank and realistic with your existing investors; you will eventually persuade them that a small multiple is better than a loss." The best investors understand how venture investing works and do not fight over such outcomes -- many treat founders extra-generously hoping to fund their next company. The longer founders delude their investors, the worse shape they end up in [1].

## The Overvaluation Trap

Raising too much money at too high a valuation severely limits optionality. "Very often I've seen cases where founders know in their hearts they have an airplane but are able to convince good investors it might still be a spaceship. This really causes a lot of heartache, and often precludes your opportunity for a good acquisition later" [1].

The answer is simple in theory but difficult in practice: do not raise a lot of money at a high price when you think the company will probably not increase 10x in value and you would be happy selling soon. This may require changing how the company is run and finding a path to profitability with current capital. "It's far less painful to deal with this now rather than putting the company in a position that will preclude the option you think is best" [1].

## Positioning for Acquisition

Companies get bought, not sold. It is very difficult to force an acquisition, and nearly impossible if the company has not built something legitimately valuable. "Somehow, this fact surprises founders who decide 'we'd like to get acquired' almost every time" [1].

What founders can do: begin developing relationships with potential acquirers early. It usually takes a long time, and personal relationships help [1].

The outcome is still worth pride. "Silicon Valley is filled with people who had one or several mid-success companies before eventually hitting it out of the park" [1].

## References

1. [Navigating Success for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4s-navigating-success-for-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)

title: Pivoting
body:
# Pivoting

A huge number of the best startups had to pivot. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] address when to pivot, how to pivot, and the psychological traps that make pivoting either too hard or too easy [1]. YC group partners share pivot stories across multiple billion-dollar companies, illustrating that working on the wrong thing before finding the right thing is not a failure mode but the standard path [2].

## Defining a Pivot

Aaron Epstein offers a useful analogy from basketball: when you pivot, you keep one foot stationary [2]. You may keep the type of product but change the audience, or build a different product for the same audience. A good pivot builds on the learnings from prior ideas [2].

## When to Pivot

The decision to pivot is one of the hardest a founder faces because there is no clean signal. Indicators that a pivot may be warranted [1]:

- The product has been launched and iterated multiple times but growth remains flat
- Users do not retain -- they sign up but do not come back
- Every conversation with users reveals that the problem is not urgent enough
- The founding team has lost genuine excitement about the problem

The critical distinction: a pivot is warranted when the market is telling you the current approach will not work, not when the founders are simply bored or discouraged by normal startup difficulty [1].

Tom Blomfield's signal was quantitative: four founders cold-calling for two weeks and getting one user [2]. The Brex founders needed three to four weeks of talking to hardware experts [2]. The GOAT founders needed two years of poor retention [2].

[[Brad Flora]] offers a KPI-based test: "If you don't immediately know what the thing is that you're chasing when you wake up every morning, then you probably need to pivot" [2].

## How to Pivot

Caldwell and Seibel recommend a structured approach [1]:

1. **Go back to your users.** Talk to the people who did engage and ask what they actually need. Often the pivot idea comes from users themselves.
2. **Look for what's working.** Even in a failing product, there may be one feature or use case generating disproportionate engagement.
3. **Move fast.** Once the decision is made, execute quickly.
4. **Keep the team.** A pivot should not mean losing co-founders unless the relationship is already broken.

## Billion-Dollar Pivot Stories

### Brex: VR Headsets to Corporate Cards

[[Harj Taggar]] describes Brex's application to YC: the idea was a virtual reality headset. The founders had no experience with hardware. What they did have was experience building a payments processing business in Brazil as high schoolers [2]. Three weeks into the YC batch, they went back to payments and built Brex, one of YC's largest companies [2].

Taggar notes a pattern: smart people feel that worthy things need to be really hard, so they ignore what seems easy to them [2].

### GOAT: Group Dinners to Sneaker Marketplace

[[Dalton Caldwell]] describes GOAT, funded as "Grub with Us" -- group dinners. Awful retention. Two years later, the founders realized they were really into sneaker collecting. In 2015, pivoting into sneaker collecting was "so far outside the norm of conventional wisdom" [2]. Sometimes founders need their morale to be so low that they think "I'm going to fail, so I might as well do something cool" -- and that lets them take the blinders off [2].

### GoCardless: Bill-Splitting to Recurring Business Payments

[[Tom Blomfield]]: a "classic fintech tar pit" for group payments. They hassled friends into using it but nobody retained. Paul Graham told them to stop building features and try to get users. They took the core payments infrastructure and applied it to businesses. Twelve years later, GoCardless is a multi-billion dollar company [2].

Blomfield reflects: "When you're young, you think of the entire world as consumer, and the older you get, the more you're like, 'Oh, I guess businesses buy software'" [2].

### Clipboard Health: Indeed for Nurses to Staffing Platform

[[Gustaf Alströmer]]: founder Wei Deng discovered that when a nurse called in sick, hospitals had to go to an agency. This transformed Clipboard from a generic job board to one of the biggest software-enabled skilled nursing staffing platforms. Deng adopted "doing things that don't scale" better than almost anyone [2].

### Twitch: General Live-Streaming to Game Streaming

Started as Justin.tv, a general live-streaming platform. Only when Emmett Shear refocused on video game streaming did it find PMF and sell to Amazon for nearly $1B [1].

## Pivot Hell

A company can pivot too much. YC calls this "pivot hell" [2]. [[Jared Friedman]] describes it as when every week the founders are working on a totally new idea [2].

The cause: perfectionism. No startup idea is ever perfect, and searching for the perfect one means you keep switching as soon as you see problems [2].

### Escaping Pivot Hell

Friedman describes a company that went through pivot hell all batch, deferred Demo Day, and kept doing office hours on new ideas. About six months post-batch, they discovered a regulatory change that created a new opportunity. The key: to arrive at this idea, they had to go through all the bad ideas first [2].

## The Founders Are the Constant

The group partners emphasize that the most important factor is the founders, not the idea [2]. YC has seen the same founders knock a company "down to the studs," rebuild on a totally different idea, and succeed. Often the founders even act like different people when working on an idea they are excited about [2].

## References

1. [All About Pivoting](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6p-all-about-pivoting) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)
2. [YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Tom Blomfield]], [[Gustaf Alströmer]], [[Jared Friedman]], Aaron Epstein, [[Brad Flora]], Diana Hu (n.d.)

title: Founder-CEO Transition
body:
# Founder-CEO Transition

The skills that make someone a great founder -- scrappiness, doing everything yourself, moving fast with incomplete information -- can become liabilities as the company grows. Several YC talks address the specific things founders must unlearn to become effective CEOs, and the pivot experiences that forge great leadership [1] [2].

## What Founders Must Unlearn

The transition from founder to CEO requires unlearning deeply ingrained habits [1]:

**Doing everything yourself.** Early-stage founders are generalists who handle product, sales, support, and hiring. This becomes a bottleneck past 25 people. The CEO's job shifts from doing to enabling -- setting context, making decisions on escalated issues, and ensuring the team has what it needs to execute [1].

**Making every decision.** Founders who insist on deciding everything create organizational learned helplessness. The team stops taking initiative because they know the founder will override them anyway. Great CEOs establish decision-making frameworks and push authority as far down the organization as possible [1].

**Prioritizing speed over process.** Early-stage speed comes from skipping process. At scale, the lack of process creates chaos. The art is introducing just enough structure -- not the heavy bureaucracy of a large company, but lightweight systems that let the organization coordinate without the CEO being the routing hub [1].

**Conflating company identity with personal identity.** Founders who cannot separate their self-worth from the company's metrics make poor decisions under pressure. The most damaging pattern: refusing to pivot because admitting the current direction is wrong feels like personal failure [1].

## From Pivot Hell to Unicorn

The journey from repeated failed pivots to a $1.4 billion valuation illustrates how the founder-CEO transition often happens through forced learning [2]. The pattern: each pivot teaches the founder something about what they were doing wrong as a leader, not just what was wrong with the product. The companies that eventually succeed often do so because the founder internalized lessons from failures that had nothing to do with market or product and everything to do with how they were leading [2].

## References

1. [What Founders Have to Unlearn to Become Great CEOs](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
2. [From Pivot Hell to $1.4 Billion Unicorn](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: AI-Native Software
body:
# AI-Native Software

Most AI applications today are "horseless carriages" -- they pack powerful technology into outdated interfaces designed for humans to do work in, rather than reimagining the product from scratch to offload work entirely [1]. [[Pete Koomen]], YC partner and founder of Optimizely, argues that the fundamental error is asking "How can we slot AI into our existing application?" instead of "How would we design this tool from scratch to offload as much repetitive work from the user as possible?" [1]. [[Andrej Karpathy]] frames this as a broader shift: we have entered the era of "Software 3.0," where natural language becomes the programming interface and models do the rest [2].

## The Horseless Carriage Problem

Koomen draws on the analogy of early automobiles, which looked like horse carriages with the horse replaced by an engine [1]. The carriage design created problems at higher speeds: less suspension, higher center of gravity, poor handling. The motor was only a small part of what was needed -- the entire vehicle had to be redesigned. The same pattern repeats across technology transitions: the first search engines were digitized yellow pages; the first mobile apps were websites wrapped in native shells that ignored GPS and multi-touch [1].

The Gmail email-writing agent illustrates the problem. Gemini, the underlying model, is "amazing" -- "absolutely incredible" [1]. But the UI makes it frustrating to use. When asked to draft a sick-day email to a boss, it produces text like "Dear Gary, I'm writing to inform you that my daughter woke up with the flu this morning. As a result, I won't be able to come into the office today. Thank you for understanding. Best regards, Pete" [1]. Two problems: the output does not sound like the user, and the prompt is roughly as long as the draft itself -- meaning the AI creates more work than doing the task manually [1].

The root cause: the system prompt is hidden from the user, generic, and corporate-safe. It tells the model to "use a formal businessy tone and correct punctuation so that it's obvious the user is really smart and serious" [1]. Every user gets the same one-size-fits-all behavior.

## Exposing the System Prompt

Koomen's central thesis: users should be able to see and edit the system prompt [1]. If Gmail allowed Pete to replace the generic prompt with "You're Pete. You're a 43-year-old husband, father, YC partner. You're busy, and so is everyone you correspond with, so you keep emails as short as possible," the output changes dramatically: "Hi Gary, my daughter's sick with the flu, so I can't come in today. Thanks" [1].

This represents a fundamental shift in the division of labor between developer and user. For decades, the developer wrote the code, hid it behind an interface, and delivered one-size-fits-all software. The system prompt, however, is just English. It does not require technical knowledge to edit. "I think basically everyone will be able to do this," Koomen argues, comparing it to how using a computer was once a nerd-only skill and is now universal [1].

The objection that most people will not want to write system prompts from scratch has a straightforward answer: AI can auto-generate the system prompt from 20 years of email history, then the user provides feedback and the system auto-updates. "In 5 years, for 99% of people, they're not touching the system prompt, but the system prompt is custom to them" [1].

The liability shift matters too. If the user controls the system prompt, bad outputs are the user's responsibility, not the developer's -- just as profane emails written in Gmail are the sender's problem, not Google's [1].

## From Chatbots to Agents with Tools

The chatbot paradigm -- a text box where you type questions and get answers -- was "the easiest way to bring the most basic LLM experience to the whole world" but is not how AI should be used in most cases [1]. The real promise is agents that can accomplish things in the world on the user's behalf.

Koomen built a demo email reading agent with a user-editable system prompt that instructs it how to label, archive, and draft replies to incoming emails based on the sender and content [1]. The agent's capabilities come from tools: a tool for labeling, a tool for archiving, a tool for drafting. With additional tools -- paying bills, making introductions, integrating with Slack, calendar, and project management -- such an agent could handle most of the repetitive inbox work that consumes hours daily [1].

A company in the current YC batch called Den is "building Cursor for knowledge work" -- chaining together MCP servers (tools for agents) so that a boss's Slack message about reviewing terms can trigger pulling from Google Docs, sending to legal via email, and publishing via GitHub, all from one place [1]. Steve Jobs described software as "a bicycle for the mind." Koomen says this feels like "a rocket ship for the mind" [1].

## Software 3.0: Natural Language as Code

[[Andrej Karpathy]] traces three eras of software [2]:

- **Software 1.0**: Traditional code written by humans in programming languages. Deterministic, explicit instructions.
- **Software 2.0**: Neural networks where the "code" is the weights learned from data. He described this [in a 2017 blog post](https://karpathy.medium.com/software-2-0-a64152b37c35).
- **Software 3.0**: Large language models where the programming interface is natural language -- prompts. The user writes what they want in English, and the model produces the behavior.

This shift means that for the first time, the technology diffusion goes in reverse: LLMs are the first technology that reaches the people first and the experts last. Non-technical users can get useful output immediately through natural language, while developers are still figuring out the best tooling and paradigms [2].

Karpathy sees the current moment as building "a new kind of computer" -- one where the CPU is an LLM, the programming language is English, and the applications are prompts plus tools. The implications for software design are fundamental: instead of building rigid UIs with predetermined flows, developers build tool ecosystems that agents navigate based on natural language instructions [2].

## Speed as the New Bottleneck

Andrew Ng observes that AI coding assistants have shifted the bottleneck in product development [3]. Writing code is faster than ever; the bottleneck has moved to figuring out what to build and evaluating whether it works. He advocates for "concreteness over vagueness" -- instead of lengthy specification documents, build a prototype in hours and iterate from there [3].

Ng emphasizes agentic workflows as a paradigm shift: instead of a single LLM call producing output, chains of agents plan, execute, critique, and revise. This changes what startups can build because multi-step workflows that previously required teams of humans can now be automated by agent pipelines [3].

## Why Coding Agents Are Ahead

The coding domain (Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code) is far ahead of other AI applications for two reasons [1]:

1. **LLMs are excellent at processing instructions into text output.** Code is text. A description in English maps naturally to code output.
2. **Developer tools are power tools.** They allow full access to the bare metal. The teams that built coding agents did not spend time preventing users from doing anything embarrassing -- they let users interact directly with the full power of the model [1].

Other domains still use "kid glove mentality" -- nerfing the model to prevent liability, which makes it useless [1]. Koomen hopes the industry moves beyond this: "I hope we're able to move beyond this moment in time where there's so much assumed liability on the part of the application developers for what these models do" [1].

## Implications for Founders

This is "one of the most exciting times to be a founder because almost every tool that we've been using for decades can be rethought from the ground up with AI" [1]. The AI-native version of most tools will look fundamentally different from what exists today. Founders should not ask "how do I insert AI into my tool" but rather "how would I design this tool from scratch to offload as much repetitive work from the user as possible so they can focus on what's important" [1].

## References

1. [AI Apps Are Broken -- Here's How To Fix Them](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WJoZK9sMwvw) -- [[Pete Koomen]], Tom Blomfield, David Lieb (2025)
2. [Andrej Karpathy: Software Is Changing (Again)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LCEmiRjPEtQ) -- [[Andrej Karpathy]] (June 2025)
3. [Andrew Ng: Building Faster with AI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RNJCfif1dPY) -- Andrew Ng (June 2025)

title: Dev Shops and Outsourced Development
aliases: should I use a dev shop
body:
# Dev Shops and Outsourced Development

[[Michael Seibel]] addresses a common question at YC: whether non-technical founders should outsource development of their initial product to a dev shop [1]. His answer is a strong caution against this path for startups seeking venture-scale outcomes.

## The Trap

The advice to use a dev shop "too easy to focus on the companies that have done this well and not hear the stories about the companies who have done this poorly" [1]. Seibel receives many emails from founders who tried this path and failed.

The trap unfolds in stages [1]:

1. **The MVP takes longer and costs more than expected.** The dev shop may be working on other projects, and the person building is not as motivated as a founder with equity.
2. **Progress feels real.** You go from nothing being built to things happening. "So you feel like you're making all this progress."
3. **The MVP launches and does not solve the problem.** This is expected -- MVPs rarely do on the first try.
4. **Iteration requires more money and more time with the dev shop.** Funds run low.
5. **The startup dies.** Unable to raise more money and unable to iterate further.

## Why Investors Care About the Team, Not the Product

The fundamental misunderstanding: non-technical founders think early investors want to see traction. In reality, "the vast majority of good early stage investors care more about whether you can build something than what you've built" [1]. At the point they invest, there is very little proof of success. What they want to see is the ability to iterate through multiple versions quickly [1].

Doing that with a dev shop takes longer and is far more expensive than doing it with a technical co-founder [1]. Additionally, "there are a lot of very good tech startup investors who do not want to invest in companies that are outsourcing development" [1].

A further problem: when a company does hire a developer, the outsourced code is often thrown away because the new developer disagrees with how it was built and wants to rebuild [1].

## The Alternative

Seibel's recommendation: invest the time in recruiting a technical co-founder. "Typically what ends up happening is you have to spend far less than you'd have to spend for that dev shop, you have a product that's released far faster, and you're much better set up to iterate on the solution" [1].

He acknowledges exceptions exist but emphasizes: "In my experience, that is by far the exception, not the rule" [1].

## References

1. [Should I Use a Dev Shop?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7v-should-i-use-a-dev-shop) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2018)

title: Founder Productivity
body:
# Founder Productivity

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] address founder productivity across two complementary perspectives: how successful founders organize their time and get work done [1], and how they maintain the physical and psychological infrastructure to sustain performance over years [2]. Both draw on patterns observed across hundreds of YC companies.

## Maker Schedule vs. Manager Schedule

The framework comes from [[Paul Graham]]'s essay "Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule" [1]. The core insight: programmers (makers) and managers organize time differently, and most companies are set up around the manager schedule -- a day packed with meetings. This is destructive for makers [1].

### The Maker's Perspective

Caldwell, a developer-founder, explains: when programming, the more of the program you can keep in your head at any one time, the easier it is to make changes and fix bugs. Loading context into "RAM" takes one to two hours. If interrupted, you must restart your state every time [1]. A great maker schedule is an eight-hour uninterrupted block. Programming in hour increments is disastrous [1].

[[Emmett Shear]] explained it to Seibel by saying: "Imagine that I'm doing ridiculously complicated word problems and you're interrupting me in the middle of them." Once Seibel heard that, it clicked -- writing email in twenty-minute increments is fine, but deep technical work cannot be sliced that way [1].

Caldwell recalls that at his startup, nothing productive happened before lunch. Email and meetings consumed the morning. The entire trick was making post-lunch time free of everything [1].

### Maker Mode Applies to Sales Too

The maker schedule concept applies to sales and customer work, not just programming [1]. Scheduling eight-hour blocks for sales, rather than squeezing it into slivers between other things, produces dramatically better results. "It's really hard to get anything at a high level done in twenty-minute blocks" [1].

## Productivity Practices

### To-Do Lists Over Notebooks

Seibel argues that a well-managed to-do list using software is essential [1]. "When founders write something down in a notebook, it's gone forever. But they look cool with their fountain pen." Any of the many software products works -- the important thing is adopting one [1].

### To-Do List Before Inbox

When in manager mode, to-do list comes first [1]. If work is inbox-driven, other people control your time. "Horrible, horrible" [1].

### Write Everything Down

The worst thing is having another meeting because no one wrote down the decisions from the first meeting [1]. Even if you think you don't need it, write down the agenda and decisions [1].

### Know Your Numbers

Seibel had his analytics dashboard up on screen at all times and could memorize key KPIs [1]. "I'm obsessed with the internal key KPIs for anything I'm working on. I'm like an addict to look at that stuff." Founders who know their stats well talk about them differently -- they don't round off numbers, and they know whether they're up or down 10% at any given time [1].

Caldwell contrasts this with founders who say "Yeah, I think it was an up week" but cannot say whether revenue went up or down. "What are you doing?" [1]

## Social Media and Information Diet

Social media is "the black hole for time" [1] [2]. Caldwell uses the metaphor of an information diet: just as what you eat affects your mood, the information you consume affects your state of mind [2]. Doomscrolling pandemic or war news, following investors who talk constantly about funding markets -- these degrade founder performance without providing useful information [2].

Successful founders explicitly block social media: grayscaling phones, turning off notifications, aggressively using screen time tracking [2]. Seibel unfollowed everyone on Twitter, installed a Chrome extension that disables most of Twitter's features, uninstalled Facebook years ago, and does not have the Twitter app on his phone [1].

"If you don't aggressively protect your time, the world is going to steal it from you" [1]. Effective people use both organizing tools and protection tools. The belief that "successful people just have stronger willpower" is false -- they reach for tools to manage their weaknesses [1].

## Things Great Founders Avoid

### Collecting Credentials Instead of Building

Accumulating mentors, advisors, accelerators, and idea labs creates a "bottomless pit of time suck" [1]. Caldwell compares it to going to Hollywood and taking acting lessons for years while never auditioning. "It feels like you're making progress, but you're not" [1].

The things that de-risk a startup: talking to customers, launching a product. "God forbid you're doing anything more than those things" [1].

### Hedging Bets

Keeping graduate school open, holding a Google job offer, exploring finance, and talking to three friends about a startup -- founders want to move all these pieces down the board simultaneously [1]. "If there's a team that's exactly as competent as you and they're not hedging, they're always going to win" [1].

## Lifestyle Infrastructure

### The Adulting Requirement

If you want to be a founder of a very successful company, you may have to "adult harder than the people around you" [2]. This includes sleep, diet, exercise, and living environment. Seibel frames the home as a potential source of strength or weakness: "At minimum, your home should be neutral. For the most successful people, the home is actually a plus" [2].

From a clinical perspective, Caldwell notes that when someone is struggling, the first things to examine are sleep disruption, diet, and exercise. "If you don't have your house in order on those fronts, you are not well-situated to deal with really hard things" [2].

### The Debt Metaphor

Founders can sacrifice healthy habits for five to seven years -- the human body allows this. But building a company is a multi-decade endeavor, and "the stuff you can get away with in your twenties, you can't get away with in your thirties" [2]. Caldwell frames it as going into "personal health debt" that eventually comes due as a breakdown or meltdown [2].

### Founder Salary

Seibel argues that small, incremental salary increases (5-10%) can make a huge lifestyle difference and are almost always worth it [2]. The philosophy: "salary is for living, equity is for upside" [2]. If startup salary allows you to save money, it is probably too high. If it creates a lifestyle that allows you to be all-in on the startup because the real world is not distracting you negatively, it is correct [2].
