### Co-Founder Conflict Resolution

Seibel identifies the anxious-vs-avoidant personality dynamic as the root cause of most co-founder problems [2]. The actual thing they are debating is rarely the issue -- it is the style of dealing with frustration. If co-founders can figure out this dance, it is "so much more helpful when dealing with external challenges" [2].

### Social Circle Selection

When taking punches, the friends you choose to spend time with matters. "The best people, stuff goes wrong and they go take a hike. The worst people, stuff goes wrong and they drink and do drugs" [2]. Therapy and medication are destigmatized tools that many successful founders use, though it rarely appears in press releases [2].

## When to Take No Action

Caldwell's most counterintuitive advice: when something bad happens, the instinct is to immediately react -- call a meeting, fire off an email. But "sleeping on it is the minimum thing I would do" because the next day brings clarity [2].

The best founders use "no action" as a key play 20-40% of the time. Other founders do not even know the play exists [2]. The exception: when someone's health is on the line or legal consequences are imminent [2].

## References

1. [How Future Billionaires Get Sh*t Done](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ik-dalton-michael-how-future-billionaires-get-sh-t-done) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)
2. [Most Important Lifestyle Habits of Successful Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ii-dalton-michael-most-important-lifestyle-habits-of-successful-founders) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)

title: Runway Management
body:
# Runway Management

Running low on runway is one of the most dangerous situations a startup can face, not only because of the financial reality but because of its psychological effects on founders. Dalton Caldwell addresses this directly: "The hardest part of dealing with a low runway situation is managing your own psychology. You have to simultaneously manage your own anxiety to not be overly negative about your prospects, but also not be irrationally positive" [1].

This article covers how to assess runway, the psychology of low-runway situations, counterproductive thinking patterns, practical cost reduction, and the decision to shut down.

## Prerequisites: Know Where You Stand

Before anything else, founders must understand exactly how much cash and runway they have. Caldwell directs founders to two essential readings by [[Paul Graham]]: [The Fatal Pinch](http://paulgraham.com/pinch.html) and [Default Alive or Default Dead](http://paulgraham.com/aord.html) [1].

The core framework: if you are Default Dead (your current trajectory leads to running out of money before reaching profitability), it is your responsibility as a founder to immediately take actions to become Default Alive [1]. The mechanisms are straightforward: "Either you need to grow revenue more quickly, cut costs, or both" [1].

## Counterproductive Ideas

Caldwell identifies seven common thought patterns that cause founders to rationalize inaction when they are Default Dead [1]:

1. **"The Fatal Pinch does not apply to me"** - Believing your company is a special exception
2. **"Investors will continue to fund my company if I run out of money"** - Assuming future fundraising is guaranteed
3. **"If I fail at fundraising I can just sell the company"** - Treating acquisition as a reliable fallback
4. **"My conversations with potential acquirers or investors are very far along and likely to happen"** - Confusing activity with commitment
5. **"Acquirers won't buy us if I cut costs"** - Believing a smaller team reduces acquisition attractiveness
6. **"My employee morale will plummet if I cut costs"** - Prioritizing comfort over survival
7. **"New investors won't fund us if I cut costs"** - Believing cost-cutting signals weakness to investors

Caldwell warns: "Don't let these ideas be the justification(s) for why you choose to remain Default Dead" [1].

## Understanding Negotiating Leverage

Caldwell presents a graph showing how leverage deteriorates over time for a company with declining runway [1]:

![Runway and Leverage Graph](../raw/posts/images/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway/a8feb33288f6.png)

Three lessons from this graph [1]:

1. **Delaying action is a bad strategy.** Make changes to become Default Alive now, not later.
2. **Game theory works against you.** From a game theory perspective, an investor or acquirer benefits by stalling and dragging you along until you have no leverage. "An opportunistic acquirer or investor is unlikely to say 'no' outright, and will keep their options open as you become increasingly desperate."
3. **Without corrective action, things get worse.** "If things look bleak now and you take no corrective action, it is overwhelmingly likely the situation will get worse, not better."

## Reducing Burn

The least painful option is to dramatically increase revenue immediately [1]. When that is not possible, cost cuts must follow a clear priority.

### What to Cut First

1. **PR, marketing expenses, perks, and parties** - The easiest to cut. "Don't blow your money on this stuff" [1]
2. **Payroll** - The most likely source of high burn. As noted in [[Paul Graham]]'s Fatal Pinch, "over-hiring is usually the root cause of high burn" [1]
3. **Real estate/lease costs** - Binding agreements and very difficult to exit. "Real estate obligations are a common cause of death for later stage companies" [1]

### Handling Layoffs

If staff reductions are necessary [1]:
- Treat former employees well
- Be transparent with remaining employees
- "You should always treat your staff as you would want to be treated"

## The Point of No Return

When a company has less than three months of cash, founders must face the possibility of shutdown head-on [1]. Caldwell identifies less than two months as "the point of no return" in many cases [1].

At this stage, the required actions are [1]:
- Immediately lay off employees and provide severance
- Pay down obligations
- Use remaining cash for shutdown costs

The consequences of not acting: "If you don't do this and instead end up with zero cash and outstanding payroll, tax or other obligations, things will get Very Bad" [1].

### Shutdown Principles

Caldwell lists specific considerations for an orderly shutdown [1]:

- **Shut down when you hit the point of no return.** Do not drag things out.
- **Do not become insolvent.** Pay debts, especially tax and payroll obligations.
- **Consult lawyers.** In messy scenarios, founders can face personal liability.
- **Avoid no-upside situations.** "Don't drag things out and end up in a no upside situation: no upside for you, for your employees, for your investors, or for your customers."
- **Behave with integrity.** "Even if things go poorly, behave in a way you would be proud of."

### On Reputation and Defeat

Caldwell acknowledges the psychological difficulty: "Often the toughest thing for a founder that has made it this far is to 'admit defeat'" [1]. His advice on reputation: "it's just as important to handle situations well when things go poorly as it is when they are going great" [1].

## Two Things to Remember

Caldwell closes with two principles that override everything else [1]:

1. **Don't lie to yourself**
2. **Act quickly and decisively**

## References

1. [Advice for Companies with Less Than 1 Year of Runway](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway) — Dalton Caldwell (n.d.)

title: Prompt Engineering
body:
# Prompt Engineering

Prompt engineering has evolved from a temporary workaround for getting useful output from LLMs into a core discipline for AI startups. YC partners [[Garry Tan]], [[Harj Taggar]], [[Jared Friedman]], and Diana Hu surveyed more than a dozen top AI companies and synthesized what the best teams are doing to make LLM outputs useful and predictable in production [1]. The practice resembles both coding ("it kind of feels like coding in 1995") and management ("how do I actually communicate the things they need to know to make a good decision") [1].

## Anatomy of a Production Prompt

Parahelp, a YC company powering AI customer support for Perplexity, Replit, and Bolt, open-sourced its production prompt -- a rare glimpse at the "crown jewels" of a vertical AI agent company [1]. The prompt is six pages long and follows a structured format:

1. **Role definition**: "You're a manager of a customer service agent" with bullet-pointed responsibilities [1].
2. **Task specification**: Approve or reject tool calls, orchestrating other agents [1].
3. **Step-by-step plan**: Numbered steps (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) for reasoning through decisions [1].
4. **Guardrails**: Instructions to avoid calling certain tools or going off-track [1].
5. **Output format**: Structured output specification (accept/reject with specific formatting) -- critical for agent-to-agent integration [1].
6. **Worked examples**: XML-tagged examples showing expected reasoning and output [1].

Diana Hu notes that the best prompts use XML tag formatting for examples because "a lot of LLMs were post-trained in RLHF with kind of XML type of input, and it turns out to produce better results" [1]. The prompt looks "more like programming than writing English" [1].

## The Three-Tier Prompt Architecture

A production AI system typically separates prompts into three tiers [1]:

- **System prompt**: Defines the high-level behavior -- the company-wide API. Parahelp's open-sourced prompt is a system prompt with nothing customer-specific [1].
- **Developer prompt**: Customer-specific context -- how to handle Perplexity RAG questions versus Bolt build questions [1].
- **User prompt**: End-user input (not all products have this; Parahelp's product is not directly consumed by end users) [1].

This architecture addresses a key challenge for vertical AI agent companies: how to have enough flexibility for customer-specific logic without becoming a consulting company that builds a new prompt for every customer [1]. Friedman calls this "forking and merging prompts across customers" -- a frontier the industry is only beginning to explore [1].

## Metaprompting

Metaprompting -- using one LLM to improve another LLM's prompts -- is "turning out to be a very powerful tool that everyone's using now" [1].

**Prompt folding**: One prompt dynamically generates better versions of itself. A classifier prompt generates a specialized prompt based on the previous query. You feed examples where the prompt failed, and instead of rewriting the prompt manually, you give it to a raw LLM and say "Help me make this prompt better." Tan describes this: "Because it knows itself so well, strangely, metaprompting is turning out to be a very, very powerful tool" [1].

**Example-driven refinement**: When the task is too complex to describe in instructions alone, worked examples steer the model. Jasberry (current YC batch) does automatic bug finding in code, feeding expert-level examples of hard-to-find bugs (like N+1 queries) into the prompt. "This pattern of giving examples turns out to work really well because it helps LLMs reason around complicated tasks" [1].

**Model distillation via metaprompting**: Companies use a larger model (Claude 3.5, GPT-4o) to do metaprompting and refine prompts, then deploy the refined prompt on a smaller, faster model (Llama). This is common for voice AI companies where latency is critical -- "if you have too much pause before the agent responds, humans can detect something is off" [1].

**Quick-start method**: Even for hobbyists, a simple approach works: give the LLM the role of "expert prompt engineer who gives really detailed, great critiques," feed it your draft prompt, and iterate. "Works surprisingly well" [1].

## Escape Hatches and Debug Info

A critical lesson: LLMs will hallucinate rather than admit uncertainty. "The model really wants to help you so much that if you just tell it 'Give me back output in this particular format,' even if it doesn't quite have the information it needs, it'll actually just tell you what it thinks you want to hear" [1].

Two solutions have emerged:

1. **Explicit escape hatch**: Tell the model "If you do not have enough information to say yes or no, don't just make it up. Stop and ask me" [1].
2. **Debug info parameter**: Friedman's approach at YC -- add an output field where the model can complain to the developer about confusing or underspecified instructions. "It literally ends up being like a to-do list that you, the agent developer, has to do" [1].

## Model Personalities

Different models have distinct personalities that affect how they handle prompts [1]:

- **O3**: "Very rigid. It really sticks to the rubric. It heavily penalizes for anything that doesn't fit" -- like a soldier doing check, check, check [1].
- **Gemini 2.5 Pro**: "Quite good at being flexible. It would apply the rubric, but it could also reason through why someone might be an exception" -- like a high-agency employee [1].
- **Claude**: "The more happy and more human steerable model" [1].
- **Llama 4**: "Needs a lot more steering. Almost like talking to a developer" -- possibly because of less RLHF [1].

Taggar describes discovering this while building an investor scoring system: the same rubric given to O3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro produced meaningfully different results, with Gemini being better at handling edge cases that fell outside the rubric's explicit parameters [1].

## Evals Are the Crown Jewels

The YC partners have been saying for over a year that evals -- the test suites for prompt quality -- are the true data asset for AI companies [1]. Parahelp was willing to open-source its prompt precisely because "they don't consider the prompts to be the crown jewels -- the evals are the crown jewels, because without the evals, you don't know why the prompt was written the way that it was" [1].

Building good evals requires sitting "literally side by side with people who are doing X, Y, or Z knowledge work" [1]. You need to sit next to the tractor sales regional manager in Nebraska and understand their reward function, then codify that into specific evals. Tan describes this as the true moat: "If you are out there in particular places understanding that user better than anyone else and having the software actually work for those people, that's the moat" [1].

This connects directly to the [[Forward Deployed Engineers]] model pioneered by Palantir (see that article for the full treatment).

## Practical Tips

- Use Gemini 2.5 Pro's long context window like a REPL: feed your prompt plus one example, watch the reasoning trace in real time to understand how to steer the model [1].
- Thinking traces (chain-of-thought outputs) are "the critical debug information to understand what's wrong with your prompt" -- Gemini recently added these to its API [1].
- Keep a Google Doc of noted failures while using the agent, then feed those notes plus the original prompt to a strong model and ask it to suggest edits [1].
- The Kaizen principle applies: "The people who are the absolute best at improving the process are the people actually doing it" -- which is what metaprompting enables [1].

## References

1. [State-Of-The-Art Prompting For AI Agents](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DL82mGde6wo) -- [[Garry Tan]], [[Harj Taggar]], Diana Hu, [[Jared Friedman]] (2025)
2. [AI Apps Are Broken -- Here's How To Fix Them](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WJoZK9sMwvw) -- [[Pete Koomen]], Tom Blomfield, David Lieb (2025)

title: Startup Leadership
body:
# Startup Leadership

Leadership in startups differs from leadership in established organizations because the role of the CEO changes dramatically as the company scales. [[Ali Rowghani]], drawing on his experience working closely with Ed Catmull, Steve Jobs, John Lasseter, and Bob Iger at Pixar, argues that leadership is measurable through three foundational traits and that a startup CEO's job shifts from building product to building a company. These two essays together form a comprehensive framework for understanding, measuring, and improving leadership in the startup context.

## Leadership Is Built on Trust

The central claim in Rowghani's framework is that all great leadership reduces to trust. Despite observing leaders with radically different styles (an introverted scientist, an extroverted artist, a college dropout infamous for brash behavior, and a career executive who was "exceptionally genteel and diplomatic"), Rowghani found they all shared the ability to "create an extraordinary amount of trust in the people around them" [1].

Trust is both a science and an art [1]:
- **The science**: Trust accrues when a leader's predictions about products, investments, and competitive landscapes prove accurate, and when their people decisions prove correct. Over time, the track record becomes known.
- **The art**: Trust is built when leaders "say the right thing at the right time, and show empathy and good judgment." It grows when leaders "stand for ideals bigger than yourself rather than caring primarily about your personal success, wealth, fame, or position."

This is why leadership styles cannot be copied: "You can't try to copy Steve Jobs or Ed Catmull in your quest to be a great leader. You can only be yourself" [1].

## The Three Foundational Traits

See [[Three Traits of Great Leadership]] for the full framework. In summary:

### 1. Clarity of Thought and Communication

Great leaders think clearly and describe a compelling vision [1]. Rowghani cites Jeff Bezos's retail strategy as an exemplary case: it was built on three enduring customer preferences (lower prices, bigger selection, faster delivery). As Bezos said: "You can build a business strategy around the things that are stable in time....when you have something that you know is true, even over the long-term, you can afford to put a lot of energy into it" [1].

Clarity of thought always precedes clarity of language. The practical advice: "spend more time thinking about what you believe is truly important for your business. Once you've crystallized what's important for everyone to understand, practice expressing it in simple terms" [1].

As companies scale, preparation becomes critical. Tobi Lütke of Shopify and his senior team spend hundreds of hours preparing for their annual employee Summit. As Lütke describes it: "We want to be a loosely coupled, highly aligned company. The Summit is the main enabler of this because it is a grand sync. We spend countless hours preparing because if we communicate well at the Summit, we achieve great alignment by the end" [1].

### 2. Judgment About People

Great leaders have strong intuition about who to give power and responsibility to. They "are able to see hidden potential in people and detect cases where ambition exceeds ability" [1]. When hiring or promotion mistakes happen, they have the courage to act.

The stakes are high: "Nothing does more damage to an organization or to the standing of a leader than picking the wrong leaders or failing to correct these mistakes when they happen" [1]. However, "leaders who fire too many of their own also lose a lot of credibility and trust" [1].

To improve people judgment, Rowghani recommends gathering more data: meet the best people in every field to sharpen pattern recognition. He cites the example of Uber CTO Thuan Pham being interviewed by Travis Kalanick for "30 hours straight, one-on-one, over two weeks" including over Skype while traveling. Pham recalled: "Throughout those 30 hours, I actually forgot it was an interview. It was just like a discussion between two colleagues" [1].

Reference checks are essential, particularly for behavioral evidence of good judgment and integrity, which are "hard to test in an interview" [1].

### 3. Personal Integrity and Commitment

Integrity means "standing for something meaningful beyond oneself rather than being motivated by narrow personal interests" [1]. It includes admitting mistakes, receiving critical feedback with humility, and avoiding behaviors that erode trust (favoritism, conflicts of interest, inappropriate relationships).

Rowghani proposes a demanding test: "if your team had full transparency into your private communications and behavior towards employees, would you be embarrassed by anything you have done or said? This is a high bar, but one that great leaders strive to meet" [1].

Commitment goes beyond working long hours. Great leaders "make their work into their core life mission in ways that inspire others" [1].

## Measuring Leadership

Rowghani argues that measuring leadership through bottom-line company performance fails because "timing and luck can be confounding factors" and because performance metrics offer "no clues as to how someone can improve as a leader" [1].

Instead, he proposes systematic employee surveys organized around the three traits. The full survey framework appears in [[Three Traits of Great Leadership]]. Key elements include asking both the CEO and employees parallel questions about clarity of mission, quality of people decisions, and integrity of leadership behavior [1].

He recommends startups begin gathering this data systematically at approximately 50 people [1].

## The CEO's Evolving Role

The CEO's job changes dramatically across three phases of company growth. See [[Startup Leadership]] for the complete framework.

### Phase 1: Doer-in-Chief

The CEO's first job is to "build a great product and find a small group of people who love it and use it enthusiastically" [2]. In this phase, the CEO must be deeply involved in building the product, acquiring users, and conserving money. "Delegation should not be a word in your vocabulary" [2].

### Phase 2: Company-Builder-in-Chief (20-500 employees)

When demand exceeds the small team's capacity, the CEO must shift to company-building [2]. This is the critical transition that determines the startup's long-term success. In practice, Phase 2 begins around 20-25 employees and ends around 400-500 [2].

The transition is psychologically difficult: "When you've been a successful Doer-in-Chief, it's hard to stop. It's hard to stop coding, designing product specs, and interacting with customers on a daily basis" [2]. It can also create confusion with the team, who may wonder why the CEO is suddenly delegating tasks they had always done [2].

The key insight: "once your startup reaches 20-30 people, you'll have to spend more time leading (i.e., directing the activities of others). And since time is finite, the only way to lead more is do less" [2].

David Rusenko, co-founder of Weebly, illustrates successful delegation: "Often, the first time I find out about a product feature is reading about it on our blog. It shocks most founders to hear this, but I know I've done my job well because I've yet to see a feature that was built poorly. You should aspire to build a team that's so good that you don't have to be involved in the product details" [2].

### Phase 3: Investment in Transformation

At the end of Phase 2, the leadership team operates at a high level with minimal CEO involvement. The CEO can then shift to taking profits from the core business and investing in transformative new products [2]. Facebook exemplifies this: it built its senior management team in Phase 2 while operating at roughly breakeven, then used Phase 3 profits to fund Messenger as a separate product and acquire Instagram, WhatsApp, and Oculus [2].

## Three Non-Delegable CEO Tasks

Rowghani identifies three tasks that only the CEO can perform. See [[Startup Leadership]] for full detail.

### 1. Hiring and Managing the Leadership Team

"Only the CEO can hire the company's senior leadership team and make sure that they work well together" [2]. The hiring decisions must be the CEO's because "the consequences are yours as well" [2]. Patrick Collison made it a point to meet with the "best-in-the-world" in each field to calibrate what great looks like [2].

Key metrics for this task:
- Expect at least 25% of leadership hires not to work out [2]
- Most CEOs take too long to fire their first executive [2]
- "The longer you leave an under-performing executive in place, the more credibility you lose with everyone else on your team" [2]
- Do not be surprised if 50% of time goes to hiring and managing the senior team [2]

### 2. Creating Purpose and Alignment (Mission-to-Metrics)

See [[Mission-to-Metrics Framework]]. The CEO defines the Mission (purpose), Strategy (direction), and Metrics (pace and performance) [2]. Rowghani illustrates perfect alignment with the SpaceX employee who, when asked "What is your job?", traced his specific task (designing a steering system) back through the company's strategy (reusable rockets) to the mission (colonize Mars) [2].

Key principles:
- Write your strategy in long form to catch flaws in thinking [2]
- Do not equate metrics with top-line results like revenue growth. Instead, find the drivers. Facebook discovered that connecting a new user to 10 friends within 14 days correlated with retained usage, so they set that as their key product metric [2]
- Communicate Mission-to-Metrics constantly. "You have to reiterate the Mission-to-Metrics much more than what feels reasonable" [2]

### 3. Nurturing Company Culture

Unlike the other two tasks, culture is not solely the CEO's job; it is everyone's responsibility [2]. The CEO should not write cultural values alone. Instead, "encourage co-founders and early employees to work together to codify a set of values and behavioral norms that feel authentic and aspirational to everyone" [2].

Rowghani tells the story of Toy Story 2 at Pixar as the defining cultural moment. With seven months to release, creative leadership felt the film was not working. Disney argued it was too late to start over. Pixar refused, tearing up the story and rewriting from scratch, pushing the studio "to the brink of collapse" to complete the new version on time [2]. The film was a success, and afterward, the executive team closed the studio for two months to let everyone recuperate.

Ed Catmull called it "the most intense and important period in the studio's history: 'Toy Story 2 defined us. It said that we couldn't be a studio that produced both great work and average work. Everything we made had to be great'" [2].

## The Measure of Success

Rowghani cites Steve Jobs at a Pixar management meeting: "When I'm at my best, 50% of my time is unscheduled. That's the time I use to think, drop in on the people I want to speak with, and let my curiosity roam. It's my time to be creative. Without this free time, I would never be able to stay ahead of the company. To lead a company, you've always got to be two steps ahead. There's no way to lead a company from behind" [2].

Having significant unscheduled time is "perhaps the clearest sign of success for a Phase 2 CEO" because it signals successful delegation, a strong leadership team, and well-codified Mission-to-Metrics [2].

## References

1. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — [[Ali Rowghani]] (n.d.)
2. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]] (n.d.)

title: Getting Press
body:
# Getting Press

[[Michael Seibel]] describes the press strategy he advises for YC companies, built on advice he received from Mike Arrington and M.G. Siegler at an SV Angel CEO conference [1]. The core principle is four words:

**"Treat PR like biz dev."**

Before hearing this advice, Seibel wasted mental energy on presumed special rules for press: catchy subject lines, blasting lists of a hundred reporters, writing press releases. Most of these efforts failed. Treating PR like business development means developing relationships with reporters as people, getting introduced through warm connections instead of cold emails, and creating a fair value exchange [1].

## Step One: Generate News

Founders commonly pitch reporters profile pieces and stories that could be written at any time. Reporters do not find these valuable. What makes a valuable story is news -- something timely, something that makes more sense to talk about today than tomorrow [1].

Four categories of startup news [1]:

**Product Launches and Features**: Things coming out today, not yesterday or tomorrow. User-facing features. At Socialcam, Seibel would reach out to the press for video filters, soundtrack capability, or user profiles.

**Product/Sales Milestones**: Hitting a million users, $100K MRR, a million guests per night (as with Airbnb). The number should be as big as possible relative to the startup's size. Seibel recommends sandbagging: wait until 25% past the milestone to announce, so the next milestone is that much closer.

**Significant BD Deals or Customers**: Landing a team at Airbnb or Instacart, or partnering with GM to build self-driving cars. If you have their permission, consider announcing it.

**Fundraising**: For better or worse, fundraising is conflated with traction and success. Angel rounds or Series A/B/etc. are considered news by most reporters and noticed by potential employees.

## Step Two: Make Your Own Press Contacts

Reporters want to talk to CEOs and cofounders, not PR people. As an early-stage founder, building reporter relationships and making pitches directly produces a higher success rate and costs less time and money. Seibel's team spent over $100,000 on PR agencies and PR people as an early-stage startup before learning this [1].

The best way to meet a tech reporter is through a warm intro from the CEO or a cofounder. For YC founders, the YC network is helpful because the target reporter has almost certainly written about someone in the network. Proper etiquette is to ask your friend for an intro to only one reporter at a time [1].

The intro message should be readable and reply-able in thirty seconds [1]:
- One sentence describing the news
- For an exclusive: say you are offering an exclusive
- For an embargo: include the date and time of the announcement
- A request for a twenty-minute call

On the call, have three to five reasons ready for why the news and the company are important. At the end, offer notes and relevant collateral (screenshots, graphs, logos, pictures). This provides reporters with the language you prefer while being courteous [1].

## Step Three: Pitch an Exclusive

Almost all startups should offer their story as an exclusive to one tech reporter at a time. The target audience -- investors, potential employees, motivated customers, early adopters -- is concentrated in a few tech outlets. Getting blanket coverage across many tech blogs is often not worth the struggle and can create problems when embargoes are broken [1].

## Step Four: Consistent Cadence

The PR industry philosophy of maximizing press mentions per story (packing all news into one big announcement) is not optimal for startups. Seibel compares PR benefits to funding basic research in science: "you can't predict the advances but they do happen when you invest consistently over time" [1].

One story every month or every other month produces better results than one huge story every year. Spread news across multiple stories instead of combining everything. Maintain a queue of three to five pieces of news to announce in the coming six months. This removes pressure if one piece does not get picked up and helps keep a regular cadence [1].

## Expectations and Limits

A 25-50% success rate on pitches is reasonable when doing everything right. After six to twelve months of good cadence and real news, a founder should be relatively friendly with two to five reporters who will reply to most emails [1].

At a later stage, when press organically pays attention (Dropbox and Airbnb are examples), it becomes strategic to create distance between founders and the press. This is when PR people become helpful as a buffer for strategic announcements [1].

PR should occupy no more than 5% of a CEO's time. Founders should not expect PR to create observable miracles (tons of users, customers, or investment offers). Most benefits only become obvious weeks or months after a story is posted [1].

## References

1. [Getting Press for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup) — [[Michael Seibel]] (February 2017)

title: Financial Survival
body:
# Financial Survival

Nearly every startup that fails, fails by running out of money [5]. [[Paul Graham]] returns to this theme across multiple essays, developing increasingly specific frameworks for how founders should think about spending, fundraising, and the critical question of whether they will survive. YC Group Partners Brad Flora, Pete Koomen, Gustaf Alströmer, and Nicolas Dessaigne reinforce these principles with updated tactical advice for the current era [6].

## The Default Alive / Default Dead Framework

Graham's October 2015 essay introduced the most important diagnostic question for startups past the first 8-9 months [1]: assuming expenses remain constant and revenue growth continues on its current trend, will the company make it to profitability on the money it has left? "Or to put it more dramatically, by default do they live or die?" [1]

The startling finding: half the founders Graham talks to do not know the answer [1]. Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) to help founders find out.

This question determines the entire conversation. If the company is default alive, you can talk about ambitious new things. If it is default dead, you need to talk about how to save it [1]. See [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] for the full framework.

## The Fatal Pinch

The most dangerous scenario is what Graham calls the "fatal pinch": default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [1]. Founders end up in it by not realizing that is where they are headed.

A common death spiral [1]: A startup makes something moderately appealing and has decent initial growth. They raise their first round fairly easily because the founders seem smart and the idea sounds plausible. But growth is only ok, not great. The founders convince themselves that hiring a bunch of people will boost growth. Their investors agree. But the growth never comes because the product is only moderately appealing. Now they are rapidly running out of runway. They hope further investment will save them, but with high expenses and slow growth, they are unappealing to investors. They are unable to raise more, and the company dies.

The correct action: address the fundamental problem -- that the product is only moderately appealing. Hiring people is rarely the way to fix that. More often it makes it harder. "At this early stage, the product needs to evolve more than to be 'built out,' and that's usually easier with fewer people" [1].

## The Right Way to Spend Money by Stage

The YC Group Partners provide stage-specific spending guidance [6]:

### Pre-Seed (Day Zero)

"Don't spend any dollar" [6]. The only necessary expenses are a laptop and a place to live. Most companies YC funds have spent close to zero before being funded. Many do not even have bank accounts or exist as entities yet [6].

### Post-Seed ($500K - $2M raised)

Hire one or maybe two engineers -- people you already know and have worked with. Use contractors for other roles. Founders should do sales themselves. "Don't hire for sales or marketing until product market fit" [6].

Pete Koomen describes a common office hour: "The founder says, 'I think this person would be better than me at selling my product, that I'm the only one in the world that understands.' We have to say, 'I'm so sorry, no one is coming to save you. You are on your own'" [6].

The money in the bank is time to find [[Product-Market Fit]]. Every hire reduces that time. "The more people, the slower you get to product market fit" [6].

### Post-Series A (PMF achieved)

Once genuine product-market fit exists, hiring for sales becomes appropriate. Gustaf Alströmer: "If you have actual product market fit, you can create a sales team. That's a good way to spend the money as long as you know that anyone you hire is going to make the company more money than you spend on them" [6].

The framework for hiring: each function should be monitored for when the person is overwhelmed by inbound demand. When someone is approaching their breaking point, plan the hire -- understanding that hiring takes time, so anticipate rather than react [6].

Monitor revenue per employee over time. That number should go up as the company benefits from scale [6].

### Post-Series B

Revenue quality becomes critical. Companies that fail at this stage "don't actually understand the quality of their revenue" [6]. They may have fooled themselves -- and their investors -- about net dollar retention, revenue retention, and customer retention. "The ones that I've seen where it doesn't work are the ones that don't actually understand their own retention" [6].

If the revenue is genuinely high quality, "the money becomes just fuel in the engine" [6].

## Spending Traps

### The "Miniature Big Company" Fallacy

Brad Flora identifies the core mistake: founders treat startups as miniature versions of big companies, assuming every function that exists in a big company should exist in their startup [6]. "Startups only exist to reach product-market fit. Most of the things you end up spending money on at big companies -- there is no equivalent in a smaller startup. It just doesn't exist at all" [6].

Founders get offices, hire chiefs of staff, build departments -- all of which feel validating but are irrelevant pre-PMF. "It makes you feel better more than it actually makes any difference whatsoever" [6].

### Premature Advertising

Spending on ads pre-PMF is one of the most common and pernicious mistakes [6]. It becomes addictive: ads produce growth numbers that impress investors, but the underlying business does not work. When the ad budget runs out, there is nothing left [6].

Worse, ad spending prevents founders from learning. "You don't learn anything when you get a customer from an ad at an early stage" [6]. Founders miss the opportunity to discover capital-efficient customer acquisition channels. One YC company was told to turn off AdWords for two weeks and match their numbers through any means necessary. "They ended up finding some really clever ways to scrape government documents and permits and reach out to these people" [6].

The only legitimate ad spend categories: (1) experimental and small, or (2) ROI-positive with known payback period [6].

### Branding Agencies Pre-PMF

"Sometimes the first thing founders do after raising is hire a branding agency for $50K to rebrand their company. A B2B company that nobody's going to find on their website anyway. Just such a waste of money" [6].

### The ZIRP Hangover

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] describe the multi-year unwinding of ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy) era spending habits [7]. During 2020-2022, companies that raised $2M at seed could quickly raise $10-15M Series A rounds and then burn $1M/month while stuck at $1M ARR. "They weren't honest with themselves. They somehow had the money and pushed the gas in all of the spending and just thinking that the revenue will catch up. But it almost never does" [6].

Signs of ZIRP recovery -- which are good signs for the company's future -- include executive turnover, founders becoming more hands-on, return to office, elimination of vanity projects, and reduction of excessive perks [7].

## Tactics for Financial Discipline

Two specific tactics from Gustaf Alströmer [6]:

1. **Send monthly investor updates.** Accountability forces discipline. Having someone else look at the numbers monthly prevents self-deception.
2. **Use two bank accounts.** Put half the money in a separate account and pretend it does not exist. "You look at your bank account and think you only have half the money that you raised."

## Overhiring: The Biggest Killer

"Don't hire too fast. Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [1].

Three reasons founders overhire [1]:
1. **There is so much work to do.** Naive founders think if they can hire enough people, it will all get done.
2. **Successful startups have lots of employees**, so it seems like that is what you do to be successful. But large staffs are probably more the effect of growth than the cause.
3. **They don't want to face the real reason for slow growth**: the product is not appealing enough.

VCs often push founders to overhire because kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for portfolios. "VCs want to blow you up, in one sense of the phrase or the other. But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive" [1].

Sam Altman reinforced this in a note on a draft of the essay: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [1].

Airbnb, by contrast, waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee. The founders were overworked but were spending that time evolving Airbnb into the astonishingly successful organism it became [1].

## Plan A and Plan B

It is not safe to be default dead even if you think you can count on investors saving you [1]. Investors' interest is a function of growth. If you have steep revenue growth -- over 5x a year -- you can start to count on investor interest even if you are not profitable. But investors are fickle [1].

Graham's rule: "You should always have a plan B as well: you should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to survive if you can't raise more money, and precisely when you'll have to switch to plan B if plan A isn't working" [1].

## Raising Too Little, Too Much, or Just Right

Graham's 2006 essay identifies three fundraising-related mistakes that kill startups [2]:

**Raising too little (#11):** Startup funding is measured in time -- runway. You must take enough to get to the next step [2].

**Spending too much (#12):** The classic way to burn cash is hiring a lot of people, which bites you twice: it increases costs and slows you down [2].

**Raising too much (#13):** Taking a lot of money changes the company's atmosphere and makes it harder to change direction. "When you raise a lot of money, your company moves to the suburbs and has kids" [2].

## The Equity Equation

When trading stock for anything -- money, employees, deals -- Graham provides a formula for whether to do it: you should give up n% of your company if what you trade it for improves your average outcome enough that the (100 - n)% you have left is worth more than the whole company was before [3]. See [[Equity Equation]] for the full treatment.

## Poverty as Advantage

Graham identifies poverty as a genuine advantage for young founders [5]. Nearly every startup that fails, fails by running out of money, and a low burn rate gives more opportunity to recover from the many mistakes startups make at first [5].

Even more important than living cheaply is thinking cheaply. The Apple II was popular partly because it was cheap -- Woz designed it for himself and couldn't afford anything more. Viaweb's top-level service was $300/month, an order of magnitude below the norm, because $300 seemed like a lot to them. "Like Apple, we created something inexpensive, and therefore popular, simply because we were poor" [5].

## References

1. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
3. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2007)
4. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
5. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
6. [The Right (And Wrong) Way to Spend Money at Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/M9-the-right-and-wrong-way-to-spend-money-at-your-startup) -- Brad Flora, Pete Koomen, Gustaf Alströmer, Nicolas Dessaigne (2025)
7. [Signs Your Company Is Recovering from ZIRP](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/La-signs-your-company-is-recovering-from-zirp) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)

title: Launching
body:
# Launching

Launching is one of the most over-thought activities in startup building. Kat Manalac, head of YC's Outreach team who has seen over 3,500 companies launch through YC, argues that founders should stop thinking about launching as a single moment in time and instead treat it as something done continually [3]. [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], and [[Brad Flora]] reinforce this: the advice to launch early is "painfully obvious" and "YC [[Paul Graham]] 101," yet founders consistently resist it [4].

## Launch Now

The answer to "when should I launch?" is ASAP -- probably right now [3]. Founders are good at lying to themselves. Putting the product out there, even in its earliest state, helps determine whether the problem is big enough that someone is willing to pay or use the product [3].

### Worst Case Scenarios Are Survivable

If you launch too early [3]:
- People might think the product is ugly or bad
- Investors might hear about it before it is ready
- Competitors might see it
- No one will see it or care

All of these are survivable. Airbnb launched three times before it started to get real users [3].

### The Parallel Universe Problem

Brad Flora identifies the core psychological barrier: founders develop a parallel universe in their head of what the launch reaction should look like [4]. Seibel describes the image founders have: it is like the Oscar ceremony, with hordes of people treating you like a celebrity. The reality is that often no one cares [4].

Using the definite article -- "the launch" -- bakes in a fallacy that it is a singular event [4]. Launching is not precious. You launch over and over again [4].

## Big Launches Don't Work

Graham explicitly warns against relying on a "Big Launch" for growth [2]. There may even be an inverse correlation between launch magnitude and success -- the only launches he remembers are famous flops like the Segway and Google Wave [2].

All you need from a launch is some initial core of users. How well you are doing a few months later depends more on how happy you made those users than how many there were [2].

## Launch Continuously

Paul Buchheit, creator of Gmail, says it is better to make a few people really happy than to make a lot of people semi-happy [3]. With even 10 users who love you, all you have to do is expand that number [3].

Segment provides a case study in continuous launching. They did five launches on Hacker News in about a month, each time adding features users had requested. This iterative launching helped them find [[Product-Market Fit]] and eventually led to a $3.2 billion acquisition by Twilio [4].

## Types of Launches

Manalac identifies several types of launches [1] [3]:

1. **Silent launch**: Ship to a small group with no fanfare. Every founder should have at minimum: a domain name, company name, short description, contact method, and call to action [3].
2. **Friends and family launch**: Broader distribution within personal networks. But do not stay in this phase too long [3].
3. **Stranger launch**: First time in front of people with no personal connection to the founders. DoorDash's founders discovered their delivery insight from Chloe, a macaroon store manager in Palo Alto [3].
4. **Online community launch**: Hacker News, Product Hunt, relevant subreddits. Robinhood got 10,000 signups the first day and 50,000 the next week after being posted on HN on a Friday night [3].
5. **Press launch**: Only when the product is strong enough to survive scrutiny [1].
6. **Ongoing launches**: New features, major milestones, and pivots each deserve their own launch moment [1].

## The One-Sentence Pitch

Before any launch, you need a clear one-sentence description [3]. Lead with what, not with why. Start with the company name and what you do.

**Example**: Pave: "Pave lets companies plan, communicate, and benchmark your compensation in real time" [3].

**Anti-example**: "IndieCloud is a know-how and synergy platform." [[Garry Tan]]: "Meaningless jargon is the number one issue I spend time trying to fight when helping startups" [3].

## Why Founders Resist Launching Early

1. **Speed calibration.** If you have only ever worked at a big company, startup fast is another level [4].
2. **Perfectionism.** Magic launched two days after the idea with just a phone number: "Text us and we'll do whatever you like" [4].
3. **"I'm the exception" thinking.** Founders who believe their situation is different [4].

### The Exception: When Slow Launches Work

Rippling took close to two years to officially launch. But Parker Conrad had already built a billion-dollar company (Zenefits) doing the exact same thing [4]. "If you're not Parker Conrad -- you haven't already built a billion-dollar company doing this exact same thing -- then you should ignore all the advice about slow launches" [4].

## Press Is Not a Launch Strategy

For early-stage companies that have not raised $1M+, landing press stories is extremely difficult [3]. Even when it happens, press is not a silver bullet -- it is not a scalable way to get users and cannot be counted on for sustained growth [3].

## References

1. [How to Launch Again and Again](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6i-how-to-launch-again-and-again) -- Kat Manalac (2019)
2. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2013)
3. [The Best Way to Launch Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ir-the-best-way-to-launch-your-startup) -- Kat Manalac (n.d.)
4. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: When to Launch Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J0-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-when-to-launch-your-startup) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]] (n.d.)

title: Talking to Users
body:
# Talking to Users

"Talk to your users" is one of the most repeated pieces of advice in the YC canon, cited by [[Paul Graham]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Gustaf Alströmer]], and virtually every YC group partner across dozens of sources. Alströmer, former Head of Growth at Airbnb and YC group partner since 2017, provides the most systematic treatment of how to do it well [1].

## Why the Best Founders Talk to Users Throughout the Company's Lifetime

Most people have the formation of new startups completely wrong. They think product ideas come from lazy Sundays or late-night coding sessions [1]. Alströmer contrasts the dramatized depiction of Facebook's founding in The Social Network with a real photograph of Brian Chesky spending a weekend with Amal, Airbnb's very first guest. The difference: a two-way conversation with a real customer [1].

In 2010, Chesky launched an experiment: he gave up his apartment and lived in 50 different Airbnbs over several months. By doing this, he got a chance to talk to every one of those 50 hosts each day [1]. Still today, Brian and Joe receive calls from hosts on their personal cell phone numbers -- the numbers they put on the website and shared with all hosts early on. They were not hiding behind anonymous email [1].

Alströmer frames this as almost radical. Most founders spend their time in front of computers trying to find scalable growth channels like Google Ads. Most founders still do not build personal connections with their users, hiding behind info@ emails or do-not-reply emails [1].

## Finding Users to Interview

Alströmer identifies several sources for finding potential users to interview [1]:

- **Personal network**: Easiest to reach but may give less honest answers to avoid offending you
- **Coworkers and former coworkers**: A great source, especially if they know the topic well
- **LinkedIn**: Search for people with relevant titles or keywords
- **Reddit forums, Slack/Discord communities**: Common sources cited by YC founders
- **In-person events**: Sometimes the most effective for certain industries

## How to Run a User Interview

The interview should be conducted over video call, phone, or in person. A five-minute video interview yields more insight than 5,000 survey responses [1].

### Core Principles

1. **Build rapport with the interviewee.** They are going to give answers to questions nobody has asked them before. They need to feel comfortable [1].

2. **Do not introduce your idea until the end of the call, or not at all.** Doing this too early biases their answers. The interviewer's role is to listen, not talk [1].

3. **Ask open-ended follow-up questions.** "Tell me about that." "What do you mean by that?" "Can you tell me more about that?" "Why is that important to you?" These techniques get people to open up about their problems [1].

4. **Take notes.** If not recording, take notes during the interview. The notes are the raw material for product decisions [1].

### Six Key Questions

Alströmer provides a specific question framework [1]:

1. "Tell me how you do X today." (X = the specific problem, task, or goal)
2. "What is the hardest thing about doing X?"
3. "Why is it hard?"
4. "How often do you have to do X?"
5. "Why is it important for your company to do X?" (Dive deep on motivation)
6. "What do you do to solve this problem for yourself today?"

On the last question, Alströmer emphasizes: if you can watch them actually do it -- screen sharing during a video call, showing their laptop -- that is the ideal state. Understanding their current behavior is critical because changing behavior is hard [1].

### Questions to Avoid

1. "Will you use our product?" (They will say yes; it means nothing) [1]
2. "Which features would make product X better?" (Their job is to tell you about the problem; your job is to think about features) [1]
3. Yes/no questions (You want concrete, real examples) [1]
4. "How would a better product X look like to you?" (They are not product developers) [1]
5. Asking two questions at the same time (confuses the interviewee and the answers) [1]

### The Biggest Danger: Focusing on Features Instead of Problems

Users generally have good problems but bad solutions [1]. Alströmer gives two specific examples:

**Gmail**: Early users asked Paul Buchheit to display both the inbox and the email they were reading on the same screen. The real reason was that Gmail was too slow, and users did not want to wait to load each email. The solution was not a split-screen view -- it was making Gmail faster [1].

**Airbnb**: Many guests wanted hosts' phone numbers so they could call them. The real reason was that guests did not fully trust the platform. Airbnb had not displayed enough trust signals, so guests needed to feel comfortable by getting to know the host before booking [1].

Users also have no incentive to say no to additional features. If asked "would you like feature A, B, or C?", they will say yes to everything. The founder must prioritize [1].

## From Interviews to MVP

After five to ten interviews [1]:

1. Organize learnings using sticky notes or similar software. Bucket them into different problem categories.
2. Identify which problem matters the most.
3. Write down conclusions and create a hypothesis for what the solution might be.
4. Do not over-intellectualize this process. Build an MVP as fast as possible.
5. Test the MVP with the same users.

### Determining if the Problem Is Valuable

Three tests [1]:

1. **Are people paying money for other solutions today?** If competitors are getting paid well, that is a good sign.
2. **Do people already have solutions they are happy with?** Excel and Google Spreadsheets are competitors to hundreds of startups. Moving someone off a spreadsheet requires a dramatically better experience.
3. **How easy is it to sell to this audience?** Selling to plumbers or contractors is notoriously difficult compared to selling to startups.

## Getting First Customers: Tactical Advice

Alströmer provides a systematic framework for converting user interviews into first customers [2].

### Founders Must Do Sales Themselves

Founders should learn to do sales because talking to customers and sales are different sides of the same coin [2]. You cannot outsource engineering, and sales has to be part of the founders' DNA. You should not hire a sales team until you know how to do sales yourself -- only then will you know what good looks like [2].

Examples of founders who took on the sales job and became great at it: Tony Xu (DoorDash), Mathilde Collin (Front), Tracy Young (PlanGrid), and Steve Jobs [2].

### The Sales Email

A great sales email should be [2]:

- Short: max six to eight sentences
- Clear language with no jargon or buzzwords
- Address the problem the potential customer is having
- Plain text only (no HTML formatting)
- State that you are the founder
- Include social proof (YC, impressive past companies)
- Include a link to a simple website with screenshots
- Include a call to action (request for a call or meeting)

### Working Backwards from Goals

Most founders do not send enough outreach because they do not work backwards from their goals [2]. Alströmer provides a conversion funnel example:

- 500 outreach emails sent
- 50% open rate = 250 opens
- 5% response rate = ~20 responses
- 50% convert to demo = 10 demos
- 20% close rate = 2 customers

The critical mistake: founders send 100 emails, get zero customers, and conclude "sales doesn't work." They did not send enough emails to reach statistically meaningful conversion rates [2].

### Easiest Customers First

First customers should be the easiest to close [2]:

1. **Sell to people you know.** Selling to strangers is harder.
2. **Sell to startups.** Bigger companies have more bureaucracy, procurement departments, and longer decision-making chains. Startups have short decision-making lines and the founder can often reach the decision-maker directly.
3. **Most people are not early adopters.** The reason you must send hundreds of emails is not that people hate your product -- most just archive the email. You are looking for the small percentage who love trying new things.

### Charge From the Start

If you do not charge your customers, they are not a customer, and you do not have a company [2]. Customers paying money is the strongest signal that you are providing real value.

Instead of free trials in B2B, offer a money-back guarantee or the ability to opt out of an annual contract after one month [2]. Increase your price until customers are complaining but still paying [2].

## Keeping Users Involved

After initial interviews and MVP testing, keep interviewees involved throughout the process [1]:

- Create a Slack instance or WhatsApp group with early customers
- Make them feel they have exclusive access to a future world-changing product
- Show them the product as it progresses
- Ship features in response to their feedback -- this builds trust
- Connect them with other people doing the same thing (they rarely get this opportunity)

## How YC Founders Actually Got Their First Customers

A survey of 50+ founders from a recent YC batch reveals the most common methods [3]:

- **Cold email and outreach**: The most frequently cited method. One founder described going door to door, standing in the sun waiting for a food truck owner to take a break
- **Personal network**: "As with a lot of founders, your first few customers come from your network"
- **Community launches**: Reddit, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, GitHub. One founder went from 50 Twitter followers to 10,000 and 20,000 GitHub stars in days
- **Waitlists and launches**: Several founders used LinkedIn posts and waitlists to generate inbound interest
- **Creative approaches**: One founder responded to recruiting emails from biotech companies by saying "No, but would you like to buy software that does the same thing?"

The pattern is consistent with the broader YC canon: [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] is how every successful company starts, regardless of what their growth channels look like at scale [2].

## References

1. [How to Talk to Users](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iq-how-to-talk-to-users) -- [[Gustaf Alströmer]] (n.d.)
2. [How to Get Your First Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ip-how-to-get-your-first-customers) -- [[Gustaf Alströmer]] (n.d.)
3. [Founder FAQ: How Did You Get Your First Customer?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JE-founder-faq-how-did-you-get-your-first-customer) -- Y Combinator (n.d.)

title: Series A Term Sheets
body:
# Series A Term Sheets

Jason Kwon and [[Aaron Harris]] of YC describe a common problem: founders seeing their first term sheet do not know what "good" looks like [1]. This puts them at a disadvantage because VCs see term sheets constantly. YC, having seen hundreds of Series A term sheets, works with founders to identify where terms diverge from standard, what can be done about divergences, and when negotiation makes sense.

YC drafted a standard, clean Series A term sheet representing what they see most often from good Silicon Valley VCs. The term sheet fits on a single page [1]. This brevity was not always the norm but became common over the last decade as some investors shortened their legalese as a signal: "We aren't going to get bogged down in the minutiae. We're going to make this easy, friendly, standard and fast" [1].

A [downloadable Word version](https://www.ycombinator.com/series_a_term_sheet/) of the standard term sheet is available from YC.

## Pricing

The lead investor in a Series A round generally wants 20% of the company, but pricing can flex depending on leverage held by each side. YC considers price important but too specific to each raise to standardize. Their focus is on control and structure terms that are less familiar to founders and more prone to cause confusion [1].

## Control Terms: What the Term Sheet Tells You

The term sheet is a signal about the investor. A contract allocates risks between parties, so the terms an investor insists on reveal their perceived risks [1].

### Board Composition

The standard term sheet is founder-friendly because founders retain board control at 2-1. The most common way founders lose control at Series A is through a 2-2-1 board structure: two founders, two investors, and one independent board member. Losing board control is significant because it means founders can be fired from their own company [1].

Another control risk is a separate provision requiring the investor director's approval for operational decisions such as setting annual budgets, hiring/firing executives, pivoting the business, or adding new lines of business. When investors structure boards to take power away from founders, the outward justification is governance or accountability. Kwon and Harris state plainly: "when an investor says that they're committed to partnering with you for the long-term -- or that they're betting everything on you -- but then tells you something else with the terms that they insist on, believe the terms" [1].

### Standard Investor Vetoes

The standard voting rights section includes vetoes on financing (clauses covering issuance of new equity) and sale of the company. Kwon and Harris note these are standard across most term sheets and use similar technical jargon, but their concrete implications are not facially obvious. Founders should have a lawyer walk through the scenarios [1].

Founders should ensure their lawyer and the investor's lawyer both specialize in startups and venture deals, where "standard" terms have accepted practice [1].

## Dirty Economic Terms to Watch For

The standard term sheet is instructive for what it does not contain. Non-standard or "dirty" economic terms include [1]:

- **Liquidation preference greater than 1x**: The investor gets back more than its invested capital first.
- **Participating preferred**: The investor double-dips by getting money back plus pro rata portion of exit proceeds, rather than choosing between the two.
- **Cumulative dividends**: The investor compounds its liquidation preference every year by X%, increasing the economic hurdle before founders and employees see value.
- **Warrant coverage**: The investor gets extra fully-diluted ownership without paying for it at the agreed valuation.

These are all ways of adding structure to reduce typical venture risk. The investor is "essentially saying, 'I'm sort of afraid of losing my money.'" It can foreshadow behavior when things go badly, such as pushing founders to sell when they do not want to, or pulling back on risk when risk is needed [1].

Good investors address economic risks by negotiating on valuation and are otherwise happy to give standard terms because "the real money in venture is not made with structure, but by building long-term value" [1].

## Series A Terms as Foundation

The Series A documents set a foundation and precedent for future rounds. Good foundations make the next term sheet and financing round fast and simple, as future investors step into the same straightforward terms. Bad foundations complicate everything: future investors ask for the same heavy terms, existing investors refuse to drop terms that new investors want removed. "Unwinding bad terms is difficult, and oftentimes impossible" [1].

## Don't Over-Optimize

The goal is a clean deal, not a perfect deal. "No one ever built an enduring company just by winning their Series A negotiation." Even if terms are not ideal, execution can outrun suboptimal terms or establish leverage to renegotiate later. The ultimate goal is closing fast and getting back to work [1].

## References

1. [A Standard and Clean Series A Term Sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) — Jason Kwon, [[Aaron Harris]] (September 2018)

title: Co-Founders
body:
# Co-Founders

The co-founder relationship is one of the most consequential decisions in a startup's life and the number one reason startups fail at YC [5]. Of YC's [top companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/topcompanies), only 7.9% were founded by solo founders [3]. [[Paul Graham]] notes that exploitation of co-founders is often the first sign of a founder who will fail [4]. [[Harj Taggar]] frames the importance in three dimensions: co-founders increase the volume and quality of work, provide irreplaceable emotional support, and match the pattern of virtually every successful startup in history [5].

Co-founder failure is a consensus concern across the YC canon. [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], [[Paul Graham]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], and multiple other YC partners independently cite co-founder breakups as the single most common cause of startup death, reinforced across 10+ sources.

## Why Co-Founders Matter

Taggar identifies three core reasons [5]:

1. **Productivity.** You can get more done and better work done with someone who has complementary skills and someone you enjoy discussing ideas with [5].
2. **Moral support.** Startups are intensely taxing. The best co-founder relationships balance each other out: when one founder is despondent, the other keeps them going. "Anyone who's working for you, you're the boss, and they're not as invested in the success of the startup as you are. And you can't really be as open or honest with them about the company as you can be with a peer" [5].
3. **Pattern matching to success.** Apple, Facebook, Google, and Microsoft all had co-founders. People associate these companies with a single person (Jobs, Zuckerberg, Gates), but at the earliest stages, all of them had co-founders they relied on [5].

## Finding the Right Co-Founder

### What to Look For

Taggar argues the single most important thing to know about a potential co-founder is how they handle stress [5]. Second: how well they help you handle stress. "Startups are a very incredibly stressful, intense experience. A great co-founder should be able to support you through those" [5].

The best co-founders are people you have worked with under stressful conditions or close friends whose character you know well [5]. Skills matter but should not be overly fixated on -- "more important than anything is just working with someone that you like working with and you trust" [5]. Care more about trajectory than current skill set [5].

Co-founders must share the same high-level goals. If one wants a high-pressure, fast-growing, VC-backed company and the other wants a lifestyle business, friction is inevitable [5].

### How Co-Founders Meet

Data from YC's top companies shows school and work are the dominant channels [3]. Google, Yahoo, and Microsoft were all founded by people who met in school [3]. College is described as "an ideal place to find a co-founder" [3].

Taggar recommends looking for people to do projects with before committing to a company [5]. Projects are lower commitment than "let's start a company," so more people say yes. Working on projects reveals what kinds of personalities and skills complement yours. "Projects that you work on in your spare time often tend to be some of the greatest sources for actually finding great startup ideas" -- Facebook started as a project [5].

### The Co-Founder Search Algorithm

For founders ready to start now, Taggar provides a concrete algorithm [5]:
1. Make a list of the people you know who would be great co-founders
2. Ask each one to coffee and propose starting a company together
3. If they say no, ask: "If you were starting a company, who would be your top 3-4 co-founders?"
4. Ask for introductions to those people
5. Repeat the process, growing the tree of potential co-founders

A common blocker: founders assume everyone they know is unavailable. "I know lots of people who would be a great potential co-founder, but they just got a job at Google and they will never leave." Taggar's advice: never assume who is available. Always make the ask [5].

## Why You Need a Technical Co-Founder

Caldwell and Seibel are direct: "Based on the thousands of companies YC has funded over the years, companies lacking a technical co-founder underperform" [8]. The argument that no-code tools, dev shops, or part-time consultants can substitute "is an inspiring story that gets clicks on LinkedIn" but does not match reality [8].

The analogy Seibel uses: "I'm going to build a rocket to the moon, but no one here knows anything about physics" [8].

Concrete reasons:

1. **Speed of iteration.** Companies like DoorDash and Airbnb "lived and died by their speed of shipping software." Using white-label software or offshore dev shops, "there's a zero percent chance those companies would have worked" [8].
2. **Engineering talent compounds.** When business people start by assessing engineers, they naturally hire worse engineers. A weak engineering foundation compounds badly over time [8].
3. **Better competitive position.** "They could raise more money. They could have nicer offices. It didn't matter. Having better engineering is the gift that gives you more shots every day" [8].

### How to Find a Technical Co-Founder

Caldwell and Seibel offer specific advice [8]:

1. **Stop disqualifying people.** Many non-technical founders want a CTO with ten years of experience managing teams of 50-100. "You're twenty-three years old with no years of experience. Why do you need that?" [8]
2. **Take a step sideways.** If you don't know the right people, go work at a startup [8].
3. **Pitch adventure, not tasks.** "Adventure isn't 'here's my idea for a social network for dogs, will you build my website?' It's 'let's build a company together'" [8].

## Do Technical Founders Need a Business Co-Founder?

The short answer: not necessarily [9]. The important question is not whether a co-founder is "technical" or "business" but whether someone on the team has the appetite to handle non-coding tasks [9].

"It's much easier for a technical person to do things that just require general intelligence than it is for a business person to learn how to code" [9]. Evidence from the most valuable companies: Google (two technical founders), Nvidia (all electrical engineers), Microsoft (two technical founders), Stripe (two technical founders) [9].

When a business co-founder genuinely helps: when selling into an industry where domain expertise and customer language matter [9]. But this is a benefit, not a requirement [9].

## Co-Founder Conflict and Relationship Debt

Seibel identifies co-founder conflict as the third biggest killer of post-seed startups [7]. He uses the concept of "relationship debt" -- analogous to technical debt: "The more and more relationship debt you get, just like technical debt, the harder and harder it is to execute. And then at some point your relationship with the co-founder falls over" [7].

### Why Co-Founder Relationships Break Down

Taggar identifies three primary causes [5]:

1. **Loss of mutual respect.** The most common cause. Co-founders often split responsibilities, and problems creep in when one feels the other is not performing. "If that respect breaks down and one co-founder feels they could do the other co-founder's job better than them, it's very hard to recover" [5].
2. **Multiple people wanting to be CEO.** The CEO title carries symbolic weight as the person trusted to make final calls. When multiple people want that role, it signals fundamental distrust [5].
3. **Mismatched work ethic expectations.** If one co-founder wants to work constantly while the other wants work-life balance, the relationship becomes strained under pressure [5].

Caldwell and Seibel expand on common mistakes in a dedicated episode [6]: equity fights, the "unequal effort" perception, failure to have hard conversations, and bringing on co-founders out of convenience rather than genuine fit.

### Preventing Breakups

**Level 3 conversations.** Set aside dedicated time, outside of any active crisis, to talk about how each co-founder feels. Pay down relationship debt regularly [7].

**Explicit roles and responsibilities.** Each person knows their domain and can be trusted to execute within it [7].

**Founder Syncs.** Recurring weekly 1-on-1 meetings between co-founders. Amy Buechler created this concept and wrote a [detailed guide](https://www.notion.so/The-Founder-Sync-54f855a12de749fa9e74e928ca7fa5a5) on running them [2].

**Don't avoid disagreements.** Taggar's primary advice: anytime co-founders disagree, have the conversation immediately. Procrastinating on hard conversations creates "death by a thousand cuts" [5].

## Protecting Technical Co-Founders

Caldwell and Seibel identify a pattern they see repeatedly in YC applications: "It's crystal clear what's going on" -- a technical person is being exploited [10].

**Equity**: Close to equal equity is recommended. The anti-pattern: a non-technical business founder has 90% and the technical person has 10% "with no good reason other than sharper elbows" [10]. Seibel's reasoning: "The vast majority of the journey is ahead of you, not behind you" [10].

**Decision-Making**: If all decisions are made in meetings the technical person is not invited to, and they are "basically used as a robot to write code" -- that is exploitation [10].

**The College Student Pattern**: The MBA who recruits a CS student to "just build the website" -- often not even offering equity -- is one of the most common exploitation patterns [10].

## Equity and CEO Decisions

Taggar recommends defaulting to a 50/50 equity split [5]. At the earliest stage, both founders will make equal contributions, and "all the work is years and years ahead of you." See [[Equity Split]] for the full treatment.

On the CEO title: practically, it does not matter at the two-person stage. The CEO title matters externally, particularly for investors who want to see a clear leader and tiebreaker [5]. A red flag: when both co-founders intensely want to be CEO. "If you think it's really important to you to be the CEO, and you both feel that way, you're not good co-founders for each other" [5].

## 10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder

YC compiled the 10 most important questions, derived from analyzing why founding teams break up [2]:

1. Why do you want to do this startup? What are your personal goals?
2. Roles, titles, and division of responsibilities. Who will be CEO?
3. How will you split equity?
4. Where will the company be based?
5. What idea will you work on? Are you willing to pivot?
6. What needs to happen for each person to go full-time?
7. Personal financial situation.
8. Working schedule.
9. Team building philosophy.
10. Conflict resolution. What happens if you decide not to work together anymore?

A [Google Doc template](https://yc.link/10-questions-cf) is available. The recommended process: each founder copies the document, writes answers independently, then shares and discusses [2].

### Checklist Before Committing

**Before deciding to work together** [2]:
- Meet in person and do a trial project
- Do reference calls on each other (transparently)
- Have your significant other meet your potential co-founder

**After deciding to work together** [2]:
- Create a system to prioritize tasks
- Set up recurring weekly Founder Syncs

## YC's Co-Founder Matching Platform

Y Combinator runs a [co-founder matching platform](https://www.ycombinator.com/cofounder-matching) where founders describe themselves and what they are looking for [1]. The platform has produced companies accepted into YC, including Sequin (YC S21, raised $5.7M) and Kiwi Biosciences (YC S21, raised $1.5M) [1].

Taggar advises that the formula that works well: find people who you have things in common with, who are the kinds of people you would have eventually met anyway. Teams where co-founders studied similar fields, are roughly the same age, and have overlapping interests work best [5].

## The Gusto Example: Long-Lasting Co-Founder Relationships

Josh Reeves, co-founder and CEO of Gusto, describes how all three Gusto co-founders have remained actively involved for 13+ years [16]. The ingredients: a shared love of technology, a desire to tackle meaningful problems, deriving joy from helping others, and "productive discontent" -- being simultaneously proud of progress and eager for the next step [16].

## Solo Founders

While YC does fund solo founders, the data is not in their favor. Only 7.9% of YC top companies were solo-founded [3]. Taggar's advice: in 90% of cases, spend all efforts trying to find a great co-founder before starting [5].

The narrow exception: if you have a very specific idea with high conviction based on domain experience AND you are an engineer who can build the first version yourself, you can start solo while searching [5]. Drew Houston of Dropbox is the canonical example [5].

## Why You Shouldn't Copy Your Heroes

Caldwell and Seibel caution against taking current advice from successful founders at face value [15]. Sam Altman's first startup was Loopt -- not a moonshot lab. Elon Musk started with Zip2 and PayPal. Peter Thiel went to Stanford and law school. "Most mere mortals need to do step one" [15].

## References

1. [Does Co-Founder Matching Work?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work) -- Catheryn Li (2021)
