2. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) -- Y Combinator (2022)
3. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) -- Tamanna Khemani, Kat Mañalac (2021)
4. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) -- [[Paul Graham]] (December 2020)
5. [How to Find the Right Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8h-how-to-find-the-right-co-founder) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (2020)
6. [Co-Founder Mistakes That Kill Companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DZ-dalton-michael-co-founder-mistakes-that-kill-companies-and-how-to-avoid-them) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
7. [The 5 Things That Kill Startups Post Seed Rounds](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8a-the-5-things-that-kill-startups-post-seed-rounds) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2020)
8. [Does Your Tech Startup Really Need a Technical Co-Founder? Yes.](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KO-does-your-tech-startup-really-need-a-technical-co-founder-yes) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
9. [Do Technical Founders Need a Business Co-Founder?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KV-do-technical-founders-need-a-business-co-founder) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
10. [How To NOT Get Screwed Over As A Software Engineer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KF-how-to-not-get-screwed-over-as-a-software-engineer) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
11. [How to Find a Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ld-how-to-find-a-co-founder) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (2024)
12. [Co-Founder Equity Mistakes to Avoid](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LP-co-founder-equity-mistakes-to-avoid) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
13. [How Do Billion Dollar Startups Start?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Li-how-do-billion-dollar-startups-start) -- Y Combinator (2024)
14. [How to Navigate Co-Founder Disputes](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/MI-how-to-navigate-co-founder-disputes) -- Y Combinator (2025)
15. [Why You Shouldn't Copy Your Tech Idols](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jw-dalton-michael-why-you-shouldn-t-copy-your-tech-idols) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)
16. [Customer Obsession Made Gusto a $9.6 Billion Company](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/MH-customer-obsession-made-gusto-a-9-6-billion-company) -- [[Harj Taggar]], Josh Reeves (2025)

title: SAFEs and Priced Rounds
body:
# SAFEs and Priced Rounds

SAFEs (Simple Agreements for Future Equity) and priced equity rounds are the two primary mechanisms for startup financing. Kirsty Nathoo, YC CFO, explains the mechanics, trade-offs, and common mistakes founders make with each instrument [1].

## SAFEs

A SAFE is not a loan. It is an agreement that converts into equity at a future financing event. YC introduced the SAFE to simplify seed-stage fundraising, and it has become the dominant instrument for early rounds [1].

Key terms:
- **Valuation cap**: The maximum valuation at which the SAFE converts. If the next round is priced above the cap, the SAFE holder converts at the cap (getting a better price). If the next round is below the cap, the SAFE holder converts at the round price.
- **Discount**: An alternative or addition to the cap. The SAFE holder gets a percentage discount on the next round price.
- **Post-money vs. pre-money**: YC's current SAFE template is post-money, meaning the cap includes the SAFE money itself. This makes dilution calculations clearer for founders [1].

Advantages of SAFEs:
- No interest rate or maturity date
- Minimal closing mechanics (sign and wire money)
- No board seat or control provisions
- Standardized documents reduce legal costs [1]

## Priced Rounds

A priced equity round sets a specific per-share price and issues new shares. This is standard for Series A and later rounds but rare at seed stage due to complexity and cost [1].

Key elements:
- Board composition is negotiated
- Investor rights (information rights, pro rata, anti-dilution)
- Vesting schedules are formalized
- Legal fees are significantly higher ($15K-$40K+ vs. minimal for SAFEs) [1]

## Common Mistakes

Nathoo identifies several mistakes founders make [1]:
- **Raising too many SAFEs at different caps**: Creates a confusing cap table when conversion happens
- **Not understanding dilution**: Post-money SAFEs make dilution predictable; pre-money SAFEs do not
- **Ignoring the pro rata**: Some SAFEs include pro rata rights, which can affect future round dynamics

## References

1. [Understanding SAFEs and Priced Equity Rounds](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6m-understanding-safes-and-priced-equity-rounds) -- Kirsty Nathoo (2019)

title: Hard Tech Startups
body:
# Hard Tech Startups

Hard tech startups -- those building physical products, aerospace technology, medical devices, energy systems, or other complex engineering -- face distinct challenges around demonstrating progress, raising capital, and navigating regulatory environments. Multiple YC founders and partners have shared lessons on how to make hard tech work within the accelerator model and beyond. The 2025 era has seen renewed interest in hard tech driven by AI's demand for energy, space infrastructure, and climate technology.

## Why Founders Matter in Hard Tech

[[Blake Scholl]], founder of [[Boom Technology]] (YC W16), argues that all the big breakthroughs in aviation came from founder-led companies. The first airplane came from bicycle entrepreneurs. The first practical airliner (DC-3) and the first jetliner (De Havilland Comet) were created by companies run by their founders. The last new commercial aircraft company was founded in 1921. The last founder retired from the industry in 1958 -- "which, coincidentally or not, was also the year we had the first jetliner" [1].

Since then, Boeing and Airbus have optimized: making existing designs more efficient and safer, but not improving human capability. Boeing's CEO publicly said their policy is "no more moonshots." Scholl identifies what founders bring that managers do not: "Founders have vision. Founders start things to make the world a better place. Founders take risks. They make bold bets" [1].

## Demonstrating Progress in Hardware

Hardware companies at YC must figure out what they can build and show at demo day. Scholl's advice: "Figure out really early on something that both is and looks like actual, substantive progress towards the product" [1]. Avoid two traps: meaningful but unappreciable progress (simulation results no one understands), and progress that is just for show (a flight simulator that looks impressive but is empty) [1].

Boom's solution: they completed the high-level aerodynamic design, 3D-printed a model of the jet engine, and physically brought to demo day one of the three actual engines that would power the first airplane. "People, of course, were like, 'OMG, jet engine.'" The key was that it was real [1].

### The Athelas Path

Tanay Tandon describes how [[Athelas]] went from a proof-of-concept at YC Hacks 2014 (a smartphone microscope using a toilet paper roll) to shipping $250 medical devices with FDA Class 2 510(k) approval [2]. The path required automated blood processing, deep learning computer vision, clinical validation (100% 5-class inter-rater agreement at 350 patients), and regulatory navigation [2].

## Selling Before Building

Scholl's experience challenges the assumption that hard tech companies cannot sell before the product exists. YC's advice: "Get your ass out of stealth mode" and "Go sell some airplanes" [1]. Scholl pursued Richard Branson through two channels simultaneously, crashed a Virgin Galactic event for a breakfast meeting, and used demo day as a deadline. The email from Virgin arrived 24 hours before Boom's demo day slot: "You can announce the following -- We'll take the first 10 airplanes." Boom set a record with $5 billion in LOIs at demo day [1].

## Fusion Energy

[[Gustaf Alströmer]] details the state of fusion energy, which has gained urgency because of AI's massive electricity demands [4]. Helion Energy is taking a unique approach: a linear, pulsed reactor using deuterium and helium-3 fuel (avoiding tritium's radioactivity), with direct energy recovery that can reach 90%+ conversion efficiency. Helion has a partnership to power a Microsoft data center by 2028 [4].

Helion's innovation is organizational as much as technical: they build, test, and design in parallel. "While operating one machine, they were already building the next and prototyping the factories needed to mass-produce future reactors" [4]. This hardware-first, iterate-fast approach helped Helion move from early prototypes to its seventh-generation Polaris system in under a decade [4].

## Reusable Rockets

The reusable rocket startup ecosystem continues to grow, building on SpaceX's demonstration that reusability transforms launch economics [5]. New entrants are tackling specific niches -- small satellite launches, orbital debris removal, and space-based AI infrastructure [5] [6].

The pattern across these companies mirrors the broader hard tech playbook: demonstrate real hardware progress early, sell before the product is complete (LOIs and pre-orders), and use demo day deadlines as forcing functions.

## Carbon Capture

Mobile carbon capture devices represent a new category of climate technology startup [7]. The challenge parallels other hard tech domains: the physics works, but commercializing at scale requires solving engineering, manufacturing, and unit economics problems simultaneously.

## The Motivation Advantage of Big Ideas

Scholl argues that bigger ideas can actually be easier because they attract better talent. "The team we have around us at Boom is phenomenal, and we wouldn't have it if we were building an app or a small airplane, even. Everyone looks at what we're building, and they're like, 'I can tell my grandkids about this'" [1].

His advice for choosing what to work on: "Find something in the world that you desperately want to exist." All startups go through hell; the question is whether it is worth it [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) -- [[Blake Scholl]] (n.d.)
2. [On Starting a Company from a Hackathon Project](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project) -- Tanay Tandon (January 2017)
3. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (n.d.)
4. [Fusion Energy Is Tech's Next Big Unlock](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XdigD0LOXx0) -- [[Gustaf Alströmer]] (2025)
5. [Inside the Startup Building Reusable Rockets](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
6. [Inside the Startup Launching AI Into Space](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
7. [The World's First Commercial Mobile Carbon Capture Device](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: Series A Fundraising
body:
# Series A Fundraising

The Series A is the first major priced equity round for most startups. It involves two distinct challenges: building and delivering a compelling pitch, and navigating the legal and organizational complexity of closing. This article covers both, drawing on Janelle Tam's guide to Series A pitches and [[Aaron Harris]] and Jason Kwon's diligence checklist.

## The Series A Pitch

Janelle Tam, YC's Series A Program Manager, compiled feedback from hundreds of hours working 1:1 with founders on their Series A pitches and decks. Every piece of advice in her guide was given to at least one YC founder internally [2].

### Purpose and Principles

The purpose is to show investors why they should invest. The pitch provides a clear and concise overview of the business already built, then paints a picture of where it could go and why raising money will help get there [2].

Six principles underlie this:

1. **It is an investor pitch, not a customer pitch.** Investors may not have encountered the problem being solved. Good pitches explain the problem and its impact, show how the product solves it, and demonstrate why it is a good business [2].
2. **Clarity is paramount.** Investor attention is a limited resource. Make the pitch as simple and straightforward as possible. The best pitchers use plain English, avoiding jargon. Where jargon is necessary, they define each term clearly on first use [2].
3. **Give an overview, not a download.** The goal is to tantalize investors with enough information to keep them interested, leaving room for questions. A pitch should be 15-20 minutes, setting up a 40-45 minute discussion. If investors are not asking questions, the slides are probably too complicated [2].
4. **Start with what you have built.** Compared to the seed round, it is harder to raise a Series A on promise alone. Investors want to see results [2].
5. **Paint a picture of where it is going.** Once you establish credibility through what you have already built, show how it could grow into a massive company [2].
6. **Show that money is the obstacle.** The deck should make clear that capital -- not incompetence, poor product quality, or lack of [[Product-Market Fit]] -- is what stands between the company and the next level [2].

### The Starting Exercise

Tam describes a structured process for building the pitch [2]:

1. Write the narrative as 10-15 bullet points -- the "fundraising vertebrae"
2. Under each bullet, list supporting data points
3. Build the deck around this list: each bullet is one slide. If more than one point per slide, the slide is too complicated. The deck should be 10-15 slides plus appendix
4. Write out every question investors could ask, especially about metrics, and prepare clear, credible, data-backed answers

### Deck Structure

**Title (1 slide):** Company name or logo and a one-sentence description. The one-liner must be concrete ("We build and operate robot greenhouses" not "Reinventing agriculture"), understandable to a layperson, and specific to what the company does [2].

![Title slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/cd9e75fb29a3.png)

**Traction Teaser (1 slide):** Placed early to grab attention and convince the investor the presentation is worth listening to [2].

![Traction teaser example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/332f6e6e9236.png)

**Problem (1 slide):** The problem must match the solution. Present from the customer's perspective. Show, don't tell -- "Medical billing is broken" is vague; "Here's how medical billing works today: patients need to do X steps, which takes Y months and results in Z% claims approved" is concrete [2].

![Problem slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/085dc4c55fa0.png)

**Solution (1 slide):** Side-by-side comparison with status quo. Quantify impact with numbers. Focus on what exists now, not future plans. Show how the customer uses it, not feature lists [2].

![Solution slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/370a5e22f179.png)

**Traction In-Depth (a few slides):** Numbers should tell a story, starting with the most important metric -- usually revenue. For enterprise: revenue growth, sales cycle, ACV, salesperson efficiency, churn. For consumer: revenue plus growth rate, organic vs. paid acquisition, CAC/LTV, engagement and retention. For hardtech/biotech: technology efficacy data, patents filed, LOIs, specific milestones [2].

Show at least 4-6 months of trend data. YC's [benchmarks](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/1k-benchmarks) assume Series A fundraising 18-24 months after seed [2].

Three traps in presenting numbers: information overload (identify "hero facts"), too few numbers (at Series A you need data), unclear numbers (cumulative numbers almost always hide weak monthly data) [2].

**Market (1 slide):** Bottoms-up calculation: number of prospective customers x value of each customer. Use real numbers from the current business, not top-down report figures [2].

![Market slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/24ae1bce2667.png)

**Competition (1 slide):** Show why the company is 10x better than alternatives. Convince the investor the moat is defensible [2].

![Competition slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/2eff98b228dc.png)

**Vision (1 slide):** Show how the company becomes a $10B company. "Here's where you can dream and talk about all of the hypotheticals you've judiciously kept from the first part of your deck" [2].

![Vision slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/0831048590eb.png)

**Team (1 slide):** If the team is a comparative advantage, place as the second or third slide. Otherwise, show who the founders are, why they are the right people, and quality of team recruited. Signaling risk: including a Series A fund among existing investors prompts the question of whether they are leading the round [2].

![Team slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/169ca6b8734c.png)

**Use of Funds (1 slide):** The climax. Cover how much is being raised, what it will fund, and where the company will be in 18-24 months [2].

![Use of Funds slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/1c1028d09c46.png)

**Appendix:** Ammunition for the Q&A. Include slides answering anticipated investor questions, financial projections, and detailed use of funds [2].

### Design and Practice

Optimize for clarity, not beauty. "Keeping it as simple and bare-bones as possible" is usually best [2].

Schedule 1-hour practice sessions: 20 minutes pitch, 20 minutes conversation, 20 minutes feedback. A quick test for individual slides: flash it to someone who has never seen it and ask what point it makes [2].

For video pitches: turn on video with good lighting and audio, one person should pitch, record and review yourself, keep a bullet-point script visible but avoid reading it [2].

### Iterating During Fundraise

Set aside 1-2 hours daily to review each meeting. After every pitch, ask: What excited investors most? Where did they zone out? What objections arose? [2]

## The Closing Process

### The Closing Timeline Problem

Founders generally underestimate how long closing a Series A takes. [[Aaron Harris]] and Jason Kwon (General Counsel of YC Continuity) observe that closing an A "can take more than a month, much of which is often spent tracking down documents for lawyers" [1]. Having all diligence materials organized in a single data room before signing a term sheet can cut as much as a week off the closing process [1].

### The Series A Diligence Checklist

Kwon assembled this checklist based on involvement in hundreds of financings with "nearly every law firm and VC fund in the valley" [1]. Everything listed below should be assembled in a data room before signing a term sheet.

**Corporate Records and Charter Documents:** All minutes of directors' and stockholders' meetings, written consents, Certificate of Incorporation, Certificates of Designation, Rights, Bylaws, subsidiary documentation, and organizational charts [1].

**Business Plan and Financials:** Current business plan, financial projections, and most recent financial statements [1].

**Intellectual Property:** List of all trademarks, patents, copyrights, and domain names with filing/registration documentation. Documentation of IP assignments to the company [1].

**Securities and Cap Table:** List of stockholders with issuance dates and prices. List of option holders with grant dates and exercise prices. Agreements relating to outstanding options, warrants, conversion rights, preemptive rights. Documents evidencing registration rights or shareholder agreements. Vesting schedules including acceleration provisions. Evidence of qualification under Rule 701 and state blue sky laws [1].

**Material Agreements:** Standard terms of service, agreements exceeding $25,000, property leases, debt documents, IP licenses, insurance policies, judgments, joint venture agreements, consulting contracts, and list of officers and directors [1].

**Disputes and Litigation:** Correspondence relating to pending or threatened actions, IP infringement allegations, labor disputes [1].

**Employee Information:** Complete employee and consultant list with title, salary, bonus, commission plan, classification, and state of residence. Standard offer letter template. Employment agreements with severance or acceleration provisions. Equity incentive plans. Employee handbook. Loans to employees. For foreign employees, country-by-country benefits listing [1].

### The Equity Grant Timing Trap

Harris and Kwon flag a critical timing issue [1]: once a term sheet is signed, most practitioners believe the company can no longer use its existing 409A valuation for option grants because a "material event" has occurred that implies a higher valuation.

The consequences cascade: team members hired earlier who have not yet received grants will get options at a higher strike price, reducing their upside, creating a morale issue at the worst possible time [1].

The solution is preventive: "make sure there isn't a significant backlog of equity grants while you are in the middle of your fundraise process" [1]. Clear all pending or promised equity grants before beginning the Series A process.

## References

1. [Series A Diligence Checklist](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3h-series-a-diligence-checklist) -- [[Aaron Harris]], Jason Kwon (n.d.)
2. [How to Build a Great Series A Pitch and Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck) -- Janelle Tam (n.d.)

title: Startup Legal Mechanics
body:
# Startup Legal Mechanics

The legal setup of a startup is one of the first operational decisions founders must make. Kirsty Nathoo, YC CFO, walks through the standard setup and common mistakes in a Startup School lecture [1].

## Standard Setup: Delaware C-Corp

YC recommends incorporating as a Delaware C-Corp. The reasons [1]:
- Delaware corporate law is the most developed and predictable in the United States
- Most VC documents are drafted for Delaware C-Corps
- Converting from an LLC or other structure later is expensive and creates tax complications

## Founder Stock and 83(b) Elections

When founders receive stock, it is typically subject to vesting (4-year vesting with a 1-year cliff is standard). The IRS treats vesting stock as income, which creates a tax problem: as the stock becomes worth more, founders owe increasing taxes on each vesting tranche [1].

The 83(b) election solves this. By filing an 83(b) election within 30 days of receiving stock, founders pay taxes on the stock's value at the time of grant (usually pennies per share) rather than at the time of vesting (potentially dollars per share). Missing the 83(b) deadline is one of the most expensive mistakes a founder can make [1].

## IP Assignment

All founders must assign their intellectual property to the company. Nathoo warns that failing to do this cleanly creates problems during due diligence for fundraising and acquisitions [1].

## References

1. [Startup Legal Mechanics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7R-startup-legal-mechanics) -- Kirsty Nathoo (2020)

title: Startups for Students
body:
# Startups for Students

When Y Combinator launched in 2005, starting a startup was an unusual career path for recent graduates. It was hard to be taken seriously by investors without an MBA or years of business experience. TechCrunch published about 40 company launches per month in 2005, and YC received only a couple hundred applications for its first batches [1]. By 2021, YC received tens of thousands of applications per year, and on any given day Product Hunt shows dozens of new company launches [1].

## Why Start a Startup

### The Three Self-Assessment Questions

[[Michael Seibel]] recommends asking three questions to determine if a startup is right for you [1]:

1. **Do I like being the underdog?**
2. **Do I seek hard challenges most people shy away from?**
3. **Do I thrive taking personal responsibility for success or failure?**

Seibel writes: "There is a certain type of person who only works at their peak capacity when there is no predictable path to follow. The odds of success are low, and they have to take personal responsibility for failure" [1]. If you answer yes three times, a startup may be right for you.

### Find a Problem You Are Obsessed With Solving

Some of the most successful YC companies formed when founders confronted real-world issues [1]:

- **Airbnb** (2007): Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia were having trouble paying rent, so they bought air mattresses and charged people $80/night during a San Francisco convention
- **Stripe** (2010): Patrick and John Collison started a side project after Patrick became frustrated with how hard it was to accept financial payments online
- **Gusto**: Founded because the founders were frustrated by payroll inefficiencies for small businesses

The advice: look at your own life, identify a problem, and build a solution [1].

## Why Not to Start a Startup

### Legitimate Reasons

- **You are still in school.** Startups demand enormous time and energy. Grades and social life will suffer. Aaron Feurer, co-founder of Panorama Education, spent over 40 hours a week building the company as a Yale undergraduate and had amassed hundreds of thousands of dollars in revenue by graduation — but this is "very difficult to achieve and highly uncommon" [1].
- **It is difficult.** Nine out of 10 startups fail in their first year. Building a company is mentally and emotionally taxing [1].
- **You have no co-founder.** Only 7.9% of YC's top companies were solo-founded. See [[Co-Founders]] [1].
- **You are not ready for the commitment.** Exits and IPOs typically take years — prepare for up to a decade of work [1].
- **You need structure.** Large corporations provide managers as mentors and collaborative teams [1].
- **You hate uncertainty.** There is no clear linear path to success at a startup [1].

### Bad Reasons Not to Start

- **"I don't have an idea."** YC puts more emphasis on the team than the idea. Reddit, Brex, and Segment were all accepted as completely different concepts before pivoting [1].
- **"My parents want me to get a real job."** YC notes that *The Social Network* (2012) caused a massive spike in applications and may have been the cultural inflection point where parents realized startups could be a viable career. Point skeptical parents to companies founded by people in their 20s: Brex, Stripe, Embark, Scale AI — all now worth billions [1].
- **"I have student loans."** Student debt makes a well-paying corporate job more enticing, but startup experience is a force multiplier for earning potential even if the company fails [1].

## What to Do in College

### Skills to Develop

1. **Coding.** Learn to code and befriend technical people. You cannot build a software company without software [1].
2. **Communication.** Learn to convey ideas clearly and simply — even enormously complex ones should be explainable to grandparents [1].
3. **People skills.** Build your network, meet potential [[Co-Founders|co-founders]], and develop deep understanding of people's needs. Talking to users is a critical founder skill [1].

### How to Spend Time

College is one of the best places to meet a potential co-founder. Work on projects and extracurricular activities with classmates. Experts in technology, business, and science are usually happy to help college students [1].

YC tells most students to finish their degree. The exception: "if the only thing you can do is think about your startup and you're spending all your free time working on it, it might be worth considering dropping out" [1].

### Non-Technical Founders

YC has funded startups without a technical co-founder. Roughly 10% of companies in each batch are founded by non-technical founders. But among YC's top companies, only three were founded by non-technical founders: Flexport, Snapdocs, and UpKeep. YC strongly recommends finding a technical co-founder for software companies. Even non-technical founders should learn the basics of coding [1].

## Working at a Startup Instead

For those not ready to found, working at a startup — especially a YC startup — provides experience building products, talking to customers directly, and transparent views of the business [1].

### Choosing the Right Company

- Find a startup solving a problem interesting to you
- For internships, a post-Series A company is recommended for more structure and mentorship
- For self-directed learners, a seed-stage company could work

### Questions to Ask Founders

- What will I work on during this internship? (Bonus if the project ships)
- Will I get to see how the business is doing? How much interaction with customers?
- What will I learn from the people I am working with?
- For full-time roles: ask about runway and burn rate [1]

## Getting First Users as a Student

1. **College campus** — easy access to a large pool of potential customers likely to try something new [1]
2. **Friends and family** — helps determine ideal demographic [1]
3. **Charge as soon as possible** — even small amounts help validate how much value you are creating [1]

## Applying to YC

YC accepts applications year-round. No specific school, degree, or business plan is required. The application is free. About half of the Summer 2021 batch was internationally based [1].

What YC looks for [1]:
- **Technical ability** — teams that can build, usually meaning a technical founder
- **A drive to build** — about 60% of funded companies do not have a launched product, but founders show inclination for action and fast progress
- **Persistence and resilience** — about 50% of funded companies have applied more than once; showing progress between applications signals seriousness

## References

1. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) — Tamanna Khemani, Kat Mañalac (2021)

title: Pitch Deck Design
body:
# Pitch Deck Design

[[Kevin Hale]], YC partner, teaches a presentation to YC startups before they create their Demo Day slide decks [1]. His core argument is that founders already have everything they need to create a great presentation. The challenge is not finding things to say but communicating them so investors can understand and remember.

## The Fundamental Constraint

There are probably 100 reasons why a company is great, but people can only remember a few after a short presentation. YC helps each startup identify the five to seven most important ideas investors should know. On Demo Day, with over 100 companies presenting, investors will be lucky to remember even one or two points from any given company [1].

The standard is not creativity or flair. If a founder just communicates their points clearly, they will do better than 99% of startups. Before anyone can remember, they have to understand [1].

## The Three Principles: Legible, Simple, Obvious

Hale's framework for making ideas easy to understand is the [[Legible Simple Obvious]] framework [1]:

![Legible, Simple, Obvious](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/35131887eed3.png)

- **Legible**: Even old people sitting in the back row with bad eyesight can read it.
- **Simple**: Each slide expresses one idea.
- **Obvious**: Can be understood at a glance.

The inverse produces failure: illegible, complicated, and subtle slides [1].

![Illegible, Complicated, Subtle](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/7f82892467cb.png)

### Legibility

At Demo Day, the room fills with over 500 people. They cannot all sit in the front row, and many are older with imperfect eyesight. Legible slides use large type, bold text, a simple font, and good contrast from the background. Text at the top of the slide is easier to read from the back [1].

![Legibility principles](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/fb3a8a6a0309.png)

Hale shows an evolution of the same slide through three versions. The first has text with poor contrast and bad positioning:

![Slide with poor contrast](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/6e447d5bfc3f.png)

The second improves but is still constrained by a screenshot:

![Improved but constrained](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/fa95fa8743e4.png)

The third strips away the screenshot and makes the text dominant and readable from anywhere in the room [1]:

![Final legible version](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/bf6463530e43.png)

He uses an example from Shred Video (YC company): their Prototype Day slide had text that was "too small and too skinny," with the explanation of what they actually do hidden "in light gray at the bottom of the slide." Their technology -- software that automatically edits hours of raw video footage into professional-looking edits in seconds -- was buried:

![Shred Video Prototype Day slide](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/ac4165ec4f6c.png)

By Demo Day, the description was simplified and the text was "large and in charge" [1].

### Simplicity

The word "simple" shares an origin with "complex." The root "plex" means braid, twist, or fold. Simple ideas are "one fold" -- one idea. A simple slide expresses one idea. Crowding a slide with multiple ideas makes it complex [1].

![Simple vs Complex etymology](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/c05df6011960.png)

![Plex means braid, twist, fold](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/8aaef1a06fa4.png)

Since there are only five to seven key ideas, there should be only about five to seven slides:

![5-7 slides](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/25c73d797368.png)

Hale shows a slide from Afrostream (YC company) that tried to pack all the nuances of their business onto one slide, resulting in too much text that no one could easily remember:

![Afrostream early version](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/f43a60dfdede.png)

Their Demo Day version was "short, sweet, and easy to recollect" [1]:

![Afrostream Demo Day version](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/3394a3d6fc83.jpg)

### Obviousness

Obvious slides can be understood at a glance. The test: show it to a stranger and ask them what it means. If they don't immediately say your idea, you lose [1].

![Obvious slides test](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/375759af9166.png)

Speed of understanding matters because Demo Day presentations are 2 minutes and 30 seconds, and investors are easily distracted. "If they don't get your point right away, they will check their email" [1].

To make slides obvious, Hale advises making them legible and simple first, then making the idea explicit. He shows a growth graph without labels -- the viewer can guess it represents growth but it is not immediately clear:

![Growth graph without labels](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/9531057ae80a.png)

Adding axis labels helps:

![Growth graph with labels](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/93712f10cafe.png)

But adding a direct caption like "10x growth in 6 months" makes it unmistakable. "It's like I put CliffsNotes right on the slide" [1]:

![Growth graph with caption](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/0f3b663f4851.png)

Another variation from a YC startup, where the caption eliminates the need to study the graph:

![Graph with explanatory caption](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/8d68036b1cb5.png)

Hale references [Hick's Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hick%27s_law): each piece of information added to a problem increases the time to make a decision logarithmically. A Dropbox diagram illustrates excessive information:

![Dropbox diagram](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/e297b4cf14ba.png)

Each piece of information in the diagram creates comprehension overhead:

![Information overload annotated](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/d2bf3d3fa7dd.png)

Diagrams are "little mazes for ideas" that make the path to understanding too long [1].

**Distractions to avoid** [1]:
- Too much text
- Excessive explanations and caveats
- Excessive branding per slide
- Photos with no titles or captions
- Animations, transitions
- Memes
- Subtle humor, accidental humor

"Investors invest in teams not slides. Your slides should make your ideas more clear. Don't let your slides distract investors from what you're saying out loud" [1].

![Investors invest in teams not slides](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/9da12d19d674.png)

### The Exception: Deliberate Complexity

One exception exists: when the point is to show complexity or overwhelm the audience, usually to illustrate how hard a problem is. Hale uses Magic (YC company) as an example. Magic's software abstracts thousands of on-demand services into a single interface:

![Magic single interface](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/99c0944f2327.png)

Their slide showed a grid of every on-demand service they could find across every industry, market, and vertical. Each logo represented a separate account a user would need, illustrating the discovery and usability problem:

![Magic grid of services](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/0c0085e4610a.png)

"It's overwhelming for a reason and that's usually when it's okay to bend the rules" [1].

## On Screenshots

Hale states he "usually hates screenshots in Demo Day presentations." Screenshots are almost always illegible (interface text is too small), complex (interfaces do multiple things), and non-obvious (take longer than a glance to understand). "They break all 3 rules" [1].

A Google image search for "screenshots" demonstrates the problem:

![Screenshots search](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/0124f324ff52.png)

Instead of showing what a product does through screenshots, Hale recommends showing the most simplified version. A bulleted list of steps is his favorite technique. He cites SparkGift's slide explaining their product through three simple steps as "far better at explaining the ease of use" than any workflow from the actual interface:

![SparkGift simplified steps](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/5bfdee5c72af.png)

Meadow's two-point explanation of their product was "far better than any combination of buttons, links and chrome they could have put up there":

![Meadow simplified explanation](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/38414710dbcc.png)

The same caution applies to screencasts and videos [1].

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) — [[Kevin Hale]] (December 2015)

title: Product Development
body:
# Product Development

[[Michael Seibel]] describes a product development process that he developed at Socialcam (previously Justin.tv) after years of running a broken process [1]. The broken version had meandering product meetings where decisions were not written down, specs were loose enough that team members had different ideas about what was being built, the team always wanted to build fully-formed products instead of MVPs, analytics were rarely specified for new features, development cycles ran months, and product decisions were made exclusively by founders in a non-transparent process [1].

This guide assumes the company has already shipped an MVP and is figuring out what to do next, which is where most startups spend most of their time [1].

## Define the Development Cycle

The cycle length should be dictated by the product. At Socialcam, building for iOS, the team settled on a two-week cycle, which allowed thorough testing before App Store releases. Web apps can use shorter cycles; hardware might need longer ones. The key constraint is that teammates must stay excited and still feel like they can brainstorm new ideas [1].

## The Product Meeting

Seibel's team ran one and only one recurring team meeting: the product meeting, held on the first day of each development cycle. These meetings sometimes lasted five hours [1].

Every product meeting focused on exactly one of three goals [1]:

1. Increasing content creation
2. Increasing new users
3. Increasing retention

Whichever goal was chosen became the focus of the meeting and the next two weeks. The product lead's role was to protect and improve the development cycle and moderate the meeting to ensure all team members felt comfortable contributing. "Oftentimes just getting the opportunity to voice your idea and having it written on the board -- even if it isn't built -- massively increases buy-in of the process" [1].

## Organized and Inclusive Brainstorm

During brainstorming, ideas were written on the whiteboard in three categories: new features/feature iterations, maintenance, and A/B tests. Everyone was expected to contribute. Debates or putting down other people's ideas was not permitted. This was the time for free contribution without fear of judgment [1].

Each brainstormed item was graded by the engineer in the meeting with the most experience in that specific area [1]:

- **Easy**: Several can be done in a day
- **Medium**: Half a day for one person
- **Hard**: Most of the development cycle

No item could be so hard it would spill into the next cycle. If it was, the team broke it into smaller chunks. This grading system had a critical side effect: it helped non-technical people understand which of their ideas were easy to build and which were hard. "With this realization they often got better at thinking up easier and easier MVPs of their ideas. These easy ideas would then get built and, if they worked, would be iterated upon" [1].

## Building Consensus

The team picked what to work on through consensus, starting with hard ideas (easy to reach consensus because only one could fit), then medium, then easy. Consensus was not difficult because everyone had contributed their own ideas, there was a clear goal, and difficulty estimates were objective. "This process allowed you to grade the quality of your own idea and didn't allow personalities to bully their pet ideas through" [1].

## Clear Spec and Measurements

After consensus, each item was specced in detail, assigned to team members, and paired with the specific stats needed to measure effectiveness. The team would never release a feature without releasing the analytics for that feature and understanding what specific measurable result they wanted to create. Need-to-haves were separated from nice-to-haves; if time ran short, nice-to-haves were dropped [1].

After speccing, the team photographed the whiteboard and erased it. There was no product roadmap outside of these two weeks. Every product meeting started from scratch with a new goal, new analytics data from the last two weeks, and often new insights from monthly in-person user testing [1].

## Working During the Cycle

After the first Monday, work was quiet. The product lead handled business and operations tasks, then dug through Mixpanel looking for product insights or potential bugs, and ran monthly user testing sessions. Engineers and the designer worked quietly on well-scoped, well-specced projects [1].

During the last three days of every cycle, the entire team stopped building and tested. They maintained a testing list in Excel with manual tests for all basic functionality, adding tests for new features each cycle and running all tests twice. Everyone tested, and competitions for fastest testing and most bugs found helped share the burden of an inherently tedious task [1].

## Results

Socialcam did not achieve the team's dream of being the "Instagram for Video" (Seibel notes that what they should have built looks closer to Snapchat). But the process allowed extremely fast iteration: video filters, borders, titles, soundtracks, feed optimizations, multiple visual redesigns, user profiles, recommended channels, front/back camera switching, and more. Growth experiments produced 16 million downloads in about three months and over 100 million people watching video on their website during the same period. The work was done "quickly, efficiently, without major arguments, issues with founder commitment, or really any team problems at all" [1].

## References

1. [Guide to Product Development](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4e-guide-to-product-development) — [[Michael Seibel]] (August 2016)

title: Growth
body:
# Growth

Growth is the defining characteristic of a startup. [[Paul Graham]] argues that a startup is not merely a newly founded company or one that works on technology -- it is a company designed to grow fast [1]. Everything else associated with startups -- venture funding, exits, technology focus -- follows from this single property. [[Sam Altman]] adds a critical caveat: growth is not step one. Founders who focus on growth before building something people love are unlikely to succeed [2].

## Growth as the Defining Property of a Startup

Graham draws a sharp distinction between startups and ordinary businesses. A barbershop is not a startup. A search engine is. The difference is not about technology but about growth potential. For a company to grow fast, it must (a) make something lots of people want and (b) reach and serve all those people [1]. Most businesses are constrained in one or both dimensions. Startups are the rare companies that are not.

This distinction matters because it shapes founder behavior. "Startup is a pole, not a threshold," Graham writes. Starting one is a declaration of ambition -- a commitment to search for one of the rare ideas that generates rapid growth [1].

The growth of a successful startup follows an S-curve with three phases: an initial period of slow or no growth while the startup figures out what it is doing; a period of rapid growth as it finds [[Product-Market Fit]] and reaches its audience; and an eventual slowdown as internal limits and market saturation set in [1]. The second phase -- the ascent -- is what defines the startup. Its length and slope determine how big the company becomes.

He sees startup founding as analogous to scientific research: "A startup founder is in effect an economic research scientist. Most don't discover anything that remarkable, but some discover relativity" [1].

## Growth Rate as a Compass

Graham advocates using a target growth rate as a decision-making compass. At YC, growth is measured weekly, partly because the batch is short and partly because early-stage startups need frequent feedback [1].

Benchmarks during YC:
- **5-7% per week**: a good growth rate
- **10% per week**: exceptionally well
- **1% per week**: a sign you have not yet figured out what you are doing [1]

The best metric to measure growth is revenue. For startups not yet charging, active users serve as a reasonable proxy because revenues will likely be a constant multiple of active users [1].

The compounding effect of small differences in weekly growth rate is dramatic:

![Growth rate compounding](../raw/posts/images/8s-startup-growth/03a34df22371.png)

| Weekly Growth Rate | Annual Multiple | 4-Year Outcome (from $1K/mo) |
|---|---|---|
| 1% | 1.7x | $7,900/month |
| 5% | 12.6x | $25 million/month |

Graham notes: "Our ancestors must rarely have encountered cases of exponential growth, because our intuitions are no guide here" [1].

Setting a weekly growth target converts the bewildering problem of starting a startup into an optimization problem. "Anything that gets you the growth you need is ipso facto right," Graham writes -- should you attend a conference, hire a programmer, focus on marketing, court a big customer? Whatever hits the number [1]. This narrow focus works because founders' intuitions about which hill to climb are usually better than they realize, and the maxima in the space of startup ideas are not spiky and isolated -- most fairly good ideas are adjacent to even better ones [1].

## The Power of Compound Growth

Founders often dismiss manual user acquisition because the absolute numbers seem so small. Graham counters with simple math [5]: if you have 100 users and grow 10% a week, after a year you have 14,000 users. After two years, 2 million. The mistake founders make is to underestimate the power of compound growth [5].

Patrick Collison described the transition at Stripe: "At some point, there was a very noticeable change in how Stripe felt. It tipped from being this boulder we had to push to being a train car that in fact had its own momentum" [5].

## Do Not Grow Before the Product Is Loved

Altman is explicit: focusing on growth before you have a product people love leads to the "leaky bucket" problem. Users come in the door but do not stay and likely will not return [2].

Facebook had a product people talked about obsessively from the start. Airbnb slogged for 1,000 days before discovering how to make their product loved. Once they had that, it grew exponentially by word of mouth [2].

When the product is loved, users become "a free marketing and sales force." Word-of-mouth growth is powerful even for enterprise and hard tech companies -- the sales process becomes easier when word has spread that what you are making is great [2].

The danger of growing on a mediocre product is that reliance on inorganic means (ads, marketing, PR) becomes unsustainable. The larger the company grows on a weak foundation, the harder it becomes to course correct. "Once your company is a big moving battleship, it becomes really hard to be nimble" [2].

Altman's test for readiness: "When they actively recommend your product to friends, you're in the right place." Net Promoter Score can be a useful measurement [2].

## Growth vs. Conversion

The industry term for turning casual visitors into users is "conversion" [4]. Graham frames it simply: either your site is catching on or it isn't, and you must know which. "If you have decent growth, you'll win in the end, no matter how obscure you are now. And if you don't, you need to fix something" [4].

The metaphor: users are a fickle wind, but more powerful than any other. "If they take you up, no competitor can keep you down" [4]. To catch this wind, do two things: (1) explain, as concisely as possible, what your site is about -- a startup should be able to explain in one or two sentences exactly what it does [4]; (2) give people everything you have, right away -- put the impressive stuff on the front page because that is the only one most visitors will see [4].

## Release Early, Keep Pumping

Graham's most repeated advice to startups: get a version 1 out fast, then improve it based on users' reactions [4]. "By 'release early' I don't mean you should release something full of bugs, but that you should release something minimal. Users hate bugs, but they don't seem to mind a minimal version 1, if there's more coming soon" [4].

Continuous improvement is itself a form of marketing. "Users love a site that's constantly improving. In fact, users expect a site to improve" [4]. If you improve in response to user comments, you generate fanatical loyalty because customers are used to companies ignoring them [4].

The test for whether your product is finished: "If your product seems finished, there are two possible explanations: (a) it is finished, or (b) you lack imagination. Experience suggests (b) is a thousand times more likely" [4].

## Growth vs. Spending: A False Dichotomy

One of the most dangerous misconceptions is that growing fast and operating cheaply are opposed [6]. Graham argues there is "surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [6].

When a startup grows fast, it is usually because the product hits a nerve -- hitting some big need straight on. When a startup spends a lot, it is usually because the product is expensive to develop or sell, or simply because they are wasteful [6]. These are independent variables.

The implication: if growth is slow, hiring more people is almost never the answer. The real problem is usually that the product is not appealing enough [6]. The right approach is to seek growth "by [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]], or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can" [6].

## Premature Scaling

Paul Buchheit warns about premature scaling: founders take a small business that is not really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers [6]. This is similar to overhiring -- it makes the business much harder to fix once it is big, and they are bleeding cash fast [6].

The common death pattern: a startup makes something moderately appealing, raises easily because the founders seem smart, then hires aggressively to try to boost growth. Growth never comes because the product is only moderately appealing. With high expenses and slow growth, they become unappealing to investors for the next round. The company dies [6].

## The Contained Fire

The [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] essay introduces the "contained fire" strategy: focus on a deliberately narrow market first, get it really hot, then expand [5]. Facebook started with only Harvard students. Microsoft started with only Altair owners. The narrowness is not the goal -- depth is. But in practice narrowness comes as a byproduct of optimizing for depth and speed [5].

Any startup that could be described as a marketplace usually has to start in a subset of the market [5]. Among companies, the best early adopters are usually other startups -- they are more open to new things and grow fast, pulling you with them [5].

## Big Launches Don't Work

Graham explicitly warns against relying on a "Big Launch" for growth [5]. There may even be an inverse correlation between launch magnitude and success -- the only launches he remembers are famous flops like the Segway and Google Wave [5].

All you need from a launch is some initial core of users. How well you are doing a few months later depends more on how happy you made those users than how many there were [5]. Partnerships also usually fail as growth strategies: "Six months later they're all saying the same thing: that was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it" [5].

## Building a Growth Team

[[Anu Hariharan]], with contributions from Gustaf Alströmer, synthesized advice from 25 growth experts at companies including Facebook, Airbnb, Uber, Stitch Fix, Square, Slack, and Instagram [3].

### When to Invest

The consensus is clear: do not build a growth team before you have proven Retention. Investing in growth before nailing retention wastes money and jeopardizes the company's future [3]. Stitch Fix, for example, hired a retention-focused PM to run experiments improving retention before investing in new customer acquisition [3].

### Retention Benchmarks

To determine if retention is good enough to justify growth investment, three checks apply:

![Retention - Cohort Analysis](../raw/posts/images/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team/f39d6a8e86db.png)

1. **Stable long-term retention**: the retention curve should flatten and run parallel to the x-axis after an initial dip
2. **In line with vertical benchmarks**: stable 10% long-term retention is poor for a social network
3. **Newer cohorts perform better**: improving cohorts signal product improvement [3]

Retention benchmarks by vertical:

| Vertical | Period | Long-Term Target | Median |
|---|---|---|---|
| Social Network | Monthly (Month 12) | 45-65% | 55% |
| On-Demand | Monthly (Month 12) | 20-30% | 22% |
| Travel | Annual (Year 2) | 20-35% | 29% |
| E-commerce | Monthly (Month 12) | 10-25% | 16% |
| Subscription | Monthly (Month 12) | 25-35% | 33% |

### The Year 1 Growth Team

The initial team composition: 1 growth-focused PM + 2-3 growth engineers + 1-2 growth data scientists [3].

Timing: most companies made their first growth hire when they had about 15 engineers on the product team. The growth PM is typically the 3rd or 4th PM. The most common mistake CEOs make is waiting too long before hiring a growth-focused PM [3].

100% of growth experts said the first few hires are "magnets" for scaling the team. Eric Colson (former VP of data science at Netflix) was one of Stitch Fix's early hires and attracted accomplished data scientists to the company [3].

### Setting Goals and Metrics

The growth team's first priority is setting an **absolute goal** -- not a percentage change. For example, "achieve 5M first-time room nights this year" rather than "improve conversion by 10%" [3]. Casey Winters (former Growth Product Lead at Pinterest) wrote extensively about this approach [3].

The absolute goal breaks into sub-goals. Facebook's growth accounting equation: Monthly Active Users = New MAUs + Retained MAUs + Resurrected MAUs. Airbnb's: Room Nights = Room nights from new users + Room nights from existing users [3]. Jonathan Hsu (Partner at Social Capital, former Facebook data scientist) published this [growth accounting equation](https://medium.com/swlh/diligence-at-social-capital-part-1-accounting-for-user-growth-4a8a449fddfc) [3].

100% of experts said the CEO must be aligned on the absolute goal and that it must be communicated to the entire company [3].

### Growth Channels

The most common framework for identifying channels is based on existing user behavior. Aatif Awan (Head of Growth at LinkedIn) mapped user behaviors to channels [3]:

| User Behavior | Channel | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Product requires connecting with another user | Product itself | Facebook, PayPal, Slack |
| Existing users talk about the product | Referrals, Community | Uber, Airbnb, Dropbox |
| Users search for a solution | SEO, SEM | Airbnb |
| Users look for expert inspiration | Affiliate bloggers, Partnerships, Content | Stitch Fix, Glossier, Intercom |
| High LTV users | Paid acquisition | Airbnb, Expedia, Uber |

Most products find 1-2 relevant channels early on. Approximately 70% of experts said referrals were the top channel in the first year [3].

### Experimentation at Scale

The four essential elements for a growth team: a clean data set, segmentation tools, a rigorous experiment dashboard, and a peer review process [3].

A company at scale typically runs **1 experiment per growth engineer per week**. Growth teams running 100+ experiments per year report only a third turn out positive. The success rate of 20-30% is expected -- the point is to encourage engineers to take more risks [3].

A common heuristic: "Don't test things you wouldn't ship to everybody" [3].

100% of experts emphasized the decision to build their own internal tools at scale. Early on, tools like [Mixpanel](https://mixpanel.com/), [Optimizely](https://www.optimizely.com/), and [Chartio](https://chartio.com) suffice [3].

### Where the Growth Team Sits

This remains the biggest source of debate. Facebook pioneered the separate growth team -- Growth as its own department, with the Head of Growth reporting to the CEO. The rationale: if no one owns MAU growth, no one drives it. Facebook grew from 50 million to 2 billion MAUs under this model [3].

Other companies (Uber, Airbnb, Slack) started with separate teams but later merged them with product. Because growth experiments often require subtle product changes, advocates argue product and growth must be in the same org [3].

The least-favored option among experts is housing growth under marketing [3].

At scale, a growth team can exceed 100 people, roughly composed of: 50% engineers, 10% PMs, 10-15% data scientists, 10% product marketing, 10-15% designers, and ~5% researchers [3].

## There Is Always Room

A common fear among would-be founders is that the market for startups is saturated. Graham dismisses this [4]. "At every point in history, even the darkest bits of the dark ages, people were discovering things that made everyone say 'why didn't anyone think of that before?'" [4].

The argument that there are only so many startups Google and Yahoo can buy conflates the limit on acquisitions with the limit on wealth creation. "There is no limit on the amount of newly created wealth consumers can absorb, any more than there is a limit on the number of theorems that can be proven" [4]. The only limit on the number of startups is the pool of people willing to work that hard.

## Growth, Fundraising, and Acquisitions

Growth explains why VCs invest in startups rather than ordinary businesses. The returns when startups succeed are so high that the ratio of return to risk passes any investment test. But VCs also prefer startups because capital gains are easier to manage than dividends from private companies, where owners can funnel revenues to themselves [1].

Raising money lets founders "choose their growth rate." The most successful startups take VC money even when profitable because it lets them grow faster and fend off competitors, especially in businesses with network effects [1].

Growth also explains why successful startups get acquisition offers. A rapidly growing company is not merely valuable but dangerous to incumbents. "Most product acquisitions have some component of fear," Graham writes. Acquirers often pay more than ordinary investors would because a startup is doubly valuable -- for what it is and for what it could become in a competitor's hands [1].

## References

1. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- [[Paul Graham]] (September 2012)
2. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)
3. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)
4. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
5. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2013)
6. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)
7. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)

title: Forward Deployed Engineers
body:
# Forward Deployed Engineers

The forward deployed engineer (FDE) model -- originating at Palantir and now the dominant go-to-market motion for vertical AI startups -- places engineers directly alongside end users to understand workflows, build custom solutions in real time, and close deals through demonstrated value rather than sales presentations [1] [2]. [[Garry Tan]] argues that every AI founder should think of themselves as a forward deployed engineer [1]. Bob McGrew, former Chief Research Officer at OpenAI, describes the FDE as the critical role for AI startups specifically because the last 5-10% of making an AI system work for a specific customer requires deep domain understanding that only comes from sitting next to the user [2].

## The Palantir Origin

[[Garry Tan]], who was early at Palantir, traces the FDE concept to a core insight shared by Peter Thiel, Alex Karp, and the other Palantir founders: if you go into any Fortune 500 company or government agency, "nobody who understands computer science and technology at the highest possible level would ever even be in that room" [1]. The problems those organizations face are multi-billion-dollar, sometimes trillion-dollar problems. They are awash in data but have no idea what to do with it [1].

The FDE title was created to solve a specific problem: how do you sit next to the FBI agent investigating domestic terrorism, see what the case coming in looks like, understand all the steps, observe that the actual artifacts are Word documents and Excel spreadsheets, and then convert those "file cabinet and fax machine things" into clean software [1]?

The difference from traditional enterprise sales: "Other companies would send a salesperson to go sit with the FBI agent. Palantir sent engineers" [1]. Instead of the next meeting being a review of 50 pages of sales documentation, "it's literally 'Okay, we built it.' And then you're getting real live feedback within days" [1].

This approach produced seven, eight, and eventually nine-figure contracts because the software was so immediately useful that "the second you see something that makes you feel seen, you want to buy it on the spot" [1].

## The AI-Era FDE

The FDE model has become even more powerful with AI because the turnaround time has collapsed [1]. In the pre-LLM era, Palantir needed a team of engineers and weeks to build a custom solution. Now, two founders can go into a meeting, take context about the customer's workflow, "stuff it basically in the prompt," and return the next day with a working demo that closes six or seven-figure deals [1].

Specific examples from YC:
- **Giger ML**: Two talented software engineers, "not natural salespeople," force themselves to be FDEs for customer support voice agents. They closed a deal with Zapier by having the most impressive demo, differentiating through technical innovation on the RAG pipeline for accurate, low-latency voice responses [1].
- **Happy Robot**: Sold seven-figure contracts to the top three largest logistics brokers in the world for AI voice agents. Started with six-figure deals, graduated to seven figures within months using the FDE model -- talking to CIOs, rapidly shipping product, and doing very fast turnaround [1].

## Why FDEs Win Against Incumbents

Tan describes the dynamic: "How does a really good engineer with a weak handshake beat [Salesforce, Oracle, Booz Allen]? You show them something that they've never seen before and make them feel super heard. You have to be super empathetic about it. You actually have to be a great designer and product person. And then come back and blow them away" [1].

The pre-AI enterprise sales model -- "hair and teeth" salespeople, steakhouse dinners, six-to-twelve-week sales cycles, software that never quite works -- loses to founders who can demonstrate working software customized to the specific customer's workflow within days [1].

## The FDE Playbook for AI Startups

Bob McGrew, having spent years building what became O1 and O3 at OpenAI, was "shocked" after leaving to find how few companies were actually using the technology. He expected "intelligence is too cheap to meter" euphoria; instead, most companies were "still on our quarterly roadmap unchanged from a year ago" [2].

McGrew's framework for AI startups emphasizes:
- The founder must be the FDE. You cannot outsource the deep customer understanding that makes the AI work [2].
- The evals (test suites for AI quality) are the true IP. They come from sitting next to users and understanding their workflow at a granular level [1].
- The vertical AI agent model works because domain expertise -- understanding the specific customer's reward function -- is the moat, not the model [1].

## Connection to YC Canon

The FDE model is a natural evolution of [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]]. Graham's consulting technique -- pick a single user and act as if you were building something just for them -- is precisely what FDEs do [3]. The Collison Installation (setting up Stripe on a user's laptop during the meeting) is the same motion [3].

The difference in the AI era: the FDE can customize the product during or immediately after the meeting by modifying prompts and tool configurations, rather than writing new code. This collapses the feedback loop from weeks to hours.

## References

1. [State-Of-The-Art Prompting For AI Agents](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DL82mGde6wo) -- [[Garry Tan]], [[Harj Taggar]], Diana Hu, [[Jared Friedman]] (2025)
2. [The FDE Playbook for AI Startups with Bob McGrew](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Bob McGrew (2025)
3. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2013)

title: Startup Essentials
body:
# Startup Essentials

Y Combinator distills its most fundamental, most transformative advice for startups into a set of core principles [1]. These are not tactical tips for day-to-day operations but structural beliefs about what makes startups succeed or fail. Many of these principles are reinforced across dozens of YC partners and alumni.

## Launch Immediately

The first thing YC tells founders is to launch right away. This is the only way to fully understand customers' problems and whether the product meets their needs. Launching a mediocre product as soon as possible and then talking to customers and iterating is much better than waiting to build a "perfect" product. The threshold is a "quantum of utility" -- the product must contain enough value for customers that its usefulness overwhelms whatever warts it has [1].

## Do Things That Don't Scale

After launching, YC advises founders to do things that cannot scale, following [[Paul Graham]]'s essay of the same name [1]. Many startup advisors push startups to scale too early. Building technology and processes to support premature scaling wastes time and effort and often leads to failure.

The right approach is to get the first customer by any means necessary, even by manual work that could not be managed for more than ten customers, let alone a hundred or a thousand. The Airbnb founders offered to "professionally" photograph the homes and apartments of their earliest customers to make listings more attractive to renters, then went and took the photographs themselves. The listings improved, conversions improved, and they had "amazing conversations with their customers." This was entirely unscalable but proved essential in learning how to build a vibrant marketplace [1].

## The 90/10 Solution

When talking to users yields a long, complicated list of features to build, Paul Buchheit advises looking for the "90/10 solution": a way to accomplish 90% of what you want with only 10% of the work, effort, or time [1]. If you search hard, there is almost always a 90/10 solution available. A 90% solution to a real customer problem that is available right away is much better than a 100% solution that takes ages to build [1].

## Write Code and Talk to Users

As companies grow, distractions multiply: conferences, dinners, meetings with VCs, corporate development inquiries, press coverage. Jessica Livingston created a comprehensive list of wrong things to focus on [1]. [[Sam Altman]] points out that it is nearly always better to take the more ambitious path. He calls the avoidance of hard problems "fake work" because it tends to be more fun than real work [1].

The most important tasks for an early-stage company are to write code and talk to users. For great companies, the cycle of launching, talking to users, taking feedback, and iterating never ends [1].

## Choose Customers Carefully

Founders often do not realize they get to choose customers as much as customers choose them. A small group of customers who love you is better than a large group who kind of like you. Recruiting 10 customers who have a burning problem is much better than 1,000 customers who have a passing annoyance [1].

Some customers cost way more than they provide in revenue or learning, and startups sometimes need to fire their customers. Justin.tv/Twitch only became a breakout success when they focused on video game broadcasters and stopped serving people trying to stream copyrighted content, as [[Michael Seibel]] describes [1].

## Growth Follows Product, Not the Reverse

Growth is a natural result of building what customers want and iterating quickly. But growth is not always the right choice. If you have not found [[Product-Market Fit]], growing merely produces poor retention. If your product is unprofitable, growth drains cash. As Paul Buchheit says, "it never makes sense to take 80 cents from a customer and then hand them a dollar back." [[Sam Altman]] reinforces that unit economics really matter, despite too many startups forgetting this [1].

## Do Less, Really Well

Founders' intuition is always to do more, but the best strategy is almost always to do less, really well. Deals between large companies and tiny startups seldom end well for the startup: they take too long, cost too much, and often fail completely [1].

Early on, a startup should choose one or two key metrics to measure success, then make decisions based nearly exclusively on how each task impacts those metrics. When the early product is not working, the temptation is to build new features for every problem. The right move is to talk to customers and focus only on the most acute problem they have [1].

## Every Startup Is Broken

Nearly every startup has deep, fundamental issues, even those that become billion-dollar companies. Success is not determined by whether the startup is broken at the beginning but by what the founders do about the inevitable problems. "Your job as a founder will often seem to be continuously righting a capsized ship. This is normal" [1].

## Ignore Competitors

Spending time worrying about competitors is nearly always a bad idea. "Startup companies always die of suicide not murder." Competitive dynamics will eventually matter, but it is highly unlikely in the first year or two [1].

## Fundraise Fast, Then Get Back to Work

The best fundraising advice is to raise money as quickly as possible and get back to work. Growth curves visibly flatten when a company is fundraising. Valuation is not equal to success or even probability of success. Some of YC's best companies (Airbnb, Dropbox, Twitch) raised on tiny initial valuations [1].

The money raised is not the founder's money. Founders have a fiduciary and ethical duty to spend it only to improve the company's prospects [1].

## Cofounder Relationships and Personal Health

Most companies fail fast because founders fall out. The relationships with cofounders matter more than founders think. Open, honest communication makes future debacles much less likely. [[Paul Graham]] argues that being nice is one of the best things you can do to make a startup successful [1].

YC also tells founders to take breaks, spend time with friends and family, get enough sleep, and exercise between bouts of intense, focused work [1].

## The Pocket Guide

YC's essential advice, condensed [1]:

- Launch now
- Build something people want
- Do things that don't scale
- Find the 90/10 solution
- Find 10-100 customers who love your product
- All startups are badly broken at some point
- Write code, talk to users
- "It's not your money"
- Growth is the result of a great product, not the precursor
- Don't scale your team/product until you have built something people want
- Valuation is not equal to success or even probability of success
- Avoid long negotiated deals with big customers if you can
- Avoid big company corporate development queries
- Avoid conferences unless they are the best way to get customers
- Pre-product market fit: do things that don't scale, remain small/nimble
- Startups can only solve one problem well at any given time
- Founder relationships matter more than you think
- Sometimes you need to fire your customers
- Ignore your competitors, you will more likely die of suicide than murder
- Most companies don't die because they run out of money
- Be nice! Or at least don't be a jerk
- Get sleep and exercise, take care of yourself

## References

1. [YC's Essential Startup Advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) — Y Combinator (September 2018)

title: Cohort Retention
body:
# Cohort Retention

Cohort retention is the practice of tracking what fraction of new users keep using a product over time, grouped by when they first started. [[David Lieb]], YC Group Partner and the person whose work at Bump became the basis for Google Photos (which serves over a billion users), describes it as "the single best way I have found" to answer whether a startup has made something people want [1].

The YC motto is "make something people want." Cohort retention is the quantitative answer to whether you have done it [1].

## Defining the Three Variables

To measure cohort retention, three things must be defined [1]:

### 1. Cohorts

How to group new users into individual groups. The most common method is by when they first used the product -- all new users in January form one cohort, February another. As analysis matures, cohorts can be sliced by country, acquisition channel, device, or customer characteristics [1].

### 2. Actions

What counts as a user being "active" in a given period. The simplest definition -- "did they open the app?" -- is often too shallow. A better approach is to pick a specific action correlated with the user actually getting value [1]:

| Product | Recommended Action |
|---|---|
| Instagram | Viewed 3+ posts (filters out accidental opens) |
| Uber | Completed a ride |
| Google Photos | Tapped to view a photo full screen |

Lieb's rubric: "Imagine you're sitting next to one of your customers. They're using your product. When you watch them, what is the thing going through your head that helps you answer whether that user is a good user?" Whatever that answer is, use it as the action [1].

### 3. Time Period

The granularity at which subsequent usage is measured. This should match the product's intended frequency of use [1]:

| Product Type | Time Period |
|---|---|
| Social/entertainment (Instagram, TikTok) | Daily |
| Utility (Google Photos, Uber) | Weekly |
| Travel (Airbnb) | Quarterly or annually |

## The Core Insight: Flatness Is Everything

The single most important thing about cohort retention curves is whether they get flat. "The only, only thing that matters is whether your cohort curves get flat. The shape of the curve is what matters, not the absolute number" [1].

Lieb illustrates with two products: Product A retains 75% of users after month two but continues declining toward zero. Product B drops to 20% quickly but then flattens. Product B is in a far stronger position because it can accumulate users over time. Product A is on a treadmill -- acquiring new users while losing old ones [1].

Even Google Photos had curves that "would drop off pretty quickly, but then they would get flat somewhere between 20% and 40%." Lieb recalls: "If you talk to a friend and told them '80% of my users leave pretty immediately,' they would think it sounds pretty bad. But the fact that 20% of users pretty immediately demonstrated that they would stay with the product and use it every week for basically forever" gave him confidence that Google Photos would eventually reach a billion users. Four years later, it did [1].

## The Triangle Chart and Layer Cake

The raw data is organized in a triangle chart: rows represent cohorts (months), columns represent subsequent months, and cells show how many users from each cohort returned [1].
