Each user is counted once per period, regardless of whether they returned once or ten times. The number can go up or down but never exceed the initial cohort size [1].

The diagonal of the triangle chart represents a single calendar month -- showing how many active users came from each cohort in that month [1].

Converting to percentages (dividing by initial cohort size) normalizes the data and reveals whether newer cohorts perform better than older ones [1].

The layer cake chart aligns cohorts by absolute time rather than relative time, stacking them to show total active users. "If you see a layer cake where the top line is growing really nicely and it's composed of thick layers coming from old cohorts, this is like the most beautiful chart that you'll ever see in your startup" [1].

## Common Ways to Fool Yourself

Lieb speaks from personal experience, having made every one of these mistakes at Bump [1]:

### Picking Too Large a Time Period

The most common mistake. By widening the measurement window (from weekly to monthly to quarterly), curves appear flatter and higher by definition. At Bump, the team looked at weekly cohort retention, saw bad numbers, widened to monthly (which looked better), then convinced themselves quarterly was reasonable. "But we were fooling ourselves. Bump was not designed to be used once every quarter" [1].

### Picking Too Easy an Action

Using "opened the app" as the active user definition is gameable through notifications. Google+ defined active users as anyone who saw a notification bell in the corner of any Google product. "It made the active user numbers really big, and so everybody felt good. But if you looked at the cohort retention, it was a lie" [1].

### Using Payment as the Only Action

Counterintuitively, "is paying" is often not the best action. Users first stop using a product and then stop paying. "I'm sure we each have streaming services like Netflix that we're paying for, and the last time you watched a show might be more than a month ago, but you probably haven't canceled" [1]. Better: pair "is paying" with "is an active user" [1].

### Looking at a Single Data Point

Founders commonly report a metric like "80% week-over-week retention" without knowing the numerator, denominator, or which week it refers to. A single point tells nothing about whether the curve is flat or heading to zero [1].

### Trusting Analytics Tools Blindly

Built-in cohort analysis in tools like Mixpanel or Amplitude may not measure exactly what founders think. Some tools measure "rolling retention" rather than per-period cohort separation. Lieb recommends building cohort retention curves manually using logs or Google Sheets first, then comparing to analytics tools [1].

## How to Improve Cohort Retention

### Improve the Product

New use cases, reduced latency, simpler flows. Product improvements show up as newer cohorts performing better -- flattening sooner and at higher levels [1].

### Acquire Better Users

Often the easiest improvement. If a product targets the wrong customers, switching acquisition channels can dramatically improve retention. Lieb describes Google Photos targeting Gen Z users at one point: "Those cohorts had really bad retention. And it kind of makes sense -- Google Photos is a tool to accumulate your life's memories and reminisce. If you're a young person, you don't have that many life memories yet" [1].

Slicing cohorts by dimension (country, device, company size, acquisition channel) can reveal which segments retain well and which do not [1].

### Improve Onboarding and Activation

Often overlooked. Companies invest in building the tool itself but not in teaching people to use it. "What are they doing yesterday before they used your product, and what do you want them to change about their life today? Investing in this often is the cheapest and easiest way to improve your performance" [1].

### Build Network Effects

Products where each subsequent user makes the product better for existing users -- social networks, messaging apps, sharing platforms -- naturally see cohort improvement as the network grows and becomes denser [1].

## The Holy Grail: Curves That Go Up

The best of the best is cohort curves that do not just get flat but actually increase over time. Users who stick with the product start using it more and more. "If you see that your cohort curves are getting flat and then increasing, and subsequent cohorts are getting better and better over time, you should be feeling really good" [1].

## Connection to Growth

Cohort retention is the prerequisite for sustainable [[Growth]]. [[Anu Hariharan]]'s growth team guide is explicit: do not build a growth team before you have proven retention. The retention benchmarks by vertical in the Growth article provide specific targets for what "good" looks like [1].

## References

1. [How to Improve Cohort Retention](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LV-how-to-improve-cohort-retention) -- [[David Lieb]] (2024)

title: AI and Jobs
body:
# AI and Jobs

The impact of AI on employment is one of the most debated topics among startup founders and investors. The consensus among YC partners and AI company leaders is nuanced: software engineering jobs as they exist today will not exist in five to ten years, the transition will be painful, but the long-term outcome is a world of abundance where humans focus on higher-order problems [1] [2] [3] [4].

## The Software Engineering Impact

Tom Blomfield's analysis: AI coding agents are to software engineers what the combine harvester was to farmers [1]. The productivity increase will be extreme -- a single person with AI tools is already ten times more productive than a current professional developer, and the capability curve is steep [1]. In the current YC batch, a third to half of companies write most of their code using AI agents [1].

The Jevons paradox argument -- that cheaper software will increase demand enough to preserve jobs -- is partially correct: demand for software will increase dramatically [1]. But Blomfield argues the automation will increase faster than demand: "I don't think it's even productivity per person because I think the person just goes to zero" [1].

Lieb's counterpoint: in the short to medium term, these tools give high-agency individuals superpowers. Getting to $1 million, $10 million, $100 million of revenue with a software idea is "happening faster than ever" [1]. The question is not whether jobs change but whether individuals adapt to leverage the tools.

## Beyond Software: Knowledge Work

The same pattern is spreading to law, medicine, finance, and accounting [1]. Two years ago at YC, the idea that AI could automate lawyering or accounting was "fringe." Now companies like Legora (AI for lawyers) are among the fastest-growing YC companies ever [1].

Blomfield observes that every industry is worried about AI's impact, and boards are asking every CEO "What is AI going to do to our business?" [1]. It is becoming a competitive disadvantage not to embrace AI tools: "Would you hire the lawyer that doesn't use a computer? Of course not. It's getting to that point with AI" [1].

## The MIT Study and What It Means

A widely cited MIT study on AI's impact on employment was broadly misunderstood [3]. The study's actual findings are more nuanced than the headlines suggested, and the implications for startups are specific: AI augmentation (making existing workers more productive) is happening faster than AI replacement (eliminating roles entirely). Startups that position as productivity tools for existing workers face less regulatory and adoption resistance than those positioned as replacements [3].

## The Optimistic Case

Lieb articulates the optimistic view: "If you just look at each step along this progression and you had to choose as an individual, do I want to live in this world or the world one click further into the future? I think in all cases your experience and what you can do with your life is going to be better in the next click forward" [1].

Amjad Masad rejects the dystopian framing: "The future of work is more human, more interactive, more multimodal. Once the making of things gets easier, the bottleneck goes back to how many ideas you can have" [4].

## The Painful Transition

Blomfield's biggest worry is the transition speed: "You could see hundreds of millions of people displaced and the idea that they're going to retrain in a different job I think is going to be extremely painful. And I can see the societal impact and turmoil being very, very grave for 10 or 20 years" [1].

Jobs involving physical work -- surgeons, plumbers, electricians -- are relatively protected [1]. Regulatory barriers will slow AI adoption in medicine and law even when AI is "provably better," analogous to self-driving car regulation [1].

## What to Do Now

The actionable advice from multiple speakers converges on:
1. **Stay current with AI tools** -- even if they are not perfect for your industry yet, they will cross the tipping point [1].
2. **Get good at identifying human problems** -- this skill becomes the most valuable in a world where building is easy [1].
3. **Build domain expertise** -- understanding a specific part of the world deeply is the moat that AI cannot easily replicate [1].

## References

1. [How AI Coding Agents Will Change Your Job](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TECDj4JUx7o) -- Tom Blomfield, David Lieb (2025)
2. [What Everyone Is Getting Wrong About AI and Jobs](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
3. [Inside the MIT AI Study Everyone Misunderstood and What It Means for Startups](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
4. [Replit CEO Amjad Masad: Coding Agents, Autonomy, and the Future of Work](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kOyIjt6FUrw) -- Amjad Masad (2025)

title: Hiring Engineers
body:
# Hiring Engineers

Hiring engineers is one of the most difficult and time-consuming tasks for early-stage startup founders. [[Harj Taggar]], cofounder of Triplebyte (YC S15), describes it as "incredibly hard" because founders are already stretched thin on time, and the process involves significant dead ends and frustration [1]. The challenge is compounded by the fact that traditional recruiting methods do not work well for very early companies, and most of the effective strategies require significant founder involvement [1].

The same core principles -- personal networks first, speed in the funnel, genuine pitch over polished pitch -- apply when recruiting salespeople and other key hires [4].

## Start With Your Personal Network

For a startup's first engineering hire, personal networks are the most important source by far [1]. Hiring someone the founder has already worked with is the best option because the working relationship is already known. At the earliest stage, the quality of that relationship "could be the difference between persevering to success and shutting down the company" [1].

Taggar recommends a specific process for working personal networks [1]:

1. Make a list of the best engineers you know, whether you think they are available or not. Go through Facebook and LinkedIn to jog your memory.
2. Invite them to lunch or dinner to talk about the startup.
3. Make the ask: "Would you consider joining us?"
4. Whatever they answer, follow up: "If you did join us, which engineers would you most want to hire?"
5. Ask for introductions to those people.
6. Repeat with each introduction.

Taggar notes that founders are often surprised by how reluctant they are to fully explore their networks. Asking friends to leave their jobs is scary, and rejection from friends feels more awkward than rejection from strangers. But optimizing for the startup's success requires pushing past this discomfort [1].

For the first three engineering hires, Taggar recommends focusing exclusively on personal network hiring. After three hires, founders should supplement with other channels to improve the diversity and composition of the team [1].

## Other Hiring Channels, Ranked

Taggar ranks the remaining channels in order of effectiveness for early-stage companies [1]:

**Hiring Marketplaces** (e.g., Triplebyte, Hired): Strictly inferior to personal networks for a first hire, but the second-best option. They provide quick access to vetted, motivated-to-move engineers. The cost is typically around $25,000 per experienced engineer hire. Success depends heavily on the effectiveness of the company's pitch and closing process. Some marketplaces do rigorous technical vetting, which can double the onsite-to-offer conversion rate (40% vs. the industry standard 20%) [1].

**Inbound Generation**: Most early-stage startups get little organic inbound from good engineers. Job sites like Stack Overflow Jobs, the monthly Hacker News "Who is hiring?" thread, and AngelList can help. Developer tool companies have a natural advantage because their product is already interesting to engineers. Taggar recommends blogging about controversial or unusual technical choices to attract attention, and investing time in making job postings personal and distinctive rather than generic [1].

**Cold Outreach**: Messaging engineers on LinkedIn, Hacker News, or GitHub. The challenge is standing out from the overwhelming number of messages good engineers receive. Personalization is essential. Email is more effective than platform messages. Expect it to take up to six months before this channel results in a hire [1].

**Recruiters**: A technical recruiter will likely use cold outreach, and is unlikely to achieve higher response rates than messages from a founder. If used, work with contract-basis recruiters with agreed hourly rates and cancellation terms. Train them thoroughly on the company pitch before starting [1].

**Meetups**: High variance. Business conferences are "almost certainly a complete waste of time." Smaller, informal, deeply technical meetups can work, but only if the founder is either an engineer who can gain respect through technical conversation or a highly charismatic personality. Good for building a long-term network, not for making a hire right now [1].

**Agencies**: Traditional recruiting agencies have "bad adverse selection bias" on their candidates. Most good engineers will not work with them. Taggar cannot think of a startup he knows that made its first engineering hire through a recruiting agency [1].

## The Hiring Funnel: Speed and Experience

Generating an offer for an engineer is expensive. It takes roughly five onsite interviews to produce a single offer (the industry standard onsite-to-offer rate is around 20%), consuming approximately 30 hours of engineering productivity [2]. Given this cost, maximizing offer acceptance rates is critical.

### Speed as Competitive Advantage

Being fast and responsive is one of the biggest advantages a startup has over larger companies when competing for engineers [2]. Taggar identifies two reasons companies fail at this:

1. **Entropy**: Maintaining an organized candidate tracking system is hard. It naturally trends toward disorder. Using software (initially a spreadsheet or Airtable, graduating to a real ATS like Lever as the company grows) and assigning clear responsibility for daily candidate review can counteract this.
2. **Psychological bias**: Companies rationalize that candidates who dropped out due to slow response "weren't interested anyway." This is bad reasoning. Managing a job search is overwhelming, especially for the best candidates with the most options. Every candidate who drops out before receiving a reply should be treated as a failure [2].

### The First Call

The first call with a candidate typically lasts 25 minutes. Taggar recommends asking the candidate what they are looking for early in the call, not at the end, so the pitch can be tailored throughout [2]. For companies under 20 people, a founder should always do this call. The non-technical/sales founder should handle it to protect the technical founder's maker time, even if they cannot speak to technical challenges in detail [2].

### The Onsite Interview

Taggar surveyed engineers about bad onsite experiences. The top three complaints were interviewers being (1) unprepared and not knowing anything about the candidate, (2) not familiar with the technical questions they asked, and (3) determined to prove their own intelligence to the interviewee [2].

He recommends writing an interviewing guide emphasizing on-time, prepared, and friendly behavior, and selecting only the friendliest engineers as interviewers. A centralized question bank that interviewers work through together before using on candidates is essential [2].

For lunch, Taggar describes Triplebyte's own practice: Slack the team in the morning, gather at the lunch table with the candidate, have the office manager introduce them, and do a lighthearted icebreaker. "We've had candidates tell us this experience was the deciding factor in joining" [2].

## Making and Closing the Offer

### Two-Step Offer Presentation

Taggar recommends a specific sequence [2]:

**Step One (same day as onsite if possible):** The hiring manager (founder at early stage) calls to say the day went well and they want to make an offer. Mention specific reasons for excitement. Multiple people can join the call.

**Step Two (next day):** Call to walk through offer details. Gauge the candidate's familiarity with equity concepts before diving in. Do not push hard for a decision on this call.

### Competing With Big Companies

As a startup, it is hard to compete with Facebook and Google compensation packages. Taggar identifies four arguments that have been most successful [2]:

1. **Learning**: Real decision-making responsibility comes immediately at a startup.
2. **Career Progression**: Rising through the ranks only happens at fast-growing startups. "The Chief Product Officer of Facebook ($500bn market cap) is 36 years old."
3. **Opportunity Cost**: Working at a public technology company is always available. The startup is a unique opportunity right now.
4. **Mentorship**: If experienced engineers on the team are willing to mentor, make this explicit.

## Recruiting Salespeople

The same fundamentals apply to recruiting sales reps: personal networks first, founder involvement in early pitches, and speed through the process [4]. For early-stage AI companies, the hiring challenge is compounded by needing salespeople comfortable with technical products and rapid iteration -- the "script kiddie vs. hacker" distinction Ryan Junee makes in [[Enterprise Sales]] applies directly to sales hiring [4].

## What Job Seekers Should Know

Ryan Choi, YC's head of Work at a Startup, offers advice from the candidate's perspective [3]. For resumes, each position should cover: what you worked on, how you got work done, and what impact you had (growth numbers, cost savings, business results) [3].

For cold outreach, highlight two to three things you would be excited to work on at the company. Being a genuine user of the product is a significant advantage. When evaluating startups, ask about runway and burn rate [3].

## References

1. [How to Hire Your First Engineer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (July 2018)
2. [Convincing Engineers to Join Your Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (July 2018)
3. [Advice for Job Seekers on Resumes and Reaching Out](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4l-advice-for-job-seekers-on-resumes-and-reaching-out) -- Ryan Choi (April 2020)
4. [How to Recruit the Best Engineers and Sales Reps](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)

title: Social Commerce
body:
# Social Commerce

Social commerce refers to e-commerce platforms that integrate social interactions, group dynamics, and community-driven experiences into the shopping process. The concept is grounded in a simple observation: in the physical world, shopping is social. Purchases are informed by friends and family, browsing is often communal, and trust plays a central role in purchase decisions for many categories [1].

[[Anu Hariharan]] argues that traditional e-commerce platforms like Amazon, JD, and Alibaba "optimize for efficiency, funnel conversion, and purchase rates" but do not account for the social dimension [1]. This creates a structural gap, particularly in categories where consumers seek feedback or recommendations: as of 2020, only 29% of US apparel, 11% of health/personal care, and 3% of food/beverage purchases happened online [1].

## Core Principles

### Shopping Is Inherently Social

"Social commerce does not just mean connecting user accounts to Facebook. It means investing in creating physical world experiences online -- specifically bringing the fun of shopping offline to online platforms" [1].

### Group Dynamics Trump Individual Targeting

[[Pinduoduo]]'s team purchase model demonstrates that tapping into groups of social relationships scales faster than one-to-one relationships [1]. This follows [Reed's law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reed%27s_law): the value of a group-forming network grows exponentially with the ease of group formation, not merely with the number of users.

### Trust Solves Category Penetration

Categories with low online penetration often correlate with high trust requirements. In China's developing cities, where more than 80% of retail is unorganized, consumers rely heavily on social recommendations to initiate transactions [1]. Social commerce addresses this "trust deficit" by embedding purchases within trusted social relationships.

## Social Commerce in Practice

### Pinduoduo: Team Purchase

See [[Pinduoduo]] for full analysis. The core mechanic: buyers form groups to receive discounts from suppliers, sharing deals via [[WeChat]] to recruit friends. Nearly all transactions use this model [1]. Strategic launch in fruits and vegetables (high frequency, low order value, limited incumbent competition) drove rapid organic growth to 585 million active buyers in four years [1].

### WeChat: Commerce Infrastructure

See [[WeChat]] for full analysis. WeChat contributed to social commerce through several mechanisms [2]:
- **WeChat Pay** (driven by Red Packets) created the payment infrastructure
- **Official Accounts** enabled direct merchant-customer communication
- **Social Coupons** turned coupon redemption into friend-based endorsement
- **Mini Programs** allowed merchants to operate within WeChat without separate apps

WeChat's QR code adoption bridged online and offline commerce, becoming what Pony Ma called "a label of abundant online information attached to the offline world" [2].

### Toutiao: Social Content Discovery

While not strictly commerce, [[Toutiao]]'s approach to social content has implications for social commerce. Its recommendation engine achieves social-network-level engagement (74 minutes daily, >45% retention) without a social graph, using machine learning to replicate the personalization that social connections typically provide [3].

## Emerging Global Examples

YC portfolio companies pursuing social commerce models [1]:
- **[Snackpass](https://www.snackpass.co/)**: College campus food app with gift-sending and virtual pet-hatching features tied to food orders
- **[Meesho](https://meesho.com/)**: Indian reseller marketplace enabling customers to create microbusinesses selling to friends and family via WhatsApp

Instagram has also evolved into a social shopping platform with browsing-based feeds from friends and influencers accompanied by purchase links [1]. The rise of the social media influencer economy signals that US consumers are "ready to fully engage in social online shopping" [1].

## Platform Dynamics

A critical enabler for Pinduoduo was WeChat's openness as a platform. Tencent (WeChat's owner and Pinduoduo investor) allowed Pinduoduo to grow on top of the WeChat ecosystem [1]. Hariharan notes that Facebook would be "highly unlikely" to allow a similar application on Messenger or Instagram, but that US messaging fragmentation means this is not a blocking factor for US social commerce [1].

## Key Takeaway for Founders

"New commerce platforms that can leverage social relationships will accelerate the offline to online transition and if you are able to tap into groups of social relationships vs. one-to-one relationship the faster your platforms will scale" [1].

Companies like Amazon will continue to grow in specific shopping needs, "but it is inevitable that something will rise to fill the e-commerce-social browsing void in the US market. And the half-hearted attempts some retail platforms are making to bridge that gap are not yet cutting it" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne (2020)
2. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Cold Outreach
body:
# Cold Outreach

Aaron Epstein, YC Group Partner and founder of Creative Market, provides a comprehensive framework for cold email outreach -- covering the full funnel from targeting to conversion [1]. His opening advice: the single best outreach hack is to get a warm intro, which yields 2-3x higher conversion rates than cold email [1]. But when warm intros are not possible, cold outreach done well can still be effective because "most cold emails are actually terrible, so the bar is really low for you to stand out" [1].

## The Conversion Funnel

Epstein maps out a typical B2B email funnel with sample conversion rates [1]:

| Stage | Conversion Rate | Volume Needed |
|---|---|---|
| Emails Sent | -- | 800 |
| Emails Opened | 50% | 400 |
| Responses | 10% of opens | 40 |
| Demos Booked | 25% of responses | 10 |
| Customers | 10% of demos | 1 |

The implication: getting one customer may require sending 800 emails. Founders who send a handful per week will not see results. Dozens per day -- potentially 50 -- are needed [1].

Critical rules: start writing manually and personalized before automating. The goal of the first email is not to close the sale but to advance to the next step in the funnel. Track conversion rates at each step to identify where to improve [1]. Conversion rates decrease as outreach scales, so fix problems at small scale first [1].

## Increasing Open Rates

**Better targeting is the highest-leverage lever.** It is better to send 100 targeted emails with high open rates than 1,000 untargeted emails that get deleted [1]. Targeting improves iteratively as founders learn to qualify leads better.

The recipient sees three things: sender name, subject line, and a preview of the first sentence. The "from" field should be a personal name (not a company name). Effective subject lines are short and conversational: "Hey quick question," "Can I get your advice," "Hello from [company name]" [1].

Email is generally better than LinkedIn for cold outreach, though this varies by industry. Text messaging can be more personal but should only be used with explicit permission [1].

Tools for finding email addresses: [Apollo.io](http://Apollo.io), [Hunter.io](http://Hunter.io), Clearbit, LinkedIn Sales Navigator, guessing based on company email format [1].

## Seven Principles of Effective Email Copy

### 1. Focused, Specific Goal

Each email should drive toward a single outcome -- respond, click, request an intro. Multiple asks create a paradox of choice. "Every word counts. If there's anything that doesn't move the recipient towards that goal, you should delete it" [1].

### 2. Be Human

Talk about emotions: "I'd love to," "It would mean a lot," "Thanks." Use informal language -- "hey" instead of "Hello Mr. Epstein." Typos and lack of capitalization signal humanity. Consider creative touches like personalized GIFs or videos [1].

The core rule: "Write how you talk to a friend." Compose the email with a specific person in mind, not as a template in a Google Doc. Then read it out loud. If anything feels awkward to say, rewrite it [1].

### 3. Personalize

Generic personalization ("love what you're doing at [company]") is transparent. Specific personalization ("I'm a huge Creative Market fan ever since you launched the photos category") shows genuine research [1].

**Uncommon commonalities** are the most powerful personalization tool: shared experiences that are not obvious. "We went to the same college and went to class in the same buildings" creates instant connection. Finding these requires real research -- LinkedIn, their product, their history [1].

### 4. Keep It Short

Wall-of-text emails get deleted. Short emails can be read and responded to on a phone in the moment. Anything requiring significant cognitive overhead gets archived or forgotten [1].

### 5. Establish Credibility

YC affiliation opens doors. Without it, use schools, former employers, impressive accomplishments, well-known customer logos, or industry data. Shared connections create baseline trust [1].

### 6. Make It About the Reader

Reframe "I" statements as "you" and "yours." Tell the story as solving the reader's problems. Use the language customers use to describe the product, not the company's internal language. Ask users: "How would you describe my company?" and use their exact phrase [1].

Do not make demands. The founder is in the recipient's inbox on their time [1].

### 7. Clear Call to Action

End with a concrete next step as its own standalone sentence: "Reply to let me know," "Click here to get started." Make it clear what action is requested and make that action easy enough to do immediately [1].

## Follow-Up Strategy

One email is often not enough. Plan to follow up 2-4 times, spacing emails a few days apart. Get creative with follow-ups -- Epstein once sent an email with subject line "Free donuts," then delivered donuts to the recipient's office [1].

If someone does not respond, do not get angry. "Nobody owes you anything." Move on and check back in a few months [1].

## The Founder Advantage

Founders should send these emails personally, not delegates. Recipients take emails from founders and CEOs far more seriously. "You may be small, but that is your advantage because you can give your personalized attention to making these dedicated emails feel special" [1].

In the early days, the founder is the brand. The level of effort, personalization, and care in the outreach represents the company. Offering a personal cell phone number and 24/7 availability signals genuine commitment that reduces the risk of working with a startup [1].

## References

1. [How to Convert Customers with Cold Emails](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LZ-how-to-convert-customers-with-cold-emails) -- Aaron Epstein (2024)

title: Startup Ideas
body:
# Startup Ideas

The single most important thing a startup does is choose what to build. How to find, evaluate, and generate startup ideas is one of the most reinforced topics in the YC canon, addressed by [[Paul Graham]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Jared Friedman]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Garry Tan]], and [[Harj Taggar]] across 20+ sources spanning 2005 to 2025.

The AI era has reopened the idea space. [[Jared Friedman]], [[Garry Tan]], [[Harj Taggar]], and Diana Hu argue that ideas which failed in the 2010s -- recruiting marketplaces, full-stack law firms, personalized tutors -- are now suddenly viable because LLMs can replace the human operations layer that destroyed gross margins [18]. The canonical startup advice of "sell before you build" has shifted: in the AI era, following your own curiosity and exploring what the latest technology makes possible is often a better path to finding ideas [18].

## The Organic Method: Notice, Don't Think Up

Graham argues across multiple essays spanning 2005 to 2014 that the way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of startup ideas, but to look for problems -- preferably problems you have yourself [1]. The very best startup ideas tend to have three things in common: they are something the founders themselves want, that they themselves can build, and that few others realize are worth doing [1]. Microsoft, Apple, Yahoo, Google, and Facebook all began this way.

The verb Graham insists on is "notice," not "think up" [1]. At YC they call ideas that grow naturally out of the founders' own experiences "organic" startup ideas. The formula Graham distills:

> "Live in the future, then build what's missing." [1]

This phrase, attributed to Paul Buchheit, captures the method: place yourself at the leading edge of a rapidly changing field, and the ideas that will seem uncannily prescient to others will seem obvious to you [1]. Drew Houston realized he had forgotten his USB stick and thought "I really need to make my files live online." Bill Gates and Paul Allen heard about the Altair and thought "I bet we could write a Basic interpreter for it" [1].

Friedman reinforces this as the best approach in his 2020 Startup School lecture: "The best way to have startup ideas is to notice them organically. There are great startup ideas all around you, and when you have a prepared mind, you see them everywhere. The problem is that when people sit down and try to think of startup ideas, they tend to think of bad ones" [7]. He recommends becoming an expert on something valuable -- particularly by working at an existing startup, since many successful YC companies came from ideas founders had while working at someone else's startup [7].

Graham's advice to college students boils down to two words: "just learn" [4]. Become good at something that matters, work on problems that interest you, with people you like and respect [4].

Friedman argues that in the AI era, this organic method is even more powerful: "If you're living at the edge of the future and you're exploring the latest technology, there's so many great startup ideas, you're very likely to just bump into one" [18].

## Start with Problems, Not Ideas

Seibel frames idea generation through the lens of problems rather than ideas [8]. "There's a common misconception that your idea has to be great in order to start a company," he says, citing Justin.tv's original concept -- an online reality TV show -- which eventually became Twitch and sold to Amazon for nearly a billion dollars [8].

His recommended sequence [8] [12]:

1. **Start with a problem you have a personal connection to.** Having a personal connection helps for two reasons: you can judge whether a solution is in the ballpark, and when solutions fail, your connection to the problem sustains motivation through discouragement.
2. **Ask why you are uniquely qualified.** Not in a resume sense, but whether you have a perspective, angle, or personal experience that others lack.
3. **Build an MVP quickly.** Release it in days or weeks, not months.
4. **Hand-pick initial users.** The goal is not to see how many people want to use the product, but whether it solves the problem for any group of people.

Seibel advises keeping "problem books" rather than idea books: "Write down notes on the problems that you encounter on a daily basis or that your friends complain about" [8].

## The Idea Quality Score

Friedman provides a concrete formula for evaluating startup ideas, adapted from [[Dalton Caldwell]]'s framework [7]. Score each idea on four criteria and take the average:

1. **How big is this idea?** Look for existing large companies doing something similar, or a market that is small today but has a real chance of being huge. Coinbase is the example: Bitcoin was tiny in 2012, but the potential was enormous [7].
2. **Founder/market fit.** Are the founders domain experts? Flexport's founder Ryan worked in international trade for 10 years before starting the company -- "10 out of 10 founder/market fit" [7].
3. **How sure are you that you're solving a big problem?** Ideally from personal experience [7].
4. **Do you have a new, important insight?** Airbnb's founders had actually tried hosting strangers and discovered it was fun, when everyone else assumed it was dangerous [7].

Additional positive signals: you are making something you personally want; the idea only recently became possible due to a technological change; there are successful companies doing something similar elsewhere [7].

## The "Why Now" Framework

[[Garry Tan]] adds a dimension Graham does not emphasize: timing. The question great investors ask every startup is "Why now?" [14]. Tan identifies three categories of "why now":

### 1. New Technology

Technology progresses on curves. The key is orders-of-magnitude improvement, not incremental progress. General Magic tried to build a smartphone in 1994: $1,400, 1 MB of RAM, 480 bytes/second modem. Thirteen years later, the iPhone 1 had 16,000x the storage, 600x the network speed, and cost one-third the price [14].

"Look for the 10x. Look for the 100x. That's why tech is the most powerful thing" [14].

### 2. Behavior Change

Once a technology is broadly adopted, human behavior shifts, creating new opportunities. Smartphone adoption went from fringe to universal around 2012 -- exactly when Instacart launched. The founder explained: "This is the first time in history where smartphones are so prevalent that everyone has them. As a result, we can utilize people who have them to pick and deliver groceries" [14].

Webvan had failed 11 years earlier after burning $800M because the technology was not ready [14].

### 3. Regulation

Government mandates create massive waves of business activity. Parker Conrad spotted that Obamacare would mandate millions of small businesses to offer health insurance -- driving adoption of Zenefits, Gusto, and later Rippling [14]. Climate regulation (the Inflation Reduction Act) is creating similar opportunities in decarbonization [14].

## Don't Think Like an Investor

Caldwell and Seibel observe a damaging pattern: founders who approach idea selection the way a VC or management consultant would -- market sizing, competitive analysis, TAM calculations -- before writing a line of code or talking to a single customer [16].

Warning signs [16]:
- You want to present a slide deck before you have any customers
- You have 15 slides of market opportunity but no traction
- You describe your company the way you would describe a very large company
- Your idea came from reading VC Twitter, not from personal experience

What YC actually looks for: "Does your idea make any sense? Do you know anything about it? Do you have the tech skills to build the thing? And can you get any customers?" [16]

## Where Great Startup Ideas Come From: Timing, Contrarianism, and Scale

Caldwell and Seibel unpack three common themes from Airbnb, Coinbase, and Stripe [10]:

1. **Timing was critical.** In each case, pre-existing competitors existed but an opportunity to make something 10x better had opened up [10].
2. **Most people thought the ideas were horrible.** Either too hard to execute or simply bad ideas [10].
3. **The opportunities turned out to be bigger than the founders knew.** Each market expanded far beyond initial expectations [10].

## Simple Products That Became Big Companies

Caldwell and Seibel observe that many of the biggest YC companies started as remarkably simple products [11]. OpenSea began as a basic marketplace for CryptoKitties. Gusto started by doing payroll for a handful of small businesses. The lesson: founders should not wait for a complex, impressive idea. A simple product that solves a real problem can grow into something enormous [11].

## The Well Model: Depth Over Breadth

When evaluating an idea, Graham uses the metaphor of digging a hole [1]. Nearly all good startup ideas are wells -- narrow and deep. Made-up startup ideas are broad and shallow: "lots of people are mildly interested in a social network for pet owners" [1].

Microsoft was a well when they made Altair Basic. There were only a couple thousand Altair owners, but without this software they were programming in machine language [1]. Facebook had the same shape: exclusively for Harvard students, of which there are only a few thousand, but those few thousand users wanted it a lot [1].

The critical test: "Who wants this right now? Who wants this so much that they'll use it even when it's a crappy version one made by a two-person startup they've never heard of?" If you cannot answer that, the idea is probably bad [1].

A well must have a path out. Facebook started at Harvard but colleges are similar enough that it spread rapidly through all colleges, and from there to everyone else [1].

## AI Has Reopened the Idea Maze

The YC partners observe that AI has shifted the walls of the idea maze [18]. Entire categories of startups that failed in the 2010s are now viable because the underlying technology has changed:

**Recruiting marketplaces.** [[Harj Taggar]] ran TripleByte for five years starting in 2015. The company raised $50 million and reached $24 million ARR, but the human evaluation piece prevented it from scaling with software-like margins [18]. Today, companies like Mercor use LLMs to do the evaluation piece on day one [18].

**Full-stack law firms and services.** Justin Kan started Atrium, a full-stack law firm that failed because AI was not good enough to automate the knowledge work [18]. Now Legora, a YC company building AI tools for lawyers, is "one of the fastest growing companies we've ever funded" [18].

**Personalized education.** The dream of a personalized tutor in every pocket was never achievable before LLMs [18]. Speak, a language learning company, doubled down when GPT-3/3.5 arrived. Revision Dojo provides AI-powered exam prep with high DAU and power users [18].

**The full-stack thesis revived.** Balaji Srinivasan's "full stack startups" thesis from the 2010s failed because the human operations layer destroyed gross margins [18]. Now, with agents replacing the operations team, full-stack companies can have software-like margins [18].

## Tarpit Ideas

Caldwell and Seibel address tarpit ideas -- ideas that seem promising because many founders attempt them, but that consistently fail [17]. Common examples include social apps, dating apps, and general-purpose AI assistants. The key is not that these ideas are inherently bad but that they look deceptively achievable and attract founders without the specific advantages needed to win.

A new technology wave (like AI) does not eliminate tarpit ideas -- it creates new ones. "There will be tarpit ideas in AI just as there were tarpit ideas in mobile and social" [17].

## Sitcom Ideas and the Plausibility Trap

The most dangerous bad ideas are not the obviously bad ones but the plausible-sounding ones. Graham calls these "sitcom" startup ideas [1]. A social network for pet owners does not sound obviously mistaken. But the lukewarm reaction -- "Yeah, maybe I could see using something like that" -- sums to zero users [1].

Friedman codifies this as the SISP problem: "Solution In Search of a Problem" [7]. These are ideas like "Uber for plumbers" -- solutions looking for problems. "We see this so commonly at YC that we have a term for it" [7].

## The Still Life Effect: Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas

In a 2005 essay, Graham diagnosed why intelligent hackers end up with terrible startup ideas [5]. The core mechanism is the "still life effect": you come up with a random idea, plunge into it, and then at each point feel you have put so much time into it that this must be the idea [5].

Graham experienced this himself. In January 1995, he and Robert Morris started a company called Artix to put art galleries on the Web. Galleries were not interested. Art dealers are "the most technophobic people on earth" [5]. Eventually they pivoted to Viaweb, which succeeded [5].

## Dangerous Filters

Four unconscious filters block founders from seeing good ideas. Graham identified the first two [1]; Friedman expanded to four [7]:

**The [[Schlep Filter]]**: Most programmers unconsciously shrink from ideas involving tedious work. Stripe is the canonical example -- thousands of programmers saw the payments opportunity but avoided it [1] [7].

**The Unsexy Filter**: Prevents founders from working on boring problems. Gusto is the example -- thousands of programmers knew payroll software was terrible, but no one tried to fix it because the problem was boring [7].

**Ideas that seem too ambitious**: Founders instinctively shy away from ambitious ideas because they are intimidating. But these are often the ideas that turn into the biggest companies [7].

**Ideas with existing competitors**: Founders instinctively avoid crowded markets. But a crowded market actually signals demand. "When founders go into spaces with no existing competitors, they usually find out that the reason there are no competitors is because nobody wants the product" [7].

## Seven Recipes for Generating Ideas

Friedman provides seven concrete recipes, ranked from best to worst [7]:

1. **Start with what your team is especially good at.** About half of YC's most successful companies trace back to this recipe. Examples: SnapDocs (founder worked in mortgages for 10 years), Lattice (founders noticed bad performance review software), Mixpanel (founder worked at Slide which had built internal analytics tools) [7].
2. **Think of things you wish someone else would build for you.** DoorDash: the founders wanted Thai food delivered in the suburbs [7].
3. **What would you work on for 10 years, even if it didn't succeed?** Boom Supersonic came from this [7].
4. **Look for things that changed in the world recently.** PlanGrid put construction blueprints on tablets -- made possible by the iPad [7].
5. **Find successful companies and look for new variants.** Warning: ideas like "Uber for X" are often SISPs [7].
6. **Ask people you know for problems they want solved.** Works best when asking other startup founders [7].
7. **Look for broken industries.** LendUp disrupted payday lending [7].

Two additional AI-era recipes from Taggar [18]:
8. **Collapse multi-sided marketplaces.** What marketplaces that are three-sided or four-sided suddenly become two-sided with AI? Any marketplace with a human intermediary is a candidate [18].
9. **Revive failed full-stack ideas.** Look at tech-enabled services that failed in the 2010s because human operations destroyed margins [18].

## Evaluating Problem Quality

Seibel provides a diagnostic framework [8]:

- **Frequency**: How often does the user encounter the problem? Car shopping happens once every seven years -- the real customer is the car dealer, who has the problem every day [8].
- **Intensity**: How painful is the problem? Uber works because transportation is high-intensity and high-frequency [8].
- **Willingness to pay**: Starting with a price is almost always better than starting free [8].
- **How easy are customers to find?** If your customers are ridiculously hard to reach, you need a solution for that upfront [8].

## First Principles Thinking

Tan argues that billion-dollar startups are built by founders who break free from analogy-based thinking through three qualities [15]:

1. **Curiosity**: asking "why" repeatedly. The Toyota Production System's five-whys analysis exemplifies this [15].
2. **Empathy**: getting direct experience with the customer. Airbnb's founders literally slept under the same roof as their first users [15].
3. **Serenity**: accepting what you cannot change and fighting battles you can win. DoorDash retreated to the suburbs where competitors were not, found PMF and profitability there, then used those profits to win the cities [15].

## Following Your Passion: A Nuanced View

Caldwell and Seibel address the "follow your passion" advice directly [13]. The naive version -- "I'm passionate about X, so I'll build a startup about X" -- often leads to solutions in search of problems. The productive version is to develop passion through deep engagement with a real problem [13].

## Ideas That Were Mocked Before Succeeding

Many of the largest companies started with ideas that seemed unpopular or foolish [19]. Airbnb was rejected by every VC. Uber was dismissed as a luxury black car service. Instagram was a pivot from Burbn. Slack emerged from the wreckage of a failed video game called Glitch [19]. The pattern: the idea sounds bad to most people, which is precisely why the opportunity exists [19].

## New Technology Creates New Businesses

Caldwell and Seibel argue that major technology shifts (like AI) create the best startup opportunities because they enable things that were previously impossible [20]. The pattern repeats: the internet created Google, Amazon, and eBay; mobile created Uber, Instacart, and Snapchat; AI is creating the next wave. The founders best positioned to capture these opportunities are those who deeply understand the new technology [20].

## Competition Is Not What Kills You

A crowded market is a good sign -- it means demand exists and no solution is good enough [1]. "Way more startups hose themselves than get crushed by competitors" [3]. The three main self-destruction patterns: internal disputes, inertia, and ignoring users [3].

## The Marginal Niche Mistake

Most YC applicants suffer from choosing a small, obscure niche to avoid competition [2]. Graham compares this to a child closing his eyes when a fly ball comes his way. "If you make anything good, you're going to have competitors, so you may as well face that" [2].

## Having No Idea Is Not Fatal

Graham argues that not having an idea at the outset is not necessarily a problem, because most startups change their idea anyway. In the average YC startup, he estimates 70% of the idea is new at the end of the first three months [6]. Friedman reinforces: "You need a good enough initial idea, combined with great execution" -- Google was the 20th search engine, Facebook the 20th social network [7].

## The MLOps Lesson: Being Early Is Not Being Wrong

Friedman recounts a cautionary-then-vindication tale: in 2019, there were more YC applications for ML tooling companies than for companies applying ML to products [18]. The tooling companies had no customers because the ML did not work yet. Companies like Replicate stopped working on their product for months -- then exploded overnight when image diffusion models arrived. Deepgram, founded by two string theory PhDs, built speech-to-text technology that "didn't really work that well for a long time" -- then exploded when voice agents needed speech-to-text infrastructure [18].

The lesson: following genuine curiosity about technology, as [[Paul Graham]] advised, is how these founders ended up positioned correctly [18].

## References

1. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
3. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
4. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
5. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2005)
6. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
7. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8g-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (2020)
8. [How to Get and Test Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7x-how-to-get-and-test-ideas) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2018)
9. [Where Do Great Startup Ideas Come From?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DU-dalton-michael-where-do-great-startup-ideas-come-from) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
10. [Where Do Great Startup Ideas Come From?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DU-dalton-michael-where-do-great-startup-ideas-come-from) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
11. [Simple Products That Became Big Companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DT-dalton-michael-simple-products-that-became-big-companies) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
12. [One Order of Operations for Starting a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/CP-one-order-of-operations-for-starting-a-startup) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
13. [Should You Follow Your Passion?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DW-dalton-michael-should-you-follow-your-passion) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2021)
14. [Why Now? The Key to Million Dollar Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jt-garry-s-channel-why-now-the-key-to-million-dollar-startup-ideas) -- [[Garry Tan]] (2021)
15. [3 Steps for Billion Dollar Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Js-garry-s-channel-3-steps-for-billion-dollar-startups-how-airbnb-doordash-succeeded) -- [[Garry Tan]] (2020)
16. [Why Founders Shouldn't Think Like Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Kk-why-founders-shouldn-t-think-like-investors) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
17. [Tarpit Ideas: The Sequel](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LH-tarpit-ideas-the-sequel) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
18. [Startup Ideas You Can Now Build With AI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K4s6Cgicw_A) -- [[Garry Tan]], [[Harj Taggar]], [[Jared Friedman]], Diana Hu (2025)
19. [Unpopular Ideas That Became Billion-Dollar Businesses](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=placeholder) -- Y Combinator (2025)
20. [How New Technology Creates New Businesses](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/L7-how-new-technology-creates-new-businesses) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)

title: Startup Terminology
body:
# Startup Terminology

[[Dalton Caldwell]] provides definitions for the most common terms in the startup world, emphasizing that many are less precise than founders assume [1].

## MVP (Minimum Viable Product)

The keyword is "viable." A product that does not work and is useless to everyone is not viable. An MVP must be useful enough to serve some purpose for the customer. "It doesn't just mean a simple product that you built that doesn't do anything. It's got to be useful to someone for it to be viable" [1].

## Product-Market Fit (PMF)

The state where a startup has built something people use and like, and the biggest issue is no longer defining what people want but growing to serve as many people as possible [1]. Pre-PMF, startups spend all their time testing assumptions and talking to customers. Post-PMF, the job is to maintain that state and scale. "The tactics and priorities you should have are very different depending on which of these two modes you're in" [1]. See [[Product-Market Fit]] for the full treatment.

## Burn Rate

How much money the bank account decreases each month. If you start a month with $1 million and end with $900,000, the burn rate is $100,000 [1]. "Not paying attention to this is harmful to your startup's health" [1].

## Venture Capital

VCs invest small amounts to buy equity in startups with the hope that enough become very large to return multiples of the fund. The majority of a VC portfolio does not succeed, but investments in the really big winners pay for everything [1]. Caldwell notes the original venture capital was in the whaling industry: investors funded multiple ships hoping one successful whale capture would pay for all the failed expeditions [1].

## Angel Investor

An individual investing their own personal money (not from a fund), typically at the earliest stage. They write smaller checks ($20K-$50K) and do not do it as a full-time job. There are no qualifications; it is usually someone who has money and invests as a hobby or side activity [1].

## Seed Round

The first meaningful amount of money a startup raises. There is no technical definition -- it can range from $300K on a SAFE to much larger amounts. It is the first round of any consequence, and "whatever amount of money it is that you raised, you would refer to as a seed round" [1].

## Series A, B, C

Subsequent rounds after the seed. Key differences from seed rounds: a lead investor is typically involved, they may take a board seat, and they traditionally get significant ownership (around 20% in a Series A). Each subsequent round increments the letter. Companies at late stages may reach Series E, G, or even H [1].

## SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity)

A financial instrument created by Y Combinator (specifically Carolyn Levy) as an alternative to convertible notes. A SAFE has fewer terms, no interest rate, no maturity date, and no repayment requirement. "The advantage of a SAFE is there's just less terms to worry about" [1]. See [[Seed Fundraising]] for detailed SAFE mechanics.

## Convertible Note

A debt-like instrument where an investor gives money that converts to equity at a future priced round. It typically includes interest and may require repayment. Caldwell advises founders to read the fine print carefully [1].

## TAM (Total Addressable Market)

A thought experiment: if 100% of potential customers purchased the product, how much revenue would the company generate? No one reaches 100% of TAM [1].

Critically, TAM calculations can be wrong. Tesla's TAM for electric cars was tiny at founding -- you had to believe the EV market would grow to approximate the entire car market. Uber's TAM for taxis was small, but Uber dramatically expanded the market by creating a better experience that attracted riders who never used taxis [1]. "Great products tend to grow the TAM" [1].

## Profitability

Revenue exceeding expenses. The important nuance: how do profit margins change at scale? Many startups are unprofitable at small scale but have attractive margins at large scale. Google made no revenue for its first few years but had extremely high margins on advertising once monetization launched [1].

## Bootstrapping

Starting a company without raising venture capital, using personal funds or business revenue instead. A good option for businesses that will reach $5-10M in annual revenue and not grow beyond that. "One of the reasons bootstrapping is a great option is it's entirely within your control. You don't need the approval of an investor" [1].

## ARR and MRR

**ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue):** Revenue from customers on contracts that renew annually. Ten customers on $100K annual contracts = $1M ARR [1].

**MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue):** Revenue from monthly subscriptions. A product like ChatGPT that bills monthly would report MRR rather than ARR [1].

The "recurring" is important: the revenue must genuinely recur through subscriptions or renewing contracts [1].

## Valuation

The price at which the last investor invested. For a company that raised $2M on a $20M post-money SAFE, the valuation is $20M. Unlike public stock prices, startup valuations do not imply there are always buyers and sellers at that price. "Startups with high valuations don't always succeed" [1].

## IPO (Initial Public Offering)

When a privately held company sells shares on public exchanges (NASDAQ, NYSE). An IPO allows employees, founders, and investors to realize value from their ownership. It signals that a company is "financially mature, growing well, and has built something of enduring value" [1].

## Equity and Stock Options

Equity is direct ownership. Stock options are rights to purchase equity at a future date at a preset price. Employees at startups commonly receive stock options rather than equity directly. Caldwell advises: "Always read the fine print and understand exactly what you are buying and selling" [1].

## References

1. [Starting a Company? The Key Terms You Should Know](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Lk-starting-a-company-the-key-terms-you-should-know) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (2024)

title: Early-Stage Mistakes
body:
# Early-Stage Mistakes

In a sense there is just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want [1]. [[Paul Graham]] argues that if you make something users want, you will probably be fine, whatever else you do or don't do. And if you don't, you are dead, whatever else you do. His 2006 essay catalogues 18 specific mistakes, nearly all of which funnel through that one root cause [1]. [[Michael Seibel]] and [[Jessica Livingston]] add additional patterns from having seen thousands of YC companies.

## The Root Cause: Not Making Something Users Want

Graham states this is so much harder than it sounds that most startups fail at it. "I don't know why it's so hard to make something people want. It seems like it should be straightforward. But you can tell it must be hard by how few startups do it" [1].

The pattern across his essays is consistent: the biggest mistake is not trying hard enough [1], not understanding users deeply enough [1], or building something nobody actually needs (the "sitcom startup idea" problem described in [[Startup Ideas]]) [3].

## First-Time Founder Mistakes (Seibel)

[[Michael Seibel]] identifies a distinct set of mistakes specific to first-time founders [7]:

**Choosing a problem you don't care about.** While not fatal on its own, many startups that fail do so because founders lose motivation. "They were trying to pick a problem that they thought people might want, or they thought could grow quickly, or they thought might be cool, but it wasn't actually something they were willing to devote a significant amount of time -- like, you know, five years plus -- of their life to solving" [7].

**Helping users you don't care about.** Seibel uses Justin.tv as his example: the founders were excited by the general idea of democratizing live video but were not in love with their initial users. Only when Emmett Shear refocused the company around video game streaming -- "Emmett was a gamer and he loved gamers" -- did the company find its stride as Twitch [7].

**Choosing co-founders you don't know well.** Pre-existing relationships matter because startups are so hard that founders need to know whether they can survive hard times together. That relationship can be a friendship, co-worker relationship, or school project -- "that you didn't just find them on the street yesterday" [7].

**Not having transparent conversations with co-founders.** The typical drama topics: performance ("Is my co-founder working as hard as I am?"), goals ("Are we trying to accomplish the same goal?"), and roles ("Whose job is it to do what?"). "Oftentimes, founders just don't have these completely transparent, honest conversations about these topics. And as a result, resentment builds up and the relationship degrades" [7].

**Not launching.** Seibel sees founders who spend months or even years building before shipping anything to users, out of fear that the product is not ready [7].

**Working on non-essential problems.** Many first-time founders get distracted by things that feel productive -- PR, conferences, partnership deals -- but do not move the core metrics [7].

## The 18 Mistakes (Graham)

Graham's complete taxonomy from October 2006 [1]:

### Team Mistakes

**1. Single Founder.** Few successful startups were founded by one person. Having a single founder is "a vote of no confidence" -- it probably means the founder couldn't talk any friends into joining, which is alarming because friends know the founder best [1]. The emotional dimension may matter most: "The low points in a startup are so low that few could bear them alone. When you have multiple founders, esprit de corps binds them together in a way that seems to violate conservation laws. Each thinks 'I can't let my friends down'" [1].

**6. Hiring Bad Programmers.** This is primarily a problem for non-technical founders. "Business guys can't tell which are the good programmers. They don't even get a shot at the best ones, because no one really good wants a job implementing the vision of a business guy" [1].

**17. Fights Between Founders.** About 20% of YC-funded startups have had a founder leave [1]. YC now advises founders to vest so there will be an orderly way for people to quit [1].

### Idea and Product Mistakes

**3. Marginal Niche.** Choosing a small, obscure niche to avoid competition is the startup equivalent of a child closing his eyes when a fly ball comes his way [1].

**4. Derivative Idea.** Imitating existing companies is one source of ideas but not the best. "Instead of starting from companies and working back to the problems they solved, look for problems and imagine the company that might solve them" [1].

**5. Obstinacy.** Startups are more like science than the Olympics: you need to follow the trail wherever it leads, not stick to a rigid plan [1].

**7. Choosing the Wrong Platform.** Java applets "probably killed just about 100% of the startups who believed that" [1].

**10. Having No Specific User in Mind.** If you are building for a group that does not include you, you are "flying on instruments" and must be empirical [1].

### Launch and Execution Mistakes

**8. Slowness in Launching.** "I've seen a lot of startups die because they were too slow to release stuff, and none because they were too quick" [2].

**9. Launching Too Early.** Less common than launching too slowly by a factor of a hundred [1].

**16. Not Wanting to Get Your Hands Dirty.** Programmers would rather write code than sell [1].

### Financial Mistakes

**11. Raising Too Little Money.** Rule of thumb: take 50-100% more than you think you will need [1].

**12. Spending Too Much.** The classic way: hiring a lot of people [1]. See [[Financial Survival]] for the full treatment.

**13. Raising Too Much Money.** "When you raise a lot of money, your company moves to the suburbs and has kids" [1].

**14. Poor Investor Management.** Founders should neither ignore investors nor let them run the company [1].

**15. Sacrificing Users to Supposed Profit.** "It is irresponsible not to think about business models. It's just ten times more irresponsible not to think about the product" [1].

### Commitment Mistakes

**18. A Half-Hearted Effort.** The most common type of failure is the startup that never gets going [1].

**2. Bad Location.** Silicon Valley dominates [1].

## The Jessica Livingston Seven-Point Framework

After seeing over 1,000 companies go through YC, [[Jessica Livingston]] distills the avoidance of failure into seven points [8]:

1. **Make something people want.** "Nothing else you do will matter if you are not making something people want" [8].
2. **Stay focused.** The most successful founders are "totally focused on their product and their users, to the point of being fanatical" [8]. Distractions that look productive but are not: grabbing coffee with investors, talking with acquirers, networking, recruiting boards of advisors, doing partnerships, spending time on PR before PMF, arguing on social media, going to conferences [8].
3. **Don't worry about being a woman.** "While I'll tell you that it is going to be harder for you as a woman, it's not going to be so much harder that it will make the difference between success and failure" [8].
4. **Measure your growth.** "You make what you measure." At least 10% per month growth means you are on the right track [8].
5. **Know if you're default alive.** See [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] [8].
6. **Keep expenses low.** "Overhiring is the big mistake in the second phase of a startup, as making something no one wants is in the first phase" [8].
7. **Fundraising gets harder.** "Series A investors have a totally different attitude from seed investors. Seed investors are looking for promise. Series A investors are looking for performance" [8].

## Playing House

Graham coined the term "playing house" for a pattern he saw repeatedly at YC [4]: young founders who go through all the outward motions of starting a startup -- make up a plausible idea, raise money, rent a cool office, hire people -- while neglecting the one essential thing: making something people want [4].

## The Self-Destruction Pattern

Graham argues consistently that competitors are not the primary threat. "Way more startups hose themselves than get crushed by competitors" [2]. The three main ways startups destroy themselves:

1. **Internal disputes** -- fights between founders, usually traceable to choosing the wrong cofounder
2. **Inertia** -- not launching, not iterating, not responding to what users need
3. **Ignoring users** -- the worst of the three [2]
